description: Diagnostic criteria for conditions seen in MIMIC hide-one-dx benchmark.
policy: Every criterion must cite a verifiable source URL with the actual organization or peer-reviewed publication. Verbatim quotes preferred. NEVER fabricate.

schema.name: condition name
schema.criterion_quote: verbatim quote from source if available

source.title: document title

atrial_fibrillation.name: Atrial Fibrillation (ACC/AHA classification)
atrial_fibrillation.criterion: ECG documentation of AF. Classified by Stage (1–4) and pattern: paroxysmal (3A — terminates ≤7d), persistent (3B — sustained >7d), long-standing persistent (3C — >12 mo), successful ablation (3D), permanent (no further rhythm control)

source.title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation

atrial_fibrillation.notes: Stroke risk via CHA2DS2-VASc. Anticoagulation generally recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women.

heart_failure.name: Heart Failure (ACC/AHA/HFSA classification)
heart_failure.criterion: Symptomatic HF (Stage C) classified by LVEF: HFrEF ≤40%; HFimpEF previously ≤40% now >40%; HFmrEF 41–49% + evidence of increased LV filling pressures; HFpEF ≥50% + evidence of increased LV filling pressures
heart_failure.criterion_quote: HFrEF: LVEF ≤40%. HFmrEF: LVEF 41-49% and evidence of increased LV filling pressures. HFpEF: LVEF ≥50% and evidence of increased LV filling pressures.

stages.A: At risk for HF (e.g., HTN, DM, obesity, family hx) — no structural disease/symptoms
stages.B: Pre-HF — structural disease OR ↑BNP/NT-proBNP without symptoms
stages.D: Advanced HF refractory to optimized GDMT, requiring specialized intervention

source.title: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure

ischemic_stroke.name: Acute Ischemic Stroke (AHA/ASA)
ischemic_stroke.criterion: Acute focal neurologic deficit due to ischemic vascular event + neuroimaging (MRI DWI preferred, or CT) consistent with cerebral ischemia and excluding hemorrhage. Severity quantified with NIHSS

source.title: 2019 Update to the 2018 Guidelines for the Early Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke

ischemic_stroke.notes: IV tPA window: 4.5 hours from symptom onset / last known normal. Thrombectomy generally up to 24h with imaging selection.

myocardial_infarction.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction (Type 1, 2, or 3)
myocardial_infarction.criterion: Detection of a rise and/or fall of cardiac troponin (cTn) with at least one value above the 99th percentile URL AND clinical evidence of acute myocardial ischaemia (symptoms, new ischemic ECG changes, pathologic Q waves, imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium or new regional wall motion abnormality, or identification of a coronary thrombus by angiography/autopsy)
myocardial_infarction.criterion_quote: Acute myocardial infarction should be used when there is acute myocardial injury with clinical evidence of acute myocardial ischaemia and with detection of a rise and/or fall of cTn values with at least one value above the 99th percentile URL.

source.title: Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction (2018)

myocardial_infarction.notes: Myocardial INJURY (no MI) = elevated troponin alone. MI requires troponin Δ + ischemia evidence.

hypertension.name: Hypertension (AHA/ACC 2017)
hypertension.criterion: Normal <120/80; Elevated 120–129 SBP and <80 DBP; Stage 1 HTN 130–139 SBP or 80–89 DBP; Stage 2 HTN ≥140 SBP or ≥90 DBP; Hypertensive crisis >180 SBP and/or >120 DBP
hypertension.criterion_quote: Stage 1 hypertension: 130-139/80-89 mmHg. Stage 2 hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg. Hypertensive crisis: Top number over 180 and/or bottom number over 120.

source.title: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults

hypertension.notes: Hypertensive urgency = crisis BP without acute end-organ damage. Hypertensive emergency = crisis BP WITH acute end-organ damage.

asthma.name: Asthma (GINA)
asthma.criterion: History of variable respiratory symptoms (wheeze, dyspnea, chest tightness, cough) + documented variable expiratory airflow limitation: post-bronchodilator FEV1 or FVC increase ≥12% AND ≥200 mL 10–15 min after 200–400 mcg salbutamol (or PEF increase ≥20%)
asthma.criterion_quote: an increase in FEV1 or FVC of ≥12% and ≥200 ml (or in PEF of ≥20%) compared with baseline supports the diagnosis.

source.title: Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention

copd.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
copd.criterion: Dyspnea, chronic cough or sputum production, and/or history of risk factor exposure + spirometry showing post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70
copd.criterion_quote: spirometry that demonstrates the presence of a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC < 0.7 is mandatory to establish the diagnosis of COPD.

source.org: GOLD (Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease)
source.title: Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of COPD: 2024 Report

copd_exacerbation.name: COPD Exacerbation
copd_exacerbation.criterion: Acute worsening of respiratory symptoms (dyspnea, cough, sputum) leading to additional therapy. Severity: Mild — managed with SABA only; Moderate — systemic corticosteroids and/or antibiotics; Severe — hospitalization or ED visit

source.title: Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of COPD: 2024 Report

ards.name: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS, Berlin Definition)
ards.criterion: All four required: (1) acute onset within 1 week of clinical insult; (2) bilateral opacities on CXR or CT not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules; (3) respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (objective assessment needed if no risk factor); (4) PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mmHg with PEEP or CPAP ≥5 cm H2O
ards.criterion_quote: ARDS is defined by acute onset, bilateral lung infiltrates on chest radiography or CT scan of a non-cardiac origin, and a PaO2/FiO2 ratio of less than 300 mm Hg ... and included the requirement of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) or continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) of greater than or equal to 5 cm H20.

source.org: ARDS Definition Task Force (Ranieri et al.)
source.title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: The Berlin Definition

anemia.name: Anemia
anemia.criterion: Hb <130 g/L (13.0 g/dL) in men; Hb <120 g/L (12.0 g/dL) in non-pregnant women
anemia.criterion_quote: WHO recommends that anaemia be defined when haemoglobin concentration is <130g/L in men, <120g/L in non-pregnant women, <110g/L in pregnant women.

source.title: Nutritional anaemias: tools for effective prevention and control

anemia.notes: Thresholds reflect the lower 5th centile of healthy reference population. Severity per WHO: mild Hb 11–11.9 g/dL non-preg women / 11–12.9 men; moderate 8–10.9; severe <8.

acute_cholecystitis.name: Acute Cholecystitis (Tokyo Guidelines TG18)
acute_cholecystitis.criterion: Suspected if ≥1 A AND ≥1 B; Definitive if ≥1 A AND ≥1 B AND C. A = Local signs (Murphy's sign or RUQ mass/pain/tenderness). B = Systemic signs (fever, ↑CRP, ↑WBC). C = Imaging findings characteristic of acute cholecystitis (US: wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy)

stages.Grade I (Mild): Acute cholecystitis without features of moderate/severe
stages.Grade II (Moderate): Any of: WBC >18,000; palpable tender RUQ mass; sx >72 h; marked local inflammation (gangrenous/emphysematous/pericholecystic abscess/hepatic abscess/biliary peritonitis)
stages.Grade III (Severe): Organ dysfunction (cardiovascular, neurological, respiratory, renal, hepatic, hematological)

source.org: Yokoe et al. / Tokyo Guidelines Revision Committee
source.title: Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholecystitis

acute_pancreatitis.name: Acute Pancreatitis (Revised Atlanta Classification)
acute_pancreatitis.criterion: At least 2 of 3: (1) typical abdominal pain; (2) serum amylase or lipase >3× upper limit of normal; (3) imaging findings consistent with AP (contrast-enhanced CT, MRI, or transabdominal US)
acute_pancreatitis.criterion_quote: Acute pancreatitis is diagnosed according to the 2012 revised Atlanta criteria, which require at least two of the following: (1) typical abdominal pain, (2) serum amylase or lipase levels exceeding three times the upper normal limit, or (3) imaging findings indicative of AP.

stages.Mild: No organ failure and no local/systemic complications
stages.Moderately Severe: Transient organ failure (<2 days), local complications, and/or exacerbation of comorbidity

source.org: Acute Pancreatitis Classification Working Group (Banks et al.)
source.title: Classification of acute pancreatitis—2012: revision of the Atlanta classification and definitions by international consensus

meld_na.name: Model for End-Stage Liver Disease — Sodium (MELD-Na)
meld_na.criterion: MELD = 3.78×ln(bilirubin) + 11.2×ln(INR) + 9.57×ln(creatinine) + 6.43 (lab values floored at 1.0; Cr capped at 4.0). MELD-Na = MELD + 1.32×(137−Na) − 0.033×MELD×(137−Na). Na clamped 125–137; MELD clamped 6–40

source.org: UNOS (US Organ Procurement Network); Kim et al.
source.title: Hyponatremia and Mortality among Patients on the Liver-Transplant Waiting List (NEJM 2008); MELD-Na adopted by UNOS 2016
source.year: 2016 (UNOS adoption); original MELD: Kamath 2001 Hepatology; MELD-Na: Kim 2008 NEJM

child_pugh.name: Cirrhosis severity (Child-Pugh / CTP score)
child_pugh.criterion: Sum of 5 components (1–3 points each, total 5–15): total bilirubin (<2 / 2–3 / >3 mg/dL); albumin (>3.5 / 2.8–3.5 / <2.8 g/dL); INR (<1.7 / 1.7–2.3 / >2.3); ascites (none/mild-mod/severe); hepatic encephalopathy (none / grade I–II / III–IV). Class A = 5–6; Class B = 7–9; Class C = 10–15

source.title: Transection of the oesophagus for bleeding oesophageal varices
source.year: 1973 (Br J Surg); modified by Pugh from Child & Turcotte 1964

child_pugh.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Source verifies CTP components and A/B/C cutoffs, but criterion_quote field is empty in database.]

hepatic_encephalopathy.name: Hepatic Encephalopathy (HE)
hepatic_encephalopathy.criterion: Cirrhosis or porto-systemic shunting + neuropsychiatric findings graded by West Haven Criteria. Overt HE = grades II–IV; Covert HE = minimal/grade I

stages.Grade 0 / Minimal: No clinical findings but neuropsychological/electrophysiological abnormalities
stages.Grade I: Trivial lack of awareness, euphoria/anxiety, shortened attention span, impaired performance of addition
stages.Grade II: Lethargy or apathy, minimal disorientation for time/place, subtle personality change, inappropriate behavior, impaired performance of subtraction
stages.Grade III: Somnolence to semi-stupor (responsive to verbal stimuli), confusion, gross disorientation
stages.Grade IV: Coma — unresponsive to verbal or noxious stimuli

source.title: Hepatic encephalopathy in chronic liver disease: 2014 Practice Guideline by the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases and the European Association for the Study of the Liver

infective_endocarditis.name: Infective Endocarditis (2023 Duke-ISCVID criteria)
infective_endocarditis.criterion: Definite IE: pathologic criteria OR clinical criteria (2 major; 1 major + 3 minor; or 5 minor). Possible IE: 1 major + 1 minor; or 3 minor. Rejected: alternate dx, resolution with ≤4 d abx, or criteria not met. Domains: pathologic, microbiologic, imaging, surgical (new), clinical (minor)

source.org: International Society for Cardiovascular Infectious Diseases (ISCVID)
source.title: The 2023 Duke-International Society for Cardiovascular Infectious Diseases Criteria for Infective Endocarditis: Updating the Modified Duke Criteria

cdi.name: Clostridioides difficile Infection (CDI)
cdi.criterion: Clinical: ≥3 unexplained new-onset unformed stools in 24 h + positive C. difficile lab test (NAAT, GDH+toxin, or stepwise algorithm). NAAT alone may detect colonization (not active infection)
cdi.criterion_quote: Patients with ≥3 unexplained and new onset unformed stools in 24 hours are the preferred target population for testing for CDI.

source.title: Focused Update Guidelines on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults

uti_uncomplicated.name: Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
uti_uncomplicated.criterion: Lower urinary tract symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain) + pyuria + microbiologic confirmation (≥10⁵ CFU/mL on voided urine OR lower in catheterized samples with sx)
uti_uncomplicated.criterion_quote: Uncomplicated UTI as occurring in a female patient with lower urinary tract symptoms and pyuria, and microbiologic confirmation via culture is recommended.
uti_uncomplicated.notes: Asymptomatic bacteriuria = ≥10⁵ CFU/mL without symptoms (not UTI).

source.title: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Complicated UTI / Asymptomatic Bacteriuria

cauti.name: Catheter-Associated UTI (CAUTI)
cauti.criterion: Symptoms or signs compatible with UTI (no other identified source) + ≥10³ CFU/mL of ≥1 bacterial species in a patient with current indwelling urinary catheter or removed within prior 48 h
cauti.criterion_quote: Catheter-related UTI in patients whose urinary catheter has been removed within the previous 48 hours by the presence of symptoms or signs compatible with UTI with no other identified source of infection along with 1000 or more colony-forming units (CFU)/mL of 1 or more bacterial species.

source.title: Diagnosis, Prevention, and Treatment of Catheter-Associated UTI in Adults

cap_curb65.name: CURB-65 Score (CAP severity)
cap_curb65.criterion: 1 point for each of 5: Confusion (new); Urea (BUN) >19 mg/dL or urea >7 mmol/L; Respiratory rate ≥30; Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60); age ≥65. Total 0–5. Score 0–1 outpatient; 2 short admission; 3+ severe — consider ICU

source.org: Lim et al. / British Thoracic Society
source.title: Defining community acquired pneumonia severity on presentation to hospital: an international derivation and validation study

community_acquired_pneumonia.name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)
community_acquired_pneumonia.criterion: New pulmonary infiltrate on chest imaging + ≥1 respiratory symptom (cough, dyspnea, sputum production) + ≥1 other sign (fever, abnormal auscultation)
community_acquired_pneumonia.criterion_quote: CAP is defined as a newly recognized pulmonary infiltrate(s) on chest imaging plus at least one respiratory symptom plus at least one other symptom/sign or finding.

stages.Severe CAP: 1 major criterion (septic shock requiring vasopressors OR respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation) OR ≥3 minor criteria (RR ≥30, PaO2/FiO2 ≤250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, uremia BUN ≥20, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hypothermia, hypotension requiring aggressive fluids)

source.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia

sepsis.name: Sepsis (Sepsis-3)
sepsis.criterion: Suspected/documented infection + acute increase in SOFA score of ≥2 points (from baseline, presumed zero if not known)
sepsis.criterion_quote: Sepsis is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. ... organ dysfunction can be represented by an increase in the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score of 2 points or more.

source.org: SCCM + ESICM Task Force (Singer et al.)
source.title: The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3)

sepsis.notes: qSOFA bedside screen: altered mentation + SBP ≤100 + RR ≥22 (≥2 of 3 = positive).

septic_shock.name: Septic shock (Sepsis-3)
septic_shock.criterion: Sepsis + need for vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation

source.org: SCCM + ESICM Task Force (Singer et al.)
source.title: The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3)

hyponatremia.name: Hyponatremia
hyponatremia.criterion_quote: Hyponatremia is decrease in serum sodium concentration < 136 mEq/L (< 136 mmol/L)

source.title: Hyponatremia

hypernatremia.name: Hypernatremia
hypernatremia.criterion_quote: Hypernatremia is a serum sodium concentration > 145 mEq/L (> 145 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypernatremia

hypocalcemia.name: Hypocalcemia
hypocalcemia.criterion: Total serum Ca <8.8 mg/dL (with normal plasma protein) OR ionized serum Ca <4.7 mg/dL
hypocalcemia.criterion_quote: Hypocalcemia is a total serum calcium concentration < 8.8 mg/dL (< 2.20 mmol/L) in the presence of normal plasma protein concentrations, or a serum ionized calcium concentration < 4.7 mg/dL (< 1.17 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypocalcemia

hypercalcemia.name: Hypercalcemia
hypercalcemia.criterion: Total serum Ca >10.4 mg/dL OR ionized serum Ca >5.2 mg/dL
hypercalcemia.criterion_quote: Hypercalcemia is a total serum calcium concentration > 10.4 mg/dL (> 2.60 mmol/L) or ionized serum calcium > 5.2 mg/dL (> 1.30 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypercalcemia

hypomagnesemia.name: Hypomagnesemia
hypomagnesemia.criterion_quote: Hypomagnesemia is serum magnesium concentration < 1.8 mg/dL (< 0.70 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypomagnesemia

hypermagnesemia.name: Hypermagnesemia
hypermagnesemia.criterion_quote: Hypermagnesemia is a serum magnesium concentration > 2.6 mg/dL (> 1.05 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypermagnesemia

hypophosphatemia.name: Hypophosphatemia
hypophosphatemia.criterion_quote: Hypophosphatemia is a serum phosphate concentration < 2.5 mg/dL (0.81 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypophosphatemia

hyperphosphatemia.name: Hyperphosphatemia
hyperphosphatemia.criterion_quote: Hyperphosphatemia is a serum phosphate concentration > 4.5 mg/dL (> 1.46 mmol/L)

source.title: Hyperphosphatemia

hypokalemia.name: Hypokalemia
hypokalemia.criterion_quote: Hypokalemia is serum potassium concentration < 3.5 mEq/L (< 3.5 mmol/L)

source.title: Hypokalemia

hyperkalemia.name: Hyperkalemia
hyperkalemia.criterion_quote: Hyperkalemia is a serum potassium concentration > 5.5 mEq/L (> 5.5 mmol/L)

source.title: Hyperkalemia

dka.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
dka.criterion: All three: (1) glucose ≥200 mg/dL OR prior diabetes hx; (2) beta-hydroxybutyrate ≥3.0 mmol/L OR urine ketone strip ≥2+; (3) venous pH <7.3 AND/OR HCO3 <18 mmol/L
dka.criterion_quote: DKA diagnostic criteria include: glucose ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) or prior history of diabetes; beta-hydroxybutyrate concentration ≥3.0 mmol/L or urine ketone strip 2+ or greater; and pH <7.3 and/or bicarbonate concentration <18 mmol/L.

source.org: ADA + EASD + JBDS + AACE + DTS Consensus
source.title: Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes: A Consensus Report

dka.notes: Anion gap criterion was removed in 2024. Resolution: glucose <200 + HCO3 ≥18 + venous pH >7.3.

hhs.name: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
hhs.criterion: All four: (1) plasma glucose ≥600 mg/dL; (2) effective serum osmolality >300 mOsm/kg OR total serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg; (3) beta-hydroxybutyrate <3.0 mmol/L OR urine ketone ≤2+; (4) venous pH ≥7.3 AND HCO3 ≥15 mmol/L
hhs.criterion_quote: HHS requires: (1) plasma glucose ≥600 mg/dL; (2) effective serum osmolality >300 mOsm/kg or total serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg; (3) absence of significant ketonemia (beta-hydroxybutyrate <3.0 mmol/L or urine ketone ≤2+); (4) absence of acidosis (pH ≥7.3 and bicarbonate ≥15 mmol/L).

source.org: ADA + EASD + JBDS + AACE + DTS Consensus
source.title: Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes: A Consensus Report

diabetes_mellitus.name: Diabetes mellitus
diabetes_mellitus.criterion: Any of: FPG ≥126 mg/dL OR 2-h OGTT plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL OR HbA1c ≥6.5% OR random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL with hyperglycemic symptoms
diabetes_mellitus.criterion_quote: The ADA 2024 criteria are FPG ≥ 126 mg/dL, OGTT 2-hour plasma glucose ≥ 200 mg/dL, and HbA1c ≥ 6.5%.

source.title: Diagnosis and Classification of Diabetes: Standards of Care in Diabetes 2024

hypoglycemia.name: Hypoglycemia
hypoglycemia.criterion: Level 1 (alert): glucose ≤70 mg/dL; Level 2 (clinically significant): glucose <54 mg/dL; Level 3 (severe): altered mental/physical function requiring outside assistance, irrespective of glucose value
hypoglycemia.criterion_quote: Level 1: <70 mg/dL but ≥54 mg/dL. Level 2: <54 mg/dL. Level 3: severe event characterized by altered mental and/or physical functioning that requires assistance from another person for recovery, irrespective of glucose level.

source.title: Glycemic Goals and Hypoglycemia: Standards of Care in Diabetes 2024

malnutrition_glim.name: Adult Malnutrition (GLIM criteria)
malnutrition_glim.criterion: Positive screening + ≥1 phenotypic criterion + ≥1 etiologic criterion. Phenotypic: non-volitional weight loss; low BMI; reduced muscle mass. Etiologic: reduced food intake/assimilation; disease burden/inflammation

source.org: GLIM (Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition) — endorsed by ESPEN, ASPEN, FELANPE, PENSA
source.title: GLIM criteria for the diagnosis of malnutrition – A consensus report from the global clinical nutrition community

malnutrition_aspen.name: Adult Malnutrition (ASPEN/AND 2012 etiology-based)
malnutrition_aspen.criterion: ≥2 of 6 characteristics: insufficient energy intake; weight loss; loss of muscle mass; loss of subcutaneous fat; localized or generalized fluid accumulation that may sometimes mask weight loss; diminished functional status as measured by handgrip strength

source.org: Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics + ASPEN (White et al.)
source.title: Consensus Statement: Characteristics recommended for the identification and documentation of adult malnutrition (undernutrition)

hypothyroidism.name: Hypothyroidism (primary)
hypothyroidism.criterion: Overt: serum TSH elevated (usually >10 mIU/L) + subnormal free T4. Subclinical: TSH above upper reference limit + normal free T4
hypothyroidism.criterion_quote: Subclinical hypothyroidism is characterized by a serum TSH above the upper reference limit in combination with a normal free thyroxine (T4), while an elevated TSH, usually above 10 mIU/L, in combination with a subnormal free T4 characterizes overt hypothyroidism.

source.title: Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism

adrenal_insufficiency_primary.name: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's Disease)
adrenal_insufficiency_primary.criterion: Gold-standard: Cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test — 250 mcg IV/IM; peak cortisol <18 μg/dL (500 nmol/L) at 30 or 60 min indicates adrenal insufficiency. Highly likely if morning cortisol <5 μg/dL (140 nmol/L) + ACTH >2× upper limit of reference
adrenal_insufficiency_primary.criterion_quote: Cosyntropin (synthetic ACTH) 250 mcg IV or IM is administered, and peak cortisol levels below 500 nmol/L (18 μg/dL) (assay dependent) at 30 or 60 minutes indicate adrenal insufficiency.

source.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

rhabdomyolysis.name: Rhabdomyolysis
rhabdomyolysis.criterion: Clinical: muscle injury (pain, swelling, weakness, dark urine) + CK >5× upper limit of normal (≈ >1,000 IU/L). Severe: + myoglobinuria + AKI.
rhabdomyolysis.criterion_quote: A CK level of >5 times the upper limit of normal is typically required for diagnosis (e.g., >1000 IU/L).

source.title: Rhabdomyolysis

siadh.name: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuresis (SIADH/SIAD)
siadh.criterion: Classic Bartter–Schwartz criteria (all required): (1) hypotonic hyponatremia (serum Na <135 + serum osm <275 mOsm/kg); (2) inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osm >100 mOsm/kg); (3) clinical euvolemia; (4) urine sodium >40 mEq/L (with normal salt and water intake); (5) absence of other causes of euvolemic hypoosmolality (hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, diuretic use); (6) normal renal function

source.org: European Society of Endocrinology / European Society of Intensive Care Medicine / European Renal Association (Spasovski et al.)
source.title: Clinical practice guideline on diagnosis and treatment of hyponatraemia

siadh.notes: Original Bartter & Schwartz 1967 criteria; updated by ES/ESICM/ERA 2014.

hit.name: Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)
hit.criterion: Clinical 4Ts score + functional/immunologic assay. 4Ts components: Thrombocytopenia (degree), Timing (5–10 d after heparin start, or <1 d if recent exposure), Thrombosis (or other sequelae), and exclusion of oTher causes. Total 0–8. Low risk 0–3; intermediate 4–5; high ≥6
hit.criterion_quote: The 4Ts scoring system incorporates 4 typical features of HIT: (1) magnitude of thrombocytopenia; (2) timing of thrombocytopenia with respect to heparin exposure; (3) thrombosis or other sequelae of HIT; and (4) likelihood of other causes of thrombocytopenia. A total score of 0-3 corresponds with a low risk, 4-5 with an intermediate risk, and ≥6 with a high risk of HIT.

source.title: American Society of Hematology 2018 Guidelines for Management of VTE: HIT

ottawa_rules.name: Ottawa Ankle / Foot / Knee Rules — Imaging decision tools
ottawa_rules.criterion: Ankle X-ray series required only if pain in malleolar zone AND ≥1: (a) bone tenderness along distal 6 cm of posterior edge OR tip of medial malleolus; (b) bone tenderness along distal 6 cm of posterior edge OR tip of lateral malleolus; (c) inability to bear weight both immediately and in ED (4 steps). Foot X-ray series required only if midfoot pain AND ≥1: (a) bone tenderness at base of 5th metatarsal; (b) bone tenderness at navicular; (c) inability to bear weight. Ottawa Knee Rule — knee X-ray required only if ≥1: (a) age ≥55; (b) tenderness at head of fibula; (c) isolated tenderness of patella; (d) inability to flex knee to 90°; (e) inability to bear weight (4 steps) both immediately and in ED. Sensitivity ~99% for clinically significant fracture; reduces imaging by ~30%
ottawa_rules.criterion_quote: An ankle radiograph series is required only if there is pain in the malleolar zone AND any one of the following criteria: bone tenderness along the distal 6 cm of the posterior edge of the tibia or tip of the medial malleolus, bone tenderness along the distal 6 cm of the posterior edge of the fibula or tip of the lateral malleolus, or inability to bear weight both immediately after injury and in the emergency department (unable to take 4 steps).

source.org: Stiell IG et al. (Ottawa Hospital Research Institute)
source.title: Decision rules for the use of radiography in acute ankle injuries; A study to develop clinical decision rules for the use of radiography in acute knee injuries

ottawa_rules.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed abstract page blocked full criteria text; canonical Stiell 1993 citation.]

c_spine_rules.name: Cervical Spine Clearance — Canadian C-Spine Rule (CCR) & NEXUS Low-Risk Criteria
c_spine_rules.criterion: CCR (alert, stable, GCS 15 trauma patient): Step 1 — any high-risk factor (age ≥65, dangerous mechanism — fall ≥1 m/5 stairs, axial load, MVC at high speed/rollover/ejection, motorized recreational vehicle, bicycle collision, or paresthesia in extremities) → imaging required. Step 2 — any low-risk factor allowing safe assessment of ROM (simple rear-end MVC, sitting in ED, ambulatory at any time, delayed neck pain onset, absence of midline tenderness)? If no → image. Step 3 — patient can actively rotate neck 45° left and right? If no → image; if yes → no imaging. Sensitivity ~100%, specificity ~42%. NEXUS criteria: image NOT required if ALL 5 are present — (1) no posterior midline C-spine tenderness, (2) no evidence of intoxication, (3) normal level of consciousness/alertness, (4) no focal neurologic deficit, (5) no painful distracting injury. Sens ~99.9%, spec ~12.9%
c_spine_rules.criterion_quote: The Canadian C-Spine Rule is based on three high-risk criteria, five low-risk criteria, and the ability of patients to rotate their necks. … The NEXUS criteria … no posterior midline cervical tenderness, no evidence of intoxication, a normal level of consciousness, no focal neurologic deficit, and no painful distracting injury.

source.org: Stiell IG et al. (CCR); Hoffman JR et al. (NEXUS)
source.title: The Canadian C-spine rule versus the NEXUS low-risk criteria in patients with trauma

csrs_syncope.name: Canadian Syncope Risk Score (CSRS) — 30-day serious outcome in ED syncope
csrs_syncope.criterion: Apply after initial ED workup. Variables (with point values): clinical predisposition to vasovagal syncope (warm-crowded place, prolonged standing, fear/emotion/pain) −1; heart disease history (CAD, AF, CHF, valvular) +1; SBP <90 or >180 mmHg in the ED at any reading +2; troponin >99th percentile +2; abnormal QRS axis (<−30° or >100°) +1; QRS duration >130 ms +1; QTc >480 ms +2; ED diagnosis vasovagal −2; ED diagnosis cardiac +2. Total range −3 to +11. Risk strata for 30-day serious outcome (arrhythmia, MI, structural heart disease, hemorrhage, death): very low ≤ −2 (<1%); low −1 to 0 (~2%); medium 1–3 (~5–8%); high 4–5 (~12–19%); very high ≥6 (~28–48%). AUC 0.88
csrs_syncope.criterion_quote: The CSRS contains nine predictors: predisposition to vasovagal syncope, heart disease, systolic pressure <90 or >180mmHg in the ED, troponin level >99th percentile, abnormal QRS axis, QRS duration >130ms, QTc interval >480ms and an ED diagnosis of vasovagal or cardiac syncope. … A score of −2 or lower confers a very low risk (<1%), scores of −1 to 3 confer a low to medium risk (1%–8%), and scores of 4 or more confer a high or very high risk (>12%).

source.title: Multicenter Emergency Department Validation of the Canadian Syncope Risk Score

myositis.name: Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies (PM / DM) — Bohan & Peter (1975) / EULAR-ACR 2017
myositis.criterion: Bohan & Peter 5 criteria: (1) symmetric proximal muscle weakness (limb-girdle and anterior neck flexors), progressive over weeks/months ± dysphagia/respiratory involvement; (2) elevated serum skeletal muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase, LDH, AST, ALT); (3) EMG triad — small/polyphasic motor unit potentials, fibrillations + positive sharp waves at rest, repetitive high-frequency discharges; (4) muscle biopsy showing necrosis of fibers, phagocytosis, regeneration, perifascicular atrophy (DM), or endomysial CD8+ infiltrate invading non-necrotic fibers (PM); (5) typical DM skin rash — Gottron papules, heliotrope rash, V-sign, shawl sign, mechanic's hands. Diagnosis: PM — definite (4 criteria), probable (3), possible (2) WITHOUT skin findings; DM — definite (3 + skin), probable (2 + skin), possible (1 + skin). 2017 EULAR/ACR adds detailed scoring with biopsy and autoantibodies (anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, anti-MDA5, anti-TIF1-γ, etc.) and probability thresholds
myositis.criterion_quote: According to the Bohan and Peter diagnostic criteria, a definite diagnosis of DM requires four criteria (including rash) and a definite diagnosis of PM requires four criteria (without rash). Probable disease comprises three criteria (including rash) for DM and three criteria (without rash) for PM.

source.org: Bohan A & Peter JB; EULAR / ACR
source.title: Polymyositis and dermatomyositis (NEJM 1975); 2017 EULAR/ACR classification criteria for adult and juvenile IIM

polymyalgia_rheumatica.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR) — 2012 ACR/EULAR provisional classification
polymyalgia_rheumatica.criterion: Apply only to patients ≥50 y with bilateral shoulder aching and abnormal ESR and/or CRP. Score: morning stiffness >45 min (+2); hip pain or limited range of motion (+1); absence of RF and anti-CCP (+2); absence of other joint involvement (+1). Plus optional ultrasound: ≥1 shoulder with subdeltoid bursitis and/or biceps tenosynovitis and/or glenohumeral synovitis AND ≥1 hip with synovitis and/or trochanteric bursitis (+1); both shoulders with abnormal ultrasound (+1). Classify as PMR: ≥4 without ultrasound; ≥5 with ultrasound. Sens ~72%, spec ~65% (lower spec for differentiating from RA — ~61%, higher for shoulder conditions — ~79%)
polymyalgia_rheumatica.criterion_quote: The factors in the new criteria were morning stiffness duration > 45 min, hip pain or limited range of motion, absence of rheumatoid factor or anti-citrullinated protein antibody, and absence of other joint involvement. … Patients scoring ≥4 without ultrasound are classified as having PMR. Patients scoring ≥5 with ultrasound are classified as having PMR.

source.title: 2012 provisional classification criteria for polymyalgia rheumatica

behcet_disease.name: Behçet Disease — ISG / ICBD criteria
behcet_disease.criterion: ISG 1990 (high specificity): recurrent oral aphthosis ≥3 times/yr (mandatory) PLUS ≥2 of: recurrent genital aphthosis (scarring), eye lesions (anterior uveitis, posterior uveitis, retinal vasculitis, or hypopyon by ophthalmologist), skin lesions (erythema nodosum, pseudofolliculitis, papulopustular lesions, or acneiform nodules in postadolescent not on corticosteroids), positive pathergy test (read at 24–48 h). ICBD 2014 (higher sensitivity, score ≥4 classifies): oral ulcers +2; genital ulcers +2; ocular lesions +2; skin manifestations +1; vascular manifestations (venous, arterial, aneurysm, thrombosis) +1; neurologic manifestations +1; positive pathergy test +1 (optional, counted only if obtained). ICBD sens 94.8%, spec 90.5%; ISG sens 85%, spec 96%
behcet_disease.criterion_quote: For ISG oral aphthosis is mandatory, and the presence of any two of the following (genital aphthosis, skin lesions, eye lesions, and positive pathergy test) will diagnose/classify the patient as BD. … The ICBD criteria set is based on a scoring system attributing 2 points for oral ulcer, genital ulcer, and ocular lesions; 1 point for positive pathergy test, neurologic, and vascular involvement. Patients having ≥4 points are classified as BD.

source.org: International Study Group; International Team for the Revision of the International Criteria for Behçet's Disease (ICBD)
source.title: Criteria for diagnosis of Behçet's disease (ISG 1990); The International Criteria for Behçet's Disease (ICBD 2014)

antiphospholipid_syndrome.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) — 2023 ACR/EULAR classification
antiphospholipid_syndrome.criterion: Entry criterion (required): ≥1 positive aPL test within 3 years of an aPL-associated clinical criterion. Then score additive weighted items in 6 clinical and 2 laboratory domains. CLASSIFY AS APS only if ≥3 points from CLINICAL domains AND ≥3 points from LABORATORY domains. Clinical domains: macrovascular venous thromboembolism (with vs without high-risk profile), macrovascular arterial thrombosis (high vs low CV risk), microvascular (livedoid vasculopathy, APS nephropathy, pulmonary hemorrhage, adrenal hemorrhage), obstetric (≥3 consecutive pre-10-wk losses, fetal death 10–<16 wk, fetal death 16–<34 wk, severe preeclampsia/PIH with placental insufficiency at <34 wk), cardiac valve (thickening, vegetation), hematologic (thrombocytopenia 20–130 ×10⁹/L). Laboratory domains: lupus anticoagulant (single or persistent positive); ELISA for IgG/IgM aCL or anti-β2GPI (moderate vs high titer; isolated vs combined; single vs persistent). Specificity 99%, sensitivity 84% vs 2006 Sapporo
antiphospholipid_syndrome.criterion_quote: The 2023 ACR/EULAR APS classification criteria include an entry criterion of at least one positive antiphospholipid antibody (aPL) test within 3 years of identification of an aPL-associated clinical criterion, followed by additive weighted criteria (score range 1–7 points each) clustered into six clinical domains … and two laboratory domains … Patients accumulating at least three points each from the clinical and laboratory domains are classified as having APS.

source.title: The 2023 ACR/EULAR Antiphospholipid Syndrome Classification Criteria

brain_death.name: Brain Death / Death by Neurologic Criteria — 2023 AAN/AAP/CNS/SCCM
brain_death.criterion: Prerequisites: identify a known proximate cause adequate to explain irreversible loss of all brain function; exclude reversible confounders — hypothermia (target temp ≥36°C), hypotension (MAP ≥60), sedative/paralytic/CNS-depressant drug effects, severe metabolic/endocrine derangements. Clinical exam (must all be present): (1) unresponsive coma; (2) absence of all brainstem reflexes — pupillary, corneal, oculocephalic, oculovestibular (cold caloric), gag, cough, no facial response to noxious stimuli; (3) apnea on formal apnea test. Apnea test target: PaCO₂ ≥60 mmHg AND ≥20 mmHg rise from baseline with pH <7.30 in absence of respiratory effort (test positive = no breathing → consistent with brain death). Ancillary studies (EEG, transcranial Doppler, nuclear cerebral perfusion) only when clinical exam or apnea test cannot be completed safely. ≥1 examination required in adults; some jurisdictions/pediatric ages require 2
brain_death.criterion_quote: A declaration of brain death is made when there is irreversible cessation of all functions in the entire brain and brainstem, with patients exhibiting a coma and brainstem areflexia and apnea with adequate stimulus. … The 2023 AAN guidelines require PaCO2 ≥ 60 mmHg AND ≥ 20 mmHg rise from baseline, with pH < 7.30.

source.title: The 2023 AAN/AAP/CNS/SCCM Pediatric and Adult Brain Death/Death by Neurologic Criteria Consensus Practice Guideline

bap65.name: BAP-65 — Severity risk in AECOPD
bap65.criterion: 4 admission variables (1 point each for B, A, P; age stratifies class): BUN ≥25 mg/dL; Altered mental status (any change from baseline); Pulse ≥109 bpm; age ≥65. Classes: I — age <65 with 0 criteria; II — age <65 with ≥1 criterion OR age ≥65 with 0 criteria; III — age ≥65 with 1 criterion; IV — age ≥65 with 2 criteria; V — age ≥65 with 3 criteria. In-hospital mortality / need for mechanical ventilation rises from <1% (class I) to >25% (class V)
bap65.criterion_quote: The BAP-65 Scoring system is done with the following risk factors: elevated Blood Urea Nitrogen ≥ 25 mg/dl, Altered mental status, Pulse ≥ 109/min and age > 65 years. … Mortality increased with increasing BAP-65 class, ranging from less than 1% in subjects in class I (score of 0) to more than 25% in those meeting all BAP-65 criteria.

source.title: Validation of a novel risk score for severity of illness in acute exacerbations of COPD

ibd.name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Crohn's & Ulcerative Colitis) — ECCO/AGA
ibd.criterion: No single gold standard — diagnosis from a combination of clinical history (chronic diarrhea, blood in stool, abdominal pain, weight loss, extraintestinal manifestations), biochemical (CRP, fecal calprotectin), endoscopy (colonoscopy with ileal intubation), histology, and cross-sectional imaging (MR/CT enterography, intestinal US). Ulcerative colitis: continuous mucosal inflammation starting in rectum and extending proximally without skip lesions; histology shows crypt distortion, basal plasmacytosis, cryptitis/crypt abscesses limited to mucosa; ASCA−, p-ANCA often + (~60%). Crohn's disease: skip lesions, transmural inflammation, often ileal involvement, perianal disease, fistulas, granulomas (~25% on biopsy), 'cobblestoning'; ASCA often + (~50%). Severity by Mayo (UC) or CDAI/Harvey-Bradshaw (CD). Indeterminate colitis if features overlap and cannot distinguish
ibd.criterion_quote: The diagnosis of Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis is based on a combination of clinical, biochemical, stool, endoscopic, and histological investigations. … There is no gold standard for diagnosis according to ECCO guidelines.

source.title: ECCO-ESGAR Guideline for Diagnostic Assessment in IBD Part 1: Initial diagnosis

myocarditis.name: Acute Myocarditis — 2018 revised Lake Louise CMR criteria
myocarditis.criterion: Clinical suspicion: chest pain/dyspnea/palpitations with troponin elevation, new ECG changes (ST elevation, T-wave inversion, AV block), or new LV dysfunction in absence of obstructive CAD. CMR Lake Louise 2018 — requires ≥1 T2-based + ≥1 T1-based criterion: T2-based (edema) — abnormal regional or global myocardial T2 mapping (above thresholds) OR high T2 signal on STIR. T1-based (non-ischemic injury) — abnormal regional or global myocardial T1 mapping OR elevated extracellular volume (ECV) OR non-ischemic LGE pattern (subepicardial or mid-wall, sparing subendocardium). Supportive: pericardial effusion or LV dysfunction. Pooled CMR accuracy ~83% (sens 80%, spec 87%). Endomyocardial biopsy remains gold standard for definitive diagnosis with histologic Dallas criteria
myocarditis.criterion_quote: The Lake Louise Criteria (LLC) were established in 2009 as consensus criteria for CMR in myocardial inflammation. … The 2018 Lake Louise criteria provide high diagnostic accuracy for the diagnosis of acute myocarditis and significantly increase the sensitivity compared with the original score. … meta-analyses evaluating the Lake Louise Criteria reported a pooled diagnostic accuracy of 83% (sensitivity, 80%; specificity, 87%).

source.org: Ferreira VM et al.; JACC expert recommendations
source.title: Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance in Nonischemic Myocardial Inflammation: Expert Recommendations

sofa.name: Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) — Organ dysfunction score
sofa.criterion: 6 organ systems, each scored 0–4 (max total 24). Respiratory — PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio: 0 ≥400; 1 <400; 2 <300; 3 <200 with respiratory support; 4 <100 with respiratory support. Coagulation — platelets (×10³/µL): 0 ≥150; 1 <150; 2 <100; 3 <50; 4 <20. Liver — bilirubin (mg/dL): 0 <1.2; 1 1.2–1.9; 2 2.0–5.9; 3 6.0–11.9; 4 ≥12. Cardiovascular — 0 MAP ≥70; 1 MAP <70; 2 dopamine ≤5 or dobutamine any dose; 3 dopamine >5 or epinephrine ≤0.1 or norepinephrine ≤0.1; 4 dopamine >15 or epinephrine >0.1 or norepinephrine >0.1 µg/kg/min. CNS — GCS: 0 15; 1 13–14; 2 10–12; 3 6–9; 4 <6. Renal — Cr (mg/dL) OR urine output: 0 <1.2; 1 1.2–1.9; 2 2.0–3.4; 3 3.5–4.9 OR UOP <500 mL/d; 4 ≥5.0 OR UOP <200 mL/d. Used in [[sepsis]] (acute change ≥2 from baseline) — see Sepsis-3
sofa.criterion_quote: The SOFA score describes and quantifies the severity of dysfunction or failure of six essential organ systems. These six systems are: respiratory, coagulation, hepatic, cardiovascular, central nervous system, and renal. … Each component ranges from 0 to 4, with overall scores ranging from 0 to 24.

source.org: Vincent JL et al. (ESICM Working Group on Sepsis-Related Problems)
source.title: The SOFA (Sepsis-related Organ Failure Assessment) score to describe organ dysfunction/failure

gcs.name: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) — Severity of brain injury / coma
gcs.criterion: Three components, scored independently and summed (range 3–15). Eye opening (E, 1–4): 4 spontaneous; 3 to verbal stimuli; 2 to pain; 1 none. Verbal response (V, 1–5): 5 oriented; 4 confused conversation; 3 inappropriate words; 2 incomprehensible sounds; 1 none. Motor response (M, 1–6): 6 obeys commands; 5 localizes to pain; 4 withdraws from pain; 3 abnormal flexion (decorticate); 2 abnormal extension (decerebrate); 1 none. TBI severity: severe ≤8; moderate 9–12; mild 13–15. Document components separately (e.g., E3V4M5 = GCS 12); intubated denoted with 'T' suffix on V. GCS ≤8 often used as indication for definitive airway
gcs.criterion_quote: The Glasgow Coma Scale score is a numerical rating from 3 to 15 based on three observable patient responses: eye opening (E), verbal response (V), and motor response (M). … A TBI with a GCS of 13 or above is mild, 9-12 is moderate, and 8 or below is severe.

source.title: Assessment of coma and impaired consciousness — a practical scale

gcs.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: TBI bands are later convention; Teasdale 1974 didn't define.]

acute_cholangitis.name: Acute Cholangitis — Tokyo Guidelines (TG18) diagnostic criteria and severity
acute_cholangitis.criterion: Diagnostic categories assess 3 domains: (A) Systemic inflammation — fever >38°C and/or shaking chills; OR lab evidence WBC <4 or >10 ×10³/µL, CRP elevation. (B) Cholestasis — jaundice (T-bili ≥2 mg/dL); OR abnormal LFTs (ALP, GGT, AST, ALT >1.5× ULN). (C) Imaging — biliary dilatation; or evidence of etiology (stricture, stone, stent). Suspected: 1 item in A + 1 item in either B or C. Definite: 1 item in A AND 1 in B AND 1 in C. Severity: Grade III (severe) — associated with dysfunction in ≥1 organ (cardiovascular needing dopamine ≥5 µg/kg/min or any norepi; neurologic disturbance; respiratory PaO₂/FiO₂ <300; renal Cr >2.0 mg/dL or oliguria; hepatic INR >1.5; hematologic plt <100). Grade II (moderate) — any 2 of: WBC >12 or <4, fever ≥39°C, age ≥75, T-bili ≥5 mg/dL, albumin <0.7× ULN. Grade I (mild) — does not meet II or III. Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) present in ~72%; Reynolds pentad (Charcot + shock + AMS) in <8%
acute_cholangitis.criterion_quote: the Tokyo cholestasis criterion was present in 96.0% of cases, while inflammation was present in 75.9%, and cholestasis should replace inflammation as an essential diagnostic criterion. … The diagnostic grading criteria of the 2018 Tokyo Guidelines (TG18) are used worldwide as the standard for acute cholangitis (AC) management.

source.title: Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholangitis

compartment_syndrome.name: Acute Compartment Syndrome
compartment_syndrome.criterion: Primarily clinical diagnosis. Classic '5 P's' (late and unreliable): Pain (out of proportion, worse with passive stretch of compartment muscles), Paresthesia, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paralysis. Confirmatory measurement: intracompartmental pressure (ICP) >30 mmHg; OR delta pressure (ΔP) = diastolic BP − ICP <30 mmHg sustained for >2 h. Surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy. Common settings: tibial fractures, crush injury, reperfusion, tight cast, anticoagulation-related hematoma, IV extravasation. Sensitivity of ICP monitoring ~94%, specificity ~98%; do NOT rule out based on a single normal reading in evolving clinical picture
compartment_syndrome.criterion_quote: Intracompartmental pressure (ICP) > 30 mmHg can be used as a threshold to aid in diagnosis. … make a diagnosis of acute compartment syndrome if the ΔP is <30 mm Hg for more than two hours. … For patients with intracompartmental pressures greater than 30 mmHg or delta pressures less than 30 mmHg, surgical fasciotomy should be done.

source.title: Tissue pressure measurements as a determinant for the need of fasciotomy; modern AAOS/StatPearls

sah_grading.name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage — Hunt and Hess & WFNS grading
sah_grading.criterion: WFNS scale (based on GCS + focal deficit; better interrater reliability): Grade 1 — GCS 15, no motor deficit; Grade 2 — GCS 13–14, no motor deficit; Grade 3 — GCS 13–14 WITH motor deficit; Grade 4 — GCS 7–12, ± motor deficit; Grade 5 — GCS 3–6, ± motor deficit. Hunt and Hess (clinical): I — asymptomatic, mild headache, slight nuchal rigidity; II — moderate-to-severe headache, nuchal rigidity, no neuro deficit except CN palsy; III — drowsiness/confusion, mild focal deficit; IV — stupor, moderate-severe hemiparesis, early decerebrate rigidity; V — deep coma, decerebrate posturing, moribund. Diagnosis of SAH: non-contrast CT (sensitivity ~100% within 6 h, falls after) → if negative and high suspicion, LP showing xanthochromia or persistently bloody CSF in successive tubes. CT angiography or DSA to identify aneurysm
sah_grading.criterion_quote: grade 1 corresponds to GCS = 15; grade 2 to 13 ≤ GCS ≤ 14 with focal neurological deficits; grade 3 to 13 ≤ GCS ≤ 14 without focal neurological deficits; grade 4 to 7 ≤ GCS ≤ 12 and grade 5 to 3 ≤ GCS ≤ 6.

source.org: World Federation of Neurosurgical Societies (WFNS); Hunt & Hess
source.title: Report of WFNS Committee on Universal SAH Grading Scale; Hunt WE, Hess RM 1968

sah_grading.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed CAPTCHA blocked verification; WFNS 1988 grades canonical and unchanged.]

bell_palsy.name: Bell's Palsy — Diagnosis + House-Brackmann grading
bell_palsy.criterion: Clinical diagnosis: acute (typically <72 h) onset of unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness (including forehead — distinguishes from central/UMN lesion) without identifiable cause; no other neurologic findings (preserved limb strength, no cerebellar signs, no other cranial nerve deficits). Imaging not routinely required at presentation. Consider alternative diagnoses if: bilateral involvement, slow progression >2 weeks, recurrent paralysis, vesicles (Ramsay Hunt = HSV/VZV reactivation), Lyme exposure in endemic area, otitis/parotid mass. House-Brackmann grading: I — normal; II — slight weakness on close inspection, complete eye closure with minimal effort; III — obvious but not disfiguring weakness, complete eye closure with effort, mild–moderate synkinesis; IV — obvious weakness/disfiguring asymmetry, incomplete eye closure; V — barely perceptible motion, asymmetry at rest; VI — total paralysis, no movement
bell_palsy.criterion_quote: The HB scale categorizes facial nerve function into six grades, ranging from Grade I (normal function) to Grade VI (complete paralysis), based on assessments of symmetry at rest, degree of voluntary movement, and presence of synkinesis. … Grades II, III, and IV all have no resting asymmetry, but are differentiated based on whether the eye can close completely with gentle effort, with full effort, or not at all.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Bell's Palsy (AAO-HNSF); Facial nerve grading system

contrast_aki.name: Contrast-Associated AKI (CA-AKI / CI-AKI)
contrast_aki.criterion: Classic definition: rise in serum creatinine of ≥0.5 mg/dL OR a relative increase ≥25% from baseline within 48–72 h after intravascular iodinated contrast exposure, in the absence of alternative cause. Modern KDIGO-aligned: meets KDIGO AKI criteria (see [[aki]]) within 48 h of contrast exposure. Typical course: Cr begins to rise within 24 h, peaks 3–5 days, returns to baseline 7–10 days. Distinguish contrast-induced AKI (causative) from contrast-associated AKI (temporal only) — the latter often more accurate given alternative explanations (hemodynamics, nephrotoxins). Risk factors: pre-existing CKD (especially eGFR <30), diabetes, volume depletion, high contrast volume, intra-arterial route, NSAIDs
contrast_aki.criterion_quote: Contrast-induced nephropathy is defined as a rise in serum creatinine of at least 0.5 mg/dL or a 25% increase from baseline within 48 to 72 hours after contrast exposure. … In contrast-induced nephropathy, serum creatinine usually begins to rise within 24 hours after the administration of contrast media, peaks between 3 and 5 days, and comes back to baseline in 7-10 days.

source.title: ACR Manual on Contrast Media; KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guideline

inpatient_glycemic_target.name: Inpatient Glycemic Targets — ADA / NICE-SUGAR
inpatient_glycemic_target.criterion: Most non-pregnant adult inpatients (both ICU and non-ICU): start insulin therapy when persistent hyperglycemia ≥180 mg/dL, with a target glycemic range of 140–180 mg/dL. Stricter targets (110–140 mg/dL) may be appropriate for selected stable patients if achievable without significant hypoglycemia. Avoid tighter target (81–108 mg/dL) — NICE-SUGAR showed a 13× rise in severe hypoglycemia and an absolute increase in 90-day mortality. Hypoglycemia thresholds: level 1 alert <70 mg/dL; level 2 clinically significant <54 mg/dL; level 3 severe = altered cognition requiring external help. Stress hyperglycemia in patients without prior DM warrants HbA1c to distinguish from undiagnosed diabetes (≥6.5%)
inpatient_glycemic_target.criterion_quote: The 2009 multicenter study NICE-SUGAR found that attempting to achieve an 81-108 mg/dL target range increases both 90-day mortality and hypoglycemic events, the latter by 13-fold. … In 2025, the American Diabetes Association (ADA) recommends that once therapy is initiated, a glycemic goal of 140–180 mg/dL (7.8–10.0 mmol/L) is recommended for most critically ill (ICU) individuals with hyperglycemia.

source.title: ADA Standards of Care — Diabetes Care in the Hospital; NICE-SUGAR Trial (NEJM 2009)

timi_nstemi.name: TIMI Risk Score — UA/NSTEMI 14-day risk of death/MI/urgent revascularization
timi_nstemi.criterion: 7 binary items (1 point each, max 7): (1) age ≥65 y; (2) ≥3 CAD risk factors (HTN, hyperlipidemia, DM, family history of premature CAD, current smoker); (3) known CAD with prior coronary stenosis ≥50%; (4) aspirin use in prior 7 days; (5) ≥2 anginal episodes within last 24 h; (6) ST-segment deviation ≥0.5 mm on presenting ECG; (7) elevated cardiac biomarkers. 14-day risk of composite endpoint (death, new/recurrent MI, urgent revascularization): 0–1 = 4.7%; 2 = 8.3%; 3 = 13.2%; 4 = 19.9%; 5 = 26.2%; 6–7 = 40.9%
timi_nstemi.criterion_quote: The TIMI Risk Score for UA/NSTEMI is a validated clinical prediction tool that uses 7 binary risk factors to estimate the 14-day risk of death, myocardial infarction, or urgent revascularization in patients with unstable angina or non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction.

source.title: The TIMI risk score for unstable angina/non-ST elevation MI: a method for prognostication and therapeutic decision making

grace_score.name: GRACE Score — ACS in-hospital and 6-month mortality risk
grace_score.criterion: 8 variables: age, heart rate, systolic BP, serum creatinine, Killip class (I no HF; II rales/S3; III pulmonary edema; IV cardiogenic shock), cardiac arrest at admission, ST-segment deviation, elevated cardiac biomarkers. In-hospital mortality risk: low <109 (<1%); intermediate 109–140 (1–3%); high >140 (>3%). 6-month risk: low ≤88; intermediate 89–118; high >118. Endorsed by ESC for NSTE-ACS risk stratification; high GRACE (>140) supports early invasive strategy
grace_score.criterion_quote: The eight components of the GRACE score — age, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, ST-segment deviation, and elevated cardiac enzymes — were selected from multivariate logistic regression as independent predictors of in-hospital mortality. … Low risk is defined as scores less than 109 with mortality less than 1%, Intermediate risk is 109-140 with mortality of 1-3%, and High risk is greater than 140 with mortality greater than 3%.

source.org: Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events (GRACE); ESC endorsement
source.title: A validated prediction model for all forms of acute coronary syndrome

grace_score.notes: ACS dx in [[acs_unstable_angina]]; TIMI is an alternative ACS risk score.

acromegaly.name: Acromegaly — Endocrine Society
acromegaly.criterion: Initial screening: serum IGF-1 above age-adjusted (not sex-adjusted) ULN in a patient with clinical features (acral enlargement, prognathism, frontal bossing, soft tissue overgrowth, sleep apnea, glucose intolerance). Confirmation: failure to suppress GH below threshold during 75-g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) — nadir GH ≥1 µg/L (older assays) OR ≥0.4 µg/L using ultrasensitive immunometric assays. Random GH not diagnostic. After biochemical confirmation, pituitary MRI to identify adenoma (>95% caused by GH-secreting adenoma). Screen for comorbidities: cardiomyopathy, OSA, colon polyps, T2DM
acromegaly.criterion_quote: The 2014 Endocrine Society guidelines recommend using IGF-I normalized to age but not sex for the diagnosis of acromegaly, confirmed by lack of suppression of GH < 1 µg/L during OGTT if necessary. … Rather than the 1-µg/L threshold recommended by the Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline >10 years ago, a nadir serum GH level less than 0.4 µg/L should be used as a normal response to glucose load when an ultrasensitive assay is used.

source.title: Acromegaly: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

hyperprolactinemia.name: Hyperprolactinemia / Prolactinoma — Endocrine Society
hyperprolactinemia.criterion: Diagnosis: single elevated serum prolactin level above lab ULN (typically >25 ng/mL [µg/L] in women, >20 ng/mL in men) drawn without excessive venipuncture stress confirms hyperprolactinemia. Exclude physiologic (pregnancy, lactation, stress, sleep), pharmacologic (antipsychotics, antidepressants, antiemetics, opioids, estrogens, verapamil), primary hypothyroidism, renal failure, cirrhosis, macroprolactinemia. Tumor likelihood by level: >250 µg/L typically indicates prolactinoma; >500 µg/L diagnostic of macroprolactinoma. Microadenoma <10 mm; macroadenoma ≥10 mm on MRI. Disproportionate mild elevation with macroadenoma suggests hook effect — request dilution
hyperprolactinemia.criterion_quote: A serum prolactin level above the upper limit of normal confirms the diagnosis of hyperprolactinemia. … A prolactin level greater than 500 μg/liter is diagnostic of a macroprolactinoma. Additionally, a prolactin level greater than 250 μg/liter usually indicates the presence of a prolactinoma.

source.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyperprolactinemia: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

morse_fall_scale.name: Morse Fall Scale — Inpatient fall risk
morse_fall_scale.criterion: 6 items (max 125): history of falling (no=0/yes=25); secondary diagnosis (no=0/yes=15); ambulatory aid (none/bed rest/nurse assist=0; crutches/cane/walker=15; furniture=30); IV therapy/heparin lock (no=0/yes=20); gait (normal/bed rest/immobile=0; weak=10; impaired=20); mental status (oriented to own ability=0; forgets limitations/overestimates=15). Risk bands: 0–24 low (standard care); 25–44 moderate (standard fall prevention); ≥45 high (high-risk fall prevention bundle)
morse_fall_scale.criterion_quote: For the MFS, scores above 45 indicate high risk, scores of 25-44 indicate medium risk, and scores of 0-24 indicate low risk.

source.title: A prospective study to identify the fall-prone patient (Morse Fall Scale)

morse_fall_scale.notes: Hendrich II and Tinetti POMA are alternative validated fall risk tools. [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed CAPTCHA blocked; thresholds 0-24/25-44/45+ are standard published cutoffs.]

perc_rule.name: PERC Rule — PE rule-out in low-pretest-probability patients
perc_rule.criterion: Apply ONLY when clinician gestalt or pretest probability for PE is already low (<15%). PE can be ruled out without D-dimer or imaging if ALL 8 are met: (1) age <50 y; (2) HR <100 bpm; (3) SpO₂ >94% on room air; (4) no unilateral leg swelling; (5) no hemoptysis; (6) no surgery or trauma in past 4 weeks requiring general anesthesia; (7) no prior history of DVT/PE; (8) no estrogen use (OCP, HRT). PERC-negative + low gestalt → PE prevalence <2% (~1%). Do NOT apply if pretest probability is moderate or high
perc_rule.criterion_quote: The patient is PERC-negative only when all eight criteria are absent. In patients with low suspicion for PE (best-guess pre-test probability <15%) and all criteria are met, only 0.9% had PE and it can be ruled-out without further testing.

source.title: Clinical criteria to prevent unnecessary diagnostic testing in emergency department patients with suspected pulmonary embolism

perc_rule.notes: PE Wells in [[pe_wells]]; full ESC PE algorithm in [[pulmonary_embolism]]. [AUDIT 2026-06: <15% pretest threshold not in original 2004.]

malignant_hyperthermia.name: Malignant Hyperthermia — Larach Clinical Grading Scale
malignant_hyperthermia.criterion: MH crisis presents intra/postoperatively after exposure to a triggering agent (volatile anesthetics: halothane, isoflurane, sevoflurane, desflurane; or succinylcholine). Larach raw score sums points across 6 indicator processes (highest weighted item per process): Muscular rigidity (generalized 15, masseter 15); Muscle breakdown (CK >20,000 IU after succinylcholine — 15, or >10,000 without succinylcholine — 15; cola-colored urine 10; myoglobinuria/urine myoglobin >60 µg/L 5; K >6 mEq/L 3); Respiratory acidosis (ETCO₂ >55 mmHg with appropriate ventilation 15, PaCO₂ >60 15); Temperature increase (>38.8°C 15, rapid increase 10); Cardiac involvement (inappropriate sinus tachycardia 3, ventricular tachy/fib 3); Family history (3 each). Clinical likelihood: 0 almost never; 3–9 unlikely; 10–19 somewhat less than likely; 20–34 somewhat greater than likely; 35–49 very likely; ≥50 almost certain
malignant_hyperthermia.criterion_quote: The clinical grading scale of Larach et al. allocates points to the presenting clinical features and aids in the diagnosis of the event as Malignant hyperthermia. … The MH Clinical Grading Score includes indicators for muscle breakdown (such as elevated creatine kinase levels, cola-colored urine, and abnormal myoglobin), and respiratory acidosis indicators (such as elevated PETCO2 or arterial PaCO2).

source.org: Larach MG et al.; AAGBI / MHAUS
source.title: A Clinical Grading Scale to Predict Malignant Hyperthermia Susceptibility

aclf.name: Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure (ACLF) — EASL-CLIF criteria
aclf.criterion: Patients with chronic liver disease (cirrhosis or non-cirrhotic chronic liver disease per region) presenting with acute decompensation AND ≥1 organ failure based on CLIF-C Organ Failure score across 6 systems: Liver — bilirubin ≥12 mg/dL; Kidney — Cr ≥2.0 mg/dL or renal replacement therapy; Brain — West Haven grade III–IV encephalopathy; Coagulation — INR ≥2.5; Circulation — use of vasopressors (dopamine, dobutamine, norepinephrine, terlipressin); Respiratory — PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤200 or SpO₂/FiO₂ ≤214. ACLF grades by # of organ failures: Grade 1 — single organ kidney failure, OR single non-kidney organ failure + Cr 1.5–1.9 mg/dL and/or grade I-II HE; Grade 2 — 2 organ failures; Grade 3 — ≥3 organ failures
aclf.criterion_quote: The failure of one or more of the six major organ systems according to the EASL-CLIF-C criteria should be used to define the severity of ACLF and the risk of 28-day mortality. A higher threshold value of serum bilirubin ≥ 12 mg/dL and INR ≥ 2.5 is utilized by EASL-CLIF criteria to define liver and coagulation failure in patients with decompensated cirrhosis.

source.title: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines on acute-on-chronic liver failure (CANONIC study CLIF-C OF)

aclf.notes: Decompensated cirrhosis in [[decompensated_cirrhosis]]; West Haven HE in [[hepatic_encephalopathy]].

hfpef_score.name: HFpEF — H2FPEF & HFA-PEFF diagnostic scores
hfpef_score.criterion: H2FPEF (Mayo, 0–9): H — Heavy (BMI >30) +2; H — Hypertensive (≥2 anti-HTN drugs) +1; F — atrial Fibrillation (any) +3; P — Pulmonary HTN (echo PASP >35 mmHg) +1; E — Elder (>60 y) +1; F — Filling pressure (echo E/e′ >9) +1. Score 0–1 rules out HFpEF; 2–5 indeterminate (consider invasive testing); 6–9 rules in HFpEF (PPV 94%). HFA-PEFF (ESC, 0–6, 2 points per domain): Functional (echo: e′ septal <7 or lateral <10 cm/s; E/e′ ≥15; TR velocity >2.8 m/s); Morphological (LA volume index >34 mL/m² in SR or >40 in AF; LV mass index ≥149 M/122 F + RWT >0.42); Biomarker (NT-proBNP >220 pg/mL in SR or >660 in AF; BNP >80 in SR or >240 in AF). 0–1 unlikely; 2–4 indeterminate (proceed to F2/F3); 5–6 HFpEF confirmed
hfpef_score.criterion_quote: A high H2FPEF score (6-9 points) could diagnose HFpEF with a high specificity of 97% and a positive predictive value of 94%, while a low H2FPEF score (0-1 point) could rule out HFpEF with a high sensitivity of 97%. HFpEF could be diagnosed with a high HFA-PEFF score (5-6 points) with specificity of 84% and positive predictive value of 82%, or ruled out with a low HFA-PEFF score (0-1 point).

source.org: Reddy YNV et al. (Mayo); Pieske B et al. (HFA-ESC)
source.title: A Simple, Evidence-Based Approach to Help Guide Diagnosis of Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction (H2FPEF); How to diagnose heart failure with preserved ejection fraction: the HFA-PEFF diagnostic algorithm

hf_phenotypes.name: Heart Failure phenotypes by LVEF — AHA/ACC/HFSA 2022
hf_phenotypes.criterion: Categorize by left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) in patients meeting clinical HF criteria: HFrEF — LVEF ≤40%. HFmrEF (mildly reduced) — LVEF 41–49% with evidence of spontaneous or provokable elevated LV filling pressures (elevated natriuretic peptide, invasive/non-invasive hemodynamics). HFpEF — LVEF ≥50% with evidence of elevated filling pressures. HFimpEF (improved EF) — previous LVEF ≤40% AND follow-up LVEF >40% (continue GDMT). NYHA functional class I–IV and ACC/AHA stages A–D apply across phenotypes. Clinical HF criteria in [[heart_failure]]
hf_phenotypes.criterion_quote: HFrEF (Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction): LVEF ≤40% … HFmrEF: LVEF 41%–49%, with evidence of spontaneous or provokable increased LV filling pressures … HFpEF: LVEF ≥50%, with evidence of spontaneous or provokable increased LV filling pressures … HFimpEF: Previous LVEF ≤40% and a follow-up measurement of LVEF >40%.

source.title: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure

ckd_epi_2021.name: eGFR — CKD-EPI 2021 race-free equation (NKF/ASN)
ckd_epi_2021.criterion: Recommended for GFR estimation in adults ≥18 y. Available variants: (1) 2021 CKD-EPI creatinine eGFRcr (race-free; uses age, sex, serum creatinine); (2) 2012 CKD-EPI cystatin C eGFRcys (age, sex, serum cystatin C); (3) 2021 CKD-EPI creatinine-cystatin C eGFRcr-cys — preferred when greater accuracy needed (e.g., near decision thresholds such as drug dosing, transplant eligibility, oncologic dosing). All equations report mL/min/1.73 m² and dropped the race coefficient present in 2009 CKD-EPI
ckd_epi_2021.criterion_quote: The race-free CKD-EPI equations for individuals ages 18 and older include: the 2021 CKD-EPI creatinine equation (eGFRcr), the 2021 CKD-EPI creatinine-cystatin C equation (eGFRcr-cys), and the 2012 CKD-EPI cystatin C equation (eGFRcys). … Using both creatinine and cystatin C to estimate GFR is preferred and more accurate than using serum creatinine alone.

source.title: Inker LA et al. — New Creatinine- and Cystatin C–Based Equations to Estimate GFR Without Race

alcoholic_ketoacidosis.name: Alcoholic Ketoacidosis (AKA)
alcoholic_ketoacidosis.criterion: Clinical setting of recent alcohol binge with subsequent abrupt cessation, nausea/vomiting, and decreased oral intake, plus laboratory: anion gap metabolic acidosis (AG >14 mmol/L, serum bicarbonate <15 mEq/L, arterial pH <7.3); elevated serum ketones (predominantly β-hydroxybutyrate — may be underdetected by nitroprusside assay which measures acetoacetate); normal, low, or mildly elevated glucose (typically <250 mg/dL — distinguishing from DKA). Other contributors to AG (lactic acidosis from thiamine deficiency, starvation ketosis) may coexist. Mixed acid-base patterns (with metabolic alkalosis from vomiting) common — bicarbonate may be higher than expected
alcoholic_ketoacidosis.criterion_quote: AKA is characterized by metabolic acidosis with an elevated anion gap, elevated serum ketone levels, and a normal or low glucose concentration. … The arterial pH is less than 7.3, and the serum bicarbonate level is less than 15 mEq/L. The calculated anion gap is greater than 14 mmol/L. … glucose levels usually mildly elevated but generally do not exceed 250 mg/dL.

source.org: Wrenn KD et al.; standard textbook consensus
source.title: The syndrome of alcoholic ketoacidosis (Am J Med); StatPearls

alcoholic_ketoacidosis.notes: DKA in [[dka]]; HHS in [[hhs]]; starvation ketosis = milder ketosis with HCO₃ rarely <18.

sarcoidosis.name: Sarcoidosis — ATS/ERS/WASOG diagnostic criteria
sarcoidosis.criterion: Diagnosis requires ALL: (1) compatible clinical and radiologic presentation; (2) histologic demonstration of non-caseating epithelioid-cell granulomas in ≥1 organ; (3) exclusion of other diseases capable of producing similar histologic or clinical picture (TB, fungal infection, beryllium, HSP, lymphoma, GPA). Histology not required when classic syndromes are present (Löfgren — bilateral hilar adenopathy + erythema nodosum + arthralgia + fever; Heerfordt — uveoparotid fever; isolated asymptomatic bilateral hilar adenopathy). Scadding chest X-ray stages: 0 — normal; I — bilateral hilar adenopathy only; II — adenopathy + parenchymal disease; III — parenchymal disease without adenopathy; IV — pulmonary fibrosis
sarcoidosis.criterion_quote: Diagnosis of sarcoidosis requires demonstration of typical lesions in more than one organ system and exclusion of other disorders known to cause granulomatous disease. … The diagnosis of sarcoidosis relies on the presence of noncaseating granuloma on histopathologic examination, compatible clinical presentation, and exclusion of other causes.

source.title: Statement on Sarcoidosis (Joint Statement); ATS Clinical Practice Guideline 2020

sarcoidosis.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: 1999 ATS statement superseded by 2020 ATS clinical practice guideline (Crouser AJRCCM).]

systemic_sclerosis.name: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) — 2013 ACR/EULAR classification
systemic_sclerosis.criterion: Sum highest weighted item per category; classify if total ≥9. Sufficient criterion (9 alone): skin thickening of the fingers extending proximal to the MCP joints. Otherwise: Skin thickening of fingers — puffy fingers (+2) OR sclerodactyly distal to MCP but proximal to PIP (+4); Fingertip lesions — digital tip ulcers (+2) OR pitting scars (+3); Telangiectasia (+2); Abnormal nailfold capillaries (+2); Pulmonary involvement — ILD or PAH (+2); Raynaud phenomenon (+3); SSc-related autoantibodies — anticentromere, anti-topoisomerase I (Scl-70), or anti-RNA polymerase III (+3). Sensitivity 91%, specificity 92%
systemic_sclerosis.criterion_quote: The criteria determine that skin thickening of the fingers extending proximal to the metacarpophalangeal joints is sufficient for classification as systemic sclerosis; if that is not present, seven additive items apply with varying weights: skin thickening of the fingers, fingertip lesions, telangiectasia, abnormal nailfold capillaries, interstitial lung disease or pulmonary arterial hypertension, Raynaud's phenomenon, and SSc-related autoantibodies. … Patients with a total score of 9 or greater are classified as having definite systemic scleroderma.

source.title: 2013 Classification Criteria for Systemic Sclerosis: An ACR/EULAR Collaborative Initiative

sjogren.name: Primary Sjögren Syndrome — 2016 ACR/EULAR classification
sjogren.criterion: Applies to patients with ≥1 symptom of ocular or oral dryness or suspected systemic Sjögren (ESSDAI domain). Sum weighted items; classify if total ≥4: (1) focal lymphocytic sialadenitis with focus score ≥1 foci/4 mm² on labial salivary gland biopsy +3; (2) anti-SSA/Ro antibody positive +3; (3) abnormal ocular staining score ≥5 (or van Bijsterveld ≥4) in at least one eye +1; (4) Schirmer test ≤5 mm/5 min in at least one eye +1; (5) unstimulated whole salivary flow rate ≤0.1 mL/min +1. Exclude: history of head/neck radiation, active HCV, AIDS, sarcoidosis, amyloidosis, GVHD, IgG4-related disease. Either salivary biopsy or anti-Ro must contribute for valid classification
sjogren.criterion_quote: The final classification criteria are based on the weighted sum of 5 items: anti-SSA(Ro) antibody positivity and focal lymphocytic sialadenitis with a focus score ≥ 1 foci/mm2, each scoring 3; an abnormal ocular staining score ≥ 5 (or van Bijsterveld score ≥ 4), a Schirmer test ≤ 5 mm/5 min … Either salivary gland biopsy or anti-Ro must be positive.

source.title: 2016 ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria for Primary Sjögren's Syndrome

gpa.name: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) — 2022 ACR/EULAR classification
gpa.criterion: Apply only after exclusion of vasculitis mimics. Sum weighted items; classify as GPA if total ≥5. Clinical: nasal involvement (bloody discharge, ulcers, crusting, congestion, blockage, septal defect/perforation) +3; cartilaginous involvement (auricular/nasal chondritis, hoarse voice/stridor, endobronchial involvement, saddle nose) +2; conductive or sensorineural hearing loss +1. Lab/imaging/biopsy: PR3-ANCA or cANCA +5; pulmonary nodules, mass, or cavitation on imaging +2; granuloma, extravascular granulomatous inflammation, or giant cells on biopsy +2; nasal/paranasal sinus inflammation/consolidation/effusion or mastoiditis on imaging +1; pauci-immune glomerulonephritis on biopsy +1; MPO-ANCA or pANCA −1; serum eosinophils ≥1×10⁹/L −4
gpa.criterion_quote: The 2022 ACR/EULAR criteria for GPA consist of 10 items with different weighted scores: for clinical criteria, +3, +2, and +1 points are assigned to nasal passage involvement, cartilaginous involvement, and conductive or sensorineural hearing loss, respectively; for laboratory criteria, +5, -1, and -4 points are assigned to PR3-ANCA (or C-ANCA) positivity, MPO-ANCA (or P-ANCA) positivity, and serum eosinophil ≥1000/µL, respectively. … threshold score is required for classification (GPA and MPA ≥5, EGPA ≥6).

source.title: 2022 American College of Rheumatology/EULAR Classification Criteria for Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

mpa.name: Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA) — 2022 ACR/EULAR classification
mpa.criterion: Sum weighted items; classify as MPA if total ≥5 (after exclusion of mimics). Clinical: nasal involvement (bloody discharge, ulcers, crusting, congestion, blockage, septal defect) −3; lab: MPO-ANCA or pANCA +6; PR3-ANCA or cANCA −1; chest imaging — fibrosis or interstitial lung disease +3; histology — pauci-immune glomerulonephritis +3; serum eosinophils ≥1×10⁹/L −4. (MPA tends to be MPO/pANCA-positive with renal-pulmonary capillaritis and no granuloma — opposite weighting of GPA on nasal/ANCA specificity)
mpa.criterion_quote: In 2022 the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) and European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR) presented new classification criteria for the three subsets of antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis (AAV): granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) and Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA). … threshold score is required for classification (GPA and MPA ≥5, EGPA ≥6).

source.title: 2022 American College of Rheumatology/EULAR Classification Criteria for Microscopic Polyangiitis

egpa.name: Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA / Churg-Strauss) — 2022 ACR/EULAR classification
egpa.criterion: Sum weighted items; classify as EGPA if total ≥6 (after exclusion of mimics). Obstructive airway disease/asthma +3; nasal polyps +3; peripheral blood eosinophils ≥1×10⁹/L +5; extravascular eosinophilic-predominant inflammation on biopsy +2; mononeuritis multiplex or motor neuropathy not due to radiculopathy +1; cytoplasmic ANCA (cANCA) or PR3-ANCA positivity −3; hematuria −1
egpa.criterion_quote: the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) and European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR) presented new classification criteria for the three subsets of antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis (AAV): granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) and Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA). … (GPA and MPA ≥5, EGPA ≥6).

source.title: 2022 American College of Rheumatology/EULAR Classification Criteria for Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

hcc_bclc.name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma — BCLC staging (AASLD-endorsed)
hcc_bclc.criterion: Stages integrate tumor burden, liver function (Child-Pugh), and performance status (ECOG). Stage 0 (very early): single lesion ≤2 cm, ECOG 0, Child-Pugh A — ablation/resection. Stage A (early): single tumor (any size) or up to 3 nodules each ≤3 cm, ECOG 0, Child-Pugh A or B — curative (resection, transplant per Milan, ablation). Stage B (intermediate): multinodular HCC beyond stage A, no vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread, ECOG 0, Child-Pugh A or B — TACE. Stage C (advanced): vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread, OR ECOG 1–2, Child-Pugh A or B — systemic therapy (atezolizumab + bevacizumab; or TKIs). Stage D (terminal): Child-Pugh C or ECOG 3–4 — best supportive care. HCC dx itself: typical hyperenhancement on arterial phase with washout on portal/venous phase ≥10 mm lesion in cirrhotic liver (LI-RADS 5) — biopsy not required
hcc_bclc.criterion_quote: Stage 0: Tumor less than 2 cm. The ECOG Performance Status is 0. The Child-Pugh score is A. Stage A (Early Stage): There may be one lesion that is more than 2 cm, or there are up to three lesions that measure less than 3 cm. The ECOG Performance Status is 0. … Stage B (Intermediate): asymptomatic multinodular HCC … Stage C (Advanced): vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread … Stage D (Terminal): Child–Pugh C, ECOG 3-4.

source.title: BCLC strategy for prognosis prediction and treatment recommendations (updated); AASLD HCC Practice Guidance

iih.name: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH) — Friedman criteria
iih.criterion: Required: (A) papilledema; (B) normal neurologic exam except cranial nerve abnormalities; (C) neuroimaging — normal brain parenchyma without hydrocephalus, mass, or structural lesion, AND no abnormal meningeal enhancement (use MRI with/without venography); (D) normal CSF composition; (E) elevated lumbar puncture opening pressure ≥250 mmH₂O in adults (≥280 mmH₂O in children non-sedated and non-obese; ≥250 otherwise). 'Definite IIH' if all A–E met. 'IIH without papilledema (IIH-WOP)' if abducens (CN VI) palsy + criteria B–E met. 'Suggested IIH-WOP' if no papilledema/VI palsy but criteria B–E met with ≥3 neuroimaging signs (empty sella, posterior globe flattening, distended optic nerve sheath, transverse venous sinus stenosis)
iih.criterion_quote: The revised diagnostic criteria for pseudotumor cerebri syndrome by Friedman set the upper limit to 250 mmH2O … cerebrospinal fluid constituents must be normal, and neuroimaging must be without other structural lesions. The neurological exam is normal aside from any cranial nerve abnormalities. Papilledema is a hallmark, but diagnosis can also be made in its absence if sixth nerve palsy is present.

source.org: Friedman DI et al. (American Academy of Neurology)
source.title: Revised diagnostic criteria for the pseudotumor cerebri syndrome in adults and children

cluster_headache.name: Cluster Headache — ICHD-3
cluster_headache.criterion: ALL of: (A) ≥5 attacks meeting B–D; (B) severe or very severe unilateral orbital, supraorbital, and/or temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes untreated; (C) either or both: (1) ≥1 ipsilateral autonomic feature — conjunctival injection/lacrimation, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, forehead/facial sweating, miosis/ptosis — AND/OR (2) sense of restlessness or agitation; (D) attacks occurring between 1 every other day and 8 per day during active period; (E) not better explained by another ICHD-3 dx. Episodic: bouts last 7 d to 1 yr separated by remission ≥3 mo. Chronic: occurring ≥1 yr without remission, or with remissions <3 mo
cluster_headache.criterion_quote: A: At least five attacks fulfilling criteria B-D. B: Severe or very severe unilateral orbital, supraorbital and/or temporal pain lasting 15-180 minutes (when untreated). C: Either or both of the following: 1. at least one of the following symptoms or signs, ipsilateral to the headache: conjunctival injection and/or lacrimation, nasal congestion and/or rhinorrhoea, eyelid oedema, forehead and facial sweating, miosis and/or ptosis; 2. a sense of restlessness or agitation. D: Occurring with a frequency between one every other day and 8 per day.

source.title: International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition (ICHD-3) — section 3.1

tension_headache.name: Tension-Type Headache — ICHD-3
tension_headache.criterion: Common criteria: headache lasting 30 minutes to 7 days with ≥2 of: bilateral location, pressing/tightening (non-pulsating) quality, mild-to-moderate intensity, not aggravated by routine physical activity. AND both of: no nausea or vomiting; no more than one of photophobia or phonophobia. Not better explained by another ICHD-3 dx. Frequency subtypes — infrequent episodic: <1 day/month (<12 d/yr); frequent episodic: ≥10 episodes occurring on 1–14 days/month for >3 months (12–179 d/yr); chronic: ≥15 days/month for >3 months (≥180 d/yr; mild nausea allowed)
