tension_headache.criterion_quote: Episodes of headache are typically bilateral, pressing or tightening in quality and of mild to moderate intensity, lasting minutes to days. The pain is not aggravated by routine physical activity such as walking or climbing stairs. … Headache occurs on ≥15 days/month on average for >3 months (≥180 days/year).

source.title: International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition (ICHD-3) — section 2

osteoporosis.name: Osteoporosis — WHO / NOF / Endocrine Society
osteoporosis.criterion: Postmenopausal women and men ≥50: diagnosis if any of (1) DXA T-score ≤ −2.5 at lumbar spine, total hip, or femoral neck (lowest); (2) clinical fragility fracture of hip or vertebra regardless of BMD; (3) other fragility fracture (proximal humerus, pelvis, sometimes distal forearm) + T-score in osteopenic range (−1.0 to −2.5); (4) FRAX 10-yr risk ≥3% hip OR ≥20% major osteoporotic fracture (US, treatment threshold). Osteopenia: T-score between −1.0 and −2.5. Premenopausal women and men <50: use Z-score; ≤ −2.0 = 'low bone density for chronological age'
osteoporosis.criterion_quote: According to the World Health Organization, osteopenia corresponds to a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5, while a T-score less than -2.5 indicates osteoporosis. … Using DXA, vertebral or hip BMD T-scores have been shown to be a key component and tool to identify patients at increased fragility fracture risk.

source.org: WHO / Bone Health & Osteoporosis Foundation / Endocrine Society
source.title: WHO Technical Report Series 843; Endocrine Society Pharmacologic Management of Osteoporosis in Postmenopausal Women

osteoporosis.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: WHO T-score canonical but not at cited Endocrine Society URL.]

migraine.name: Migraine Without Aura — ICHD-3
migraine.criterion: ALL of: (A) ≥5 attacks fulfilling B–D; (B) attacks last 4–72 hours (untreated or unsuccessfully treated); (C) ≥2 of: unilateral location, pulsating quality, moderate or severe pain intensity, aggravation by or causing avoidance of routine physical activity; (D) during headache ≥1 of: nausea and/or vomiting, photophobia and phonophobia; (E) not better accounted for by another ICHD-3 diagnosis. Migraine with aura (1.2) requires ≥2 attacks with ≥1 fully reversible aura symptom (visual, sensory, speech/language, motor, brainstem, retinal) plus other characteristic features. Chronic migraine: ≥15 headache days/month for >3 months, with migraine features on ≥8 days/month
migraine.criterion_quote: A. At least five attacks fulfilling criteria B–D. B. Headache attacks lasting 4–72 hours (when untreated or unsuccessfully treated). C. Headache has at least two of the following four characteristics: unilateral location, pulsating quality, moderate or severe pain intensity, aggravation by or causing avoidance of routine physical activity. D. During headache at least one of the following: nausea and/or vomiting, photophobia and phonophobia.

source.title: International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition (ICHD-3)

pulmonary_hypertension.name: Pulmonary Hypertension — ESC/ERS 2022 hemodynamic definition
pulmonary_hypertension.criterion: Pulmonary hypertension (PH): mean pulmonary arterial pressure (mPAP) >20 mmHg at rest, measured by right heart catheterization (lowered from prior 25 mmHg threshold). Hemodynamic subtypes — Pre-capillary PH: mPAP >20 + PAWP ≤15 mmHg + PVR >2 WU. Isolated post-capillary PH (IpcPH): mPAP >20 + PAWP >15 + PVR ≤2 WU. Combined post- and pre-capillary PH (CpcPH): mPAP >20 + PAWP >15 + PVR >2 WU. Clinical groups: 1 PAH; 2 PH due to left heart disease; 3 PH due to lung disease/hypoxia; 4 PH due to pulmonary artery obstruction (CTEPH); 5 PH with unclear/multifactorial mechanisms
pulmonary_hypertension.criterion_quote: Pulmonary hypertension is now hemodynamically defined as an mPAP > 20 mmHg, reduced from the previous threshold of ≥25 mmHg. … For pulmonary arterial hypertension specifically, the definition implies a pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) >2 Wood Units and pulmonary arterial wedge pressure ≤15 mm Hg.

source.title: 2022 ESC/ERS Guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of pulmonary hypertension

nms.name: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) — DSM-5-TR / consensus
nms.criterion: Exposure to a dopamine antagonist (antipsychotic, antiemetic) or withdrawal of a dopamine agonist within 72 h, PLUS hyperthermia (temperature ≥38°C on ≥2 occasions, often higher) AND severe generalized muscle rigidity ('lead-pipe'), AND ≥2 of: altered mental status, tachycardia, labile/elevated BP, diaphoresis or sialorrhea, tremor, incontinence, elevated CK (often markedly), leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis. Not better explained by another drug, neurologic, or medical condition. Onset over 1–3 days. Distinguish from serotonin syndrome (hyperreflexia/clonus) — see [[serotonin_syndrome]]
nms.criterion_quote: The DSM-5-TR criteria require exposure to a dopamine antagonist or dopamine agonist withdrawal within the past 72 hours, hyperthermia, and diaphoresis. DSM-IV-TR research criteria require that both severe muscle rigidity and elevated temperature be present after recent administration of an antipsychotic as well as two associated signs, symptoms, or laboratory findings.

source.org: APA (DSM-5-TR); international expert consensus (Gurrera et al.)
source.title: An international consensus study of neuroleptic malignant syndrome diagnostic criteria using the Delphi method

serotonin_syndrome.name: Serotonin Toxicity (Serotonin Syndrome) — Hunter criteria
serotonin_syndrome.criterion: In the setting of recent exposure to a serotonergic agent, diagnose if ANY ONE of: (1) spontaneous clonus; (2) inducible clonus AND (agitation OR diaphoresis); (3) ocular clonus AND (agitation OR diaphoresis); (4) tremor AND hyperreflexia; (5) hypertonia AND temperature >38°C AND (ocular clonus OR inducible clonus). Sensitivity 84%, specificity 97% — superior to Sternbach criteria. Clonus is the central diagnostic feature
serotonin_syndrome.criterion_quote: The Hunter criteria require at least one of the following features to be present: spontaneous clonus; inducible clonus with agitation or diaphoresis; ocular clonus with agitation or diaphoresis; tremor with hyperreflexia; or hypertonia with temperature above 100.4°F/38°C and ocular or inducible clonus.

source.org: Dunkley EJC, Isbister GK et al.
source.title: The Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria: simple and accurate diagnostic decision rules for serotonin toxicity

guillain_barre.name: Guillain-Barré Syndrome — Brighton Collaboration criteria
guillain_barre.criterion: Level 1 (highest certainty) requires ALL: (1) bilateral flaccid limb weakness; (2) decreased or absent deep tendon reflexes in weak limbs; (3) monophasic illness; interval from onset to nadir 12 h–28 days followed by clinical plateau; (4) CSF cell count <50/µL AND CSF protein > normal (albuminocytologic dissociation); (5) electrophysiology consistent with GBS; (6) absence of alternative diagnosis. Level 2: as level 1 but CSF/EMG result missing or non-confirmatory. Level 3: clinical features only without CSF/EMG. Level 4: reported as GBS but criteria not fully met. Variants (Miller-Fisher, AMAN, etc.) have their own subcriteria
guillain_barre.criterion_quote: Level 1 of Diagnostic Certainty requires bilateral and flaccid weakness of the limbs AND decreased or absent deep tendon reflexes in weak limbs AND a monophasic illness pattern with an interval between onset and nadir of weakness between 12 hours and 28 days with subsequent clinical plateau AND electrophysiologic findings consistent with Guillain-Barré syndrome AND albuminocytologic dissociation (elevation of CSF protein level above normal value and CSF total white blood cell count less than 50 cells/µL) AND absence of an identified alternative diagnosis for weakness.

source.title: Guillain-Barré syndrome and Fisher syndrome: case definitions and guidelines for collection, analysis, and presentation of immunization safety data

parkinson_disease.name: Parkinson Disease — MDS 2015 clinical diagnostic criteria
parkinson_disease.criterion: Step 1: parkinsonism = bradykinesia + ≥1 of rest tremor or rigidity. Step 2: classify using three feature sets. Absolute exclusion criteria (any one rules out PD): cerebellar signs, downward vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, behavioral-variant FTD or PPA <5 yr after onset, parkinsonism restricted to legs >3 yr, dopamine-blocker exposure, absent response to high-dose levodopa, cortical sensory loss/limb ideomotor apraxia/PPA, normal presynaptic dopaminergic imaging, alternative dx more likely. Supportive criteria (+): clear/dramatic response to dopaminergic therapy, levodopa-induced dyskinesia, rest tremor of a limb, ANS hyposmia or MIBG denervation. Red flags include rapid wheelchair use within 5 yr, falls in 3 yr, bulbar dysfunction, etc. Categories: 'Clinically Established PD' — no absolute exclusion, no red flags, ≥2 supportive; 'Clinically Probable PD' — no absolute exclusion, ≤2 red flags counterbalanced by ≥equal supportive criteria
parkinson_disease.criterion_quote: The first essential criterion is parkinsonism, which is defined as bradykinesia, in combination with at least 1 of rest tremor or rigidity. … determination of PD as the cause of parkinsonism relies on three categories of diagnostic features: absolute exclusion criteria (which rule out PD), red flags (which must be counterbalanced by additional supportive criteria to allow diagnosis of PD), and supportive criteria.

source.org: International Parkinson and Movement Disorder Society (MDS)
source.title: MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Parkinson's Disease

masld.name: Metabolic Dysfunction–Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) — 2023 multi-society nomenclature
masld.criterion: Hepatic steatosis on imaging/biopsy AND ≥1 of 5 cardiometabolic risk factors: (a) BMI ≥25 kg/m² (≥23 in Asians) OR WC >94 cm M / >80 cm F (ethnicity-adjusted); (b) fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL OR 2-h post-load ≥140 mg/dL OR HbA1c ≥5.7% OR T2DM OR DM treatment; (c) BP ≥130/85 or treatment; (d) plasma triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL or lipid-lowering therapy; (e) plasma HDL-C ≤40 mg/dL M / ≤50 mg/dL F or lipid-lowering therapy. MetALD: MASLD + moderate alcohol use (140–350 g/wk women, 210–420 g/wk men). ALD: MASLD + heavier alcohol use. Replaces 'NAFLD'; MASH replaces 'NASH'
masld.criterion_quote: Whereas NAFLD was based on the exclusion of any other liver disease, MASLD encompasses patients who have hepatic steatosis and at least 1 of 5 cardiometabolic risk factors. … MetALD, to represent a separate group of patients with MASLD who consume alcohol at a rate of 140–350 g/wk for females and 210–420 g/wk for males.

source.title: A multisociety Delphi consensus statement on new fatty liver disease nomenclature

autoimmune_hepatitis.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis (AIH) — IAIHG simplified criteria
autoimmune_hepatitis.criterion: Simplified score (max 8 — biopsy required): (1) ANA or SMA ≥1:40 = +1; ANA or SMA ≥1:80 OR LKM ≥1:40 OR SLA positive = +2 (max 2 points). (2) IgG > ULN = +1; IgG >1.1× ULN = +2. (3) Liver histology — typical (all 3: interface hepatitis, lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate including plasma cells, rosetting of hepatocytes) = +2; compatible = +1; atypical = 0. (4) Absence of viral hepatitis = +2. Total ≥6 = probable AIH; ≥7 = definite AIH
autoimmune_hepatitis.criterion_quote: If the ANA or SMA cutoff is ≥1:40, assign 1 point, or if the liver-kidney microsome (LKM) cutoff is ≥1:40, assign 2 points … If immunoglobulin G (IgG) is over the upper limit of normal (ULN), assign 1 point … Score ≥6 = Probable AIH; ≥7: Definite AIH.

source.org: International Autoimmune Hepatitis Group (IAIHG); AASLD endorsement
source.title: Simplified criteria for the diagnosis of autoimmune hepatitis

psc.name: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) — AASLD 2022
psc.criterion: Cholestatic liver biochemistry (typically ALP elevation) PLUS characteristic cholangiographic findings on MRCP/MRI — intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic strictures with alternating areas of normal or slightly dilated segments (beaded appearance) — PLUS exclusion of secondary causes of sclerosing cholangitis (IgG4-SC, ischemic, infectious, neoplastic). Measure serum IgG4 in all suspected cases; >5.6 g/L suggests IgG4-SC. Small-duct PSC: normal cholangiogram + characteristic liver biopsy findings (fibro-obliterative cholangitis). Liver biopsy reserved for suspected small-duct PSC, AIH overlap, or unexplained features. ERCP avoided for diagnosis (used therapeutically)
psc.criterion_quote: In patients with suspected PSC, a 3D MRI/MRCP with T1w and T2w axial images and contrast enhancement should be obtained to evaluate for cholangiographic features of PSC, including intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic strictures alternating with normal or slightly dilated segments. ERCP should be avoided for the diagnosis of PSC. … In all patients with possible PSC, serum IgG4 levels should be measured to exclude IgG4-sclerosing cholangitis.

source.title: AASLD practice guidance on primary sclerosing cholangitis and cholangiocarcinoma

pbc.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) — AASLD 2018
pbc.criterion: Diagnose if ≥2 of 3: (1) biochemical cholestasis with serum ALP ≥1.5× ULN for ≥6 months; (2) positive anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) at titer ≥1:40 — or, if AMA-negative, positive PBC-specific ANA (sp100 or gp210); (3) histology showing nonsuppurative destructive cholangitis with interlobular and septal bile duct destruction ('florid duct lesion'). Biopsy not required if criteria 1 + 2 met; biopsy recommended if AMA-negative, ALT >5× ULN (rule out overlap with AIH), or atypical features
pbc.criterion_quote: A PBC diagnosis requires two of the following: (1) ALP elevation, (2) positive anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) or positive sp100 or gp210 antibodies if AMA is negative … and (3) histology showing nonsuppurative destructive cholangitis. … ALP elevation should be at least 1.5 times the upper limit of normal. … A liver biopsy is not required to diagnose PBC.

source.title: Primary Biliary Cholangitis: 2018 Practice Guidance from the AASLD

preeclampsia.name: Preeclampsia (with and without severe features) — ACOG
preeclampsia.criterion: New onset at ≥20 weeks gestation of: SBP ≥140 OR DBP ≥90 mmHg on 2 measurements ≥4 h apart, PLUS either proteinuria (≥300 mg/24 h, protein/creatinine ratio ≥0.3, or dipstick 2+ if no other quantitative method) OR (in absence of proteinuria) any of: thrombocytopenia <100×10⁹/L, serum Cr >1.1 mg/dL or doubling, AST/ALT ≥2× ULN, pulmonary edema, new headache unresponsive to medication and not accounted for by alternative dx, visual symptoms. Severe features (any one): SBP ≥160 or DBP ≥110 on ≥2 occasions ≥4 h apart; platelets <100×10⁹/L; Cr >1.1 mg/dL or doubling; AST/ALT ≥2× ULN; pulmonary edema; severe headache; visual disturbance. Eclampsia: preeclampsia + new-onset tonic-clonic, focal, or multifocal seizure
preeclampsia.criterion_quote: Preeclampsia … new-onset hypertension, usually with accompanying proteinuria, which occurs most often after 20 weeks of gestation. … Severe features of preeclampsia (as per ACOG 2019 guidelines) include: systolic blood pressure ≥ 160 mm Hg or diastolic ≥ 110 mmHg measured ≥ twice in ≥ 4 hours; thrombocytopenia with platelet count less than 100,000 X10⁹/L; renal insufficiency with serum creatinine > 1.1 mg/dL or doubling of serum creatinine; impaired liver function with elevated transaminases to twice normal; pulmonary edema; new onset headache unresponsive to medication; and changes in vision.

source.title: Gestational Hypertension and Preeclampsia: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 222

giant_cell_arteritis.name: Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) — 2022 ACR/EULAR classification
giant_cell_arteritis.criterion: Absolute requirement: age ≥50 years at diagnosis. Score additive weighted items; classify as GCA if total ≥6: positive temporal artery biopsy OR temporal artery halo sign on ultrasound (+5); ESR ≥50 mm/h or CRP ≥10 mg/L (+3); sudden visual loss (+3); morning stiffness in shoulders or neck (+2); jaw or tongue claudication (+2); new temporal headache (+2); scalp tenderness (+2); abnormal temporal artery on exam (+2); bilateral axillary involvement on imaging (+2); FDG-PET activity throughout the aorta (+2). Sensitivity 87%, specificity 95% in validation
giant_cell_arteritis.criterion_quote: Age ≥50 years at diagnosis was an absolute requirement for classification. The final criteria items and weights were as follows: positive temporal artery biopsy or temporal artery halo sign on ultrasound (+5); erythrocyte sedimentation rate ≥50 mm/hour or C reactive protein ≥10 mg/L (+3); sudden visual loss (+3); morning stiffness in shoulders or neck, jaw or tongue claudication, new temporal headache, scalp tenderness, temporal artery abnormality on vascular examination, bilateral axillary involvement on imaging and fluorodeoxyglucose–positron emission tomography activity throughout the aorta (+2 each). A patient could be classified as having GCA with a cumulative score of ≥6 points.

source.title: 2022 American College of Rheumatology/EULAR Classification Criteria for Giant Cell Arteritis

rheumatic_fever.name: Acute Rheumatic Fever — 2015 Revised Jones criteria
rheumatic_fever.criterion: Initial ARF: 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor PLUS evidence of preceding group A streptococcal infection (positive throat culture, RADT, or elevated/rising ASO or anti-DNase B). Recurrent ARF: 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor OR 3 minor + evidence of GAS infection. Major criteria — Low-risk population: carditis (clinical + subclinical by echo), polyarthritis (clinical only), chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. Moderate/high-risk: carditis (clinical + subclinical), polyarthritis or monoarthritis or polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. Minor (low-risk): polyarthralgia, fever ≥38.5°C, ESR ≥60 mm/h and/or CRP ≥3.0 mg/dL, prolonged PR interval. Minor (moderate/high-risk): monoarthralgia, fever ≥38°C, ESR ≥30 mm/h and/or CRP ≥3.0 mg/dL, prolonged PR interval
rheumatic_fever.criterion_quote: In 2015, the American Heart Association took on an ambitious and successful revision … For the first time, the criteria consider the risk within a population and offer two separate diagnostic pathways. … Major criteria include: carditis (clinical and/or subclinical), arthritis (polyarthritis), chorea, erythema marginatum, and subcutaneous nodules. Echocardiography is now recommended in all patients with suspected or confirmed ARF.

source.title: Revision of the Jones Criteria for the Diagnosis of Acute Rheumatic Fever in the Era of Doppler Echocardiography

refeeding_syndrome.name: Refeeding Syndrome — ASPEN 2020 consensus
refeeding_syndrome.criterion: Diagnosis: decrease in ≥1 of serum phosphorus, potassium, and/or magnesium of 10–30% OR organ dysfunction from electrolyte fall or thiamine deficiency, occurring within 5 days of reintroduction of calories. Severity: Mild — 10–20% decrease in 1, 2, or 3 of phos/K/Mg; Moderate — 20–30% decrease; Severe — >30% decrease and/or organ dysfunction attributable to electrolyte drop or thiamine deficiency. Risk factors include BMI <18.5, weight loss ≥10% in 3–6 mo, little/no caloric intake ≥5–7 d, history of alcohol use disorder, malabsorption
refeeding_syndrome.criterion_quote: The ASPEN criteria stratify refeeding syndrome diagnosis as follows: a decrease in any 1, 2, or 3 of serum phosphorus, potassium, and/or magnesium levels by 10%–20% (mild), 20%–30% (moderate), or >30% and/or organ dysfunction resulting from a decrease in any of these and/or due to thiamin deficiency (severe), occurring within 5 days of reintroduction of calories.

source.title: ASPEN Consensus Recommendations for Refeeding Syndrome

wernicke_encephalopathy.name: Wernicke Encephalopathy — Caine criteria
wernicke_encephalopathy.criterion: Diagnose if ≥2 of 4 are present: (1) dietary deficiency (alcohol use disorder, malnutrition, hyperemesis, bariatric surgery, malabsorption, prolonged fasting/TPN without thiamine); (2) oculomotor abnormalities (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, abducens/conjugate gaze palsy); (3) cerebellar dysfunction (ataxia, gait disturbance); (4) altered mental status OR mild memory impairment. Classical triad (encephalopathy + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia) present in only ~10–16% of cases. Caine criteria sensitivity approaches 100% in patients with alcohol use disorder. Treat empirically with IV thiamine before confirmation
wernicke_encephalopathy.criterion_quote: The Caine criteria state that a diagnosis of Wernicke encephalopathy should be considered in any patient with two of the following: nutritional deficiency, altered mental state or memory, oculomotor abnormalities, cerebellar dysfunction. … Sensitivity of the Caine criteria approaches 100% in patients with alcoholism without hepatic encephalopathy.

source.title: Operational criteria for the classification of chronic alcoholics: identification of Wernicke's encephalopathy; EFNS guidelines

ttp_plasmic.name: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP) — PLASMIC score for ADAMTS13 <10%
ttp_plasmic.criterion: 1 point each (max 7): P — Platelet count <30×10⁹/L; L — heMolysis (any of: reticulocyte >2.5%, undetectable haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin >2.0 mg/dL); A — no Active cancer (past year); S — no history of Stem cell or solid organ transplant; M — MCV <90 fL; I — INR <1.5; C — Creatinine <2.0 mg/dL. Risk: 0–4 low; 5 intermediate; 6–7 high (PPV ~72%, NPV ~98% for ADAMTS13 <10%). High score → empiric plasma exchange while awaiting ADAMTS13 confirmation
ttp_plasmic.criterion_quote: The name 'PLASMIC' refers to the score's seven components: Platelet count; combined hemoLysis variable; absence of Active cancer; absence of Stem-cell or solid-organ transplant; MCV; INR; Creatinine. … The PLASMIC scores of 0–4, 5, and 6–7 indicate low, intermediate, and high risk for severe ADAMTS13 deficiency.

source.org: Bendapudi PK et al.; ISTH endorsement
source.title: Derivation and external validation of the PLASMIC score for rapid assessment of adults with thrombotic microangiopathies

ttp_plasmic.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed did not display components; PLASMIC score components/thresholds widely published Bendapudi 2017.]

nephrotic_syndrome.name: Nephrotic Syndrome — KDIGO
nephrotic_syndrome.criterion: Adults: nephrotic-range proteinuria (≥3.5 g/24 h OR urine protein-to-creatinine ratio ≥3500 mg/g) AND hypoalbuminemia (serum albumin <3 g/dL [30 g/L]) AND clinical edema (or edema substituted when albumin unavailable). Hyperlipidemia and lipiduria are supportive. Children: uPCR ≥200 mg/mmol (≥2000 mg/g) or proteinuria ≥1000 mg/m²/day, with same hypoalbuminemia/edema features. Etiology: minimal change disease, FSGS, membranous nephropathy, diabetic nephropathy, amyloidosis, SLE, etc. — typically requires biopsy in adults
nephrotic_syndrome.criterion_quote: Nephrotic syndrome is defined as nephrotic-range proteinuria and either hypoalbuminemia (serum albumin <30 g/L or 3 g/dL) or edema when albumin level is not available. … The threshold of 3.5 g of protein excretion per day has remained central to the definition of nephrotic syndrome in adults to date.

source.title: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases (2021); KDIGO 2025 Nephrotic Syndrome in Children

acute_kidney_disease.name: Acute Kidney Disease (AKD) — KDIGO 2024
acute_kidney_disease.criterion: Abnormalities of kidney function/structure with implications for health and duration ≤3 months. Includes both AKI (≤7 days) and subacute decline. Operational definitions: GFR <60 mL/min/1.73 m² for <3 months, OR decrease in GFR by ≥35% for <3 months, OR increase in serum creatinine by >50% for <3 months, OR markers of kidney damage for <3 months. AKD without AKI is distinct from AKD following AKI (different causes/management). CKD = abnormalities present >3 months
acute_kidney_disease.criterion_quote: AKD includes clinical conditions of acute (AKI) or subacute decreases in GFR (i.e., GFR<60 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for <3 months or decrease in GFR by ≥35% or increase in serum creatinine by >50% for <3 months).

source.title: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD

hcm.name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy — AHA/ACC 2020
hcm.criterion: Adult diagnosis: maximal end-diastolic LV wall thickness ≥15 mm in any segment by 2D echocardiography or cardiac MRI, in the absence of another cause sufficient to explain hypertrophy (e.g., long-standing hypertension, aortic stenosis, infiltrative disease). Wall thickness 13–14 mm can be diagnostic in family members of a HCM proband or with a positive genetic test (pathogenic sarcomere variant). Children: BSA-adjusted z-score ≥2 SD above the mean. LVOT obstruction = peak LVOT gradient ≥30 mmHg at rest or with provocation; severe obstruction ≥50 mmHg
hcm.criterion_quote: A clinical diagnosis of HCM in adult patients can be established by imaging with 2D echocardiography or cardiovascular magnetic resonance (CMR) showing a maximal end-diastolic wall thickness of ≥15 mm anywhere in the left ventricle, in the absence of another cause of hypertrophy in adults. More limited hypertrophy (13–14 mm) can be diagnostic when present in family members of a patient with HCM or in conjunction with a positive genetic test.

source.title: 2020 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Patients With Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy

ipf.name: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) — ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT
ipf.criterion: Diagnosis requires: (1) exclusion of known causes of interstitial lung disease (environmental exposure, connective tissue disease, drug toxicity); AND (2) one of: definite UIP pattern on HRCT alone, OR specific combinations of HRCT pattern and histopathology if biopsy obtained. HRCT UIP pattern (all 4): subpleural and basal predominance, often heterogeneous; reticular abnormality; honeycombing with or without peripheral traction bronchiectasis/bronchiolectasis; absence of features suggesting an alternative diagnosis. 2018 categories: definite UIP, probable UIP, indeterminate for UIP, alternative diagnosis
ipf.criterion_quote: Previously defined patterns of usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) were refined to patterns of UIP, probable UIP, indeterminate, and alternate diagnosis in the updated 2018 guidelines. … If the following four features are present, the HRCT image fulfills the criteria for 'UIP pattern': subpleural, basal predominance, reticular abnormalities, honeycombing with or without traction bronchiectasis, and absence of features inconsistent with UIP pattern.

source.title: Diagnosis of Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis: An Official ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT Clinical Practice Guideline

respiratory_failure.name: Acute Respiratory Failure — Type I vs Type II
respiratory_failure.criterion: Type I (hypoxemic): PaO₂ <60 mmHg on room air with normal or low PaCO₂ — most commonly from V/Q mismatch (pneumonia, pulmonary edema, ARDS, PE, atelectasis). Type II (hypercapnic): PaCO₂ >45–50 mmHg producing respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35) — from alveolar hypoventilation (COPD/asthma exacerbation, neuromuscular weakness, central depression, severe chest wall disease). Type III (perioperative atelectasis) and Type IV (shock-related, requiring intubation) sometimes added. Severity by P/F ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂) ratio overlapping with ARDS Berlin criteria — see [[ards]]
respiratory_failure.criterion_quote: Type 1 respiratory failure is a partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2) < 60 mmHg with a normal or decreased partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO2). … Hypercapnic respiratory failure (type II) is characterized by a PaCO2 higher than 50 mm Hg … producing a respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35).

source.title: Respiratory Failure (StatPearls)

respiratory_failure.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: StatPearls says PaCO2 >45; quote says >50 — discrepancy.]

acute_pericarditis.name: Acute Pericarditis — ESC
acute_pericarditis.criterion: Diagnosis requires ≥2 of 4: (1) chest pain — characteristically pleuritic, sharp, worse supine and relieved sitting forward; (2) pericardial friction rub; (3) ECG showing new widespread ST-segment elevation (concave up) and/or PR depression (PR elevation in aVR); (4) new or worsening pericardial effusion on imaging. Supportive findings (not required): elevated CRP/ESR, leukocytosis, mild troponin elevation (if myopericarditis). Recurrent pericarditis: documented first episode + symptom-free interval ≥4–6 wk + recurrence
acute_pericarditis.criterion_quote: The ESC guidelines require two of the following four criteria to diagnose acute pericarditis: (1) chest pain consistent with pericarditis, (2) pericardial friction rub, (3) electrocardiogram (ECG) showing diffuse ST-segment elevation or PR-segment depression, and (4) new or worsening pericardial effusion.

source.title: 2015 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of pericardial diseases; 2025 ESC Guidelines for management of myocarditis and pericarditis

aortic_dissection.name: Acute Aortic Dissection — ADD-RS + Stanford/DeBakey
aortic_dissection.criterion: Risk stratification (ADD-RS, 0–3): 1 point each for ANY feature in 3 categories — Predisposing condition (Marfan, connective tissue disease, FH aortic disease, known aortic valve disease, recent aortic manipulation, known thoracic aortic aneurysm); Pain features (abrupt onset, severe intensity, ripping/tearing quality); Exam findings (pulse deficit/BP differential >20 mmHg, focal neurologic deficit + pain, new murmur of AI + pain, hypotension/shock). ADD-RS 0=low, 1=intermediate, >1=high. ADD-RS ≤1 + D-dimer <500 ng/mL → 98.8% NPV (rule out). Definitive imaging: CT angiography (preferred), TEE, or MRA. Anatomic — Stanford: A = involves ascending aorta (surgical emergency); B = limited to descending aorta (medical first-line). DeBakey: I = ascending + descending; II = ascending only; III = descending only (IIIa above diaphragm, IIIb below)
aortic_dissection.criterion_quote: The Aortic Dissection Detection Risk Score (ADD-RS) is a clinical risk stratification tool where patients are considered low risk if the score is 0, intermediate risk if the score is 1, and high risk if the score is >1. … Stanford Type A dissection includes any part of the proximal aorta to the origin of the left subclavian artery, while Type B is limited to the descending aorta, distal to the left subclavian artery origin.

source.title: 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease

acs_unstable_angina.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome — STEMI / NSTEMI / Unstable Angina
acs_unstable_angina.criterion: ACS spectrum: STEMI — typical symptoms + new ST-elevation at the J point in ≥2 contiguous leads (≥1 mm except V2–V3 where ≥2 mm in men ≥40, ≥2.5 mm men <40, ≥1.5 mm women) OR new LBBB with criteria for ischemia. NSTEMI — ischemic symptoms + cardiac troponin elevation above 99th percentile URL with rise/fall pattern, WITHOUT ST elevation (often ST depression or T-wave inversion). Unstable Angina — ischemic symptoms (new/worsening/rest angina) WITHOUT troponin elevation. NSTE-ACS diagnostic timing per 0/1-h or 0/3-h hs-cTn algorithms (ESC). Universal MI definition: see [[myocardial_infarction]]
acs_unstable_angina.criterion_quote: ACS is divided into ST elevation MI (STEMI) and non–ST elevation ACS, which includes unstable angina and non–ST elevation MI (NSTEMI) … The current definition of NSTEMI requires a typical clinical syndrome plus elevated troponin (or creatine kinase isoenzyme MB [CK-MB]) levels to over 99% of the normal reference. … NSTE-ACS is further categorized as NSTEMI if serum cTn levels are elevated, or unstable angina if cTn levels are not elevated.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the Management of ACS; 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients with ACS

shock_classification.name: Shock — etiologic classification + SCAI cardiogenic shock stages
shock_classification.criterion: Four etiologic categories: Hypovolemic (intravascular volume loss); Distributive (pathologic vasodilation — septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic, adrenal); Cardiogenic (primary pump failure — MI, myocarditis, decompensated CMP); Obstructive (extracardiac flow obstruction — tension PTX, tamponade, massive PE). SCAI cardiogenic shock stages: A (At Risk) — no signs of CS; B (Beginning) — elevated filling pressures or hypotension with preserved perfusion; C (Classic) — hypoperfusion requiring intervention (vasopressor/inotrope/mechanical support); D (Deteriorating) — failing to respond to initial intervention; E (Extremis) — refractory cardiac arrest or near-arrest, requiring CPR/ECMO. End-organ hypoperfusion signs: cool/mottled extremities, urine output <30 mL/hr, altered mentation, lactate elevation
shock_classification.criterion_quote: There are mainly four broad categories of shock: distributive, hypovolemic, cardiogenic, and obstructive. … There are five stages of shock labeled A-E in the proposed schema. Stage A: 'At Risk'… Stage B (Beginning)… Stage C (Classic)… Stage D (Deteriorating / Doom)… Stage E (Extremis) manifests with cardiovascular collapse.

source.title: SCAI SHOCK Stage Classification Expert Consensus Update

sickle_cell_disease.name: Sickle Cell Disease — diagnostic criteria (hemoglobin electrophoresis)
sickle_cell_disease.criterion: Definitive diagnosis by hemoglobin separation (HPLC, isoelectric focusing, capillary electrophoresis, or cellulose acetate electrophoresis confirmed by citrate agar). Sickle cell anemia (HbSS): HbS predominant (>80%), HbF variable (often <30%), no HbA; supportive: sickled cells on smear, MCV often normal/low, reticulocytosis. HbSC: roughly 50% HbS / 50% HbC, no HbA. HbS/β⁰-thalassemia: HbS predominant, no HbA, elevated HbA2 (>3.5%), microcytosis. HbS/β⁺-thalassemia: HbS predominant + reduced HbA (5–30%). Sickle cell trait (HbAS): HbA >50%, HbS <50%. Confirm by molecular testing in ambiguous cases. Universal US newborn screening identifies disease at birth
sickle_cell_disease.criterion_quote: In children with normocytic hemolytic anemia, if results of electrophoresis show only HbS with an HbF concentration of less than 30%, the diagnosis is sickle cell anemia. If HbS and HbC are present in roughly equal amounts, the diagnosis is HbSC disease. If the patient does not make β-globin chains, as seen in HbSS/HbSC and HbSβ0 disease, they will not have any HbA in their electrophoresis report.

source.title: ASH-SAP / ASH 2020 Guidelines for Sickle Cell Disease

itp.name: Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) — ASH
itp.criterion: Diagnosis of exclusion: isolated thrombocytopenia (platelet count <100×10⁹/L) with otherwise normal CBC (normal WBC, normal hemoglobin unless bleeding) and no other identifiable cause. Workup excludes pseudothrombocytopenia (EDTA clumping → recheck citrate), drug-induced, HIV/HCV, H. pylori, autoimmune disease (SLE, APS), congenital thrombocytopenia, MDS, marrow infiltration, splenic sequestration. Phases: newly diagnosed (<3 mo), persistent (3–12 mo), chronic (>12 mo). Severe ITP = bleeding sufficient to need intervention
itp.criterion_quote: The American Society of Hematology (ASH) defines ITP as a generalized purpuric rash accompanied by a platelet count less than 100,000/μL and normal white blood cell (WBC) count and hemoglobin level. … ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion.

source.title: ASH 2019 Guidelines for Immune Thrombocytopenia

hypertensive_emergency.name: Hypertensive Emergency vs Severe Hypertension Without Acute Target-Organ Damage
hypertensive_emergency.criterion: Hypertensive emergency: SBP >180 and/or DBP >110–120 mmHg WITH evidence of new or worsening acute target-organ damage — hypertensive encephalopathy, acute ischemic stroke (with BP threshold for treatment >185/110 if tPA candidate or >220/120 otherwise), intracranial hemorrhage, acute MI/ACS, acute LV failure with pulmonary edema, acute aortic dissection, acute kidney injury, eclampsia/severe preeclampsia, sympathetic crisis (pheo, cocaine, MAOI). Severe HTN without target-organ damage (formerly 'hypertensive urgency'): SBP >180 and/or DBP >110–120 mmHg with NO acute organ damage — managed with oral medications and outpatient follow-up, NOT IV therapy
hypertensive_emergency.criterion_quote: The 2024 AHA scientific statement defines hypertensive emergency as SBP/DBP >180/110-120 mm Hg with evidence of new or worsening target-organ damage. 'Asymptomatic markedly elevated BP' (which replaces the older term 'hypertensive urgency') is defined as SBP/DBP >180/110-120 mm Hg in the absence of evidence of new or worsening target-organ damage.

source.title: The Management of Elevated Blood Pressure in the Acute Care Setting: A Scientific Statement

iron_deficiency.name: Iron Deficiency / Iron Deficiency Anemia — ASH
iron_deficiency.criterion: Absolute iron deficiency: serum ferritin <30 ng/mL (sens 92%, spec 98%) — most reliable single test in absence of inflammation. Ferritin alone insufficient with inflammation/CKD/heart failure (acute-phase reactant). In inflammatory states use combined criteria: ferritin <100 ng/mL AND TSAT <20% indicates iron deficiency; TSAT <20% is supportive even when ferritin is 100–300 ng/mL (functional/iron-restricted erythropoiesis). Iron deficiency anemia = iron deficiency + Hb < WHO anemia cutoff (men <13, non-pregnant women <12, pregnant women <11 g/dL). MCV typically <80 fL when established, but normocytic in early or mixed states
iron_deficiency.criterion_quote: A serum ferritin level <30 ng/mL is the most sensitive (92%) and specific (98%) cutoff level for the identification of absolute iron deficiency, with or without anemia. … A cutoff of 20% for TSAT should be considered in most conditions for diagnosing iron deficiency. … For adults with anemia of inflammation, the ASH Guideline Panel suggests performing both serum ferritin and TSAT rather than serum ferritin alone.

source.title: ASH 2023 Guidelines for the Diagnosis of Iron Deficiency (draft); ASH 2023 Anemia of Inflammation guideline

status_epilepticus.name: Status Epilepticus — ILAE 2015
status_epilepticus.criterion: Two operational time points. Convulsive (generalized tonic-clonic) SE: t1 = 5 min (continuous seizure or recurrent seizures without recovery to baseline → start treatment); t2 = 30 min (risk of long-term neuronal injury). Focal SE with impaired consciousness: t1 = 10 min, t2 = >60 min. Absence SE: t1 = 10–15 min, t2 unknown. Refractory SE: persists despite first-line benzodiazepine + second-line ASM. Super-refractory: persists ≥24 h on anesthesia or recurs on weaning
status_epilepticus.criterion_quote: For convulsive (tonic-clonic) status epilepticus, both time points are t1 at 5 minutes and t2 at 30 minutes. … If the convulsive seizure lasts more than 5 minutes (t1), the diagnosis of SE should be made and treatment should be started.

source.title: A definition and classification of status epilepticus — Report of the ILAE Task Force on Classification of Status Epilepticus

delirium_cam.name: Delirium — Confusion Assessment Method (CAM)
delirium_cam.criterion: Delirium present if BOTH features 1 AND 2 are present, AND EITHER feature 3 OR feature 4: (1) Acute onset and fluctuating course (change from baseline; came and went or fluctuated in severity); (2) Inattention (difficulty focusing/keeping track; e.g., months backward, digit span); (3) Disorganized thinking (rambling, irrelevant, illogical flow of ideas, unclear switching); (4) Altered level of consciousness (anything other than alert: vigilant, lethargic, stuporous, comatose). Validation: 94–100% sensitivity, 90–95% specificity vs DSM standard
delirium_cam.criterion_quote: delirium is considered present based on the CAM diagnostic algorithm: presence of (acute onset or fluctuating course AND inattention) AND EITHER (disorganized thinking or altered level of consciousness).

source.title: Clarifying confusion: the Confusion Assessment Method. A new method for detection of delirium

delirium_cam.notes: CAM-ICU adaptation for ventilated patients; aligns with DSM-5 neurocognitive disorder/delirium criteria.

khorana_score.name: Khorana Score — VTE risk in ambulatory cancer patients
khorana_score.criterion: 5 variables: (1) site of cancer — very high risk (stomach, pancreas) +2; high risk (lung, lymphoma, gynecologic, bladder, testicular) +1; (2) prechemotherapy platelet count ≥350×10⁹/L +1; (3) hemoglobin <10 g/dL or use of red cell growth factor +1; (4) prechemotherapy leukocyte count >11×10⁹/L +1; (5) BMI ≥35 kg/m² +1. Risk strata: 0 = low; 1–2 = intermediate; ≥3 = high (most guidelines justify primary thromboprophylaxis at score ≥2)
khorana_score.criterion_quote: The score relies on 5 variables (type of cancer, components of the complete blood count [hemoglobin, platelet, and white blood cells], and body mass index), with each variable assigned 1 point, except for the subclass of very high-risk tumors, which counts for 2.

source.org: Khorana et al.; ASH 2021 guideline endorsement
source.title: Development and validation of a predictive model for chemotherapy-associated thrombosis

tuberculosis.name: Tuberculosis (TB) — CDC/ATS/IDSA
tuberculosis.criterion: Latent TB Infection (LTBI): positive IGRA OR positive TST (induration cutoff varies — ≥5 mm if HIV+/recent contact/immunosuppressed/fibrotic CXR; ≥10 mm if from high-prevalence area, IVDU, healthcare worker, comorbidities; ≥15 mm otherwise) AND no clinical/radiographic/microbiologic evidence of active disease. Active TB disease: clinical and/or radiographic findings consistent with TB PLUS bacteriologic confirmation — positive AFB sputum smear and/or NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) and/or positive culture for M. tuberculosis (gold standard). Drug susceptibility testing required on initial isolate. Probable case: clinical/radiographic features consistent with TB and clinician decision to treat, without bacteriologic confirmation
tuberculosis.criterion_quote: A diagnosis of latent TB infection is made if a person has a positive TB blood test or TB skin test result, and a medical evaluation does not indicate TB disease. … a complete medical evaluation for TB disease includes bacteriologic examination (sputum smear microscopy, nucleic acid amplification testing, culture, and drug susceptibility testing), with culture being the gold standard.

source.title: Official ATS/IDSA/CDC Clinical Practice Guidelines: Diagnosis of Tuberculosis in Adults and Children

hiv_diagnosis.name: HIV Infection — CDC laboratory diagnostic algorithm
hiv_diagnosis.criterion: Three-step algorithm: (1) 4th-generation HIV-1/2 antigen/antibody combination immunoassay. If non-reactive → HIV negative (consider RNA if acute exposure suspected). If reactive → (2) HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation immunoassay. If reactive for HIV-1 or HIV-2 → established HIV-1 or HIV-2 infection. If non-reactive or indeterminate → (3) HIV-1 nucleic acid (RNA) test. Detectable HIV-1 RNA = acute HIV-1 infection. Undetectable RNA = false positive screen. CDC AIDS surveillance definition: HIV(+) with CD4 <200 cells/µL or CD4% <14, or AIDS-defining illness
hiv_diagnosis.criterion_quote: Initial testing was to be done using a fourth-generation antigen (Ag) and antibody (Ab) combination assay, with a follow-up HIV-1/2 Ab differentiation test for reactive results. … Specimens negative or indeterminate by the HIV-1/2 antibody differentiation assay undergo an HIV-1 nucleic acid test (NAT).

source.title: Laboratory Testing for the Diagnosis of HIV Infection: Updated Recommendations

multiple_sclerosis.name: Multiple Sclerosis — McDonald criteria (2017 revision)
multiple_sclerosis.criterion: Diagnosis requires demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) AND dissemination in time (DIT), with no better explanation. DIS: ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 CNS regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord); symptomatic lesions now count. DIT: simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single scan, OR new T2/enhancing lesion on a follow-up MRI. In a typical clinically isolated syndrome with DIS met, CSF-specific oligoclonal bands may substitute for DIT. For primary progressive MS: ≥1 year of disability progression PLUS 2 of: DIS in brain (≥1 T2 lesion in periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, or infratentorial regions); DIS in spinal cord (≥2 T2 lesions); positive CSF (OCBs)
multiple_sclerosis.criterion_quote: In patients with a typical clinically isolated syndrome and clinical or MRI demonstration of dissemination in space, the presence of CSF-specific oligoclonal bands allows a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis. … Symptomatic lesions were permitted to count toward dissemination in space.

source.org: International Panel on Diagnosis of MS / IFMSS
source.title: Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria

qsofa.name: qSOFA — Bedside screening for sepsis in non-ICU settings
qsofa.criterion: 1 point each: (1) RR ≥22 breaths/min; (2) altered mentation (GCS <15); (3) SBP ≤100 mmHg. Score ≥2 in a patient with suspected infection identifies those at high risk of in-hospital mortality and prolonged ICU stay. qSOFA is a screening tool — not a diagnostic criterion for sepsis itself (see Sepsis-3 SOFA Δ≥2 in [[sepsis]])
qsofa.criterion_quote: The qSOFA score is calculated by assigning one point to each of the following three diagnostic criteria: low blood pressure (systolic blood pressure of 100 mmHg or less), high respiratory rate (22 breaths per minute or more), and altered mentation (Glasgow Coma Scale score of less than 15). … a qSOFA score of 2 or more is associated with a greater risk of death or prolonged ICU stay.

source.title: The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3)

nihss.name: NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS) — severity stratification
nihss.criterion: 15-item neurologic exam scored 0–42. Items: LOC (consciousness/questions/commands), gaze, visual fields, facial palsy, motor arm L/R, motor leg L/R, limb ataxia, sensory, best language, dysarthria, extinction/inattention. NIH severity bands: 0 = no stroke symptoms; 1–4 minor; 5–15 moderate; 16–20 moderate-to-severe; 21–42 severe. NIHSS ≥6 commonly used as threshold for large-vessel-occlusion suspicion in thrombectomy decision pathways
nihss.criterion_quote: NIH Classification: 0 No stroke symptoms, 1-4 Minor stroke, 5-15 Moderate stroke, 16-20 Moderate to severe stroke, 21-42 Severe stroke.

source.org: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS)
source.title: NIH Stroke Scale

nihss.notes: Ischemic stroke diagnosis in [[ischemic_stroke]]. [AUDIT 2026-06: NINDS 403; bands convention not in NIH primary.]

primary_aldosteronism.name: Primary Aldosteronism — Endocrine Society
primary_aldosteronism.criterion: Screen with plasma aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR): suspect PA if ARR elevated (commonly ≥20–30 ng/dL per ng/mL/hr, lab-specific) AND plasma aldosterone concentration is elevated (often >10–15 ng/dL) WITH suppressed renin. Confirm with one of 4 tests: oral sodium loading, saline infusion, fludrocortisone suppression, or captopril challenge — failure of aldosterone suppression confirms PA. 2025 update: biochemical triad (suppressed renin + elevated aldosterone + elevated ARR) can establish diagnosis without confirmatory test in select cases. Subtyping (adenoma vs bilateral hyperplasia) by CT and adrenal venous sampling
primary_aldosteronism.criterion_quote: The Endocrine Society recommends using the plasma aldosterone/renin ratio (ARR) to detect possible cases of primary aldosteronism. … Patients with a positive ARR should undergo one or more confirmatory tests. … the 2025 Endocrine Society guidelines revised diagnostic criteria, allowing diagnosis based on a biochemical triad (suppressed renin, elevated aldosterone and aldosterone-to-renin ratio, ARR) without mandatory confirmatory testing.

source.title: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism: Case Detection, Diagnosis, and Treatment (Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline)

cushing_syndrome.name: Cushing Syndrome — Endocrine Society diagnostic algorithm
cushing_syndrome.criterion: After excluding exogenous glucocorticoid exposure, perform ONE of these first-line tests; require ≥2 abnormal tests (or 2 separate abnormal results of the same test) to establish diagnosis: (1) 24-h urinary free cortisol (≥2 collections) — abnormal if above assay-specific ULN. (2) Late-night salivary cortisol (×2) — abnormal if elevated (e.g., >0.15 µg/dL or assay-specific cutoff). (3) 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test — abnormal if morning serum cortisol >1.8 µg/dL (50 nmol/L) post-1 mg dex. (4) 2 mg/48 h low-dose DST — abnormal if serum cortisol >1.8 µg/dL. Subtyping (ACTH-dependent vs independent) follows confirmed hypercortisolism
cushing_syndrome.criterion_quote: for initial testing for Cushing's syndrome, one of the following tests is recommended based on its suitability for a given patient: Urine free cortisol (UFC; at least two measurements), Late-night salivary cortisol (two measurements), or 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (DST).

source.title: The Diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

ugib_forrest.name: Bleeding Peptic Ulcer — Forrest classification (endoscopic stigmata)
ugib_forrest.criterion: Endoscopic appearance: Ia — active spurting (high risk); Ib — active oozing (high risk); IIa — non-bleeding visible vessel (high risk); IIb — adherent clot (intermediate; clot removal recommended); IIc — flat pigmented spot (low risk); III — clean ulcer base (low risk). Forrest Ia, Ib, IIa warrant endoscopic hemostasis; IIc/III do not (rebleed risk ≤10%)
ugib_forrest.criterion_quote: Forrest IA (active spurting), Forrest IB (active oozing), and Forrest IIA (nonbleeding visible vessel) ulcers should undergo endoscopic therapy. … Ulcers with red spots (Forrest IIC) or a clean base (Forrest III) can be observed without endoscopic therapy.

source.title: Endoscopy in gastrointestinal bleeding (original); endorsed in modern UGIB guidelines

ugib_forrest.notes: Pre-endoscopy risk stratification in [[upper_gi_bleed_gbs]] (Glasgow-Blatchford). [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed lacks abstract; Forrest 1974 classification canonical and matches endoscopic stigmata.]

osa.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea — AASM diagnostic criteria
osa.criterion: Diagnosis requires PSG/HSAT showing EITHER: (A) AHI ≥15 events/hr; OR (B) AHI ≥5 events/hr WITH ≥1 of: excessive daytime sleepiness, non-restorative sleep, fatigue, insomnia; waking with gasping/choking; reported snoring or breathing pauses; or comorbidity (hypertension, mood/cognitive dysfunction, CAD, stroke, CHF, AF, T2DM). Severity (adults): AHI <5 normal; 5–<15 mild; 15–<30 moderate; ≥30 severe. Apnea = ≥90% airflow reduction ≥10 s with effort; hypopnea = ≥30% airflow reduction ≥10 s + ≥3% SpO₂ desat or arousal
osa.criterion_quote: clinically diagnosed OSA is defined as either: an AHI of ≥15 determined by polysomnography (PSG), or an AHI of ≥5 but <15 events in addition to one of the following symptoms or conditions: daytime sleepiness, non-restorative sleep, fatigue or insomnia symptoms; waking up with gasping or choking sensations; reported snoring or breathing interruptions during sleep; or a known history of hypertension, mood or cognitive dysfunction, coronary artery disease, stroke, congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation or diabetes mellitus.

source.title: International Classification of Sleep Disorders, 3rd ed. (ICSD-3)

anaphylaxis.name: Anaphylaxis — WAO 2020 (modified NIAID/FAAN)
anaphylaxis.criterion: Highly likely if EITHER: (1) Acute onset (min–hrs) of an illness with simultaneous involvement of skin and/or mucosal tissue (e.g., generalized hives, pruritus/flushing, swollen lips/tongue/uvula) AND ≥1 of: (a) respiratory compromise (dyspnea, wheeze/bronchospasm, stridor, reduced PEF, hypoxemia), (b) reduced BP or symptoms of end-organ dysfunction (hypotonia, collapse, syncope, incontinence), (c) severe GI symptoms (severe crampy abdominal pain, repetitive vomiting), especially after non-food allergen exposure; OR (2) Acute onset of hypotension OR bronchospasm OR laryngeal involvement (stridor, vocal changes, odynophagia) after exposure to a known or highly probable allergen (even without skin involvement)
anaphylaxis.criterion_quote: The WAO Anaphylaxis Committee proposed amended criteria that simplified the existing NIAID/FAAN criteria by combining the first 2 criteria and modifying the third: 1) Typical skin symptoms AND significant symptoms from at least 1 other organ system; OR 2) Exposure to a known or probable allergen for that patient, with respiratory and/or cardiovascular compromise.

source.title: World Allergy Organization Anaphylaxis Guidance 2020

fibromyalgia.name: Fibromyalgia — ACR 2016 diagnostic criteria
fibromyalgia.criterion: ALL three of: (1) (WPI ≥7 AND SSS ≥5) OR (WPI 4–6 AND SSS ≥9); (2) generalized pain — pain in ≥4 of 5 regions (excluding jaw, chest, abdomen); (3) symptoms present at similar level ≥3 months. WPI counts painful body areas (0–19). SSS = sum of severity (0–3 each) of fatigue, waking unrefreshed, cognitive symptoms (0–9) + count of additional somatic symptoms (0–3) → total 0–12. Diagnosis is valid irrespective of other diagnoses
fibromyalgia.criterion_quote: Fibromyalgia may be diagnosed in adults when the following criteria are met: 1) Widespread pain index (WPI) ≥7 and symptom severity scale (SSS) score ≥5 OR WPI 4–6 and SSS score ≥9; 2) Generalized pain, defined as pain in at least 4 of 5 regions, is present; and 3) Symptoms have been present at a similar level for at least 3 months.

source.title: 2016 Revisions to the 2010/2011 Fibromyalgia Diagnostic Criteria

ibs_rome_iv.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome — Rome IV
ibs_rome_iv.criterion: Recurrent abdominal pain on average ≥1 day/week in the last 3 months, associated with ≥2 of: (1) related to defecation, (2) associated with a change in stool frequency, (3) associated with a change in stool form (appearance). Criteria fulfilled for last 3 months with symptom onset ≥6 months before diagnosis. Subtypes by predominant stool form on abnormal-bowel-movement days (Bristol scale): IBS-C (>25% Type 1/2, <25% Type 6/7), IBS-D (>25% Type 6/7, <25% Type 1/2), IBS-M (>25% each), IBS-U
ibs_rome_iv.criterion_quote: abdominal pain should be present at least 1 day a week on average during the preceding 3 months, and the pain must be related to defecation or associated with changes in frequency and form of stools. … Rome IV criteria classify IBS into 4 distinct subtypes … IBS-C, IBS-D, IBS-M, IBS-U.

source.title: Rome IV Diagnostic Criteria for Functional Gastrointestinal Disorders

gerd.name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) — Lyon Consensus 2.0
gerd.criterion: Conclusive evidence for GERD (any one): LA Grade B, C, or D esophagitis; biopsy-proven Barrett's esophagus; peptic stricture; or pathologic acid exposure time (AET) >6% on ambulatory pH/impedance off PPI. Borderline/supportive: LA Grade A esophagitis (needs adjunctive evidence), AET 4–6%, reflux episode counts. Los Angeles classification of erosive esophagitis: A — ≥1 mucosal break ≤5 mm; B — ≥1 mucosal break >5 mm, not continuous between fold tops; C — mucosal breaks continuous between ≥2 fold tops, <75% of circumference; D — ≥75% circumferential
gerd.criterion_quote: Lyon Consensus 2.0 recommended Los Angeles (LA)-B esophagitis as conclusive evidence and LA-A esophagitis as borderline evidence for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). … Grade A: One or more mucosal breaks not more than 5 mm. Grade B: more than 5 mm, but not continuous between the tops of 2 mucosal folds. Grade C: continuous between the tops of 2 or more mucosal folds, but involve less than 75% of the esophageal circumference. Grade D: at least 75% of the esophageal circumference.

source.title: Updates to the modern diagnosis of GERD: Lyon consensus 2.0; ACG Clinical Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of GERD

pancreatitis_bisap.name: Acute Pancreatitis — BISAP severity index
pancreatitis_bisap.criterion: Score 0–5, 1 point each: B — BUN >25 mg/dL; I — Impaired mental status (GCS <15); S — SIRS present (≥2 of T <36 or >38°C, HR >90, RR >20 or PaCO₂ <32, WBC <4 or >12 or >10% bands); A — Age >60; P — Pleural effusion on imaging. Score ≥3 within 24 h of admission predicts severe acute pancreatitis and increased mortality (specificity ~91%)
pancreatitis_bisap.criterion_quote: BISAP … is a 5-point scoring system comprised of five variables: blood urea nitrogen level > 25 mg/dl, impaired mental status, development of systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS), age > 60 years, and presence of pleural effusion. … The overall sensitivity of a BISAP score of ≥3 for mortality was 56% (95% CI, 53%-60%), with a specificity of 91%.

source.org: Wu BU, Banks PA et al.
source.title: The early prediction of mortality in acute pancreatitis: a large population-based study (BISAP)

pancreatitis_bisap.notes: Diagnostic criteria for AP itself in [[acute_pancreatitis]] (Atlanta).

diverticulitis_hinchey.name: Acute Diverticulitis — Hinchey classification (modified)
diverticulitis_hinchey.criterion: Severity staging based on intraoperative/imaging findings. Stage I — pericolic or mesenteric abscess/phlegmon (Ia: confined phlegmon; Ib: pericolic abscess). Stage II — walled-off pelvic, intra-abdominal, or retroperitoneal abscess (distant). Stage III — generalized purulent peritonitis (rupture without continuing fecal contamination). Stage IV — generalized fecal peritonitis (free fecal contamination). Uncomplicated diverticulitis = without abscess/perforation/fistula/obstruction (precedes Hinchey I)
diverticulitis_hinchey.criterion_quote: Stage I (pericolic or mesenteric abscess), stage II (walled-off pelvic or distant intra-abdominal abscess), stage III (generalized purulent peritonitis), and stage IV (generalized fecal peritonitis).

source.org: Hinchey EJ, Schaal PG, Richards GK
source.title: Treatment of perforated diverticular disease of the colon

diverticulitis_hinchey.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Quote reflects modified Hinchey, not 1978 original wording.]

appendicitis_alvarado.name: Acute Appendicitis — Alvarado (MANTRELS) score
appendicitis_alvarado.criterion: 8 components (max 10 points). M — Migration of pain to RLQ (+1); A — Anorexia (+1); N — Nausea/vomiting (+1); T — Tenderness in RLQ (+2); R — Rebound tenderness (+1); E — Elevated temperature ≥37.3°C (+1); L — Leukocytosis WBC >10×10⁹/L (+2); S — Shift of WBC count to the left (>75% PMN) (+1). 0–4 unlikely; 5–6 possible (observe/image); 7–10 probable appendicitis → surgical consult
appendicitis_alvarado.criterion_quote: Migration of pain - 1 point, anorexia - 1 point, nausea - 1 point, tenderness in the right lower quadrant - 2 points, rebound pain - 1 point, elevated temperature - 1 point, leukocytosis - 2 points, and shift of white blood cell count to the left - 1 point. … A score of 0-4 suggests appendicitis is unlikely, 5-6 indicates possible appendicitis requiring further imaging or observation, and 7-10 strongly suggests appendicitis.

source.title: A practical score for the early diagnosis of acute appendicitis

hepatitis_b.name: Hepatitis B Virus Infection — AASLD
hepatitis_b.criterion: Acute HBV: HBsAg(+) and IgM anti-HBc(+) within 6 months of exposure; usually with elevated ALT. Chronic HBV: HBsAg(+) persisting >6 months. Phases — HBeAg(+) chronic infection (immune tolerant): HBV DNA very high (often >10⁷), normal ALT; HBeAg(+) chronic hepatitis: HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL, elevated ALT; HBeAg(–) chronic hepatitis: HBV DNA usually 2,000–20,000 IU/mL with elevated ALT; HBeAg(–) chronic infection (inactive carrier): HBsAg(+) >6 mo, HBeAg(–)/anti-HBe(+), HBV DNA <2,000 IU/mL, persistently normal ALT/AST. Resolved: HBsAg(–), anti-HBc(+), HBV DNA undetectable. Flare: ALT >10× ULN and >2× baseline
hepatitis_b.criterion_quote: Chronic hepatitis B is defined as HBsAg positive for more than six months with serum HBV DNA greater than 20,000 IU per mL (lower values of 2,000 to 20,000 IU per mL often occur with HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B). The inactive HBsAg carrier state is characterized by HBsAg positive for more than six months, HBeAg negative and anti-HBeAg positive, serum HBV DNA less than 2,000 IU per mL, persistently normal ALT and AST levels.

source.title: Update on Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Chronic Hepatitis B: AASLD 2018 Hepatitis B Guidance

hepatitis_c.name: Hepatitis C Virus Infection — AASLD/IDSA
hepatitis_c.criterion: Two-step diagnostic: (1) FDA-approved anti-HCV antibody test. If positive → (2) HCV RNA (quantitative or qualitative NAT, detection level ≤25 IU/mL) to confirm active infection. Anti-HCV(+) / HCV-RNA(+) = active infection. Anti-HCV(+) / HCV-RNA(–) = past infection or spontaneous/treatment clearance. Test HCV RNA directly in anti-HCV(–) immunocompromised patients or recent exposure <6 mo. CDC recommends universal one-time screening of adults ≥18 y
hepatitis_c.criterion_quote: All persons recommended for HCV testing should first be tested for anti-HCV using an FDA-approved test. … FDA-approved quantitative or qualitative nucleic acid testing with a detection level of 25 IU/mL or lower should be used to detect HCV RNA to confirm active HCV infection and guide clinical management.

source.title: Hepatitis C Guidance: AASLD-IDSA Recommendations for Testing, Managing, and Treating HCV Infection (2023 update)

strep_pharyngitis.name: Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis — Centor / McIsaac
strep_pharyngitis.criterion: Centor score (1 pt each): tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, absence of cough, history of fever. McIsaac modification adds age: 3–14 y (+1), 15–44 y (0), ≥45 y (−1). Interpretation: ≤0–1 GAS unlikely, no test/no abx; 2–3 perform RADT/throat culture, treat if positive; ≥4 high probability — IDSA still recommends confirmatory test in adults before treating
strep_pharyngitis.criterion_quote: IDSA guidelines recommend confirmatory testing even at high scores in adults before prescribing antibiotics. … Score ≤ 1: GAS unlikely — neither testing nor antibiotics recommended. Score 2–3: Test with rapid antigen detection; treat if positive. Score ≥ 4: Empiric treatment reasonable while awaiting test results in some guidelines.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis (IDSA 2012)

cha2ds2_vasc.name: CHA₂DS₂-VASc — Stroke risk in non-valvular AF
cha2ds2_vasc.criterion: Score (max 9): CHF (+1), Hypertension (+1), Age ≥75 (+2), Diabetes (+1), Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism history (+2), Vascular disease — prior MI, PAD, or aortic plaque (+1), Age 65–74 (+1), Sex category female (+1). ACC/AHA/HRS recommend oral anticoagulation: men ≥2, women ≥3; consider in men =1, women =2. No OAC if men =0 or women =1 (sex-only)
cha2ds2_vasc.criterion_quote: The CHA2DS2-VASc score includes: congestive heart failure, hypertension, age ≥ 75 years (doubled), diabetes, stroke/transient ischemic attack/thromboembolism (doubled), vascular disease, age 65–75 years, and sex category (female).

source.org: Lip et al. / ACC / AHA / HRS
source.title: Refining clinical risk stratification for predicting stroke and thromboembolism in atrial fibrillation; ACC/AHA/HRS 2023 AF guideline

has_bled.name: HAS-BLED — Bleeding risk on anticoagulation in AF
has_bled.criterion: Score (max 9): Hypertension uncontrolled SBP >160 (+1), Abnormal renal function — dialysis, transplant, Cr >2.26 mg/dL/200 µmol/L (+1) and/or Abnormal liver function — cirrhosis, bilirubin >2× ULN with AST/ALT/AP >3× ULN (+1), Stroke history (+1), Bleeding history or predisposition (+1), Labile INR — time in therapeutic range <60% (+1), Elderly >65 (+1), Drugs — antiplatelets/NSAIDs (+1) and/or Alcohol ≥8 drinks/week (+1). Score ≥3 indicates high bleeding risk → caution and reversible risk factor modification
has_bled.criterion_quote: HAS-BLED score (hypertension, abnormal renal/liver function, stroke, bleeding history or predisposition, labile international normalized ratio [INR], elderly [age ≥ 65 years], drugs/alcohol concomitantly) was developed to predict bleeding amongst AF patients.

source.title: A novel user-friendly score (HAS-BLED) to assess 1-year risk of major bleeding in patients with atrial fibrillation

pleural_effusion_lights.name: Pleural Effusion — Light's Criteria (exudate vs transudate)
pleural_effusion_lights.criterion: An effusion is exudative if ANY ONE of: (1) pleural-fluid protein / serum protein >0.5; (2) pleural-fluid LDH / serum LDH >0.6; (3) pleural-fluid LDH >2/3 the upper limit of normal serum LDH. If NONE met → transudate. Diuresed heart failure can falsely classify as exudate; in suspected HF use serum-to-pleural albumin gradient >1.2 g/dL or serum-to-pleural protein gradient >3.1 g/dL to support transudate
pleural_effusion_lights.criterion_quote: An effusion is classified as exudate if one or more of the following are present: (1) the ratio of pleural fluid protein to serum protein is greater than 0.5, (2) the ratio of pleural fluid lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) to serum LDH is greater than 0.6, or (3) the pleural fluid LDH level is greater than two thirds of the upper limit of normal for serum LDH.

source.title: Pleural effusions: the diagnostic separation of transudates and exudates

pleural_effusion_lights.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed CAPTCHA blocked; Light 1972 thresholds (0.5, 0.6, 2/3 ULN) canonical.]

alcohol_use_disorder.name: Alcohol Use Disorder — DSM-5
alcohol_use_disorder.criterion: ≥2 of 11 criteria within a 12-month period: (1) drinking more/longer than intended; (2) persistent desire or failed attempts to cut down; (3) significant time spent obtaining/using/recovering; (4) craving; (5) failure to fulfill role obligations; (6) continued use despite social/interpersonal problems; (7) important activities given up; (8) recurrent use in hazardous situations; (9) continued use despite physical/psychological problems; (10) tolerance; (11) withdrawal. Severity: 2–3 mild, 4–5 moderate, ≥6 severe
alcohol_use_disorder.criterion_quote: Under DSM–5, anyone meeting any 2 of the 11 criteria during the same 12-month period would receive a diagnosis of AUD. … severity levels: mild (2–3), moderate (4–5), severe (6+).

source.title: DSM-5 Alcohol Use Disorder criteria; NIAAA reference

generalized_anxiety_disorder.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder — DSM-5
generalized_anxiety_disorder.criterion: Excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for ≥6 months, about ≥2 events or activities; difficult to control; associated with ≥3 of 6 symptoms (only 1 required in children): restlessness/keyed-up, easily fatigued, difficulty concentrating/mind going blank, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance. Causes clinically significant distress or impairment; not attributable to substance/medical condition; not better explained by another mental disorder
generalized_anxiety_disorder.criterion_quote: Excessive anxiety and worry (apprehensive expectation) occurring more days than not for at least six months, about a number of events or activities. … associated with three or more of the following six symptoms: restlessness, being easily fatigued, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, and sleep disturbance.

source.title: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5/DSM-5-TR)

major_depressive_disorder.name: Major Depressive Disorder — DSM-5
major_depressive_disorder.criterion: ≥5 of 9 symptoms present during the same 2-week period, representing a change from prior functioning; AT LEAST ONE must be (1) depressed mood OR (2) anhedonia. The 9 symptoms (SIGECAPS + depressed mood): depressed mood, anhedonia, Sleep change (insomnia/hypersomnia), Interest loss, Guilt/worthlessness, Energy loss/fatigue, Concentration impairment/indecisiveness, Appetite or weight change, Psychomotor agitation/retardation, Suicidal ideation. Symptoms cause clinically significant distress or impairment; not attributable to substance/medical condition; no history of mania/hypomania
major_depressive_disorder.criterion_quote: To diagnose Major Depressive Disorder according to the DSM-5, there must be at least five symptoms present for two weeks, with at least one symptom being either depressed mood or loss of interest/pleasure.

source.title: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5/DSM-5-TR)

hap_vap.name: Hospital-Acquired & Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (HAP / VAP)
hap_vap.criterion: HAP: new pneumonia developing >48 h after admission in non-intubated patients. VAP: new pneumonia developing >48 h after endotracheal intubation. Diagnostic: new or progressive radiographic infiltrate PLUS ≥2 clinical signs of infection (fever ≥38°C, leukocytosis or leukopenia, purulent secretions/new or increased sputum, worsening oxygenation/new O₂ requirement). IDSA recommends clinical criteria alone, not biomarkers (PCT, sTREM-1, CRP)
hap_vap.criterion_quote: HAP is a new pneumonia … that develops more than 48 hours after admission in nonintubated patients. VAP is a new pneumonia that develops after 48 hours of endotracheal intubation. … The diagnosis of HAP and VAP requires abnormal radiological findings of new or progressed pulmonary infiltrate(s) plus clinical signs of infection.

source.title: Management of Adults With Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia: 2016 Clinical Practice Guidelines

alcoholic_hepatitis.name: Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis — NIAAA + Maddrey/MELD severity
alcoholic_hepatitis.criterion: NIAAA 'probable AH' (clinical): onset of jaundice within 8 weeks; ongoing alcohol use >40 g/day (women) / >60 g/day (men) for >6 months with <60 days abstinence before onset; AST >50 IU/L, AST/ALT >1.5, both <400 IU/L; serum total bilirubin >3 mg/dL; no other liver disease. Severity — Maddrey Discriminant Function (MDF) = 4.6×(PT−control PT) + total bilirubin (mg/dL); severe ≥32. MELD ≥21 also defines severe AH
alcoholic_hepatitis.criterion_quote: The NIAAA definition of probable AH includes a recent onset of jaundice within 8 weeks of admission, persistent alcohol consumption greater than 40 g and 60 g daily for females and males, respectively, for more than 6 months. … Severe alcoholic hepatitis is defined as a score of at least 32 using the Maddrey Discriminant Function tool or at least 21 using the Model for End-Stage Liver Disease.

source.title: Standard Definitions and Common Data Elements for Clinical Trials in Patients With Alcoholic Hepatitis (NIAAA); 2019 AASLD Guidance on ALD

thyroid_storm.name: Thyroid Storm — Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale (BWPS)
thyroid_storm.criterion: Sum points across 5 domains: thermoregulatory (5–30 for temp 99–104+°F), CNS (0–30: agitation 10, delirium/psychosis 20, seizure/coma 30), GI/hepatic (0–20: diarrhea/N/V 10, unexplained jaundice 20), cardiovascular HR (5–25 for HR 90–140+), CHF (0–15: pedal edema 5, basilar rales 10, pulmonary edema 15), atrial fibrillation (10), precipitant history (0 or 10). Score ≥45 highly suggestive of thyroid storm; 25–44 impending storm; <25 unlikely
thyroid_storm.criterion_quote: A score of 45 or greater is highly suggestive of thyroid storm; a score of 25–44 is suggestive of impending thyroid storm, and a score below 25 is unlikely to represent thyroid storm.

source.title: Life-threatening thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid storm

thyroid_storm.notes: Confirmation requires concurrent thyrotoxicosis (suppressed TSH + elevated T4/T3). [AUDIT 2026-06: BWPS thresholds universal but abstract truncated.]

hepatorenal_syndrome.name: Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS-AKI) — ICA 2015
hepatorenal_syndrome.criterion: ALL of: (1) cirrhosis with ascites; (2) AKI per ICA-AKI (≥0.3 mg/dL Cr rise within 48 h or ≥50% rise from baseline within 7 days); (3) no response to 2 consecutive days of diuretic withdrawal AND volume expansion with albumin 1 g/kg/day (max 100 g/day); (4) no shock; (5) no current/recent nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast); (6) no signs of structural kidney injury (no proteinuria >500 mg/d, no microhematuria >50 RBC/HPF, normal renal US)
hepatorenal_syndrome.criterion_quote: The new criteria adopted the AKI definitions used by the Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) guidelines, with AKI defined as an increase in Cr by ≥0.3 mg/dl within 48 hours or ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days. … The HRS-1 diagnostic requirement for sCr to double to a value above 2.5 mg/dL over 2 weeks was removed.

source.title: Diagnosis and management of acute kidney injury in patients with cirrhosis: revised consensus

hepatorenal_syndrome.notes: AKI definition in [[aki]]. SBP often precipitates HRS.

sbp.name: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
sbp.criterion: Ascitic fluid absolute PMN count ≥250 cells/mm³, with or without positive ascitic fluid culture, in the absence of an intra-abdominal surgically treatable infection source. Culture-negative neutrocytic ascites (PMN ≥250 but culture negative) treated as SBP. Bacterascites = positive culture with PMN <250 — generally not treated unless symptomatic
sbp.criterion_quote: SBP is defined as an ascitic fluid absolute polymorphonuclear leukocyte (PMN) count of at least 250 cells/mm3, with or without a positive ascitic fluid culture, in the absence of an intra-abdominal surgically treatable source of infection.

source.title: Diagnosis, Evaluation, and Management of Ascites, Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis, and Hepatorenal Syndrome

decompensated_cirrhosis.name: Decompensated Cirrhosis — AASLD/EASL definition
decompensated_cirrhosis.criterion: Cirrhosis (histologic, imaging, or composite clinical/lab) PLUS ≥1 decompensating event: (1) Ascites — clinical signs + confirmed by US or paracentesis. (2) Variceal hemorrhage — hematemesis/melena or Hb drop ≥2 g/dL; endoscopy showing active varix bleeding, white nipple, clot on varix, or varices with no other source. (3) Overt hepatic encephalopathy — West Haven grade ≥II. Also: jaundice (bilirubin substantial elevation), HRS, SBP. HVPG ≥10 mmHg = clinically significant portal hypertension
decompensated_cirrhosis.criterion_quote: Hepatic decompensation is defined by the presence of ascites, hepatic encephalopathy (HE), or esophageal variceal bleeding. … Variceal bleeding is suspected if patients present with haematemesis or melaena or a decrease in hemoglobin level of at least 2 g/dL. … Overt hepatic encephalopathy is evidenced if there is symptom or sign compatible with West Haven criteria grade II or above.

source.title: AASLD Practice Guidance: Risk Stratification and Management of Portal Hypertension and Varices; EASL CPG on decompensated cirrhosis

decompensated_cirrhosis.notes: Hepatic encephalopathy criteria in [[hepatic_encephalopathy]]. Severity scores in [[child_pugh]] and [[meld_na]].

pulmonary_embolism.name: Pulmonary Embolism — ESC 2019 diagnosis & severity stratification
pulmonary_embolism.criterion: Diagnosis: Low clinical probability (Wells/Geneva) + age-adjusted D-dimer <500 ng/mL (or <age×10 if >50 y) rules out PE; otherwise CTPA confirms with filling defect, or V/Q scan. Severity: High-risk = hemodynamic instability (cardiac arrest, obstructive shock SBP <90 or vasopressors, or persistent SBP <90 or drop ≥40 mmHg ≥15 min). Intermediate-high = no instability + PESI III–V or sPESI ≥1 + BOTH RV dysfunction (echo/CT) AND positive troponin. Intermediate-low = no instability + PESI III–V or sPESI ≥1 + only one or none of RV/troponin. Low = PESI class I–II or sPESI 0
pulmonary_embolism.criterion_quote: The presence of haemodynamic instability is the main determinant to classify patients as having a high risk PE. Other patients are divided into intermediate-high risk (no hemodynamic instability, but clinical criteria of severity, positive PESI or sPESI, and both signs of right ventricular (RV) dilation and positive troponin). … A PESI of class I–II or an sPESI of 0 is a reliable predictor of low-risk PE.

source.title: 2019 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of acute pulmonary embolism

hypercholesterolemia.name: Hypercholesterolemia / Hyperlipidemia — ACC/AHA risk classification
hypercholesterolemia.criterion: Severe primary hypercholesterolemia: LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL (high-intensity statin without ASCVD risk calculation). For primary prevention age 40–75: 10-yr ASCVD risk bands — low <5%, borderline 5 to <7.5%, intermediate 7.5 to <20%, high ≥20%. Diabetes age 40–75 with LDL ≥70 mg/dL → moderate-intensity statin regardless of risk. Goal: maximally tolerated statin to lower LDL-C ≥50%
hypercholesterolemia.criterion_quote: In primary prevention, patients are divided into those at low (<5%), borderline (5-<7.5%), intermediate (7.5-<20%), and high (≥20%) risk based on the ASCVD risk estimator. In patients with severe primary hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C level ≥190 mg/dL), high-intensity statin therapy should begin without calculating 10-year ASCVD risk.

source.title: 2018 Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol

gout.name: Gout — ACR/EULAR 2015 classification
gout.criterion: Entry: ≥1 episode of swelling/pain/tenderness in a peripheral joint or bursa. Sufficient: MSU crystals in symptomatic joint/bursa or tophus (classify as gout outright). Otherwise score across 8 domains (max 23) — clinical pattern of involvement, time-course of episode (erythema/can't bear touch/difficulty walking), tophus, serum urate level (<4 mg/dL: −4; 4–<6: 0; 6–<8: +2; 8–<10: +3; ≥10: +4), MSU-negative aspirate (−2), imaging evidence of urate deposition (US double-contour or DECT: +4) and gout-related erosions (+4). Classify as gout if score ≥8
gout.criterion_quote: If MSU crystals are present in synovial fluid from a symptomatic joint/bursa or in a tophus, the patient can be classified as having gout without further scoring. … a score of 8 or more constitutes gout.

source.title: 2015 Gout Classification Criteria: An ACR/EULAR Collaborative Initiative

sle.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus — EULAR/ACR 2019 classification
sle.criterion: Entry criterion (obligatory): ANA ≥1:80 on HEp-2 cells (ever). If met, score additive weighted items (count highest-weighted item per domain) across 7 clinical domains (constitutional, hematologic, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal) and 3 immunologic domains (antiphospholipid antibodies, complement proteins, SLE-specific antibodies). Each criterion attributable to SLE not better explained by another diagnosis. Classify as SLE if total score ≥10
sle.criterion_quote: The 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria for SLE include positive ANA at least once as obligatory entry criterion. … additive weighted criteria grouped in 7 clinical and 3 immunologic domains, and weighted from 2 to 10. Patients accumulating ≥10 points are classified as having systemic lupus erythematosus.

source.title: 2019 EULAR/ACR Classification Criteria for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

rheumatoid_arthritis.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis — ACR/EULAR 2010 classification
rheumatoid_arthritis.criterion: Apply to patients with ≥1 swollen joint not better explained by another disease. Score (max 10), classify as definite RA if ≥6: Joint involvement — 1 large joint=0, 2–10 large=1, 1–3 small=2, 4–10 small=3, >10 (≥1 small)=5. Serology — neg RF & ACPA=0, low-positive=2, high-positive (>3× ULN)=3. Acute-phase reactants — normal CRP & ESR=0, abnormal=1. Duration of symptoms — <6 wk=0, ≥6 wk=1
rheumatoid_arthritis.criterion_quote: The categories of the 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria are grouped into four classifications, with point scores for each: joint symptoms; serology (including RF and/or ACPA); symptom duration, whether <6 weeks or >6 weeks; and acute-phase reactants (CRP and/or ESR).
