A score ≥6 with synovitis in ≥1 joint and absence of an alternative diagnosis is highly indicative of RA.

source.title: 2010 Rheumatoid arthritis classification criteria: an ACR/EULAR collaborative initiative

alcohol_withdrawal_ciwa.name: Alcohol Withdrawal — CIWA-Ar severity
alcohol_withdrawal_ciwa.criterion: 10 items, each 0–7 except orientation (0–4); max total 67. Items: nausea/vomiting, tremor, paroxysmal sweats, anxiety, agitation, tactile disturbances, auditory disturbances, visual disturbances, headache, orientation/clouding of sensorium. Severity bands: <10 mild; 10–15 moderate (some sources 8–15); >15 severe with risk of DTs (some sources ≥20 = severe)
alcohol_withdrawal_ciwa.criterion_quote: The Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol, revised (CIWA-Ar) is a 10-item clinical scale developed by Sullivan et al. in 1989 to quantify the severity of alcohol withdrawal syndrome. The maximum score is 67. … Scores of 15 or more than 15 indicate severe withdrawal with impending possible delirium tremens.

source.title: Assessment of alcohol withdrawal: the revised clinical institute withdrawal assessment for alcohol scale (CIWA-Ar)

alcohol_withdrawal_ciwa.notes: Often documented as a score in the chart; threshold ≥8–10 typically triggers benzo dosing.

acute_liver_failure.name: Acute Liver Failure — AASLD definition + King's College Criteria
acute_liver_failure.criterion: AASLD definition: coagulopathy (INR ≥1.5) AND any-degree encephalopathy in a patient without preexisting cirrhosis, with illness duration <26 weeks. King's College transplant criteria — Acetaminophen: arterial pH <7.3 OR (all of: INR >6.5 AND creatinine >300 µmol/L (~3.4 mg/dL) AND grade III–IV encephalopathy). Non-acetaminophen: INR >6.5 OR any 3 of {age <10 or >40, etiology non-A/non-B hepatitis or drug-induced, jaundice >7 d before encephalopathy, INR >3.5, bilirubin >300 µmol/L (~17.5 mg/dL)}
acute_liver_failure.criterion_quote: For acetaminophen-induced liver failure, the King's College Criteria identify two groups of patients with poor prognosis: Arterial pH < 7.3; or all three of an international normalized ratio (INR) of greater than 6.5, serum creatinine of greater than 300 micromoles per litre and the presence of encephalopathy (grade III or IV).

source.title: AASLD Position Paper: The Management of Acute Liver Failure / Early indicators of prognosis in fulminant hepatic failure

bacterial_meningitis.name: Acute Bacterial Meningitis (CSF diagnostic criteria)
bacterial_meningitis.criterion: Diagnostic CSF findings: WBC >1000/µL (>50% PMN), glucose <2.2 mmol/L (~40 mg/dL) or CSF:serum glucose ratio ≤0.4, protein >1 g/L (>100 mg/dL). >99% certainty thresholds: glucose <34 mg/dL, protein >220 mg/dL, WBC >2000/µL, OR neutrophils >1180/µL. Confirmation: positive Gram stain (60–90% sensitive) or CSF culture
bacterial_meningitis.criterion_quote: Typical CSF findings among adult patients are elevated white blood cell (WBC) count (>1000 × 10⁶/L and >50% polymorphonuclear leukocytes), decreased glucose level (<2.2 mmol/L), and increased protein concentration (>1 g/L). … Bacterial meningitis can be diagnosed with more than 99% certainty in patients with: glucose less than 34 mg per dL, protein greater than 220 mg per dL, WBCs greater than 2,000 per μL, or neutrophils greater than 1,180 per μL.

source.title: Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis

bacterial_meningitis.notes: Spanos cutoffs (>99% certainty) widely cited; restated in AAFP CSF analysis review 2021.

cryptococcosis.name: Cryptococcosis (incl. cryptococcal meningoencephalitis) — IDSA 2010
cryptococcosis.criterion: Asymptomatic cryptococcal antigenemia warrants LP + blood culture; if positive, treat as meningoencephalitis/disseminated disease. CSF opening pressure must be measured at baseline (raised ICP is a key prognostic marker)
cryptococcosis.criterion_quote: For asymptomatic antigenemia, perform lumbar puncture and blood culture; if results are positive, treat as symptomatic meningoencephalitis. Identify CSF pressure at baseline. A prompt baseline lumbar puncture is strongly encouraged.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Cryptococcal Disease: 2010 Update

histoplasmosis.name: Disseminated Histoplasmosis — IDSA 2007
histoplasmosis.criterion: Detection of Histoplasma antigen in urine (>90% sensitivity in disseminated disease) or serum (~80%) supports diagnosis. Culture of H. capsulatum from blood, BM, lung, or sterile site is confirmatory. Serology (immunodiffusion + complement fixation) supportive but less sensitive in immunocompromised
histoplasmosis.criterion_quote: Histoplasma antigen can be detected in the urine in >90% of patients and in the serum in 80% of patients who have disseminated histoplasmosis

source.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Patients with Histoplasmosis: 2007 Update

blastomycosis.name: Blastomycosis — IDSA 2008
blastomycosis.criterion: Definitive diagnosis by culture growth of B. dermatitidis from clinical specimen. Presumptive diagnosis by direct visualization of characteristic broad-based budding yeast (~8–15 µm) in cytologic/histologic specimens. Commercial urine/serum antigen test available but cross-reacts with Histoplasma
blastomycosis.criterion_quote: Direct visualization of the organism in cytologic and histologic specimens has been the most commonly used method for rapid diagnosis of blastomycosis.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Blastomycosis: 2008 Update

prosthetic_joint_infection.name: Prosthetic Joint Infection (PJI) — IDSA 2013
prosthetic_joint_infection.criterion: Definitive PJI: (a) sinus tract communicating with the prosthesis; OR (b) purulence without other etiology surrounding the prosthesis; OR (c) ≥2 intraoperative cultures (or combination of preop aspiration + intraoperative cultures) yielding the same organism. Highly suggestive: acute inflammation on periprosthetic histopathology. Synovial WBC and PMN% thresholds (knee >1100 WBC/µL or >64% PMN; hip >3000/µL or >80% PMN — chronic PJI cutoffs) commonly applied
prosthetic_joint_infection.criterion_quote: The presence of a sinus tract that communicates with the prosthesis is definitive evidence of PJI. The presence of purulence without another known etiology surrounding the prosthesis is definitive evidence of PJI. Two or more intraoperative cultures or combination of preoperative aspiration and intraoperative cultures that yield the same organism…may be considered definitive evidence of PJI. The presence of acute inflammation as seen on histopathologic examination of the periprosthetic tissue…is highly suggestive evidence of PJI.

source.title: Diagnosis and Management of Prosthetic Joint Infection: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline (Osmon)

complicated_iai.name: Complicated Intra-abdominal Infection (cIAI) — IDSA 2024
complicated_iai.criterion: cIAI: infection extending beyond the hollow viscus of origin into the peritoneal space or other sterile abdominal region, with peritonitis ± abscess formation. Uncomplicated IAI is limited to intramural inflammation of the GI tract. Risk stratification by clinical severity drives empiric antibiotic spectrum and source-control timing
complicated_iai.criterion_quote: A complicated intra-abdominal infection (cIAI) extends beyond the hollow viscus of origin into the peritoneal space or an otherwise sterile region of the abdominal cavity and is associated with peritonitis with or without abscess formation.

source.title: 2024 Guideline Update on the Risk Assessment, Diagnostic Imaging, and Microbiological Evaluation of cIAI in Adults, Children, and Pregnant People

encephalitis_idsa.name: Infectious Encephalitis — IDSA 2008
encephalitis_idsa.criterion: Encephalitis = inflammatory process of the brain PLUS clinical evidence of neurologic dysfunction (fever, headache, altered consciousness, cognitive/behavioral changes, focal neuro signs, seizures). Workup includes CSF analysis (lymphocytic pleocytosis common), MRI, EEG, HSV PCR, autoimmune encephalitis panel when appropriate
encephalitis_idsa.criterion_quote: Encephalitis is defined by the presence of an inflammatory process of the brain in association with clinical evidence of neurologic dysfunction.

source.title: The Management of Encephalitis: IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines (Tunkel)

leishmaniasis.name: Leishmaniasis (cutaneous & visceral) — ASTMH/IDSA 2016
leishmaniasis.criterion: Parasitologic confirmation by visualization of amastigotes on smear/histopathology, in vitro culture, or molecular amplification (most sensitive). Serology used for visceral leishmaniasis when parasitologic tests cannot be done or are negative
leishmaniasis.criterion_quote: Molecular amplification assays typically should be performed because they are the most sensitive Leishmania tests currently available. Serologic testing is recommended for persons with suspected VL in whom definitive diagnostic tests for the parasite…cannot be conducted or have negative results.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Leishmaniasis

covid19_severity.name: COVID-19 Disease Severity Stratification — IDSA / NIH
covid19_severity.criterion: Mild: symptomatic but no shortness of breath, dyspnea, or abnormal imaging. Moderate: lower respiratory disease + SpO₂ ≥94% on room air. Severe: SpO₂ <94% on room air, or PaO₂/FiO₂ <300, or RR >30, or lung infiltrates >50%. Critical: respiratory failure (high-flow O₂, NIV, mechanical ventilation, ECMO), septic shock, and/or multi-organ dysfunction
covid19_severity.criterion_quote: Patients with severe COVID-19 are those whose infection has pulmonary involvement resulting in hypoxia while breathing room air and/or needing treatment with low flow oxygen. Most existing criteria for trials consider either a SpO2 level less than 94% or 90% or tachypnea (respiratory rate >30 breaths per minute) as severe COVID-19.

source.title: IDSA Guidelines on the Treatment and Management of Patients with COVID-19

ntm_pulmonary.name: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial (NTM) Pulmonary Disease — ATS/ERS/ESCMID/IDSA 2020
ntm_pulmonary.criterion: All three required: (1) pulmonary or systemic symptoms; (2) radiographic — nodular or cavitary opacities on CXR, or HRCT showing bronchiectasis with multiple small nodules, with appropriate exclusion of other diagnoses; (3) microbiologic — ≥2 positive expectorated sputum cultures (same NTM species/subspecies), OR ≥1 positive bronchial wash/lavage culture, OR transbronchial/other lung biopsy with mycobacterial histologic features (granulomatous inflammation or AFB) + positive NTM culture
ntm_pulmonary.criterion_quote: Positive culture results from at least two separate expectorated sputum samples … Positive culture results from at least one bronchial wash or lavage … Transbronchial or other lung biopsy with mycobacterial histologic features (granulomatous inflammation or AFB) and positive culture for NTM.

source.title: Treatment of Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Pulmonary Disease: ATS/ERS/ESCMID/IDSA CPG (Daley 2020)

ntm_pulmonary.notes: Diagnostic criteria carried forward from 2007 ATS/IDSA statement. Common species: MAC, M. kansasii, M. xenopi; M. abscessus subspecies relevant.

hypersensitivity_pneumonitis.name: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP) — ATS/JRS/ALAT 2020
hypersensitivity_pneumonitis.criterion: Diagnosis integrates 4 domains: (1) antigen exposure identification; (2) HRCT pattern; (3) BAL lymphocytosis; (4) histopathology if needed. HRCT classified 'typical', 'compatible', or 'indeterminate' for nonfibrotic and for fibrotic HP. Diagnostic confidence: definite ≥90%, high 80–89%, moderate 70–79%, low 51–69%. Phenotypes nonfibrotic vs fibrotic HP replace acute/subacute/chronic. BAL lymphocytosis suggested cutoffs: ≥30% nonfibrotic, ≥20% fibrotic
hypersensitivity_pneumonitis.criterion_quote: definite (≥90% confidence), high-confidence (80–89%), moderate-confidence (70–79%), and low-confidence (51–69%) … Nonfibrotic HP HRCT: Typical (ground-glass opacity, mosaic attenuation, profuse ill-defined small centrilobular nodules in diffuse distribution); Compatible with HP; Indeterminate for HP. Fibrotic HP HRCT: Typical (lung fibrosis + small airway disease abnormality); Compatible with HP; Indeterminate for HP.

source.title: Diagnosis of Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis in Adults: An Official ATS/JRS/ALAT Clinical Practice Guideline (Raghu)

bronchiectasis.name: Bronchiectasis — BTS 2019 HRCT criteria
bronchiectasis.criterion: HRCT diagnosis requires bronchial dilatation indicated by ≥1: (1) bronchoarterial ratio >1 (internal airway lumen vs adjacent pulmonary artery; >1.5 proposed in older adults for higher specificity); (2) lack of tapering of the airways; (3) airway visibility within 1 cm of the costal pleural surface, or touching the mediastinal pleura. Indirect signs: bronchial wall thickening, mucus impaction, mosaic perfusion/air trapping on expiratory imaging
bronchiectasis.criterion_quote: Bronchoarterial ratio >1 (internal airway lumen vs adjacent pulmonary artery) … Lack of tapering … Airway visibility within 1 cm of costal pleural surface or touching mediastinal pleura.

source.title: BTS Guideline for Bronchiectasis in Adults (Hill 2019)

pneumoconiosis_ilo.name: Pneumoconiosis — ILO 2022 Radiographic Classification
pneumoconiosis_ilo.criterion: CXR classified by: (a) profusion of small opacities — major 4-point scale (0–3), 12-point subdivided (0/-, 0/0, 0/1, 1/0, 1/1, 1/2, 2/1, 2/2, 2/3, 3/2, 3/3, 3/+); (b) small opacity shape/size — rounded: p ≤1.5 mm, q >1.5–3 mm, r >3–10 mm; irregular: s, t, u (same size bands); (c) large opacities — A combined ≤5 cm, B >5 cm but ≤ area of right upper zone, C > area of right upper zone; (d) pleural abnormalities by location, width, extent, calcification
pneumoconiosis_ilo.criterion_quote: Profusion of small opacities is classified on a 4-point major category scale (0 – 3). Each major category is divided into three, giving a 12-point scale between 0/- and 3/+ … p (up to 1.5 mm), q (1.5-3 mm), r (3-10 mm) … Category A: one or more large opacities whose combined dimension does not exceed 5 cm. Category B: one or more large opacities whose combined dimension exceeds 5 cm but does not exceed the equivalent area of the right upper lung zone. Category C: size is greater than the equivalent area of the right upper lung zone.

source.title: ILO International Classification of Radiographs of Pneumoconioses (Revised 2022)

dcm.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM) — ESC 2023
dcm.criterion: DCM = presence of LV dilatation + global or regional systolic dysfunction, NOT explained solely by abnormal loading conditions (hypertension, valve disease, CHD) or coronary artery disease. Defers numeric BSA-indexed LVEDV/LVEDD cutoffs to standard imaging reference ranges (echo/CMR z-scores by sex and BSA)
dcm.criterion_quote: DCM is defined as the presence of LV dilatation and global or regional systolic dysfunction unexplained solely by abnormal loading conditions (e.g. hypertension, valve disease, congenital heart disease) or coronary artery disease.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of cardiomyopathies

ndlvc.name: Non-Dilated Left Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (NDLVC) — ESC 2023
ndlvc.criterion: NDLVC = non-ischemic LV scarring or fatty replacement (with or without wall motion abnormalities) OR isolated global LV hypokinesia without scarring, WITHOUT LV dilatation. Newly introduced phenotype that supplants the prior term 'hypokinetic non-dilated cardiomyopathy.' Requires CMR for scar/fatty replacement assessment
ndlvc.criterion_quote: The NDLVC phenotype is defined as the presence of non-ischaemic LV scarring or fatty replacement regardless of the presence of global or regional wall motion abnormalities, or isolated global LV hypokinesia without scarring.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of cardiomyopathies

rcm.name: Restrictive Cardiomyopathy (RCM) — ESC 2023
rcm.criterion: RCM = restrictive LV and/or RV pathophysiology in the presence of normal or reduced diastolic volumes (of one or both ventricles), normal or reduced systolic volumes, and normal ventricular wall thickness. Echo features: restrictive filling, biatrial dilation, non-dilated ventricles. Wall thickness can be normal or increased (infiltrative)
rcm.criterion_quote: RCM is defined as restrictive left and/or RV pathophysiology in the presence of normal or reduced diastolic volumes (of one or both ventricles), normal or reduced systolic volumes, and normal ventricular wall thickness.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of cardiomyopathies

lv_hypertrabeculation.name: LV Hypertrabeculation (replaces LV non-compaction/LVNC) — ESC 2023
lv_hypertrabeculation.criterion: ESC 2023 recommends terminology 'hypertrabeculation' rather than LVNC; isolated trabeculation is a morphologic trait, not a standalone cardiomyopathy. Steps back from numeric NC/C ratio cutoffs (Jenni echo NC/C >2 at end-systole; Petersen CMR NC/C >2.3 at end-diastole) as standalone disease-defining thresholds
lv_hypertrabeculation.criterion_quote: Given the lack of morphometric evidence for ventricular compaction in humans, the term 'hypertrabeculation,' rather than LVNC, is recommended.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of cardiomyopathies

attr_amyloidosis.name: ATTR Cardiac Amyloidosis — non-biopsy diagnostic algorithm (ESC 2023)
attr_amyloidosis.criterion: Bone-tracer scintigraphy ([99mTc]-DPD/PYP/HMDP) with cardiac uptake Perugini grade 2 or 3 + absence of monoclonal protein on serum/urine immunofixation electrophoresis AND normal serum free light chain ratio confirms ATTR-CM without endomyocardial biopsy. If monoclonal component present → biopsy required to exclude AL amyloidosis (and distinguish AL vs ATTR)
attr_amyloidosis.criterion_quote: Bone-tracer scintigraphy or single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) represent the gold standard for the diagnosis of ATTR-related cardiac amyloidosis.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of cardiomyopathies (Figure 22 / Section 7.7)

cmp_family_screening.name: Cardiomyopathy Family Screening — ESC 2023 cascade
cmp_family_screening.criterion: First-degree relatives of cardiomyopathy probands should undergo clinical evaluation, 12-lead ECG, and cardiac imaging (echocardiography ± CMR). Cascade genetic testing offered when a pathogenic/likely pathogenic (P/LP) variant is identified in proband. When no P/LP variant identified or genetics not performed, relatives undergo periodic re-screening (more frequent during adolescence)
cmp_family_screening.criterion_quote: Cascade genetic testing, with pre- and post-test counselling, is recommended in adult at-risk relatives if a confident genetic diagnosis (a pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant) has been established in an individual with cardiomyopathy in the family.

source.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of cardiomyopathies (Recommendation Table 14)

wilson_disease.name: Wilson Disease — AASLD 2023 + Leipzig score
wilson_disease.criterion: Leipzig composite score uses 7 items: Kayser-Fleischer rings (+2 present, 0 absent), neurologic symptoms (+2 severe, +1 mild, 0 absent), serum ceruloplasmin (+2 if <5 mg/dL, +1 if 5–20 mg/dL, 0 if normal), Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia (+1 present, 0 absent), hepatic copper (+2 if >250 µg/g dry weight, +1 if 50–250, 0 if normal), 24-h urinary copper (+2 if >2× ULN, +1 if 1–2× ULN, 0 if normal), ATP7B mutation analysis (+4 if 2 disease-causing variants, +1 if 1, 0 if none). Total: ≤2 unlikely; 3 possible; ≥4 establishes diagnosis. Key thresholds: serum ceruloplasmin <5 mg/dL strongly suggests WD; 24-h urinary copper >100 µg/d (>1.6 µmol/d) typical for symptomatic WD; >40 µg/d may indicate WD in asymptomatic/pediatric
wilson_disease.criterion_quote: If the total score is 2 or less, WD diagnosis is unlikely, 3 is considered as diagnosis possible and 4 or above are considered as diagnosis established. … An extremely low serum ceruloplasmin level (<5 mg/dl) more strongly suggests Wilson disease diagnosis than modestly subnormal levels. … 24-h urinary copper excretion, with cut-off values >100 μg/24 h (>1.6 μmol/24 h) being typical for symptomatic patients with Wilson disease.

source.org: AASLD; Leipzig 8th International Meeting on WD (Ferenci)
source.title: Wilson Disease 2022 AASLD Practice Guidance; Leipzig score

a1at_deficiency.name: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency — AASLD 2023
a1at_deficiency.criterion: Triage: serum AAT <50 mg/dL (<11 µmol/L) raises suspicion of severe AATD. Confirmation: genotyping for SERPINA1 variants (at minimum S and Z alleles); phenotyping (Pi-typing) acceptable when speed is needed. Pi*ZZ homozygous = severe AATD, dominant cause of AATD-associated pediatric and adult liver disease and emphysema. Pi*MZ heterozygous = disease modifier, manifests primarily with co-existing comorbidities (metabolic risk factors, alcohol, viral hepatitis)
a1at_deficiency.criterion_quote: A cut-off of 50 mg/dL is used as a triage tool that raises suspicion of severe AATD. … Phenotyping can be used when a quick decision is needed, whereas genotyping should be performed for a definitive diagnosis. … In its homozygous form (Pi*ZZ genotype), it is responsible for the majority of severe AATD cases and can cause both pediatric and adult liver disease. … The heterozygous form (Pi*MZ) is considered a disease modifier that becomes apparent primarily in the presence of other comorbidities or risk factors.

source.title: AASLD 2023 Practice Guidance on AATD-associated liver disease (Hepatology)

budd_chiari.name: Budd-Chiari Syndrome (BCS) — AASLD 2020
budd_chiari.criterion: BCS = obstruction of hepatic venous outflow anywhere from the small hepatic veins to the junction of the IVC and the right atrium, in the absence of intracardiac or pericardial obstruction. Diagnostic imaging — Doppler US: no flow in part or all hepatic veins; reversed/turbulent/continuous flow; intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (specific finding). CT/MRI: non-visualized or non-opacified hepatic veins, filling defects, marked caudate lobe hypertrophy (80–91%). Concomitant portal vein thrombosis in up to 15%. Etiology workup: myeloproliferative neoplasms (JAK2 V617F), antiphospholipid syndrome, paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, OCP/pregnancy, malignancy
budd_chiari.criterion_quote: Budd-Chiari syndrome (BCS) is characterized by obstruction of the hepatic venous outflow anywhere from the small hepatic veins to the junction of the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the right atrium in the absence of intracardiac or pericardial obstruction.

source.title: Vascular Liver Disorders, Portal Vein Thrombosis, and Procedural Bleeding in Patients With Liver Disease: 2020 Practice Guidance

colorectal_cancer_ajcc8.name: Colorectal Cancer — AJCC TNM 8
colorectal_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: Tis intramucosal/in situ; T1 invades submucosa; T2 invades muscularis propria; T3 invades through muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues; T4a perforates visceral peritoneum; T4b directly invades adjacent organs. N: N0; N1a 1 node; N1b 2–3 nodes; N1c tumor deposit(s) in subserosa/mesentery/nonperitonealized pericolic/perirectal tissues without nodal mets; N2a 4–6 nodes; N2b ≥7 nodes. M: M0; M1a metastasis to one site/organ without peritoneal metastasis; M1b two or more sites/organs without peritoneal metastasis; M1c peritoneal metastasis ± other organs
colorectal_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: Nodules containing no identifiable lymph node tissue or vascular/neural structures should be considered tumor deposits and designated N1c. N1c is defined as tumor deposit(s) in the subserosa, mesentery, or nonperitonealized pericolic or perirectal tissues without regional nodal metastasis.

source.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual 8th edition — Colorectal Cancer (Weiser, Ann Surg Oncol 2018)

breast_cancer_ajcc8.name: Breast Cancer — AJCC TNM 8 (anatomic)
breast_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: Tis (DCIS or Paget without invasive tumor); T1 ≤2 cm (T1mi ≤1 mm, T1a 1–5 mm, T1b 5–10 mm, T1c 10–20 mm); T2 >2 cm but ≤5 cm; T3 >5 cm; T4 any size with chest wall and/or skin extension (T4a chest wall, T4b skin ulceration/satellites/edema, T4c both, T4d inflammatory). N: N0; N1 1–3 axillary; N2 4–9 axillary or clinically detected internal mammary; N3 ≥10 axillary, infraclavicular, supraclavicular, or combined IM + axillary. AJCC 8 prognostic stage incorporates ER/PR/HER2, grade, and Oncotype DX (when applicable)
breast_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1: Tumor is 2 cm (3/4 of an inch) or less across. T2: Tumor is more than 2 cm but not more than 5 cm (2 inches) across. T3: Tumor is more than 5 cm across. T4: Tumor of any size growing into the chest wall or skin. … N1: Cancer has spread to 1 to 3 axillary (underarm) lymph node(s). N2: Cancer has spread to 4 to 9 lymph nodes under the arm. N3: Cancer has spread to 10 or more axillary lymph nodes.

source.title: Stages of Breast Cancer

prostate_cancer_ajcc8.name: Prostate Cancer — AJCC TNM 8
prostate_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: T1 not palpable/visible (T1a ≤5% of TURP tissue, T1b >5%, T1c needle biopsy); T2 confined to prostate (T2a ≤½ of one lobe, T2b >½ of one lobe, T2c both lobes); T3a extracapsular extension; T3b invades seminal vesicle(s); T4 invades adjacent structures (bladder neck, sphincter, rectum, levator, pelvic wall). N0/N1 regional pelvic nodes. M1a non-regional nodes; M1b bone(s); M1c other distant site(s) ± bone. Stage grouping incorporates PSA strata (<10, 10–<20, ≥20) and ISUP Grade Group 1–5 (Gleason ≤6, 3+4, 4+3, 8, 9–10)
prostate_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T3a involves extracapsular extension (unilateral or bilateral), while T3b involves tumor invasion of seminal vesicle(s)... M1a refers to metastasis to [non-regional/distant nodes], M1b refers to bones, and M1c refers to other organs.

source.title: Assessment of the prognostic value of the 8th AJCC staging system for clinically staged prostate cancer

nsclc_ajcc8.name: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer — AJCC/UICC TNM 8
nsclc_ajcc8.criterion: T: T1a ≤1 cm; T1b >1–≤2 cm; T1c >2–≤3 cm; T2a >3–≤4 cm (or visceral pleura invasion, main bronchus without carina, atelectasis); T2b >4–≤5 cm; T3 >5–≤7 cm or parietal pleura/chest wall/phrenic nerve/parietal pericardium or separate same-lobe nodule; T4 >7 cm or diaphragm/mediastinum/heart/great vessels/trachea/RLN/esophagus/vertebra/carina, or different ipsilateral lobe nodule. N0/N1 ipsilateral peribronchial/hilar; N2 ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal; N3 contralateral mediastinal/hilar or scalene/supraclavicular. M1a contralateral lung/pleural/pericardial nodules or malignant effusion; M1b single extrathoracic met in single organ; M1c multiple extrathoracic mets
nsclc_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1 is subclassified into T1a (≤1 cm), T1b (>1 to ≤2 cm), and T1c (>2 to ≤3 cm); T2 is subclassified into T2a (>3 to ≤4 cm) and T2b (>4 to ≤5 cm); tumors greater than 5 to less than or equal to 7 cm are reclassified as T3; and tumors greater than 7 cm are reclassified as T4... M1b and M1c based on the number of the metastatic foci (single [M1b] versus multiple [M1c]).

source.title: The 8th UICC/AJCC TNM edition for non-small cell lung cancer staging

nsclc_ajcc8.notes: AJCC 9 effective 2024 further splits N2/M1c — this entry is for AJCC 8.

melanoma_ajcc8.name: Cutaneous Melanoma — AJCC TNM 8
melanoma_ajcc8.criterion: T by Breslow thickness (a = no ulceration, b = ulceration): Tis melanoma in situ; T1a <0.8 mm without ulceration; T1b 0.8–1.0 mm ± ulceration OR <0.8 mm with ulceration; T2a >1.0–2.0 mm no ulceration; T2b with ulceration; T3a >2.0–4.0 mm no ulceration; T3b with ulceration; T4a >4.0 mm no ulceration; T4b with ulceration. N stratified by number of nodes (1; 2–3; ≥4 or matted), clinically occult vs detected, and presence of microsatellite/satellite/in-transit metastasis (designated 'c'). M1a distant skin/soft tissue/non-regional node; M1b lung ± M1a; M1c non-CNS visceral ± M1a/b; M1d CNS ± M1a/b/c — each with LDH suffix (0) not elevated or (1) elevated
melanoma_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1a — Tumor <0.8 mm thick without ulceration; T1b — Tumor 0.8–1 mm with/without ulceration OR Tumor <0.8 mm with ulceration; T2a — Tumor >1-2 mm without ulceration; T2b — Tumor >1-2 mm with ulceration; T3a — Tumor >2-4 mm without ulceration; T3b — Tumor >2-4 mm with ulceration; T4a — Tumor >4 mm without ulceration; T4b — Tumor >4 mm with ulceration. M1a — Distant metastases to the skin, soft tissue, including muscle and non-regional lymph node; M1b — Distant metastases to the lung; M1c — Distant metastases to non-CNS sites; M1d — Distant metastases to CNS sites.

source.title: Melanoma (Evaluation and Management) (8th Edition AJCC) — Iowa Head and Neck Protocols

melanoma_ajcc8.notes: Major change from AJCC 7: T1a/T1b threshold moved to 0.8 mm; mitotic rate removed from T category. [AUDIT 2026-06: Iowa mirror tertiary.]

gastric_cancer_ajcc8.name: Gastric Cancer — AJCC TNM 8
gastric_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: Tis high-grade dysplasia/intraepithelial carcinoma; T1a lamina propria/muscularis mucosae; T1b submucosa; T2 muscularis propria; T3 subserosa; T4a serosa (visceral peritoneum); T4b invades adjacent structures. N0; N1 1–2 nodes; N2 3–6 nodes; N3a 7–15 nodes; N3b ≥16 nodes. M0/M1 (including positive peritoneal cytology)
gastric_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1: Growth into lamina propria, muscularis mucosa, or submucosa. T2: Growth into muscularis propria layer. T3: Growth into subserosa layer. T4a: Growth through the stomach wall into the serosa, but hasn't grown into nearby organs. T4b: Growth through the stomach wall and into nearby organs or structures. N1: Spread to 1 to 2 nearby lymph nodes. N2: Spread to 3 to 6 nearby lymph nodes. N3a: Spread to 7 to 15 nearby lymph nodes. N3b: Spread to 16 or more nearby lymph nodes.

source.title: Stomach Cancer Stages

esophageal_cancer_ajcc8.name: Esophageal Cancer — AJCC TNM 8
esophageal_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: Tis high-grade dysplasia; T1 invades lamina propria, muscularis mucosae, or submucosa (T1a/T1b); T2 muscularis propria; T3 adventitia; T4a resectable adjacent structures (pleura, pericardium, azygos, diaphragm, peritoneum); T4b unresectable (aorta, vertebra, trachea). N0; N1 1–2 regional nodes; N2 3–6 nodes; N3 ≥7 nodes. Grade and tumor location modify stage groupings; AJCC 8 provides distinct stage tables for squamous cell carcinoma vs adenocarcinoma and separate clinical/pathologic/post-neoadjuvant (cTNM, pTNM, ypTNM)
esophageal_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1: Invasion into lamina propria, muscularis mucosa, or submucosa. T2: Invasion into the thick muscle layer (muscularis propria). T3: Invasion into the adventitia. T4a: Invasion into pleura, pericardium, diaphragm, or peritoneum. T4b: Invasion into trachea, aorta, spine, or other vital structures. N1: Spread to 1-2 nearby lymph nodes. N2: Spread to 3-6 nearby lymph nodes. N3: Spread to 7 or more nearby lymph nodes.

source.title: Esophagus Cancer Stages

pancreatic_cancer_ajcc8.name: Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma — AJCC TNM 8
pancreatic_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: Tis (PanIN-3, IPMN/MCN with high-grade dysplasia); T1 ≤2 cm (T1a ≤0.5, T1b >0.5–<1, T1c 1–2 cm); T2 >2–≤4 cm; T3 >4 cm; T4 involves celiac axis, SMA, and/or common hepatic artery regardless of size. N0; N1 1–3 regional nodes; N2 ≥4 regional nodes. M0/M1
pancreatic_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1: no bigger than 2 cm across. T2: larger than 2 cm but no more than 4 cm across. T3: bigger than 4 cm across. T4: growing outside the pancreas and into nearby major blood vessels. N1: spread to no more than 3 nearby lymph nodes. N2: spread to 4 or more nearby lymph nodes.

source.title: Pancreatic Cancer Stages

pancreatic_cancer_ajcc8.notes: AJCC 8 changed T to size-based (replacing 'confined to pancreas') and split N1 vs N2 by node count.

rcc_ajcc8.name: Renal Cell Carcinoma — AJCC TNM 8
rcc_ajcc8.criterion: T: T1a ≤4 cm, confined; T1b >4–≤7 cm, confined; T2a >7–≤10 cm, confined; T2b >10 cm, confined; T3a renal vein/segmental branches OR pelvicalyceal system OR perirenal/renal sinus fat but not beyond Gerota's; T3b IVC below diaphragm; T3c IVC above diaphragm or wall of vena cava; T4 beyond Gerota's fascia (including contiguous ipsilateral adrenal). N0/N1. M0/M1
rcc_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T3a disease is a tumor that extends into the renal vein or its segmental branches, or invades the pelvicalyceal system, or invades perirenal and/or renal sinus fat but not beyond Gerota's fascia. T3b tumors invade inferior vena cava (IVC) below diaphragm. T3c tumors invade IVC above diaphragm or wall of vena cava... T4 tumors invade beyond perirenal (Gerota and Zuckerkandl) fascia or invade ipsilateral adrenal gland.

source.title: Update on the Role of Imaging in Clinical Staging and Restaging of Renal Cell Carcinoma Based on the AJCC 8th Edition

rcc_ajcc8.notes: Invasion of pelvicalyceal system newly explicit in AJCC 8 T3a.

bladder_cancer_ajcc8.name: Urothelial Bladder Cancer — AJCC TNM 8
bladder_cancer_ajcc8.criterion: T: Ta non-invasive papillary; Tis carcinoma in situ (flat); T1 invades lamina propria; T2a superficial muscularis propria (inner half); T2b deep muscularis propria (outer half); T3a microscopic perivesical fat; T3b macroscopic perivesical fat; T4a prostatic stroma/seminal vesicles/uterus/vagina; T4b pelvic or abdominal wall. N0; N1 single node in true pelvis; N2 multiple nodes in true pelvis; N3 common iliac node(s). M1a distant nodes beyond common iliacs; M1b non-LN distant metastasis
bladder_cancer_ajcc8.criterion_quote: Ta: Non-invasive papillary carcinoma. Tis: Carcinoma in situ (flat, non-invasive). T1: Invasion into connective tissue (no muscle involvement). T2a: Inner muscle layer invasion. T2b: Outer muscle layer invasion. T3a/T3b: Extension through muscle into fatty tissue. T4a: Invasion of prostate, seminal vesicles, uterus, or vagina. T4b: Invasion into pelvic or abdominal wall. N1: Single lymph node in true pelvis. N2: Multiple lymph nodes in true pelvis. N3: Common iliac artery lymph nodes. M1a: Distant lymph node spread. M1b: Distant organ metastasis (bones, liver, lungs).

source.title: Bladder Cancer Stages

bladder_cancer_ajcc8.notes: AJCC 8 newly subdivided M1 into M1a (distant LN) vs M1b (non-LN distant), affecting stage IVA vs IVB.

uspstf_lung_cancer_screening.name: Lung Cancer Screening — USPSTF 2021 (LDCT)
uspstf_lung_cancer_screening.criterion: Annual low-dose CT screening for adults 50–80 with ≥20 pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years (Grade B). Discontinue once person has not smoked for 15 years or develops a health problem limiting life expectancy or willingness/ability to undergo curative lung surgery
uspstf_lung_cancer_screening.criterion_quote: Adults aged 50 to 80 years who have a 20 pack-year smoking history and currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening

uspstf_lung_cancer_screening.notes: Grade B. Lowered start age from 55 → 50 and pack-year threshold from 30 → 20 vs prior 2013 recommendation.

uspstf_aaa_screening.name: AAA Screening — USPSTF 2019
uspstf_aaa_screening.criterion: One-time AAA screening with ultrasonography in men aged 65–75 who have ever smoked (Grade B). Selective offering in men 65–75 never-smokers (Grade C). Against routine screening in never-smoker women (Grade D). Insufficient evidence in ever-smoker women (Grade I)
uspstf_aaa_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends 1-time screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) with ultrasonography in men aged 65 to 75 years who have ever smoked.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm: Screening

uspstf_statin_primary_prevention.name: Statin for Primary Prevention of CVD — USPSTF 2022
uspstf_statin_primary_prevention.criterion: Initiate statin in adults 40–75 with ≥1 CVD risk factor (dyslipidemia, diabetes, hypertension, or smoking) AND estimated 10-year CVD risk ≥10% (Grade B). For risk 7.5%–<10%, individualize (Grade C). Insufficient evidence for adults ≥76 (Grade I). Excludes LDL-C >190 mg/dL or familial hypercholesterolemia (separate recommendations)
uspstf_statin_primary_prevention.criterion_quote: Adults aged 40 to 75 years who have 1 or more cardiovascular risk factors...and an estimated 10-year risk of a cardiovascular event of 10% or greater.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Statin Use for the Primary Prevention of CVD in Adults

uspstf_aspirin_cvd.name: Aspirin for Primary CVD Prevention — USPSTF 2022
uspstf_aspirin_cvd.criterion: Individualized decision for low-dose aspirin in adults 40–59 with ≥10% 10-year CVD risk (Grade C). Recommends AGAINST initiating low-dose aspirin in adults ≥60 (Grade D). Significant change from 2016 recommendation
uspstf_aspirin_cvd.criterion_quote: Adults aged 40 to 59 years with a 10% or greater 10-year cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Aspirin Use to Prevent Cardiovascular Disease

uspstf_hcv_screening.name: Hepatitis C Screening — USPSTF 2020
uspstf_hcv_screening.criterion: Universal one-time HCV screening for adults 18–79 (Grade B). Expanded from prior 1945–1965 birth-cohort-only recommendation
uspstf_hcv_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection in adults aged 18 to 79 years.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Hepatitis C Virus Infection in Adolescents and Adults: Screening

uspstf_crc_screening.name: Colorectal Cancer Screening — USPSTF 2021
uspstf_crc_screening.criterion: Screen all adults 50–75 (Grade A); 45–49 (Grade B); selectively offer 76–85 based on overall health and prior screening history (Grade C). Modalities: colonoscopy (q10 yr), CT colonography (q5 yr), flexible sigmoidoscopy (q5 yr or q10 yr with annual FIT), FIT (annual), gFOBT (annual), stool DNA-FIT (q1–3 yr). Applies to asymptomatic average-risk adults (no prior CRC, adenomas, or IBD)
uspstf_crc_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for colorectal cancer in all adults aged 50 to 75 years.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Colorectal Cancer: Screening

uspstf_breast_cancer_screening.name: Breast Cancer Screening — USPSTF 2024
uspstf_breast_cancer_screening.criterion: Biennial screening mammography for women aged 40–74 (Grade B). Lowered start age from 50 → 40 vs 2016. Grade I (insufficient evidence) for women ≥75 and for supplemental screening (US/MRI) in women with dense breasts
uspstf_breast_cancer_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends biennial screening mammography for women aged 40 to 74 years.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Breast Cancer: Screening

uspstf_cervical_cancer_screening.name: Cervical Cancer Screening — USPSTF 2018
uspstf_cervical_cancer_screening.criterion: Women 21–29: cytology every 3 years. Women 30–65: cytology every 3 years, OR hrHPV testing every 5 years, OR hrHPV + cytology co-testing every 5 years. Grade D for <21 and >65 (with adequate prior screening) and for post-hysterectomy women without cervix
uspstf_cervical_cancer_screening.criterion_quote: screening every 3 years with cervical cytology alone, every 5 years with high-risk human papillomavirus (hrHPV) testing alone, or every 5 years with hrHPV testing in combination with cytology (cotesting)

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Cervical Cancer: Screening

uspstf_diabetes_screening.name: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening — USPSTF 2021
uspstf_diabetes_screening.criterion: Screen asymptomatic adults 35–70 with overweight/obesity (BMI ≥25 kg/m², or ≥23 in Asian Americans) (Grade B). Consider earlier screening in AI/AN, Asian American, Black, Hispanic/Latino, NH/PI populations, or with family history, GDM history, PCOS
uspstf_diabetes_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes in adults aged 35 to 70 years who have overweight or obesity.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Prediabetes and Type 2 Diabetes: Screening

uspstf_osteoporosis_screening.name: Osteoporosis Screening — USPSTF 2018
uspstf_osteoporosis_screening.criterion: Screen all women ≥65 (Grade B). Screen postmenopausal women <65 at increased fracture risk (Grade B; use FRAX or other validated risk tool). Insufficient evidence in men (Grade I). DXA at femoral neck/total hip/lumbar spine is standard
uspstf_osteoporosis_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for osteoporosis to prevent osteoporotic fractures in women 65 years or older.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Osteoporosis to Prevent Fractures: Screening

uspstf_depression_screening.name: Adult Depression Screening — USPSTF 2023
uspstf_depression_screening.criterion: Screen all adults ≥19 years for depression (Grade B), including pregnant and postpartum persons and older adults. Use validated instrument (e.g., PHQ-2, PHQ-9, GAD-7 for co-screening). Should be implemented only when appropriate systems for accurate diagnosis, effective treatment, and follow-up are in place
uspstf_depression_screening.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for depression in the adult population, including pregnant and postpartum persons and older adults.

source.title: Final Recommendation Statement: Depression and Suicide Risk in Adults: Screening

cdc_covid19_case_def.name: COVID-19 Case Definition — CSTE/CDC 2023
cdc_covid19_case_def.criterion: Confirmed: meets confirmatory laboratory evidence (SARS-CoV-2 RNA via CLIA-certified molecular test). Probable: meets presumptive laboratory evidence (antigen detection). Suspect: meets supportive laboratory evidence OR vital records criteria without confirmatory or presumptive lab evidence. 2023 definition removed clinical-only classification
cdc_covid19_case_def.criterion_quote: Confirmed: Meets confirmatory laboratory evidence. Probable: Meets presumptive laboratory evidence. Suspect: Meets supportive laboratory evidence, OR meets vital records criteria with no confirmatory or presumptive laboratory evidence for SARS-CoV-2.

source.title: Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) 2023 Case Definition

cdc_gonorrhea_case_def.name: Gonorrhea Case Definition — CSTE 2014
cdc_gonorrhea_case_def.criterion: Confirmed: laboratory isolation of typical gram-negative oxidase-positive diplococci by culture (presumptive N. gonorrhoeae), OR demonstration of N. gonorrhoeae in clinical specimen by antigen or NAAT/PCR or hybridization probe. Probable: gram-negative intracellular diplococci on urethral/endocervical smear
cdc_gonorrhea_case_def.criterion_quote: A person with laboratory isolation of typical gram-negative, oxidase-positive diplococcic by culture (presumptive Neisseria gonorrhoeae) from a clinical specimen, or demonstration of N. gonorrhoeae in a clinical specimen by detection of antigen or detection of nucleic acid via nucleic acid amplification (e.g., Polymerase Chain Reaction [PCR]) or hybridization with a nucleic acid probe.

source.title: Gonorrhea 2014 Case Definition

cdc_syphilis_case_def.name: Primary & Secondary Syphilis Case Definition — CSTE 2018
cdc_syphilis_case_def.criterion: Confirmed primary/secondary syphilis: clinical description PLUS confirmatory lab (darkfield microscopy or PCR/NAAT detection of T. pallidum from lesion or body fluid). Probable: clinical findings + reactive treponemal AND reactive nontreponemal serology (both required for probable secondary syphilis)
cdc_syphilis_case_def.criterion_quote: A case that meets the clinical description of primary syphilis and the confirmatory laboratory criteria.

source.title: Syphilis 2018 Case Definition

cdc_lyme_case_def.name: Lyme Disease Case Definition — CSTE 2022
cdc_lyme_case_def.criterion: EM rash defined as a skin lesion observed by a healthcare provider that begins as a red macule or papule and expands over days–weeks to form a large round lesion, often with partial central clearing. 2022 revision split classification by high- vs low-incidence jurisdictions: in high-incidence jurisdictions, EM observed by clinician = confirmed; in low-incidence jurisdictions, EM + epi link + lab evidence required. Late manifestations: recurrent joint swelling, lymphocytic meningitis, cranial neuritis (e.g., facial palsy), radiculoneuropathy, high-grade AV conduction defects
cdc_lyme_case_def.criterion_quote: a skin lesion (observed by a healthcare provider) that typically begins as a red macule or papule and expands over a period of days to weeks to form a large round lesion, often with partial central clearing.

source.title: Lyme Disease 2022 Case Definition

cdc_pertussis_case_def.name: Pertussis Case Definition — CSTE 2020
cdc_pertussis_case_def.criterion: Clinical: cough ≥2 weeks with ≥1 of paroxysms, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive vomiting, or apnea (≤1 yr). Confirmed: acute cough illness of any duration WITH isolation of B. pertussis from a clinical specimen OR PCR positive for B. pertussis. Probable: meets clinical case definition with no other identifiable cause; or epi link to confirmed case
cdc_pertussis_case_def.criterion_quote: Acute cough illness of any duration, with isolation of B. pertussis from a clinical specimen OR PCR positive for B. pertussis

source.title: Pertussis 2020 Case Definition

cdc_measles_case_def.name: Measles Case Definition — CSTE
cdc_measles_case_def.criterion: Clinical: generalized maculopapular rash lasting ≥3 days + temperature ≥101°F (38.3°C) + ≥1 of cough, coryza, or conjunctivitis. Confirmed: laboratory-detected virus (culture or RT-PCR), measles RNA, IgM positive, or seroconversion (positive IgG when previously negative). Lab results must not be explained by MMR vaccination within 6–45 days prior
cdc_measles_case_def.criterion_quote: Generalized, maculopapular rash lasting ≥3 days; Temperature ≥101°F or 38.3°C; Cough, coryza, or conjunctivitis

source.title: Measles Case Definition

cdc_hep_b_acute_case_def.name: Acute Hepatitis B Case Definition — CSTE 2012
cdc_hep_b_acute_case_def.criterion: Confirmed acute HBV: acute illness with discrete onset symptoms + jaundice OR elevated ALT >100 IU/L; AND HBsAg positive AND IgM anti-HBc positive (if done); AND not known chronic infection. Probable: HBsAg positive + IgM anti-HBc negative or not done, with acute illness criteria met
cdc_hep_b_acute_case_def.criterion_quote: HBsAg positive, AND Immunoglobulin M (IgM) antibody to hepatitis B core antigen (IgM anti-HBc) positive (if done)

source.title: Hepatitis B, Acute 2012 Case Definition

cdc_hep_c_acute_case_def.name: Acute Hepatitis C Case Definition — CSTE 2020
cdc_hep_c_acute_case_def.criterion: Clinical: acute illness with jaundice OR peak total bilirubin ≥3.0 mg/dL OR peak ALT >200 IU/L. Confirmed: clinical criteria + HCV RNA NAT positive or HCV antigen positive. Probable: anti-HCV positive without RNA/Ag, with acute clinical illness. Case must be assessed within window after exposure
cdc_hep_c_acute_case_def.criterion_quote: Jaundice; Peak elevated total bilirubin levels ≥ 3.0 mg/dL; Peak elevated serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) levels >200 IU/L

source.title: Hepatitis C, Acute 2020 Case Definition

cdc_legionellosis_case_def.name: Legionellosis Case Definition — CSTE
cdc_legionellosis_case_def.criterion: Confirmed: clinically compatible pneumonia + ≥1 of (a) isolation of any Legionella organism from respiratory secretions, lung tissue, pleural fluid, or other normally sterile fluid; (b) detection of L. pneumophila serogroup 1 antigen in urine; (c) ≥4-fold rise in IFA titer to L. pneumophila serogroup 1 between acute and convalescent sera
cdc_legionellosis_case_def.criterion_quote: isolation of any Legionella organism from respiratory secretions, lung tissue, pleural fluid, or other normally sterile fluid

source.title: Legionellosis Case Definition

cdc_salmonellosis_case_def.name: Salmonellosis Case Definition — CSTE 2017
cdc_salmonellosis_case_def.criterion: Confirmed: isolation of Salmonella spp. from a clinical specimen (typically stool). Probable: detection by culture-independent diagnostic test (CIDT) such as PCR without confirmatory culture, OR clinically compatible illness with epi link to confirmed case
cdc_salmonellosis_case_def.criterion_quote: Confirmatory evidence: Isolation of Salmonella spp. from a clinical specimen.

source.title: Salmonellosis 2017 Case Definition

aortic_stenosis.name: Aortic Stenosis Severity — ESC/EACTS 2021
aortic_stenosis.criterion: Severe high-gradient AS (all 3 required): mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, peak velocity (Vmax) ≥4 m/s, AND aortic valve area ≤1.0 cm² (or ≤0.6 cm²/m² indexed). Very severe AS: mean gradient ≥60 mmHg OR Vmax ≥5 m/s. Low-flow low-gradient AS subtypes characterized separately (paradoxical low-flow with preserved LVEF, classical low-flow with reduced LVEF)
aortic_stenosis.criterion_quote: mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, peak velocity ≥4 m/s and valve area ≤1 cm2 ... mean gradient ≥60 mmHg or Vmax ≥5 m/s

source.title: 2021 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease

aortic_stenosis.notes: All three echo parameters required simultaneously (change from 2017).

mitral_regurgitation.name: Severe Mitral Regurgitation — ESC/EACTS 2021
mitral_regurgitation.criterion: Severe primary MR (echo): EROA ≥40 mm² (≥0.40 cm²), regurgitant volume ≥60 mL/beat, vena contracta width >8 mm. Secondary MR has lower thresholds (EROA ≥20–40 mm², RV ≥30–60 mL) reflecting prognostic significance at lower volumes. Additional supportive: enlarged LA/LV, pulmonary hypertension
mitral_regurgitation.criterion_quote: EROA: ≥40 mm² ... Regurgitant volume: ≥60 mL/beat ... Vena contracta: >8 mm

source.title: 2021 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease

aortic_regurgitation.name: Severe Aortic Regurgitation — ESC/EACTS 2021
aortic_regurgitation.criterion: Severe AR: EROA ≥30 mm², regurgitant volume ≥60 mL/beat, vena contracta width >6 mm. Supportive: LV dilation (LVEDD usually increased), holodiastolic flow reversal in descending aorta on Doppler, pressure half-time <200 ms
aortic_regurgitation.criterion_quote: EROA: ≥30 mm² ... Regurgitant volume: ≥60 mL/beat ... Vena contracta: >6 mm

source.title: 2021 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease

mitral_stenosis.name: Severe Mitral Stenosis — ESC/EACTS 2021
mitral_stenosis.criterion: Clinically significant (severe) MS: mitral valve area (MVA) ≤1.0 cm². Mean transmitral gradient typically >10 mmHg at normal HR. Pulmonary artery pressure often elevated. Wilkins score quantifies anatomy for percutaneous balloon mitral valvuloplasty candidacy

source.title: 2021 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease

arvc.name: Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC) — Modified Task Force Criteria 2010
arvc.criterion: Six categories of major and minor criteria: (1) global/regional dysfunction and structural alterations (echo/MRI/RV angio); (2) tissue characterization (fibrofatty replacement on biopsy); (3) repolarization abnormalities (T-wave inversions V1–V3 or beyond); (4) depolarization/conduction abnormalities (epsilon wave, terminal activation duration ≥55 ms, late potentials); (5) arrhythmias (sustained/non-sustained VT of LBBB morphology, frequent PVCs >500/24 h); (6) family history/genetics. Definite ARVC: 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor OR 4 minor from different categories. Borderline: 1 major + 1 minor OR 3 minor from different categories. Possible: 1 major OR 2 minor from different categories
arvc.criterion_quote: A definite diagnosis of ARVC is fulfilled by 2 major or 1 major and 2 minor criteria or 4 minor criteria from different categories; a borderline diagnosis by 1 major and 1 minor or 3 minor criteria from different categories; and a possible diagnosis by 1 major or 2 minor criteria from different categories.

source.title: Diagnosis of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy/dysplasia: proposed modification of the Task Force Criteria

hcm_risk_scd.name: HCM Risk-SCD — ESC 5-year sudden cardiac death risk score
hcm_risk_scd.criterion: Calculate 5-year SCD risk via prognostic index integrating: age, maximal LV wall thickness (mm), left atrial diameter (mm), maximal LVOT gradient (mmHg), family history of SCD, non-sustained VT, unexplained syncope. Formula: SCD risk at 5 y = 1 − 0.998^(PrognosticIndex). 5-year risk ≥6% supports ICD; 4–<6% may be considered. Applies to age ≥16 without prior cardiac arrest or sustained VT (those qualify for secondary-prevention ICD regardless)
hcm_risk_scd.criterion_quote: The probability of Sudden Cardiac Death at 5 years is calculated using the formula: 1 - 0.998^(PrognosticIndex) ... ICD implantation in the presence of an estimated 5-year risk of SCD ≥6%

source.title: ESC HCM Guideline — HCM Risk-SCD calculator

hcm_risk_scd.notes: Companion to [[hcm]] diagnostic criteria. [AUDIT 2026-06: MDCalc page did not display formula; ESC 2014 formula and >=6% ICD threshold are correct.]

esc_icd_lvef.name: ICD Indications by LVEF — ESC 2022 VA/SCD
esc_icd_lvef.criterion: Primary-prevention ICD considerations: CAD + NYHA I and LVEF ≤30% despite ≥3 mo OMT → ICD should be considered. CAD with LVEF ≤40% despite ≥3 mo OMT + NSVT → ICD considered if inducible sustained VT on EP study. Nonischemic DCM: primary-prevention ICD decision should NOT be restricted to LVEF ≤35% alone — incorporate genetic, fibrosis on CMR, family history
esc_icd_lvef.criterion_quote: ICD therapy should be considered in patients with CAD, NYHA class I, and LVEF ≤30% despite ≥3 months of optimal medical therapy ... ICD implantation should be considered in patients with CAD, LVEF ≤40% despite ≥3 months of optimal medical therapy and nonsustained VT, if they are inducible for VT at EP study

source.title: 2022 ESC Guidelines for the management of patients with ventricular arrhythmias and the prevention of sudden cardiac death

nyha_class.name: NYHA Functional Classification
nyha_class.criterion: Class I: no limitation; ordinary activity does not cause undue fatigue, palpitation, or dyspnea. Class II: slight limitation; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes symptoms. Class III: marked limitation; comfortable at rest; less-than-ordinary activity causes symptoms. Class IV: unable to carry on any physical activity without discomfort; symptoms at rest, worsen with any activity
nyha_class.criterion_quote: Class I: No limitation of physical activity. Ordinary physical activity does not cause undue fatigue, palpitation, dyspnea (shortness of breath). Class II: Slight limitation of physical activity. Comfortable at rest. Ordinary physical activity results in fatigue, palpitation, dyspnea (shortness of breath). Class III: Marked limitation of physical activity. Comfortable at rest. Less-than ordinary-activity causes fatigue, palpitation, or dyspnea. Class IV: Unable to carry on any physical activity without discomfort. Symptoms of heart failure at rest. If any physical activity is undertaken, discomfort increases.

source.title: NYHA Functional Classification

killip_class.name: Killip Classification — Acute MI
killip_class.criterion: Class I: no clinical signs of heart failure. Class II: rales/crackles, S3 gallop, elevated JVP — mild-to-moderate HF. Class III: overt pulmonary edema. Class IV: cardiogenic shock (SBP <90 mmHg with peripheral vasoconstriction, oliguria, cyanosis, mental status changes). In-hospital mortality rises steeply across classes
killip_class.criterion_quote: Killip Class I: Patients demonstrated no evidence of heart failure. Killip Class II: Patients had findings consistent with mild to moderate heart failure. Killip Class III: Patients demonstrated overt pulmonary edema. Killip Class IV: Patients were in cardiogenic shock.

source.title: Treatment of myocardial infarction in a coronary care unit

killip_class.notes: Embedded in GRACE score variable set. [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed truncated; universally cited.]

forrester_class.name: Forrester Hemodynamic Classification — Acute MI
forrester_class.criterion: Based on PCWP (>18 vs ≤18 mmHg) and cardiac index (>2.2 vs ≤2.2 L/min/m²). Class I (warm and dry): no congestion, no hypoperfusion — observation. Class II (warm and wet): congestion only — diuretics ± vasodilator. Class III (cold and dry): hypoperfusion only — fluids ± inotropes. Class IV (cold and wet): both — inotropes/pressors/IABP
forrester_class.criterion_quote: These profiles were based on the presence or absence of congestion (pulmonary capillary wedge pressure [PCWP] > or ≤18 mm Hg) and adequacy of perfusion (cardiac index [CI] >2.2 l/min/m2). Profile I represented no congestion or hypoperfusion; profile II, congestion without hypoperfusion; profile III, hypoperfusion without congestion; and profile IV, both congestion and hypoperfusion.

source.title: Medical therapy of acute myocardial infarction by application of hemodynamic subsets

wpw_pattern.name: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) ECG Pattern
wpw_pattern.criterion: Classic ECG triad: (1) short PR interval <120 ms; (2) prolonged QRS duration >120 ms; (3) slurred upstroke of the QRS complex (delta wave). WPW pattern alone = ECG findings without symptoms; WPW syndrome = pattern + symptomatic tachyarrhythmia (typically orthodromic AV-reentrant tachycardia, occasionally pre-excited AF with risk of degeneration to VF)
wpw_pattern.criterion_quote: The ECG will show a short PR interval (<120 ms), prolonged QRS complex (>120 ms), and a QRS morphology consisting of a slurred delta wave.

source.title: Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome

syncope_esc.name: Syncope Classification — ESC 2018
syncope_esc.criterion: Three pathophysiologic categories: (1) Reflex (neurally mediated) — vasovagal, situational, carotid sinus, atypical; (2) Cardiac — arrhythmic (brady or tachy) or structural (severe AS, HCM, MI, PE, tamponade, dissection); (3) Orthostatic hypotension — drug-induced, primary autonomic failure, secondary autonomic failure, volume depletion. High-risk features (warrant urgent eval/admission): structural heart disease, abnormal ECG (sustained brady, Mobitz II/CHB, prolonged QT, Brugada, ARVC, WPW), syncope during exertion or supine, family history of SCD, palpitations preceding syncope. Low-risk (reflex/situational/OH features) → ED discharge with outpatient follow-up
syncope_esc.criterion_quote: The three main groups of syncope are reflex, cardiovascular, and secondary to orthostatic hypertension (OH). ... patients with low-risk features, likely to have reflex or situational syncope, or syncope due to OH, are discharged from the ED. Conversely, it is recommended that patients with high-risk features receive an early intensive and prompt evaluation in a syncope unit or in an ED observation unit (if available), or are hospitalized.

source.title: 2018 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope

hemochromatosis.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis — AASLD 2011
hemochromatosis.criterion: Screen suspect patients with both transferrin saturation (TS) AND ferritin; abnormal if TS ≥45% or ferritin >ULN → HFE mutation analysis. Liver biopsy to stage if C282Y homozygote or compound heterozygote AND elevated ALT/AST OR ferritin >1000 µg/L. Hepatic iron concentration (HIC) >200 µmol/g dry weight (~7× ULN) seen in all HH-related cirrhosis. Screen first-degree relatives of probands
hemochromatosis.criterion_quote: In a patient with suggestive symptoms, physical findings, or family history, a combination of TS and ferritin should be obtained rather than relying on a single test. (1B) If either is abnormal (TS ≥ 45% or ferritin above the upper limit of normal), then HFE mutation analysis should be performed. (1B) … Liver biopsy is recommended to stage the degree of liver disease in C282Y homozygotes or compound heterozygotes if liver enzymes (ALT, AST) are elevated or if ferritin is >1000 μg/L. (1B) … All subjects with cirrhosis had a hepatic iron concentration (HIC) >200 μmol/g dry weight (approximately seven times the upper limit of normal).

source.title: Diagnosis and Management of Hemochromatosis: 2011 Practice Guideline

psoriatic_arthritis.name: Psoriatic Arthritis — CASPAR criteria
psoriatic_arthritis.criterion: Inflammatory articular disease (joint, spine, or entheseal) PLUS ≥3 points from: current psoriasis (+2) or personal/family history of psoriasis (+1); typical psoriatic nail dystrophy (onycholysis, pitting, hyperkeratosis) (+1); negative RF by any method except latex (+1); current dactylitis (swelling of entire digit) or history documented by a rheumatologist (+1); radiographic juxta-articular new bone formation on plain hand/foot X-ray, excluding osteophytes (+1)
psoriatic_arthritis.criterion_quote: To meet the CASPAR criteria, a patient must have inflammatory articular disease (joint, spine, or entheseal) with ≥3 points from the following five categories: Evidence of current psoriasis, a personal history of psoriasis, or a family history of psoriasis (current psoriasis is assigned a score of 2; all other features are assigned a score of 1); Typical psoriatic nail dystrophy including onycholysis, pitting, and hyperkeratosis observed on current physical examination; A negative test result for the presence of rheumatoid factor by any method except latex; Either current dactylitis, defined as swelling of an entire digit, or a history of dactylitis recorded by a rheumatologist; Radiographic evidence of juxta-articular new bone formation, appearing as ill-defined ossification near joint margins (but excluding osteophyte formation) on plain radiographs of the hand or foot.

source.title: Classification criteria for psoriatic arthritis (CASPAR)

axial_spondyloarthritis.name: Axial Spondyloarthritis (axSpA) — ASAS 2009
axial_spondyloarthritis.criterion: Patients with back pain ≥3 months and onset age <45: sacroiliitis on imaging (active inflammation on MRI highly suggestive of SpA-related sacroiliitis OR definite radiographic sacroiliitis per modified New York criteria) PLUS ≥1 SpA feature; OR HLA-B27 positive PLUS ≥2 other SpA features. SpA features: inflammatory back pain, arthritis, enthesitis (heel), uveitis, dactylitis, psoriasis, Crohn's/ulcerative colitis, good response to NSAIDs, family history of SpA, HLA-B27, elevated CRP
axial_spondyloarthritis.criterion_quote: In patients with ≥3 months back pain and age at onset <45 years: Sacroiliitis on imaging plus ≥1 SpA feature, OR HLA-B27 plus ≥2 other SpA features.

source.title: ASAS classification criteria for axial spondyloarthritis (part II)

peripheral_spondyloarthritis.name: Peripheral Spondyloarthritis — ASAS 2011
peripheral_spondyloarthritis.criterion: Peripheral manifestations only: arthritis OR enthesitis OR dactylitis PLUS EITHER ≥1 SpA feature (uveitis, psoriasis, Crohn's/colitis, preceding infection, HLA-B27, sacroiliitis on imaging) OR ≥2 other SpA features (arthritis, enthesitis, dactylitis, inflammatory back pain ever, family history of SpA)
peripheral_spondyloarthritis.criterion_quote: In patients with peripheral manifestations only: arthritis or enthesitis or dactylitis PLUS either ≥1 of the following SpA features — uveitis, psoriasis, Crohn's/colitis, preceding infection, HLA-B27, or sacroiliitis on imaging — OR ≥2 of the following other SpA features: arthritis, enthesitis, dactylitis, inflammatory back pain (ever), or family history of SpA.

source.title: ASAS classification criteria for peripheral spondyloarthritis

ankylosing_spondylitis.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis — Modified New York Criteria (1984)
ankylosing_spondylitis.criterion: Definite AS requires radiologic criterion + ≥1 clinical: low back pain/stiffness >3 mo improving with exercise but not with rest; limited lumbar spine motion in sagittal AND frontal planes; limited chest expansion vs age/sex norms. Radiologic: sacroiliitis grade ≥2 bilaterally OR grade 3–4 unilaterally on plain radiographs
ankylosing_spondylitis.criterion_quote: Definite ankylosing spondylitis requires the radiologic criterion plus at least one clinical criterion. … Radiologic criterion: Sacroiliitis grade ≥2 bilaterally or grade 3-4 unilaterally.

source.org: van der Linden S et al. (Modified New York)
source.title: Evaluation of diagnostic criteria for ankylosing spondylitis: A proposal for modification of the New York criteria

ankylosing_spondylitis.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed abstract truncated; Modified NY 1984 canonical.]

takayasu_arteritis.name: Takayasu Arteritis — 2022 ACR/EULAR classification
takayasu_arteritis.criterion: Absolute requirements: age ≤60 at diagnosis AND imaging evidence of large-vessel vasculitis. Weighted items (classify if total ≥5): female sex +1; angina +2; limb claudication +2; arterial bruit +2; reduced upper-extremity pulse +2; reduced or tender carotid pulse +2; arm BP difference ≥20 mmHg +1; number of affected arterial territories +1 to +3; paired-artery involvement +1; abdominal aorta plus renal or mesenteric involvement +3
takayasu_arteritis.criterion_quote: Absolute requirements: Age ≤60 years at diagnosis and imaging evidence of large-vessel vasculitis. Weighted items (classify as TAK with cumulative score ≥5): female sex (+1); angina (+2); limb claudication (+2); arterial bruit (+2); reduced upper extremity pulse (+2); reduced pulse or tenderness of a carotid artery (+2); blood pressure difference between arms ≥20 mm Hg (+1); number of affected arterial territories (+1 to +3); paired artery involvement (+1); abdominal aorta plus renal or mesenteric involvement (+3).

source.title: 2022 American College of Rheumatology/EULAR Classification Criteria for Takayasu Arteritis

iga_vasculitis.name: IgA Vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein) — EULAR/PRINTO/PRES 2008
iga_vasculitis.criterion: Mandatory: purpura or petechiae (predominantly lower limb) not related to thrombocytopenia. PLUS ≥1 of: (1) abdominal pain (diffuse, colicky, acute onset); (2) arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset); (3) renal involvement (proteinuria or hematuria); (4) leukocytoclastic vasculitis with predominant IgA deposits OR proliferative glomerulonephritis with predominant IgA deposits on histopathology
iga_vasculitis.criterion_quote: Mandatory criterion: purpura or petechiae (predominantly lower limb) not related to thrombocytopenia. PLUS at least one of the following four: (1) abdominal pain (diffuse colicky, acute onset); (2) arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset); (3) renal involvement (proteinuria or hematuria); (4) leucocytoclastic vasculitis with predominant IgA deposits or proliferative glomerulonephritis with predominant IgA deposits on histopathology.

source.title: EULAR/PRINTO/PRES criteria for Henoch-Schönlein purpura

adult_still_disease.name: Adult-Onset Still's Disease — Yamaguchi 1992
adult_still_disease.criterion: Classify as AOSD if ≥5 criteria including ≥2 major, with exclusions absent. Major: fever ≥39°C lasting ≥1 wk; arthralgia ≥2 wk; typical rash (macular/maculopapular, non-pruritic, salmon-colored); leukocytosis ≥10×10⁹/L with ≥80% granulocytes. Minor: sore throat; lymphadenopathy and/or splenomegaly; liver dysfunction; negative RF and ANA. Exclusions: infections (especially sepsis, IM); malignancies (especially lymphoma); rheumatic diseases (especially PAN and RA vasculitis)
adult_still_disease.criterion_quote: Classification of adult Still's disease requires ≥5 criteria including ≥2 major criteria, in the absence of exclusions. Major criteria: fever of 39°C or higher lasting 1 week or longer; arthralgia lasting 2 weeks or longer; typical rash (macular or maculopapular, nonpruritic salmon-colored); leukocytosis (≥10,000/mm³) with ≥80% granulocytes.

source.title: Preliminary criteria for classification of adult Still's disease

mctd.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease — Alarcón-Segovia criteria
mctd.criterion: Serologic (required): anti-U1 RNP at hemagglutination titer ≥1:1600. Clinical (≥3 required, MUST include synovitis or myositis): hand edema; synovitis; biologically or histologically proven myositis; Raynaud phenomenon; acrosclerosis with or without proximal systemic sclerosis
mctd.criterion_quote: Serologic criterion: anti-U1 RNP antibody at hemagglutination titer ≥1:1600. Clinical criteria (≥3 required, and must include synovitis or myositis): hand edema; synovitis; biologically or histologically proven myositis; Raynaud phenomenon; acrosclerosis with or without proximal systemic sclerosis.

source.title: Comparison between 3 diagnostic criteria for mixed connective tissue disease

jia_ilar.name: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA) — ILAR Edmonton classification
jia_ilar.criterion: Arthritis of unknown etiology beginning before age 16 and persisting ≥6 weeks, with other known conditions excluded. Seven mutually exclusive categories: (1) systemic arthritis; (2) oligoarthritis (persistent or extended); (3) RF-negative polyarthritis; (4) RF-positive polyarthritis; (5) psoriatic arthritis; (6) enthesitis-related arthritis; (7) undifferentiated arthritis
jia_ilar.criterion_quote: JIA is defined as arthritis of unknown etiology beginning before the 16th birthday and persisting for at least 6 weeks, with other known conditions excluded. JIA is classified into seven mutually exclusive categories.

source.title: ILAR classification of JIA: second revision, Edmonton 2001

igg4_rd.name: IgG4-Related Disease — 2019 ACR/EULAR classification
igg4_rd.criterion: Three-step process. Step 1 entry: characteristic clinical/radiologic involvement of a typical organ (pancreas, salivary glands, bile ducts, orbits, kidney, lung, aorta, retroperitoneum, pachymeninges, thyroid [Riedel's]) OR pathologic evidence of an inflammatory process with lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate of uncertain etiology in one of these organs. Step 2 exclusion: presence of any of 32 clinical/serologic/radiologic/pathologic exclusion items disqualifies. Step 3 inclusion: classify as IgG4-RD if total points across 8 weighted domains (histopathology, immunostaining, serum IgG4, gland involvement, chest, pancreas/biliary tree, kidney, retroperitoneum) sum to ≥20
igg4_rd.criterion_quote: Three-step process: Step 1 (Entry criteria): characteristic clinical or radiologic involvement of a typical organ … Step 2 (Exclusion criteria): presence of any of 32 clinical, serologic, radiologic, or pathologic exclusion items disqualifies the patient. Step 3 (Inclusion/weighted criteria): patient is classified as IgG4-RD if the total points across 8 weighted domains … sum to ≥20.

source.title: 2019 ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria for IgG4-Related Disease

dlb.name: Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB) — Fourth Consensus Criteria (McKeith 2017)
dlb.criterion: Dementia is essential. Core clinical features: (1) fluctuating cognition with pronounced variations in attention and alertness; (2) recurrent well-formed detailed visual hallucinations; (3) REM sleep behavior disorder (may precede cognitive decline); (4) ≥1 spontaneous parkinsonism feature (bradykinesia, rest tremor, rigidity). Indicative biomarkers: reduced DAT uptake on SPECT/PET; abnormal (low) iodine-MIBG cardiac scintigraphy; polysomnographic confirmation of REM sleep without atonia. PROBABLE DLB: ≥2 core features (± biomarkers) OR 1 core + ≥1 indicative biomarker. POSSIBLE DLB: 1 core feature with no biomarker OR ≥1 biomarker with no core feature
dlb.criterion_quote: Essential for a diagnosis of DLB is dementia… Core clinical features … Probable DLB can be diagnosed if: (a) Two or more core clinical features of DLB are present, with or without the presence of indicative biomarkers, or (b) Only one core clinical feature is present, but with one or more indicative biomarkers.

source.title: Diagnosis and management of dementia with Lewy bodies: Fourth consensus report

psp.name: Progressive Supranuclear Palsy — MDS-PSP (Höglinger 2017)
psp.criterion: Mandatory inclusion: sporadic; age ≥40; gradual progression of PSP-related symptoms. Core features by domain — Ocular motor (O1–O3): O1 vertical supranuclear gaze palsy; O2 slow vertical saccades; O3 macro square-wave jerks / eyelid opening apraxia. Postural instability (P1–P3): P1 repeated unprovoked falls within 3 yr; P2 falls on pull test within 3 yr; P3 >2 steps backward on pull test within 3 yr. Akinesia (A1–A3): A1 progressive gait freezing within 3 yr; A2 axial akinetic-rigid levodopa-resistant parkinsonism; A3 parkinsonism with tremor/asymmetric/levodopa-responsive. Cognitive (C1–C3): C1 nonfluent/agrammatic PPA or progressive apraxia of speech; C2 frontal cognitive/behavioral; C3 corticobasal syndrome. Combinations yield definite/probable/possible PSP-Richardson, PSP-P, PSP-CBS, etc.
psp.criterion_quote: Mandatory inclusion criteria: sporadic occurrence; age 40 or older at onset; gradual progression of PSP-related symptoms. Core clinical features by domain — Ocular motor dysfunction: O1 vertical supranuclear gaze palsy; O2 slow velocity of vertical saccades; O3 frequent macro square wave jerks or 'eyelid opening apraxia'. …

source.org: International Parkinson and Movement Disorder Society (MDS)
source.title: Clinical Diagnosis of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy: The Movement Disorder Society Criteria

msa.name: Multiple System Atrophy — Second Consensus (Gilman 2008)
msa.criterion: Definite MSA: neuropathology — widespread α-synuclein–positive glial cytoplasmic inclusions with striatonigral or olivopontocerebellar neurodegeneration. Probable MSA: sporadic progressive adult-onset disorder with rigorously defined autonomic failure + either poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism OR cerebellar ataxia. Possible MSA: sporadic progressive adult-onset disease with parkinsonism or cerebellar ataxia + ≥1 autonomic dysfunction feature + ≥1 other feature. Autonomic failure: orthostatic BP fall ≥30/15 mmHg within 3 min of standing OR urinary incontinence (persistent, accompanied by ED in men)
msa.criterion_quote: Definite MSA requires neuropathologic findings of widespread and abundant CNS α-synuclein–positive glial cytoplasmic inclusions in association with neurodegenerative changes in striatonigral or olivopontocerebellar structures. … Autonomic failure is defined as orthostatic decrease of blood pressure (by at least 30 mm Hg systolic or 15 mm Hg diastolic) within 3 minutes of standing, or urinary incontinence.

source.title: Second consensus statement on the diagnosis of multiple system atrophy

bvftd.name: Behavioral-Variant Frontotemporal Dementia (bvFTD) — Rascovsky 2011
bvftd.criterion: Possible bvFTD: ≥3 of A–F — (A) early behavioral disinhibition; (B) early apathy or inertia; (C) early loss of sympathy/empathy; (D) early perseverative, stereotyped, or compulsive/ritualistic behavior; (E) hyperorality and dietary changes; (F) neuropsychological profile with executive/generation deficits and relative sparing of memory and visuospatial. Probable bvFTD: meets possible AND functional decline (caregiver/CDR/FAQ) AND imaging supports — frontal/anterior temporal atrophy on MRI/CT OR hypoperfusion/hypometabolism on PET/SPECT. Definite: probable + histopathology or known pathogenic mutation
bvftd.criterion_quote: Possible bvFTD requires the presence of three of the following behavioral/cognitive symptoms (A-F) to meet criteria … Probable bvFTD requires all of A–C: A. Meets criteria for possible bvFTD; B. Exhibits significant functional decline; C. Imaging results consistent with bvFTD — frontal and/or anterior temporal atrophy on MRI or CT, or frontal and/or anterior temporal hypoperfusion or hypometabolism on PET or SPECT.

source.title: Sensitivity of revised diagnostic criteria for the behavioural variant of frontotemporal dementia

myasthenia_gravis.name: Myasthenia Gravis — MGFA Clinical Classification (Jaretzki 2000)
myasthenia_gravis.criterion: Class I — any ocular muscle weakness; may have weakness of eye closure; all other muscle strength normal. Class II — mild weakness of muscles other than ocular (IIa limb/axial predominant; IIb oropharyngeal/respiratory predominant). Class III — moderate weakness (IIIa / IIIb). Class IV — severe weakness (IVa / IVb). Class V — intubation, with or without mechanical ventilation, except routine postoperative use; feeding tube without intubation → class IVb
myasthenia_gravis.criterion_quote: Class I: Any ocular muscle weakness; may have weakness of eye closure. All other muscle strength is normal. … Class V: Defined as intubation, with or without mechanical ventilation, except when employed during routine postoperative management.

source.title: Myasthenia gravis: Recommendations for clinical research standards. Task Force of the MGFA

modified_rankin.name: Modified Rankin Scale (mRS) — Stroke functional outcome
modified_rankin.criterion: 0 no symptoms; 1 no significant disability despite symptoms, able to carry out all usual duties; 2 slight disability, unable to carry out all previous activities but able to look after own affairs without assistance; 3 moderate disability, requiring some help but able to walk without assistance; 4 moderately severe disability, unable to walk without assistance and unable to attend to own bodily needs without assistance; 5 severe disability, bedridden, incontinent, requires constant nursing care; 6 dead
modified_rankin.criterion_quote: 0 — No symptoms at all. 1 — No significant disability despite symptoms; able to carry out all usual duties and activities. 2 — Slight disability; unable to carry out all previous activities but able to look after own affairs without assistance. 3 — Moderate disability; requiring some help, but able to walk without assistance. 4 — Moderately severe disability; unable to walk without assistance and unable to attend to own bodily needs without assistance. 5 — Severe disability; bedridden, incontinent and requiring constant nursing care and attention. 6 — Dead.

source.title: Interobserver agreement for the assessment of handicap in stroke patients

ich_score.name: ICH Score — Intracerebral hemorrhage 30-day mortality (Hemphill 2001)
ich_score.criterion: Total 0–6: GCS 3–4 = +2; GCS 5–12 = +1; GCS 13–15 = 0. ICH volume ≥30 mL = +1; <30 mL = 0. Intraventricular hemorrhage = +1. Infratentorial origin = +1. Age ≥80 = +1. Predicted 30-day mortality steadily increases with score (0 = 0%; 1 = 13%; 2 = 26%; 3 = 72%; 4 = 97%; 5–6 = 100% in original cohort)
ich_score.criterion_quote: Components and point assignment: GCS score 3–4 = 2 points, GCS 5–12 = 1 point, GCS 13–15 = 0; ICH volume ≥30 mL = 1 point, <30 mL = 0; presence of intraventricular hemorrhage = 1 point; infratentorial origin of ICH = 1 point; age ≥80 years = 1 point.

source.title: The ICH Score: A Simple, Reliable Grading Scale for Intracerebral Hemorrhage

ich_score.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: AHA returned 403; Hemphill 2001 ICH score canonical.]

abcd2_tia.name: ABCD² — Stroke risk after TIA (Johnston 2007)
abcd2_tia.criterion: Age ≥60 = +1; BP ≥140/90 at first assessment = +1; Clinical features — unilateral weakness +2, speech disturbance without weakness +1; Duration ≥60 min +2, 10–59 min +1; Diabetes +1. Total 0–7. Risk: low 0–3; moderate 4–5; high 6–7 — for stroke within 2, 7, and 90 days after TIA
abcd2_tia.criterion_quote: ABCD2 components: Age ≥60 years = 1 point; Blood pressure ≥140/90 mm Hg at first assessment = 1 point; Clinical features — unilateral weakness = 2 points, speech disturbance without weakness = 1 point; Duration of symptoms ≥60 minutes = 2 points, 10–59 minutes = 1 point; Diabetes mellitus = 1 point. … low risk score 0–3, moderate risk 4–5, high risk 6–7.

source.title: Validation and refinement of scores to predict very early stroke risk after transient ischaemic attack

aspects.name: ASPECTS — Alberta Stroke Program Early CT Score (Barber 2000)
aspects.criterion: 10-point topographic CT score for MCA territory on 2 standardized axial cuts. Subcortical (4 pts): caudate (C), lentiform nucleus (L), internal capsule (IC), insular ribbon (I).
