MCA cortex (6 pts): M1 anterior MCA cortex; M2 cortex lateral to insular ribbon; M3 posterior MCA cortex; M4/M5/M6 anterior/lateral/posterior MCA territories superior to M1/M2/M3. Subtract 1 from 10 for every region with early ischemic change. Normal CT = 10; ASPECTS ≤7 associated with worse outcome after thrombolysis
aspects.criterion_quote: ASPECTS is a 10-point quantitative topographic CT scan score dividing the middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory into 10 regions of interest assessed on two standardized axial CT cuts. … One point is subtracted from 10 for every region showing early ischemic change.

source.title: Validity and reliability of a quantitative computed tomography score in predicting outcome of hyperacute stroke before thrombolytic therapy

ilae_seizure_classification.name: Seizure Type Classification — ILAE 2017 (Fisher)
ilae_seizure_classification.criterion: Onset classified as focal (originates within networks limited to one hemisphere), generalized (originates within and rapidly engages bilaterally distributed networks), or unknown onset. Focal seizures characterized by awareness: focal aware vs focal impaired awareness. Motor vs non-motor onset further subdivided (e.g., tonic-clonic, atonic, myoclonic; behavior arrest, sensory, cognitive). Onset designation uses 80% confidence level threshold to decide focal vs generalized
ilae_seizure_classification.criterion_quote: Focal-onset seizures originate within networks limited to one hemisphere. … Generalized-onset seizures originate at some point within, and rapidly engaging, bilaterally distributed networks. … Onset classification uses an 80% confidence level to decide whether seizure onset is focal or generalized.

source.title: Operational classification of seizure types by the International League Against Epilepsy

alzheimer_atn.name: Alzheimer's Disease — NIA-AA 2018 Research Framework AT(N) biomarkers
alzheimer_atn.criterion: Biological definition of AD via 3 biomarker categories, each rated +/−. A (amyloid-β): amyloid PET OR CSF Aβ42 (or Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio). T (tau): CSF phosphorylated tau OR tau PET. (N) (neurodegeneration/neuronal injury): FDG-PET hypometabolism, structural MRI atrophy, or CSF total tau. AD = A+T+ (any N). Alzheimer's pathologic change = A+T−. Non-AD pathologic change = A−T+ or A−T−(N)+. Replaces clinical-only AD definition for research; clinical 2011 NIA-AA criteria still used in practice
alzheimer_atn.criterion_quote: AT(N) biomarker classification: 'A' refers to the value of an amyloid-β biomarker (amyloid PET or CSF Aβ42, or Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio); 'T' refers to the value of a tau biomarker (CSF phosphorylated tau, or tau PET); '(N)' refers to biomarkers of neurodegeneration or neuronal injury (FDG-PET hypometabolism, structural MRI atrophy, or CSF total tau). Each biomarker category is rated as positive (+) or negative (−). In this research framework, Alzheimer's disease is defined by its underlying pathologic processes that can be documented by post-mortem examination or in vivo by biomarkers.

source.title: NIA-AA Research Framework: Toward a biological definition of Alzheimer's disease

mci_petersen.name: Mild Cognitive Impairment — Petersen criteria
mci_petersen.criterion: Amnestic MCI: (1) subjective memory complaint, preferably corroborated by an informant; (2) objective memory impairment for age; (3) preserved general cognitive function; (4) intact activities of daily living; (5) absence of dementia. Non-amnestic MCI: same framework but objective impairment in non-memory domain (executive function, language, visuospatial). Single vs multiple domain subtypes
mci_petersen.criterion_quote: Criteria for amnestic MCI: (1) a subjective memory complaint, preferably corroborated by an informant; (2) objective memory impairment for age; (3) preserved general cognitive function; (4) intact activities of daily living; and (5) absence of dementia.

source.title: Mild cognitive impairment: clinical characterization and outcome

rls.name: Restless Legs Syndrome — IRLSSG 2014 (Willis-Ekbom disease)
rls.criterion: All 5 essential criteria required: (1) urge to move the legs, usually but not always with uncomfortable/unpleasant leg sensations; (2) urge and sensations begin or worsen during rest or inactivity; (3) urge and sensations are partially or totally relieved by movement (walking, stretching) at least as long as activity continues; (4) urge and sensations only occur or are worse in the evening/night than during the day; (5) not solely accounted for by another condition (myalgia, venous stasis, leg edema, arthritis, leg cramps, positional discomfort, habitual foot tapping)
rls.criterion_quote: All five essential criteria must be met: (1) An urge to move the legs … (2) … begin or worsen during periods of rest or inactivity … (3) … partially or totally relieved by movement … (4) … only occur or are worse in the evening or night … (5) The occurrence of the above features are not solely accounted for as symptoms primary to another medical or a behavioral condition.

source.title: Restless legs syndrome/Willis-Ekbom disease diagnostic criteria: updated IRLSSG consensus criteria

menieres_disease.name: Ménière's Disease — Bárány Society / AAO-HNS 2015
menieres_disease.criterion: Definite Ménière's: (A) ≥2 spontaneous episodes of vertigo, each 20 min–12 h; (B) audiometrically documented low- to mid-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear on ≥1 occasion before, during, or after a vertigo episode; (C) fluctuating aural symptoms (hearing, tinnitus, fullness) in the affected ear; (D) not better accounted for by another vestibular diagnosis. Probable Ménière's: (A) ≥2 episodes of vertigo or dizziness, each 20 min–24 h; (B) fluctuating aural symptoms; (C) not better explained otherwise
menieres_disease.criterion_quote: Definite Ménière's disease: (A) Two or more spontaneous episodes of vertigo, each lasting 20 minutes to 12 hours; (B) Audiometrically documented low- to medium-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear on at least one occasion before, during, or after one of the episodes of vertigo; (C) Fluctuating aural symptoms (hearing, tinnitus, or fullness) in the affected ear; (D) Not better accounted for by another vestibular diagnosis.

source.title: Diagnostic criteria for Menière's disease

bppv_dix_hallpike.name: Posterior Canal BPPV — Dix-Hallpike (AAO-HNS CPG)
bppv_dix_hallpike.criterion: In Dix-Hallpike position, patient reports vertigo accompanied by characteristic mixed torsional + vertical nystagmus: upper pole of eye beats toward the dependent (undermost) ear with upbeating vertical component. Features: latency 2–5 s between maneuver completion and nystagmus onset; duration <1 min; reverses direction on returning to upright; fatigues with repeated testing
bppv_dix_hallpike.criterion_quote: The diagnosis of posterior semicircular canal BPPV is made when, in the Dix-Hallpike test position, the patient reports vertigo accompanied by characteristic mixed torsional and vertical nystagmus: the upper pole of the eye beats toward the dependent (undermost) ear with an upbeating vertical component. Features include: a brief latency period of typically 2 to 5 seconds between completion of the maneuver and onset of nystagmus; nystagmus duration less than 1 minute; reversal of direction when the patient returns upright; and fatigability with repeated positional testing.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (Update)

schizophrenia.name: Schizophrenia — DSM-5-TR
schizophrenia.criterion: Two or more of the following, each present for a significant portion of time during 1 month (less if treated). At least one must be (1), (2), or (3): (1) delusions; (2) hallucinations; (3) disorganized speech; (4) grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior; (5) negative symptoms (diminished emotional expression or avolition). Continuous signs of disturbance persist for ≥6 months with ≥1 month of active-phase symptoms. Significant impairment in social/occupational functioning. Schizoaffective and mood disorders with psychotic features ruled out. Not attributable to substance or medical condition
schizophrenia.criterion_quote: Two (or more) of the following, each present for a significant portion of time during 1 month (or less if successfully treated). At least one of these must be (1), (2), or (3): (1) Delusions. (2) Hallucinations. (3) Disorganized speech. (4) Grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior. (5) Negative symptoms. Continuous signs of the disturbance persist for at least 6 months, including at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms (Criterion A).

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

bipolar_i.name: Bipolar I Disorder — Manic Episode (DSM-5/5-TR)
bipolar_i.criterion: Distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood AND abnormally and persistently increased goal-directed activity or energy, lasting ≥1 wk (any duration if hospitalization is necessary), most of the day, nearly every day. During the mood disturbance: ≥3 (or ≥4 if mood is only irritable) of — inflated self-esteem/grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, more talkative/pressured speech, flight of ideas/racing thoughts, distractibility, increase in goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation, excessive involvement in activities with high painful-consequence potential (spending, sex, foolish investments). Symptoms cause marked impairment or require hospitalization or have psychotic features
bipolar_i.criterion_quote: Criterion A: A distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and abnormally and persistently increased goal-directed activity or energy, lasting at least 1 week and present most of the day, nearly every day (or any duration if hospitalization is necessary). Criterion B: During the period of mood disturbance and increased energy or activity, three (or more) of the following symptoms (four if the mood is only irritable) are present to a significant degree: inflated self-esteem or grandiosity; decreased need for sleep; more talkative than usual or pressure to keep talking; flight of ideas or racing thoughts; distractibility; increase in goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation; excessive involvement in activities that have a high potential for painful consequences.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

bipolar_i.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: DSM-5-TR text correct but StatPearls only paraphrases.]

bipolar_ii.name: Bipolar II Disorder — Hypomanic Episode (DSM-5/5-TR)
bipolar_ii.criterion: Distinct period of persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood AND persistently increased activity or energy, lasting ≥4 consecutive days, most of the day, nearly every day. Same 7 B symptoms as mania (≥3, or ≥4 if irritable). Unequivocal change in functioning observable by others; not severe enough to cause marked impairment or to require hospitalization; no psychotic features. Bipolar II requires ≥1 hypomanic episode + ≥1 MDE + NO history of manic episode
bipolar_ii.criterion_quote: A distinct period of persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and persistently increased activity or energy, lasting at least 4 consecutive days and present most of the day, nearly every day. … Bipolar II requires at least one hypomanic episode and at least one major depressive episode, with no history of a manic episode.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

ptsd.name: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) — DSM-5
ptsd.criterion: Criterion A — exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence (directly, witnessed in person, learned of in close family member/friend [violent or accidental], or repeated/extreme exposure to aversive details). B — intrusion (≥1). C — persistent avoidance (≥1). D — negative alterations in cognitions and mood (≥2). E — marked alterations in arousal and reactivity (≥2). F — duration of B–E >1 month. G — clinically significant distress or impairment. H — not attributable to substance/medical condition
ptsd.criterion_quote: Criterion A: Exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence … Criterion B: Intrusion symptoms (≥1). Criterion C: Persistent avoidance of stimuli associated with the traumatic event(s) (≥1). Criterion D: Negative alterations in cognitions and mood (≥2). Criterion E: Marked alterations in arousal and reactivity (≥2). Criterion F: Duration of the disturbance (Criteria B, C, D and E) is more than 1 month.

source.title: DSM-5 (via SAMHSA TIP 57, NCBI Bookshelf)

panic_disorder.name: Panic Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
panic_disorder.criterion: Recurrent unexpected panic attacks. A panic attack = abrupt surge of intense fear or discomfort peaking within minutes with ≥4 of 13 symptoms (palpitations, sweating, trembling, dyspnea, choking, chest pain, nausea, dizziness, chills/heat, paresthesias, derealization/depersonalization, fear of losing control, fear of dying). ≥1 attack followed by ≥1 month of persistent concern about additional attacks, worry about consequences, or significant maladaptive behavior change (e.g., avoidance). Not attributable to substance/medical condition; not better explained by another mental disorder
panic_disorder.criterion_quote: Recurrent unexpected panic attacks. A panic attack is an abrupt surge of intense fear or discomfort that reaches a peak within minutes, during which four (or more) of 13 symptoms occur. … At least one attack has been followed by 1 month (or more) of persistent concern about additional attacks, worry about their consequences, or a significant maladaptive change in behavior related to the attacks (e.g., avoidance).

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

ocd.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) — DSM-5/5-TR
ocd.criterion: A — presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both. Obsessions = recurrent persistent intrusive unwanted thoughts/urges/images causing marked anxiety; person attempts to ignore/suppress/neutralize. Compulsions = repetitive behaviors or mental acts driven by an obsession; aimed at reducing distress but not realistically connected or clearly excessive. B — obsessions or compulsions time-consuming (>1 h/day) or cause clinically significant distress/impairment. C — not attributable to substance/medical condition. D — not better explained by another mental disorder
ocd.criterion_quote: Criterion A: Presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both. … Criterion B: The obsessions or compulsions are time-consuming (e.g., take more than 1 hour per day) or cause clinically significant distress or impairment.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

anorexia_nervosa.name: Anorexia Nervosa — DSM-5/5-TR
anorexia_nervosa.criterion: A — restriction of energy intake relative to requirements, leading to significantly low body weight in context of age, sex, developmental trajectory, physical health. B — intense fear of gaining weight or of becoming fat, OR persistent behavior interfering with weight gain, even though at significantly low weight. C — disturbance in the way body weight/shape is experienced, undue influence of body weight/shape on self-evaluation, OR persistent lack of recognition of the seriousness of the current low body weight. Subtypes: restricting vs binge-eating/purging
anorexia_nervosa.criterion_quote: A. Restriction of energy intake relative to requirements, leading to a significantly low body weight in the context of age, sex, developmental trajectory, and physical health. … B. Intense fear of gaining weight or of becoming fat, or persistent behavior that interferes with weight gain. … C. Disturbance in the way in which one's body weight or shape is experienced, undue influence of body weight or shape on self-evaluation, or persistent lack of recognition of the seriousness of the current low body weight.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via NCBI Bookshelf)

bulimia_nervosa.name: Bulimia Nervosa — DSM-5/5-TR
bulimia_nervosa.criterion: Recurrent episodes of binge eating (eating in a discrete period, e.g., 2 h, an amount of food definitely larger than most would eat under similar circumstances, with a sense of lack of control). Recurrent inappropriate compensatory behaviors to prevent weight gain (self-induced vomiting; misuse of laxatives/diuretics/medications; fasting; or excessive exercise). Binge eating and compensatory behaviors both occur ≥1× per week ×3 months. Self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape and weight. Disturbance does not occur exclusively during anorexia
bulimia_nervosa.criterion_quote: Recurrent episodes of binge eating, characterized by eating, in a discrete period of time (e.g., within any 2-hour period), an amount of food that is definitely larger than what most individuals would eat in a similar period under similar circumstances, with a sense of lack of control over eating during the episode. Recurrent inappropriate compensatory behaviors in order to prevent weight gain. … The binge eating and inappropriate compensatory behaviors both occur, on average, at least once a week for 3 months.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

bulimia_nervosa.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Quote is verbatim DSM-5-TR but StatPearls source paraphrases it; quote accurate to DSM but not cited URL.]

binge_eating_disorder.name: Binge-Eating Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
binge_eating_disorder.criterion: Recurrent episodes of binge eating (same definition as bulimia). Binge-eating episodes associated with ≥3 of: eating much more rapidly than normal; eating until uncomfortably full; eating large amounts when not physically hungry; eating alone because embarrassed by quantity; feeling disgusted/depressed/very guilty afterward. Marked distress about binge eating. Episodes occur ≥1× per week ×3 months. Not associated with recurrent inappropriate compensatory behavior (no purging)
binge_eating_disorder.criterion_quote: Recurrent episodes of binge eating … Binge-eating episodes are associated with three (or more) of the following: eating much more rapidly than normal; eating until feeling uncomfortably full; eating large amounts of food when not feeling physically hungry; eating alone because of feeling embarrassed by how much one is eating; feeling disgusted with oneself, depressed, or very guilty afterward. Marked distress regarding binge eating. The binge eating occurs, on average, at least once a week for 3 months. Not associated with recurrent inappropriate compensatory behavior.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

borderline_pd.name: Borderline Personality Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
borderline_pd.criterion: Pervasive pattern of instability of interpersonal relationships, self-image, and affects, AND marked impulsivity, beginning by early adulthood and present in a variety of contexts. ≥5 of: (1) frantic efforts to avoid real/imagined abandonment; (2) unstable and intense interpersonal relationships alternating between idealization and devaluation; (3) identity disturbance; (4) impulsivity in ≥2 potentially self-damaging areas (spending, sex, substance abuse, reckless driving, binge eating); (5) recurrent suicidal behavior/gestures/threats or self-mutilating behavior; (6) affective instability due to marked mood reactivity; (7) chronic feelings of emptiness; (8) inappropriate intense anger or difficulty controlling anger; (9) transient stress-related paranoid ideation or severe dissociative symptoms
borderline_pd.criterion_quote: A pervasive pattern of instability of interpersonal relationships, self-image, and affects, and marked impulsivity, beginning by early adulthood and present in a variety of contexts, as indicated by five (or more) of the following …

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

opioid_use_disorder.name: Opioid Use Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
opioid_use_disorder.criterion: Problematic pattern of opioid use leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, manifested by ≥2 of 11 within a 12-month period: same 11-criterion framework as AUD (taken in larger amounts/longer than intended; desire/unsuccessful efforts to cut down; time spent obtaining/using/recovering; craving; failure to fulfill role obligations; continued use despite social/interpersonal problems; activities given up; recurrent use in hazardous situations; continued use despite physical/psychological problems; tolerance; withdrawal). Severity: 2–3 mild; 4–5 moderate; ≥6 severe
opioid_use_disorder.criterion_quote: A problematic pattern of opioid use leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, as manifested by at least two of the following, occurring within a 12-month period … Severity: mild = 2–3, moderate = 4–5, severe = 6+ symptoms.

source.title: DSM-5 (via SAMHSA TIP 63, NCBI Bookshelf)

autism_spectrum.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder — DSM-5-TR
autism_spectrum.criterion: A — persistent deficits in social communication and social interaction across multiple contexts, manifested by ALL THREE: (1) deficits in social-emotional reciprocity; (2) deficits in nonverbal communicative behaviors; (3) deficits in developing/maintaining/understanding relationships. B — restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities, manifested by ≥2 of 4: (1) stereotyped or repetitive motor movements/objects/speech; (2) insistence on sameness, inflexible adherence to routines, ritualized patterns; (3) highly restricted, fixated interests abnormal in intensity or focus; (4) hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input. C — symptoms present in early developmental period. D — symptoms cause clinically significant impairment. E — disturbances not better explained by intellectual developmental disorder or global developmental delay
autism_spectrum.criterion_quote: A. Persistent deficits in social communication and social interaction across multiple contexts, as manifested by all three of the following … B. Restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities, as manifested by at least two of four …

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

schizoaffective.name: Schizoaffective Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
schizoaffective.criterion: A — uninterrupted period of illness during which there is a major mood episode (MDE or manic) concurrent with Criterion A of schizophrenia. B — delusions or hallucinations for ≥2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode during the lifetime duration of the illness. C — symptoms meeting criteria for a major mood episode are present for the majority of the total duration of the active and residual portions of the illness. D — disturbance not attributable to substance or medical condition. Subtypes: bipolar type or depressive type
schizoaffective.criterion_quote: A. An uninterrupted period of illness during which there is a major mood episode (major depressive or manic) concurrent with Criterion A of schizophrenia. B. Delusions or hallucinations for 2 or more weeks in the absence of a major mood episode (depressive or manic) during the lifetime duration of the illness. C. Symptoms that meet criteria for a major mood episode are present for the majority of the total duration of the active and residual portions of the illness.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

celiac_disease.name: Celiac Disease — ACG 2023 + Marsh classification
celiac_disease.criterion: First-line: tTG-IgA + total IgA (to exclude IgA deficiency); tTG-IgA >10× ULN strongly supports diagnosis. Endoscopic biopsy: 1–2 from duodenal bulb AND ≥4 from post-bulbar duodenum (disease can be patchy). Histology graded by modified Marsh-Oberhuber: Marsh 0 normal; Marsh 1 increased IELs (>25/100 enterocytes), normal villi; Marsh 2 IEL increase + crypt hyperplasia; Marsh 3a partial villous atrophy; Marsh 3b subtotal villous atrophy; Marsh 3c total villous atrophy
celiac_disease.criterion_quote: Obtaining 1 or 2 biopsies from the duodenal bulb and at least 4 biopsies from the post-bulbar duodenum is important for an adequate diagnosis as celiac disease can be patchy. … Marsh III is sub-divided into IIIa (partial villus atrophy), Marsh IIIb (subtotal villus atrophy) and Marsh IIIc (total villus atrophy) to explain the spectrum of villus atrophy along with crypt hypertrophy and increased intra-epithelial lymphocytes.

source.title: ACG Guidelines Update: Diagnosis and Management of Celiac Disease (Rubio-Tapia 2023); Marsh classification updates

eoe.name: Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE) — ACG 2025
eoe.criterion: Diagnosis based on esophageal dysfunction symptoms AND ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field (eos/HPF) on esophageal biopsy AND exclusion of non-EoE disorders. Obtain ≥6 biopsies from ≥2 esophageal levels (proximal/mid and distal). EREFS score for endoscopic characterization (edema, rings, exudates, furrows, strictures)
eoe.criterion_quote: A diagnosis should be based on the presence of esophageal dysfunction symptoms and at least 15 eosinophils per high-power field on esophageal biopsy, particularly after ruling out non-EoE disorders.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Eosinophilic Esophagitis (Dellon 2025)

barretts_esophagus.name: Barrett's Esophagus — ACG 2022 + Prague C&M
barretts_esophagus.criterion: Diagnosis requires endoscopic columnar-appearing salmon-colored mucosa ≥1 cm above the GE junction AND histologic confirmation of intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells) on biopsy. Z-line displacement <1 cm should NOT be biopsied routinely. Length reported using Prague C (circumferential extent in cm) & M (maximal extent in cm including tongues) classification; reliability excellent for segments >1 cm
barretts_esophagus.criterion_quote: The current definition of BE requires columnar mucosa of at least 1 cm in length in conjunction with intestinal metaplasia on biopsies. … The Prague criteria used to measure BE demonstrate excellent reliability for segments >1 cm, but not for segments <1 cm.

source.title: Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline (Shaheen 2022); Prague C&M criteria (Sharma 2006)

h_pylori.name: Helicobacter pylori Infection — ACG 2017
h_pylori.criterion: Active-infection tests for new diagnosis or PUD history: urea breath test, fecal antigen test, or biopsy-based test (rapid urease, histology, culture). Test-of-cure ≥4 weeks after antibiotic completion AND PPI withheld ≥1–2 weeks before testing. Indications: active PUD, history of PUD, low-grade gastric MALT lymphoma, post-resection early gastric cancer
h_pylori.criterion_quote: A test that identifies active infection should be used for patients with a new diagnosis or a history of PUD, including urea breath tests, fecal antigen tests, or, if endoscopy is performed, mucosal biopsy-based tests.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Treatment of Helicobacter pylori Infection (Chey 2017)

gastroparesis.name: Gastroparesis — ACG 2022
gastroparesis.criterion: Symptoms (nausea, early satiety, postprandial fullness, vomiting) AND objective documentation of delayed gastric emptying of solid food on 4-hour standardized scintigraphy (255 kcal, 2% fat Eggbeaters meal): >10% gastric retention at 4 h. Mechanical obstruction must be excluded (EGD). Withhold opioids, prokinetics, antiemetics, cannabinoids 48 h prior to test
gastroparesis.criterion_quote: Gastroparesis is a motility disorder characterized by symptoms and objective documentation of delayed gastric emptying of solid food without mechanical obstruction.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Gastroparesis (Camilleri 2022)

sibo.name: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (SIBO) — ACG 2020
sibo.criterion: Glucose or lactulose breath test positive: H₂ rise >20 ppm above baseline within first 90 min (SIBO); CH₄ rise >10 ppm at any point (intestinal methanogen overgrowth / IMO). Alternative gold standard: small bowel aspirate culture ≥10³ CFU/mL (lower than historical 10⁵)
sibo.criterion_quote: A positive breath test is defined as a >20-ppm increase of hydrogen from baseline in the first 90 minutes or a >10-ppm increase of methane at any point during the test.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (Pimentel 2020)

lgib_oakland.name: Acute Lower GI Bleeding — Oakland Score (ACG 2023)
lgib_oakland.criterion: Oakland score components: age, sex, prior LGIB admission, DRE findings, heart rate, systolic BP, hemoglobin. Score ≤8 identifies low-risk LGIB patients suitable for outpatient evaluation (sensitivity ~98% for safe discharge). Colonoscopy is the recommended initial diagnostic test for hospitalized patients without hemodynamic instability
lgib_oakland.criterion_quote: The 2023 American College of Gastroenterology (ACG) guideline recommended using risk stratification tools such as the Oakland Score to triage hospitalized patients.

source.title: Management of Patients With Acute Lower GI Bleeding: An Updated ACG Guideline (Sengupta 2023)

achalasia.name: Achalasia — Chicago Classification 4.0
achalasia.criterion: Conclusive achalasia: abnormal median integrated relaxation pressure (IRP) >15 mmHg supine (Medtronic) or >22 mmHg (Laborie) AND 100% absent peristalsis (all swallows failed peristalsis OR premature contraction). Subtypes — Type I: 100% failed peristalsis, no panesophageal pressurization (PEP). Type II: PEP in ≥20% of swallows. Type III: ≥20% premature swallows (distal latency <4.5 s) with no appreciable peristalsis. Supportive testing for inconclusive cases: timed barium esophagram (TBE) or FLIP
achalasia.criterion_quote: In CCv4.0 a definition of achalasia requires 100% absent peristalsis, defined as all swallows with either failed peristalsis or premature contraction.

source.title: Chicago classification update (version 4.0): Technical review on diagnostic criteria for achalasia

functional_dyspepsia.name: Functional Dyspepsia — Rome IV (B1)
functional_dyspepsia.criterion: ≥1 of: bothersome postprandial fullness, bothersome early satiation, bothersome epigastric pain, or bothersome epigastric burning, with no evidence of structural disease (incl. upper endoscopy). Symptoms ≥3 months with onset ≥6 months before diagnosis. Subtypes: PDS (B1a) — postprandial fullness/early satiation ≥3 d/wk; EPS (B1b) — epigastric pain/burning ≥1 d/wk, not generalized, not relieved by defecation/flatus
functional_dyspepsia.criterion_quote: One or more of the following: Bothersome postprandial fullness, Bothersome early satiation, Bothersome epigastric pain, or Bothersome epigastric burning [with] no evidence of structural disease (including at upper endoscopy).

source.title: Rome IV Criteria — Functional Gastroduodenal Disorders

globus.name: Globus — Rome IV (A4) functional esophageal disorder
globus.criterion: Persistent or intermittent non-painful sensation of a lump or foreign body in the throat between meals; no dysphagia or odynophagia; no gastric inlet patch in proximal esophagus; no evidence GERD or EoE is causing the symptom; absence of major esophageal motor disorder. Symptoms ≥3 months with onset ≥6 months prior, ≥1 day/week frequency
globus.criterion_quote: Persistent or intermittent, non-painful sensation of a lump or foreign body in the throat with no structural lesion identified.

source.title: Rome IV Criteria — Functional Esophageal Disorders

microscopic_colitis.name: Microscopic Colitis (collagenous & lymphocytic) — AGA 2016
microscopic_colitis.criterion: Diagnosis requires colon biopsy showing characteristic histology in setting of chronic watery diarrhea with macroscopically normal or near-normal colonic mucosa. Collagenous colitis: subepithelial collagen band ≥10 µm on H&E + increased lamina propria inflammatory infiltrate. Lymphocytic colitis: ≥20 intraepithelial lymphocytes per 100 surface epithelial cells + increased lamina propria inflammation + collagen band <10 µm
microscopic_colitis.criterion_quote: The diagnosis of microscopic colitis requires histologic assessment of colon biopsies with findings including increased intraepithelial lymphocytes with or without expansion of the subepithelial collagen band.

source.title: AGA Institute Guideline on the Medical Management of Microscopic Colitis

ida_gi_workup.name: Iron-Deficiency Anemia — GI evaluation (AGA 2020)
ida_gi_workup.criterion: Iron-deficiency defined for GI workup as ferritin <45 ng/mL (sens 85%, spec 92%; historical <15 cutoff had only 59% sens). Bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) recommended for asymptomatic postmenopausal women and men with IDA; conditional recommendation for premenopausal women. After negative bidirectional endoscopy in asymptomatic patients: trial of iron supplementation preferred over routine video capsule endoscopy
ida_gi_workup.criterion_quote: Iron deficiency anemia should be defined as a ferritin level <45 ng/mL.

source.title: AGA Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Gastrointestinal Evaluation of Iron Deficiency Anemia

chronic_pancreatitis.name: Chronic Pancreatitis — M-ANNHEIM + TIGAR-O
chronic_pancreatitis.criterion: M-ANNHEIM categories: Definite (≥1 of pancreatic calcification; moderate/marked ductal lesions per Cambridge; marked + persistent exocrine insufficiency; typical histology); Probable; Borderline; Associated CP. Imaging (US/CT/MRI/MRCP/EUS) graded per Cambridge: normal, equivocal, mild, moderate, marked. TIGAR-O risk-factor axes: T (Toxic-metabolic — alcohol, smoking), I (Idiopathic), G (Genetic — PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR, CTRC), A (Autoimmune — type 1/IgG4, type 2), R (Recurrent acute/severe pancreatitis), O (Obstructive)
chronic_pancreatitis.criterion_quote: M-ANNHEIM pancreatic imaging criteria for US, CT, MRI/MRCP, and EUS are based on imaging features as defined by the Cambridge classification and include normal, equivocal, mild, moderate and marked changes.

source.title: The M-ANNHEIM classification of chronic pancreatitis; TIGAR-O classification system

chronic_pancreatitis.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed abstract doesn't display Cambridge imaging detail; M-ANNHEIM Schneider 2007 correct.]

pheochromocytoma_paraganglioma.name: Pheochromocytoma / Paraganglioma — initial biochemical testing
pheochromocytoma_paraganglioma.criterion: Initial biochemical testing for PPGLs should include plasma free metanephrines or urinary fractionated metanephrines (24-h collection). Elevation >3× ULN strongly suggests PPGL.
pheochromocytoma_paraganglioma.criterion_quote: We recommend that initial biochemical testing for PPGLs should include measurements of plasma free metanephrines or urinary fractionated metanephrines.

source.title: Pheochromocytoma and Paraganglioma: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

primary_hyperparathyroidism.name: Asymptomatic Primary Hyperparathyroidism — Severity / Surgical criteria (Fourth International Workshop)
primary_hyperparathyroidism.criterion: Severity criteria in asymptomatic PHPT: serum calcium >1.0 mg/dL above ULN; creatinine clearance <60 mL/min; T-score ≤ −2.5 at spine/hip/distal radius or vertebral fracture; 24-h urine calcium >400 mg/d with increased stone risk; age <50
primary_hyperparathyroidism.criterion_quote: Serum calcium (>1.0 mg/dL [0.25 mmol/L] above upper limit of normal) … Creatinine clearance < 60 cc/min … 24-h urine for calcium >400 mg/d (>10 mmol/d)

source.title: Guidelines for the Management of Asymptomatic Primary Hyperparathyroidism: Summary Statement from the Fourth International Workshop

primary_hyperparathyroidism.notes: Plus T-score ≤ −2.5 at LS / FN / total hip / distal 1/3 radius or vertebral fracture by imaging.

male_hypogonadism.name: Male Hypogonadism — Endocrine Society
male_hypogonadism.criterion: Diagnose hypogonadism with symptoms plus consistently low fasting morning total testosterone; CDC-harmonized lower normal limit in healthy nonobese young men is 264 ng/dL (9.2 nmol/L). Confirm with repeat fasting AM testosterone on separate day
male_hypogonadism.criterion_quote: The lower limit of the normal total testosterone (TT) harmonized to the CDC standard in healthy nonobese young men is 264 ng/dL (9.2 nmol/L).

source.title: Testosterone Therapy in Men With Hypogonadism: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

central_adrenal_insufficiency.name: Central Adrenal Insufficiency (Hypopituitarism) — Endocrine Society
central_adrenal_insufficiency.criterion: Morning cortisol <3 µg/dL indicates AI; >15 µg/dL likely excludes AI; peak cortisol <18.1 µg/dL (500 nmol/L) at 30 or 60 min on stimulation indicates AI
central_adrenal_insufficiency.criterion_quote: A cortisol level <3 μg/dL is indicative of AI and a cortisol level >15 μg/dL likely excludes an AI diagnosis.

source.title: Hormonal Replacement in Hypopituitarism in Adults: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

adult_ghd.name: Adult Growth Hormone Deficiency — Endocrine Society
adult_ghd.criterion: Diagnose adult GHD with provocative testing: peak GH >3–5 µg/L on ITT (gold standard); >4 µg/L on GHRH+arginine; >3 µg/L on glucagon (BMI-adjusted)
adult_ghd.criterion_quote: GH should be >3–5 μg/L. Cutoffs for GH response are BMI related.

source.title: Hormonal Replacement in Hypopituitarism in Adults: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

central_hypothyroidism.name: Central Hypothyroidism — Endocrine Society
central_hypothyroidism.criterion: Low fT4 with low/normal/mildly elevated TSH in the setting of pituitary disease confirms central hypothyroidism. TSH cannot be used to monitor replacement
central_hypothyroidism.criterion_quote: An fT4 level below the laboratory reference range in conjunction with a low, normal, or mildly elevated TSH in the setting of pituitary disease usually confirms a CH diagnosis.

source.title: Hormonal Replacement in Hypopituitarism in Adults: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

cah_21ohd.name: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (21-OHD) — Endocrine Society
cah_21ohd.criterion: In symptomatic individuals past infancy, screen with early-morning (before 8 am) baseline serum 17-OHP by LC-MS/MS; if borderline, perform cosyntropin stimulation with full adrenocortical profile
cah_21ohd.criterion_quote: In symptomatic individuals past infancy, we recommend screening with an early morning (before 8 am) baseline serum 17OHP measurement by LC-MS/MS.

source.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Due to Steroid 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

pcos.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) — Rotterdam / Endocrine Society
pcos.criterion: Diagnose PCOS when ≥2 of 3 are met after excluding mimics (thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, nonclassical CAH): (1) androgen excess (clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism); (2) ovulatory dysfunction (oligo/anovulation); (3) polycystic ovaries on US
pcos.criterion_quote: We suggest that the diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) be made if two of the three following criteria are met: androgen excess, ovulatory dysfunction, or polycystic ovaries.

source.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

pagets_bone.name: Paget's Disease of Bone — Endocrine Society
pagets_bone.criterion: Obtain plain radiographs of suspicious skeletal regions; after radiologic diagnosis, evaluate biochemically with serum total alkaline phosphatase (or a more specific bone formation marker)
pagets_bone.criterion_quote: In patients with suspected Paget's disease, we recommend obtaining plain radiographs of the suspicious regions of the skeleton.

source.title: Paget's Disease of Bone: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

vitamin_d_2024.name: Vitamin D (2024) — Endocrine Society guidance against routine 25(OH)D testing
vitamin_d_2024.criterion: In generally healthy populations, do NOT routinely test serum 25(OH)D; the 2024 guideline no longer endorses specific 25(OH)D thresholds for sufficiency/insufficiency/deficiency (replaces 2011 cutoffs)
vitamin_d_2024.criterion_quote: the panel suggests against routine 25(OH)D testing in all populations considered

source.title: Vitamin D for the Prevention of Disease: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline

multiple_myeloma.name: Multiple Myeloma — IMWG 2014 (SLiM-CRAB)
multiple_myeloma.criterion: Diagnose MM with clonal BM plasma cells ≥10% or biopsy-proven plasmacytoma PLUS ≥1 myeloma-defining event. CRAB end-organ damage: hyperCalcemia (>11 mg/dL or >1 mg/dL above ULN); Renal insufficiency (CrCl <40 mL/min or Cr >2 mg/dL); Anemia (Hb <10 g/dL or >2 g/dL below normal); Bone lesions (≥1 lytic lesion on imaging). SLiM biomarkers (added 2014): clonal BMPCs ≥60%; involved/uninvolved serum FLC ratio ≥100 (with involved iFLC ≥100 mg/L); >1 focal MRI lesion ≥5 mm
multiple_myeloma.criterion_quote: The CRAB criteria include: Calcium – elevated level in the serum (blood) more than 11mg/dL (higher than the upper limit of normal); Renal (kidney) impairment – creatinine clearance <40 mL per minute or serum creatinine >2 mg/dL; Anemia (low red blood cell count) – hemoglobin value <10 g/dL; and Bone damage (at least one osteolytic lesion). In 2014, the IMWG expanded the CRAB criteria by adding 3 new SLiM biomarkers that indicate active myeloma. This includes ≥60% bone marrow plasma cells (BMPCs), free light chain ratio (FLCratio) ≥100, and >1 MRI-defined ≥5 mm focal lesion, also called SLiM CRAB.

source.title: International Myeloma Working Group updated criteria for the diagnosis of multiple myeloma

cll_iwcll.name: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) — iwCLL 2018
cll_iwcll.criterion: Diagnosis: ≥5000 monoclonal B-lymphocytes/µL in peripheral blood, with clonality confirmed by flow cytometry showing characteristic CLL immunophenotype (CD5+, CD19+, CD20 dim, CD23+, κ or λ light-chain restriction, sIg dim). MBL (monoclonal B lymphocytosis) = same phenotype with <5000/µL and no other features of lymphoproliferative disorder
cll_iwcll.criterion_quote: The diagnosis of CLL is established by the presence of ≥5000 monoclonal B lymphocytes/μL in the peripheral blood, with clonality of these B lymphocytes confirmed by flow cytometry.

source.title: iwCLL guidelines for diagnosis, indications for treatment, response assessment, and supportive management of CLL

aml_eln_2022.name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia — ELN 2022 genetic risk stratification
aml_eln_2022.criterion: Favorable: t(8;21) RUNX1::RUNX1T1; inv(16) or t(16;16) CBFB::MYH11; biallelic mutated CEBPA (or in-frame bZIP CEBPA); mutated NPM1 without FLT3-ITD. Intermediate: mutated NPM1 with FLT3-ITD; wild-type NPM1 with FLT3-ITD (without adverse risk); t(9;11) MLLT3::KMT2A; cytogenetic/molecular abnormalities not classified as favorable or adverse. Adverse: t(6;9); t(v;11q23.3) KMT2A; t(9;22) BCR::ABL1; inv(3) or t(3;3); −5 or del(5q); −7; −17/abn(17p); complex karyotype; monosomal karyotype; mutated TP53; myelodysplasia-related (MR) gene mutations (ASXL1, BCOR, EZH2, RUNX1, SF3B1, SRSF2, STAG2, U2AF1, ZRSR2)
aml_eln_2022.criterion_quote: The 2022 European Leukemia Network (ELN) risk stratification system for acute myeloid leukemia added several myelodysplasia-related (MR) genetic mutations to the adverse risk category, assigned FLT3-ITD mutations to the intermediate risk category, and designated bZIP in-frame mutations in CEBPA as favorable risk.

source.title: Diagnosis and management of AML in adults: 2022 recommendations from an international expert panel on behalf of the ELN

apl_sanz.name: Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL) — Sanz/PETHEMA risk score
apl_sanz.criterion: Stratify newly diagnosed APL by baseline CBC. High risk: WBC >10×10⁹/L. Intermediate risk: WBC ≤10×10⁹/L AND platelets ≤40×10⁹/L. Low risk: WBC ≤10×10⁹/L AND platelets >40×10⁹/L
apl_sanz.criterion_quote: In what became known as the Sanz risk classification, high-risk (HR) patients were identified by a WBC count >10x10⁹/L, whereas a platelet count below or above 40x10⁹/L stratified the remainder into intermediate-risk (IR) and low-risk (LR) subgroups respectively.

source.title: Sanz risk classification — referenced in modern APL outcome analyses

lymphoma_lugano.name: Lymphoma — Lugano 2014 staging & response criteria
lymphoma_lugano.criterion: Response by PET-CT (5-point Deauville scale, score relative to mediastinal blood pool and liver): Complete metabolic response = score 1–3 with/without residual mass. By CT: CR requires target nodes regress to ≤1.5 cm longest diameter; no extralymphatic disease; non-measured lesions absent; organ enlargement regress to normal; no new lesions. Ann Arbor staging I–IV with E (extranodal) modifier preserved
lymphoma_lugano.criterion_quote: For PET-CT-Based Response: Score 1, 2, or 3 with or without a residual mass on the 5-point scale. For CT-Based Response (all of the following must be met): Target nodes/nodal masses must regress to ≤1.5 cm in longest diameter; No extralymphatic sites of disease; Nonmeasured lesions must be absent; Organ enlargement must regress to normal; No new lesions.

source.title: Recommendations for initial evaluation, staging, and response assessment of Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma: the Lugano classification

dlbcl_ipi.name: DLBCL — International Prognostic Index (IPI)
dlbcl_ipi.criterion: 5 binary clinical parameters (1 point each): age >60 y; serum LDH > ULN; Ann Arbor stage III–IV; ECOG performance status ≥2; ≥2 sites of extranodal involvement. Risk: 0–1 low; 2 low-intermediate; 3 high-intermediate; 4–5 high. R-IPI (rituximab era) regroups: 0 very good; 1–2 good; 3–5 poor. NCCN-IPI uses continuous age and LDH bands
dlbcl_ipi.criterion_quote: The IPI is based on 5 clinical parameters: age > 60 years, serum lactate dehydrogenase [LDH] above the upper limit of normal, Ann Arbor stage III-IV disease, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group [ECOG] performance status score ≥2, and ≥2 sites of extranodal disease.

source.org: International Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma Prognostic Factors Project
source.title: International prognostic indices in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma: a comparison of IPI, R-IPI, and NCCN-IPI

tls_cairo_bishop.name: Tumor Lysis Syndrome — Cairo-Bishop criteria
tls_cairo_bishop.criterion: Laboratory TLS (≥2 abnormalities within 3 days before or 7 days after chemotherapy): uric acid ≥8.0 mg/dL (476 µmol/L) or 25% rise; potassium ≥6.0 mmol/L or 25% rise; phosphate ≥4.5 mg/dL (1.45 mmol/L) adults or 25% rise; calcium ≤7.0 mg/dL (1.75 mmol/L) or 25% drop. Clinical TLS = laboratory TLS PLUS ≥1: creatinine ≥1.5× ULN; cardiac arrhythmia / sudden death; seizure
tls_cairo_bishop.criterion_quote: Laboratory tumor lysis syndrome is defined as either a 25% change or level above or below normal for any two or more serum values of uric acid, potassium, phosphate, and calcium within 3 days before or 7 days after the initiation of chemotherapy. Specific thresholds: Uric Acid ≥ 476 μmol/L (≥ 8.0 mg/dL); Potassium ≥ 6.0 mmol/L; Phosphorous ≥1.45 mmol/L (≥ 4.5 mg/dL); Calcium ≤ 1.75 mmol/L (≤ 7.0 mg/dL), or 25% change from baseline. Clinical TLS requires laboratory TLS plus: Creatinine ≥ 1.5 ULN, cardiac arrhythmia/sudden death, or seizure.

source.title: Tumour lysis syndrome: new therapeutic strategies and classification

hlh_2004.name: Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) — HLH-2004 criteria
hlh_2004.criterion: Diagnose if molecular diagnosis of HLH OR ≥5 of 8 met: (1) fever ≥38.3°C; (2) splenomegaly; (3) cytopenias affecting ≥2 of 3 lineages — Hb ≤9 g/dL, plt <100×10⁹/L, ANC <1.0×10⁹/L; (4) hypertriglyceridemia (fasting ≥265 mg/dL) and/or hypofibrinogenemia (≤150 mg/dL); (5) hemophagocytosis in BM, spleen, lymph node, or liver; (6) low or absent NK-cell activity; (7) ferritin >500 ng/mL; (8) elevated soluble CD25 (sIL-2Rα)
hlh_2004.criterion_quote: The diagnosis of HLH may be established by a molecular diagnosis consistent with HLH, or by fulfilling five out of eight criteria: 1. Fever ≥ 38.3°C; 2. Splenomegaly; 3. Cytopenias affecting at least 2 of 3 lineages (Hemoglobin ≤ 9 g/dL, Platelets < 100×10³/mL, Neutrophils < 1×10³/mL); 4. Hypertriglyceridemia (fasting, ≥ 265 mg/dL) and/or hypofibrinogenemia (≤ 150 mg/dL); 5. Hemophagocytosis in bone marrow or spleen or lymph nodes or liver; 6. Low or absent NK-cell activity; 7. Ferritin > 500 ng/mL; 8. Elevated Soluble CD25 (soluble IL-2 receptor alpha).

source.title: HLH-2004: Diagnostic and therapeutic guidelines for hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis

hlh_2004.notes: Companion: [[hlh_hscore]]. [AUDIT 2026-06: Fever threshold quoted ≥38.3°C; HLH-2004 specifies ≥38.5°C.]

hlh_hscore.name: Reactive Hemophagocytic Syndrome — H-Score (Fardet 2014)
hlh_hscore.criterion: Weighted score using 9 items: known immunosuppression, fever (max 1 month), organomegaly (hepato/splenomegaly), cytopenias (1, 2, or 3 lineages), ferritin (>2000, 6000+), triglycerides, fibrinogen, AST, hemophagocytosis on aspirate. Higher score → higher probability of HLH (calculator-based; median in adults with HLH ~235; threshold often ≥169 for ~99% sensitivity, ~86% specificity)
hlh_hscore.criterion_quote: The H-Score was developed and validated as a diagnostic score for reactive hemophagocytic syndrome using a multicenter retrospective cohort of 312 patients. The HScore uses a weighted combination of clinical, laboratory, and histologic findings (known immunosuppression, fever, organomegaly, cytopenias, ferritin, triglycerides, fibrinogen, AST, hemophagocytosis) to estimate the probability of HLH. The median H-score was 235 (152-274) for adults with HLH.

source.title: Development and Validation of the HScore, a Score for the Diagnosis of Reactive Hemophagocytic Syndrome

hlh_hscore.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Wiley paywall; Fardet 2014 H-Score components canonical.]

polycythemia_vera.name: Polycythemia Vera — WHO diagnostic criteria
polycythemia_vera.criterion: Major criteria: (1) Hb >16.5 g/dL or Hct >49% in men; Hb >16 g/dL or Hct >48% in women (OR increased red cell mass); (2) bone marrow biopsy showing hypercellularity for age with trilineage growth (panmyelosis) including pleomorphic megakaryocytes; (3) presence of JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutation. Minor: serum EPO below reference range. Diagnosis: all 3 major OR first 2 major + minor
polycythemia_vera.criterion_quote: The hemoglobin/hematocrit threshold for diagnosis was set to 16.5 g/dl/49% in men and 16 g/dl/48% in women, and characteristic bone marrow morphology was added as one of three major diagnostic criteria. The presence of a JAK2 mutation remains the third major diagnostic criterion in PV.

source.title: Rationale for revision and proposed changes of the WHO diagnostic criteria for polycythemia vera, essential thrombocythemia and primary myelofibrosis

mpn_drivers.name: Essential Thrombocythemia / Primary Myelofibrosis — WHO driver mutations
mpn_drivers.criterion: BCR-ABL1-negative MPN diagnosis uses combination of bone marrow morphology, blood counts, and driver mutation testing. Driver mutations (confirm clonality and diagnosis): JAK2 V617F, CALR (exon 9), MPL (W515L/K). About 50–60% of ET and PMF are JAK2+; 20–30% CALR+; 5–10% MPL+; remainder 'triple-negative'
mpn_drivers.criterion_quote: Essential thrombocythemia, polycythemia vera, and primary myelofibrosis are grouped under the category of BCR-ABL1-negative MPN subtypes, and the combination of molecular data with bone marrow morphology and blood counts remain the current recommended diagnostic markers. Mutations that are considered to confirm clonality and specific diagnosis now include CALR, in addition to JAK2 and MPL.

source.title: Evolution of WHO diagnostic criteria in Classical Myeloproliferative Neoplasms compared with the International Consensus Classification

hyperviscosity.name: Hyperviscosity Syndrome — serum viscosity thresholds
hyperviscosity.criterion: Symptoms typically occur with serum viscosity >4 centipoise (~4× water); most patients are symptomatic with viscosity >6 cP. Waldenström macroglobulinemia accounts for ~90% of cases; symptoms often emerge when IgM >4 g/dL. IgG/IgA hyperviscosity less common (require higher concentrations). Classic triad: mucosal bleeding, visual changes, neurologic symptoms
hyperviscosity.criterion_quote: Symptoms from hyperviscosity typically occur with greater than 4 centipoise, which is about 4 times more viscous than water… most patients are symptomatic when serum viscosity is greater than 6 cP. Waldenström's macroglobulinemia is associated with 90% of all cases of hyperviscosity syndrome. In Waldenström's macroglobulinemia the IgM protein may exceed 4 g/dL, and the relative plasma viscosity can exceed 4 centipoise. IgM is predominantly distributed intravascularly and increased viscosity can become exponential above a concentration of 3 g/dL.

source.title: Hyperviscosity Syndrome in Paraprotein Secreting Conditions Including Waldenstrom Macroglobulinemia

hyperviscosity.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: >6 cP threshold and 90% Waldenstrom not in cited source.]

b12_deficiency.name: Vitamin B12 Deficiency — diagnostic cutoffs
b12_deficiency.criterion: Serum B12 <200 pg/mL (<145 pmol/L): deficiency very likely. >300 pg/mL: deficiency unlikely. 200–300 pg/mL: indeterminate — confirm with elevated methylmalonic acid (MMA) >0.4 µmol/L and/or homocysteine >15 µmol/L (more sensitive). Macrocytosis = MCV >100 fL but absent in 25–30% of B12-deficient patients (mixed iron deficiency can mask)
b12_deficiency.criterion_quote: A vitamin B12 level < 200 pg/mL (< 145 pmol/L) indicates vitamin B12 deficiency. If serum vitamin B12 levels are <200 pg/mL, vitamin B12 deficiency is very likely. If serum vitamin B12 levels are >300 pg/mL, vitamin B12 deficiency is unlikely. If serum vitamin B12 levels are between 200 and 300 pg/mL, metabolite testing should be reserved for those patients most likely to have a vitamin B12 deficiency. Macrocytosis in adults is defined as a red blood cell (RBC) mean corpuscular volume (MCV) >100 femtoliter (fL).

source.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

asymptomatic_bacteriuria.name: Asymptomatic Bacteriuria — IDSA 2019
asymptomatic_bacteriuria.criterion: ASB defined by ≥10⁵ CFU/mL in voided urine without UTI signs/symptoms; women require 2 consecutive specimens, men require 1.
asymptomatic_bacteriuria.criterion_quote: ≥10^5 colony-forming units (CFU)/mL (≥10^8 CFU/L) in a voided urine specimen without signs or symptoms attributable to UTI. For women, 2 consecutive specimens should be obtained, preferably within 2 weeks, to confirm the persistence of bacteriuria. For men, a single urine specimen meeting these quantitative criteria is sufficient for diagnosis.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Asymptomatic Bacteriuria: 2019 Update

asymptomatic_bacteriuria.notes: Distinguish from symptomatic UTI; do not treat in non-pregnant adults absent indication.

native_vertebral_osteomyelitis.name: Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis — IDSA 2015
native_vertebral_osteomyelitis.criterion: Back pain + elevated ESR/CRP, MRI showing loss of T1 disc-vertebral margin with T2 hyperintensity; positive blood culture (especially S. aureus) may obviate biopsy.
native_vertebral_osteomyelitis.criterion_quote: An elevated ESR or CRP result in patients with back pain, though not specific, has a sensitivity that can range from 94% to 100%. … MRI of the spine has a sensitivity of 97%, specificity of 93%, and an accuracy of 94% in diagnosing NVO. A positive blood culture for S. aureus obviates the need for an image-guided aspiration specimen in patients with clinical, laboratory, and radiologic findings suggestive of NVO.

source.title: 2015 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis

native_vertebral_osteomyelitis.notes: ESR >50 mm/hr and CRP >2.75 mg/dL associated with higher treatment-failure risk.

lyme_disease.name: Lyme Disease — IDSA/AAN/ACR 2020
lyme_disease.criterion: Two-tier serologic testing (EIA/IFA followed by IgM/IgG immunoblot, OR sequential/concurrent 2 EIAs). Clinical diagnosis of erythema migrans in endemic exposure without serology.
lyme_disease.criterion_quote: Serum antibody tests should be performed using clinically validated assays in a conventional 2-tiered testing protocol, in which an enzyme immunoassay (EIA) or indirect fluorescent antibody test (IFA) is followed by immunoglobulin M (IgM) and IgG immunoblots, or in a modified 2-tiered testing protocol, in which 2 different EIAs are performed sequentially or concurrently without the use of immunoblots. In patients with potential tick exposure in a Lyme disease endemic area who have 1 or more skin lesions compatible with erythema migrans, we recommend clinical diagnosis rather than laboratory testing.

source.title: 2020 Guidelines for the Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease

invasive_candidiasis.name: Invasive Candidiasis / Candidemia — IDSA 2016
invasive_candidiasis.criterion: Diagnosis primarily by blood culture (sensitivity ~50%); β-D-glucan adjunctive (sens 75–80%, spec 80%); treat 2 weeks after documented bloodstream clearance.
invasive_candidiasis.criterion_quote: The overall sensitivity of blood cultures for diagnosing invasive candidiasis is roughly 50%. The limit of detection of blood cultures is ≤1 colony-forming unit/mL. In meta-analyses of β-D-glucan studies, the pooled sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing invasive candidiasis were 75%–80% and 80%, respectively. Recommended duration of therapy for candidemia without obvious metastatic complications is for 2 weeks after documented clearance of Candida species from the bloodstream and resolution of symptoms attributable to candidemia.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Candidiasis: 2016 Update

invasive_aspergillosis.name: Invasive Aspergillosis — IDSA 2016
invasive_aspergillosis.criterion: Galactomannan optical density index ≥0.5 (some experts) up to FDA-cleared OD 1.0; proven IA requires microbiologic/histopathologic confirmation per EORTC/MSG.
invasive_aspergillosis.criterion_quote: an OD of 1.0 has been cleared by the FDA for clinical testing, although some experts consider positivity at OD > 0.5. … GMI (ratio of the optical density [OD] of the patient samples to the mean OD of control samples) of ≥0.5 … The EORTC/MSG revised criteria for defining IFIs, including IA, require a microbiologic and/or histopathologic diagnosis to define proven infection.

source.title: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Aspergillosis: 2016 Update

invasive_aspergillosis.notes: Full EORTC/MSG host/clinical/mycology criteria referenced but not reproduced in IDSA summary.

coccidioidomycosis.name: Coccidioidomycosis — IDSA 2016
coccidioidomycosis.criterion: Diagnosis via EIA IgM/IgG and complement-fixation titers; disseminated disease confirmed by histopathology or culture from extrapulmonary site, not by CF titer alone.
coccidioidomycosis.criterion_quote: Enzyme immunoassays (EIAs) for anticoccidioidal immunoglobulin M (IgM) and immunoglobulin G (IgG) are commercially available. A positive EIA IgM test, when it is the only positive serologic test result, is the least compelling diagnostic evidence. Groups of patients with disseminated infection have generally higher titers of complement-fixing (CF) antibodies than do those with infection confined to the lungs. The diagnosis of disseminated coccidioidomycosis should usually rely on the histopathologic identification in or fungal isolation from an extrapulmonary lesion.

source.title: 2016 IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Coccidioidomycosis

covid19_diagnostics.name: COVID-19 Diagnostics — IDSA 2023
covid19_diagnostics.criterion: SARS-CoV-2 NAAT recommended in symptomatic individuals suspected of COVID-19; conditional in asymptomatic exposed; against routine NAAT in asymptomatic unexposed before hospitalization or procedure.
covid19_diagnostics.criterion_quote: recommends a SARS-CoV-2 NAAT in symptomatic individuals suspected of having COVID-19 … suggests SARS-CoV-2 RNA testing in asymptomatic individuals who are either known or suspected to have been exposed to COVID-19 … suggests against routine SARS-CoV-2 NAAT in asymptomatic individuals without a known exposure to COVID-19 who are being hospitalized … suggests collecting and testing swab specimens from either the nasopharynx, anterior nares, oropharynx, or mid-turbinate regions; saliva, or mouth gargle.

source.title: IDSA Guidelines on the Diagnosis of COVID-19: Molecular Diagnostic Testing

febrile_neutropenia.name: Febrile Neutropenia — IDSA 2010
febrile_neutropenia.criterion: Fever = single oral temp ≥38.3°C (101°F) or ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) sustained ≥1 hour; neutropenia = ANC <500 cells/mm³ or expected to fall <500 within 48 h.
febrile_neutropenia.criterion_quote: Fever is defined as a single oral temperature measurement of ≥38.3°C (101°F) or a temperature of ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) sustained over a 1-h period. Neutropenia is defined as an ANC of <500 cells/mm3 or an ANC that is expected to decrease to <500 cells/mm3 during the next 48 h.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Use of Antimicrobial Agents in Neutropenic Patients with Cancer: 2010 Update

crbsi.name: Catheter-Related Bloodstream Infection (CRBSI) — IDSA 2009
crbsi.criterion: Diagnosis without catheter removal requires quantitative blood cultures or differential time to positivity (DTP) ≥120 minutes (catheter culture positive ≥2 hours before peripheral).
crbsi.criterion_quote: A differential time to positivity (DTP) of 120 min or more between blood cultures obtained through the catheter vs peripheral vein is an indicator of CRBSI with high sensitivity and specificity … the diagnosis of CR-BSI without removal of the catheter is only possible by performing quantitative blood cultures or calculating the differential time to positivity.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Intravascular Catheter-Related Infection: 2009 Update

crbsi.notes: Quote from IDSA-published Oxford CID article — confirm exact PDF wording before pilot use.

diabetic_foot_infection.name: Diabetes-Related Foot Infection — IWGDF/IDSA 2023
diabetic_foot_infection.criterion: Grade 1 mild: erythema <2 cm with local infection signs. Grade 2 moderate: cellulitis >2 cm or deeper tissue involvement, systemically well. Grade 3 severe: SIRS/sepsis. '(O)' suffix denotes bone involvement.
diabetic_foot_infection.criterion_quote: Grade 1 (Mild): >2 manifestations of infection with erythema <2cm, pain, tenderness, warmth or induration, with no local complications or systemic illness. Grade 2 (Moderate): Infection in a patient who is systemically stable but has ≥1 of: cellulitis >2 cm, lymphangitis, spread beneath fascia, deep tissue abscess, gangrene, or muscle, tendon, joint or bone involvement. Grade 3 (Severe): Infection in a patient with systemic or metabolic toxicity (SIRS/sepsis). … we separated it out by indicating the presence of bone infection with '(O)' after the grade number (3 or 4).

source.title: IWGDF/IDSA Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-related Foot Infections (2023)

diabetic_foot_infection.notes: Grade thresholds verified via IDSA summary + IWGDF search; verify exact Table 1 of CID ciad527 before deployment.

ssti_idsa.name: Skin & Soft Tissue Infection — IDSA classification
ssti_idsa.criterion: Classify by purulence and severity. Non-purulent (cellulitis/erysipelas/necrotizing): mild = no SIRS; moderate = systemic signs of infection; severe = SIRS + hypotension OR immunocompromised OR clinical signs of deeper infection (bullae, skin sloughing, organ dysfunction). Purulent (abscess/furuncle/carbuncle): mild = no SIRS; moderate = SIRS; severe = SIRS + failed I&D + oral abx OR immunocompromised
ssti_idsa.criterion_quote: The 2014 IDSA guidelines divided infections by purulent and non-purulent status, severity (mild, moderate, and severe), and tissue necrosis (necrotizing versus non-necrotizing). 'Cellulitis' and 'erysipelas' refer to diffuse, superficial, spreading skin infections. The term 'cellulitis' is not appropriate for cutaneous inflammation associated with collections of pus, such as in septic bursitis, furuncles, or skin abscesses.

source.title: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections: 2014 Update

dvt_wells.name: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) — Wells Clinical Prediction Rule
dvt_wells.criterion: Score components (+1 each unless noted): active cancer; paralysis/paresis/recent immobilization; bedridden >3d or major surgery <12 wk; localized tenderness along deep venous distribution; entire leg swelling; calf swelling >3 cm vs asymptomatic side; pitting edema (symptomatic leg); collateral superficial veins; previously documented DVT; alternative dx as likely or more likely than DVT (−2). Low ≤0; moderate 1–2; high ≥3

source.title: Value of assessment of pretest probability of deep-vein thrombosis in clinical management

upper_gi_bleed_gbs.name: Upper GI Bleed — Glasgow-Blatchford Score (risk stratification)
upper_gi_bleed_gbs.criterion: Score 0–23 based on: BUN, hemoglobin, systolic BP, pulse, melena, syncope, hepatic disease, heart failure. Score 0–1 = very low risk (outpatient candidate); higher score = need for intervention

source.title: A risk score to predict need for treatment for upper-gastrointestinal haemorrhage

upper_gi_bleed_gbs.notes: MDCalc implementation: https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/518/glasgow-blatchford-bleeding-score-gbs [AUDIT 2026-06: Lancet paywall and criterion_quote empty; Blatchford 2000 canonical.]

dic.name: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) — Overt (ISTH)
dic.criterion: Underlying disorder + ISTH score ≥5: Platelet count >100 = 0 pts, 50–100 = 1, <50 = 2; Fibrin-related marker (D-dimer) no increase = 0, moderate = 2, strong = 3; PT prolongation <3s = 0, 3–6s = 1, >6s = 2; Fibrinogen >1 g/L = 0, <1 g/L = 1
dic.criterion_quote: For overt DIC, a cumulative score of 5 or more from prolonged prothrombin time (PT), reduced platelets and fibrinogen, and elevated fibrin-related markers was proposed.

source.org: International Society on Thrombosis and Haemostasis (ISTH) SSC on DIC (Taylor et al.)
source.title: Towards definition, clinical and laboratory criteria, and a scoring system for disseminated intravascular coagulation

dic.notes: MDCalc implementation: https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/10203/isth-criteria-disseminated-intravascular-coagulation-dic [AUDIT 2026-06: PDF unreliable; ISTH 2001 canonical.]

anemia_in_ckd.name: Anemia in CKD
anemia_in_ckd.criterion: In adults and children ≥15y with CKD: Hb <13.0 g/dL (males) or <12.0 g/dL (females)
anemia_in_ckd.criterion_quote: KDIGO 2012 guideline recommends diagnosing anemia in adults and children ≥15 years with CKD when the hemoglobin (Hb) concentration is <13.0 g/dL (<130 g/L) in males and <12.0 g/dL (<120 g/L) in females.

source.title: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Anemia in Chronic Kidney Disease

ckd_bp_target.name: Hypertension in CKD (KDIGO target)
ckd_bp_target.criterion: Adults with high BP and CKD: target SBP <120 mmHg (using standardized office BP measurement), when tolerated
ckd_bp_target.criterion_quote: The KDIGO 2021 guideline recommends that adults with high blood pressure and CKD be treated with a target systolic blood pressure (SBP) of <120 mmHg, when tolerated, using standardized office BP measurement.

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Blood Pressure in CKD

ckd_mbd.name: CKD–Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD)
ckd_mbd.criterion: Systemic disorder in CKD G3a–G5D manifested by any combination of: (1) abnormalities of Ca, P, PTH, or vitamin D metabolism; (2) abnormalities in bone turnover/mineralization/volume; (3) vascular or other soft tissue calcification.

stages.treatment_targets_G5D: PTH approximately 2–9× upper normal limit; phosphate toward normal range; avoid hypercalcemia

source.title: KDIGO 2017 Clinical Practice Guideline Update for CKD-MBD

ckd.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
ckd.criterion: Abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for ≥3 months. Classified by Cause + GFR category (G1–G5) + Albuminuria category (A1–A3) (CGA).
ckd.criterion_quote: CKD is defined as abnormalities of kidney structure or function, present for a minimum of 3 months, with implications for health.

stages.G1: GFR ≥90 mL/min/1.73 m² (normal or high) — only CKD if other evidence of damage
stages.G2: GFR 60–89 (mildly decreased) — only CKD if other evidence of damage

source.title: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease

aki.name: Acute Kidney Injury
aki.criterion: Any of: SCr ≥0.3 mg/dL (≥26.5 μmol/L) increase within 48h OR SCr ≥1.5× baseline within the prior 7d OR urine volume <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6h
aki.criterion_quote: AKI is defined as any of the following: an increase in serum creatinine value of ≥0.3 mg/dL (26.5 micromol/L) in 48 hours, or an increase in serum creatinine of ≥1.5 times baseline within the prior 7 days.

stages.1: SCr 1.5–1.9× baseline OR ≥0.3 mg/dL increase; UOP <0.5 mL/kg/h × 6–12 h
stages.3: SCr 3.0× baseline OR SCr ≥4.0 mg/dL OR RRT initiated; UOP <0.3 mL/kg/h × ≥24 h OR anuria × ≥12 h

source.title: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury

aiha.name: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA) — warm vs cold
aiha.criterion: Hemolytic anemia (low Hb + raised LDH + raised indirect bilirubin + low haptoglobin + reticulocytosis) PLUS DAT positive. Warm AIHA (~70%): DAT IgG ± C3d, reactive at 37°C. Cold agglutinin disease (CAD): chronic hemolysis, polyspecific DAT positive, monospecific DAT strongly positive for C3d, cold agglutinin titer >64 at 4°C, no overt malignancy or relevant infection (primary CAD); cold agglutinin syndrome (CAS) is secondary to lymphoma, Mycoplasma, or EBV
aiha.criterion_quote: warm AIHA (DAT positive for IgG ± C3d) … Cold AIHA (CAD): Chronic hemolysis; Polyspecific DAT positive; Monospecific DAT strongly positive for C3d; CA titer > 64 at 4°C; No overt malignant disease or relevant infection

source.org: First International Consensus Group (Jäger et al.)
source.title: Diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune hemolytic anemia in adults

hemophilia.name: Hemophilia A & B — ISTH SSC severity classification
hemophilia.criterion: Based on baseline plasma factor VIII (hemophilia A) or factor IX (hemophilia B) activity: severe <1 IU/dL (<0.01 IU/mL); moderate 1–5 IU/dL (0.01–0.05); mild >5 to <40 IU/dL. Confirmed by reduced FVIII:C (A) or FIX:C (B) with normal VWF. X-linked recessive
hemophilia.criterion_quote: severe if < 1%, moderate if between 1 and 5% and mild if > 5 and < 40% of normal

source.title: Definitions in hemophilia: communication from the SSC of the ISTH

vwd.name: Von Willebrand Disease (VWD) — ASH/ISTH/NHF/WFH 2021
vwd.criterion: Type 1 VWD: VWF level <0.30 IU/mL regardless of bleeding history OR <0.50 IU/mL with abnormal bleeding (positive bleeding assessment tool). Type 2: qualitative defects (decreased ratio of activity to antigen). Type 3: near-absent VWF (<3 IU/dL)
vwd.criterion_quote: a VWF level of <0.30 IU/mL regardless of bleeding, and for patients with abnormal bleeding, a VWF level of <0.50 IU/mL to confirm the diagnosis of type 1 VWD

source.title: ASH ISTH NHF WFH 2021 guidelines on the diagnosis of von Willebrand disease

beta_thalassemia.name: Beta-Thalassemia Trait
beta_thalassemia.criterion: Microcytic hypochromic anemia (MCV <80 fL) with normal iron studies, PLUS hemoglobin electrophoresis/HPLC showing HbA2 >3.5% (often with mildly elevated HbF). Borderline HbA2 3.2–3.6% warrants further investigation. Coexistent iron deficiency can mask diagnosis by lowering HbA2 into normal range
beta_thalassemia.criterion_quote: Values between 3.6% to 7% are considered beta thalassemia carriers

source.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia

acute_chest_syndrome.name: Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS) in Sickle Cell Disease
acute_chest_syndrome.criterion: New pulmonary infiltrate involving ≥1 complete lung segment on imaging (consistent with alveolar consolidation, excluding atelectasis) in a patient with SCD, PLUS ≥1 of: chest pain, temperature >38.5°C, tachypnea, wheezing, or cough. Leading cause of mortality in SCD
acute_chest_syndrome.criterion_quote: the acute chest syndrome was defined on the basis of the finding of a new pulmonary infiltrate involving at least one complete lung segment that was consistent with the presence of alveolar consolidation, but excluding atelectasis. In addition, the patients had to have chest pain, a temperature of more than 38.5°C, tachypnea, wheezing, or cough

source.title: Causes and Outcomes of the Acute Chest Syndrome in Sickle Cell Disease (NEJM)

hereditary_spherocytosis.name: Hereditary Spherocytosis (HS) — BSH 2012
hereditary_spherocytosis.criterion: Hemolytic anemia + spherocytes on peripheral blood smear + family history OR positive screening test. BSH 2012 recommends eosin-5-maleimide (EMA) binding test and/or cryohemolysis test as first-line screening (replaces classical osmotic fragility). Confirmatory: SDS-PAGE of red cell membrane proteins (spectrin, ankyrin, band 3, or protein 4.2 deficiency)
hereditary_spherocytosis.criterion_quote: The eosin 5-maleimide (EMA) binding test and hypertonic cryohemolysis (HCH) test have been proposed as screening tests in recently published guidelines for the diagnosis of hereditary spherocytosis

source.title: Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Hereditary Spherocytosis — 2011 update

g6pd_deficiency.name: G6PD Deficiency — WHO 2022 variant classification
g6pd_deficiency.criterion: Class A: enzyme activity <20% of normal (chronic non-spherocytic hemolytic anemia). Class B: <45% of normal (acute hemolysis from medicines, fava beans, infection, or neonatal jaundice). Class C: >60% of normal (no hemolysis). Class U: uncertain clinical significance. Classification applies to genetic variants, not individuals
g6pd_deficiency.criterion_quote: Class A: < 20% activity with chronic haemolytic anaemia; Class B: < 45% activity associated with neonatal jaundice and acute haemolytic anaemia from medicines, fava beans, or infection; Class C: > 60% activity with no haemolysis

source.title: Technical consultation to review the classification of G6PD genetic variants

aplastic_anemia.name: Severe Aplastic Anemia — Camitta criteria
aplastic_anemia.criterion: Bone marrow cellularity <25% (or 25–50% with <30% residual hematopoietic cells) PLUS ≥2 of 3 peripheral blood criteria: ANC <0.5×10⁹/L; platelets <20×10⁹/L; reticulocytes <60×10⁹/L (or <1% corrected). Very severe AA: same plus ANC <0.2×10⁹/L
aplastic_anemia.criterion_quote: Severe aplastic anemia (SAA) is defined by bone marrow cellularity <25% (or 25%-50% with <30% residual hematopoietic cells), plus at least 2 of the following peripheral blood findings: neutrophils of <0.5 × 10⁹/L, platelets of <20 × 10⁹/L, and reticulocytes of <60 × 10⁹/L

source.org: Camitta criteria; ASH Blood Advances (DeZern & Churpek)
source.title: Approach to the diagnosis of aplastic anemia

mds_who_2022.name: Myelodysplastic Syndromes (MDS) — WHO 2022 / ICC 2022
mds_who_2022.criterion: Cytopenia thresholds: Hb <13.0 g/dL (M) or <12.0 g/dL (F); platelets <150×10⁹/L; ANC <1.5×10⁹/L. Cytopenia persistent ≥4 months (unless MDS-defining genetics). Morphologic dysplasia ≥10% in ≥1 lineage. MDS distinguished from AML by blast threshold <20% (WHO 2022). ICC 2022 reclassifies MDS with 10–19% blasts as MDS/AML. Defining genetic lesions (SF3B1, del(5q), TP53 multi-hit) can establish MDS subtypes regardless of dysplasia
mds_who_2022.criterion_quote: Both the ICC and WHO have defined the lower reference ranges for anemia, thrombocytopenia, and neutropenia as hemoglobin < 13.0 g/dL (male) and < 12.0 g/dL (female), platelets < 150 × 10⁹/L, and neutrophils < 1.5 × 10⁹/L, respectively

source.title: Diagnosis and classification of myelodysplastic syndromes (Blood)

fsgs.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) — KDIGO 2021
fsgs.criterion: Primary FSGS: LM shows FSGS lesions + EM shows DIFFUSE foot process effacement + clinical nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria >3.5 g/d + hypoalbuminemia <30 g/L), with no other identifiable cause. Secondary FSGS: FSGS lesion (± diffuse podocyte effacement) in setting of established pathophysiologic process (APOL1, HIV, HCV, obesity, mTOR/CNI, NSAIDs, SARS-CoV-2). Genetic FSGS: podocyte/GBM protein mutations. FSGS-UC: FSGS without genetic/secondary cause, without NS, without diffuse foot process effacement. Columbia variants (tip/cellular/perihilar/NOS/collapsing) have inconsistent predictive value per KDIGO
fsgs.criterion_quote: We define primary FSGS as a clinical–pathologic syndrome in which light microscopy of the kidney biopsy demonstrates FSGS lesions, electron microscopy of the kidney biopsy demonstrates diffuse foot process effacement, and clinically the patients display NS.

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases (Chapter 6)

mcd.name: Minimal Change Disease (MCD) — KDIGO 2021
mcd.criterion: Adult diagnosis requires kidney biopsy. Light microscopy: no glomerular lesions, or only minimal mesangial prominence. Immunofluorescence: negative or low-intensity staining for C3 and/or IgM. Electron microscopy: extensive foot process effacement WITHOUT electron-dense deposits. Clinically nephrotic syndrome
mcd.criterion_quote: Light microscopy shows no glomerular lesions, or only minimal mesangial prominence. Immunofluorescence microscopy is negative or shows low-intensity staining for C3 and/or IgM. Electron microscopy demonstrates extensive foot process effacement but no electron-dense deposits, and in the presence of unremarkable light and immunofluorescence, findings are diagnostic for MCD.

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline (Chapter 5)

membranous_nephropathy.name: Membranous Nephropathy (MN) — KDIGO 2021 / anti-PLA2R
membranous_nephropathy.criterion: Biopsy not required if patient has nephrotic syndrome AND positive anti-PLA2R antibody test (sens 78%, spec 99%). Anti-THSD7A insufficient to replace biopsy. Complete immunologic remission: PLA2R antibody <2 RU/mL by ELISA. Otherwise diagnosis by biopsy showing subepithelial immune deposits, granular IgG/C3 along GBM
membranous_nephropathy.criterion_quote: A kidney biopsy is not required to confirm the diagnosis of membranous nephropathy (MN) in patients with nephrotic syndrome and a positive anti-PLA2R antibody test. … a cutoff value of 2 RU/ml should be used to define complete immunologic remission.

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline (Chapter 3)

iga_nephropathy.name: IgA Nephropathy (IgAN) — KDIGO 2021 + Oxford MEST-C
iga_nephropathy.criterion: Diagnosis requires kidney biopsy. Determine MEST-C score per revised Oxford Classification: M (mesangial hypercellularity 0/1), E (endocapillary hypercellularity 0/1), S (segmental sclerosis 0/1), T (interstitial fibrosis/tubular atrophy 0/1/2 for ≤25%, 26–50%, >50%), C (crescents 0/1/2 for none, <25%, ≥25%). No validated serum or urine biomarkers. Assess for secondary causes (IgA vasculitis, cirrhosis, IBD, HIV)
iga_nephropathy.criterion_quote: IgAN can only be diagnosed with a kidney biopsy. Determine the MEST-C score (mesangial [M] and endocapillary [E] hypercellularity, segmental sclerosis [S], interstitial fibrosis/tubular atrophy [T], and crescents [C]) according to the revised Oxford Classification.

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline; Oxford MEST-C

c3_glomerulopathy.name: C3 Glomerulopathy (C3G) / MPGN pattern — KDIGO 2021
c3_glomerulopathy.criterion: MPGN is a pattern of injury, not a disease — evaluate for: (1) immune complex-mediated GN (ICGN) — evaluate for underlying disease; (2) C3 glomerulopathy — must first rule out infection-related GN or post-infectious GN; evaluate for monoclonal protein in patients ≥50 y at first diagnosis; (3) thrombotic microangiopathy. ICGN with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposits → evaluate for hematologic malignancy. If no etiology found for ICGN, evaluate for complement dysregulation
