c3_glomerulopathy.criterion_quote: Rule out infection-related GN or post-infectious GN prior to assigning the diagnosis of C3 glomerulopathy (C3G). Evaluate for the presence of a monoclonal protein in patients who present for the first time with a C3G diagnosis at ≥50 years of age.

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline (Chapter 8)

anti_gbm.name: Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture) — KDIGO 2021
anti_gbm.criterion: Suspected RPGN: urgent serologic testing for anti-GBM antibodies via ELISA. Up to 10% may be antibody-negative; if so, diagnosis by kidney biopsy showing LINEAR IgG deposition along the GBM. Caused by autoantibodies against noncollagenous (NC1) domain of α3 chain of type IV collagen. Treatment futility threshold (recommend AGAINST immunosuppression + plasmapheresis): all 3 of — dialysis-dependent at presentation, 100% crescents or >50% global glomerulosclerosis on adequate biopsy, AND no pulmonary hemorrhage
anti_gbm.criterion_quote: We recommend initiating immunosuppression with cyclophosphamide and glucocorticoids plus plasmapheresis in all patients with anti-GBM GN except those who are treated with dialysis at presentation, have 100% crescents or >50% global glomerulosclerosis in an adequate biopsy sample, and do not have pulmonary hemorrhage

source.title: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline (Chapter 11)

lupus_nephritis.name: Lupus Nephritis — ISN/RPS classification (2003/2018)
lupus_nephritis.criterion: Class I (minimal mesangial): normal LM with mesangial immune deposits on IF/EM. Class II (mesangial proliferative): mesangial hypercellularity (2018: ≥4 nuclei/mesangial area) with deposits limited to mesangium. Class III (focal): <50% glomeruli with endo/extracapillary GN; subclassified A (active), A/C, C (chronic). Class IV (diffuse): ≥50% glomeruli involved; 2018 retired S vs G subdivision. Class V (membranous): global/segmental continuous granular subepithelial deposits (>50% of tuft in >50% of glomeruli). Class VI (advanced sclerosing): ≥90% globally sclerosed glomeruli without residual activity. Lupus podocytopathy = Class I/II histology with NS
lupus_nephritis.criterion_quote: Minimal mesangial lupus nephritis with mesangial accumulation of immune complexes identified by immunofluorescence (or electron microscopy). … Diffuse lupus nephritis involving more than 50% of all glomeruli. … Advanced-stage lupus nephritis involving >90% global glomerulosclerosis.

source.title: The classification of glomerulonephritis in SLE revisited

als.name: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) — revised El Escorial / Awaji 2008
als.criterion: Requires evidence of UMN + LMN signs in defined CNS regions (bulbar, cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral). Clinically Definite ALS: UMN+LMN in bulbar + ≥2 spinal regions, OR UMN+LMN in 3 spinal regions. Clinically Probable: UMN+LMN in ≥2 regions with some UMN rostral to LMN. Clinically Probable-Laboratory-supported (El Escorial; retired by Awaji): UMN+LMN in 1 region (or UMN alone) + LMN by EMG in ≥2 limbs. Clinically Possible: UMN+LMN in 1 region only. Awaji 2008 modification: EMG fasciculation potentials + chronic neurogenic changes count as LMN evidence equivalent to clinical LMN signs (eliminates laboratory-supported probable category)
als.criterion_quote: Clinically Definite ALS is defined on clinical evidence alone by the presence of UMN, as well as LMN signs, in the bulbar region and at least two spinal regions or the presence of UMN and LMN signs in three spinal regions. … For the purpose of diagnosing ALS, neurophysiological evidence of chronic neurogenic change should be regarded as equivalent to clinical information on the detection of LMN dysfunction in a muscle.

source.title: El Escorial revisited (Brooks 2000); Awaji electrodiagnostic criteria (de Carvalho 2008)

caa_boston.name: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy — Boston Criteria v2.0 (2022)
caa_boston.criterion: Age ≥50 + appropriate clinical presentation (spontaneous ICH, transient focal neurological episodes, or cognitive impairment/dementia). Probable CAA: ≥2 strictly lobar hemorrhagic lesions (ICH, microbleeds, or cortical superficial siderosis/cSS), OR 1 strictly lobar hemorrhagic lesion + 1 white-matter feature (severe centrum semiovale enlarged perivascular spaces OR WMH in multispot pattern). Possible CAA: 1 strictly lobar hemorrhagic lesion OR 1 white-matter feature alone. Required for both: absence of any deep hemorrhagic lesions AND no other cause. Sens 64.5%, spec 95.0% for probable
caa_boston.criterion_quote: Probable CAA: clinical presentation and at least one of the following two on MRI: (1) at least two strictly lobar haemorrhagic lesions (intracerebral haemorrhage, cerebral microbleeds, or foci of cortical superficial siderosis or convexity subarachnoid haemorrhage); or (2) one strictly lobar haemorrhagic lesion and one white-matter feature (severe visible perivascular spaces in centrum semiovale or white-matter hyperintensities in a multispot pattern). Age ≥50 years. Absence of any deep haemorrhagic lesions and absence of other cause of haemorrhagic lesions.

source.title: The Boston criteria version 2.0 for cerebral amyloid angiopathy

cidp.name: Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP) — EFNS/PNS 2010
cidp.criterion: Typical CIDP: chronically progressive, stepwise, or recurrent SYMMETRIC proximal AND distal weakness AND sensory dysfunction of all extremities, developing over ≥2 months; cranial nerves may be affected; absent or reduced tendon reflexes in all extremities. Diagnosis requires clinical criteria + electrodiagnostic criteria for demyelination (prolonged distal latency, reduced conduction velocity, prolonged/absent F-waves, partial motor conduction block, abnormal temporal dispersion across ≥2 nerves). Categories: definite/probable/possible
cidp.criterion_quote: Chronically progressive, stepwise, or recurrent symmetric proximal and distal weakness and sensory dysfunction of all extremities, developing over at least 2 months; cranial nerves may be affected; and Absent or reduced tendon reflexes in all extremities.

source.title: EFNS/PNS Guideline on management of CIDP — First Revision

narcolepsy.name: Narcolepsy Type 1 & Type 2 — ICSD-3 (2014)
narcolepsy.criterion: Type 1 (BOTH required): (A) daily periods of irrepressible need to sleep or daytime lapses into sleep ≥3 months; (B) one or both of: (1) cataplexy AND mean sleep latency ≤8 min with ≥2 SOREMPs on MSLT (SOREMP within 15 min of sleep onset on preceding PSG may replace one MSLT SOREMP); (2) CSF hypocretin-1 ≤110 pg/mL or <1/3 of normal mean. Type 2 (ALL FIVE required): (A) same ≥3 mo hypersomnolence; (B) MSLT criteria as above; (C) cataplexy ABSENT; (D) CSF hypocretin not measured OR >110 pg/mL; (E) not better explained by another cause
narcolepsy.criterion_quote: Cataplexy and a mean sleep latency of ≤8 minutes and two or more sleep onset REM periods (SOREMPs) on a MSLT… CSF hypocretin-1 concentration, measured by immunoreactivity, is either ≤110 pg/mL or <1/3 of mean values obtained in normal subjects with the same standardized assay.

source.title: ICSD-3 — International Classification of Sleep Disorders, 3rd edition

alzheimer_clinical_2011.name: Alzheimer's Disease Dementia — NIA-AA 2011 Core Clinical Criteria
alzheimer_clinical_2011.criterion: Probable AD dementia: meets all-cause dementia criteria PLUS (1) insidious onset over months-years; (2) clear history of cognitive worsening; (3) initial and most prominent deficits in amnestic (impaired learning/recall + ≥1 other domain) OR nonamnestic (language, visuospatial, or executive predominant + ≥1 other domain) presentation. NOT applied when substantial concomitant cerebrovascular disease, core DLB features, prominent bvFTD/PPA features, or other concurrent active neurologic/medical condition affecting cognition. Possible AD: meets clinical AD criteria but atypical course OR mixed etiology
alzheimer_clinical_2011.criterion_quote: Probable AD dementia is diagnosed when the patient (1) Meets criteria for dementia… and in addition, has the following characteristics: A. Insidious onset. Symptoms have a gradual onset over months to years, not sudden over hours or days; B. Clear-cut history of worsening of cognition by report or observation; and C. The initial and most prominent cognitive deficits are evident on history and examination in one of the following categories: amnestic presentation… [or] nonamnestic presentations.

source.title: The diagnosis of dementia due to Alzheimer's disease: Recommendations from the NIA-AA workgroups

transient_global_amnesia.name: Transient Global Amnesia — Hodges & Warlow criteria (1990)
transient_global_amnesia.criterion: All 7 required: (1) attacks witnessed and information from a capable observer; (2) clear-cut anterograde amnesia during attack; (3) absence of clouding of consciousness and loss of personal identity; cognitive impairment limited to amnesia; (4) no focal neurologic symptoms during attack and no significant neurologic signs afterward; (5) no epileptic features; (6) attacks resolve within 24 hours; (7) exclude recent head injury or active epilepsy. DWI MRI 24–72h post-event may show punctate hippocampal CA1 hyperintensity (supportive)
transient_global_amnesia.criterion_quote: (1) Attacks must be witnessed and information available from a capable observer who was present for most of the attack. (2) There must be clear-cut anterograde amnesia during the attack. (3) Clouding of consciousness and loss of personal identity must be absent, and the cognitive impairment limited to amnesia. (4) There should be no accompanying focal neurological symptoms during the attack and no significant neurological signs afterwards. (5) Epileptic features must be absent. (6) Attacks must resolve within 24 hours. (7) Patients with recent head injury or active epilepsy are excluded.

source.title: Syndromes of transient amnesia: towards a classification

transient_global_amnesia.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PMC text references criteria but didn't display verbatim; Hodges-Warlow 1990 standard.]

gustilo_anderson.name: Open Fracture — Gustilo-Anderson classification
gustilo_anderson.criterion: Type I: wound <1 cm long and clean. Type II: laceration >1 cm long without extensive soft tissue damage, flaps, or avulsions. Type III: open segmental fracture, extensive soft tissue damage, or traumatic amputation. Type IIIA: adequate soft tissue coverage despite extensive laceration/flaps, or high-energy trauma regardless of wound size. Type IIIB: extensive soft tissue injury with periosteal stripping and bone exposure (usually massive contamination). Type IIIC: open fracture with arterial injury requiring repair
gustilo_anderson.criterion_quote: Type I: an open fracture with a wound less than 1 cm long and clean. Type II: an open fracture with a laceration greater than 1 cm long without extensive soft tissue damage, flaps, or avulsions. Type IIIA: open fractures with adequate soft tissue coverage of a fractured bone despite extensive soft tissue laceration or flaps. Type IIIB: open fractures with extensive soft tissue injury loss with periosteal stripping and bone exposure. Type IIIC: open fractures associated with arterial injury requiring repair.

source.title: Classifications in Brief: Gustilo-Anderson Classification of Open Fractures

arco_onfh.name: Osteonecrosis of Femoral Head — ARCO 2019
arco_onfh.criterion: Stage I: X-ray normal; MRI low-signal band on T1. Stage II: X-ray abnormal (osteosclerosis, focal osteoporosis, or cystic change in head); MRI abnormal; no subchondral fracture or flattening. Stage III: subchondral fracture on X-ray or CT; IIIA early (head depression ≤2 mm); IIIB late (depression >2 mm). Stage IV: X-ray evidence of osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing, acetabular changes, and/or joint destruction
arco_onfh.criterion_quote: Stage I — X-ray: normal; MRI: low-signal band on T1-weighted MRI. Stage II — X-ray: abnormal; MRI: abnormal; no evidence of subchondral fracture. Stage III — Subchondral fracture on X-ray or CT; IIIA (early): Femoral head depression ≤ 2 mm; IIIB (late): Femoral head depression > 2 mm. Stage IV — X-ray evidence of osteoarthritis.

source.org: Yoon BH, Mont MA, Koo KH et al. (ARCO)
source.title: The 2019 Revised Version of ARCO Staging System of ONFH

pji_msis_2018.name: Periprosthetic Joint Infection — 2018 MSIS scoring
pji_msis_2018.criterion: Major criteria (one alone confirms PJI): 2 positive cultures of the same organism OR sinus tract with evidence of communication to the joint or visualization of the prosthesis. Minor criteria (preoperative): elevated serum CRP or D-dimer (+2); elevated ESR (+1); elevated synovial WBC or leukocyte esterase (+3); positive synovial alpha-defensin (+3); elevated synovial PMN% (+2); elevated synovial CRP (+1). Score ≥6 = infected; 4–5 = inconclusive; ≤3 = not infected
pji_msis_2018.criterion_quote: Two positive cultures of the same organism; OR Sinus tract with evidence of communication to the joint or visualization of the prosthesis. The diagnosis of PJI can be made if one major criterion is met. … Score ≥6 = Infected; 4-5 = Inconclusive; ≤3 = Not infected.

source.title: The 2018 Definition of Periprosthetic Hip and Knee Infection

garden_femoral_neck.name: Femoral Neck Fracture — Garden classification
garden_femoral_neck.criterion: Type I: incomplete and valgus impacted. Type II: complete and nondisplaced. Type III: complete and partially displaced. Type IV: complete and fully displaced. Types I-II treated with internal fixation; Types III-IV typically require arthroplasty in elderly
garden_femoral_neck.criterion_quote: Type I: Incomplete and valgus impacted. Type II: Complete and nondisplaced. Type III: Complete and partially displaced. Type IV: Complete and fully displaced.

source.title: Classifications in Brief: Garden Classification of Femoral Neck Fractures

acg_acute_diarrhea.name: Acute Infectious Diarrhea — ACG 2016 testing thresholds
acg_acute_diarrhea.criterion: Stool diagnostic studies indicated for dysentery (bloody/mucoid diarrhea ± fever), moderate-to-severe disease, or symptoms lasting >7 days. Fecal leukocytes and stool lactoferrin are imprecise/probably unnecessary to guide further workup. FDA-approved culture-independent multiplex PCR panels preferred over older fecal-marker workflows
acg_acute_diarrhea.criterion_quote: Stool diagnostic studies may be used if available in cases of dysentery, moderate-to-severe disease, and symptoms lasting >7 days to clarify the etiology of the patient's illness and enable specific directed therapy.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Prevention of Acute Diarrheal Infections in Adults

cdi_acg_severity.name: C. difficile Severity — ACG 2021
cdi_acg_severity.criterion: Test only symptomatic patients with ≥3 unformed stools in 24 h. Severe CDI: WBC ≥15,000 cells/mm³ OR serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL. Fulminant CDI: severe CDI PLUS hypotension/shock OR ileus OR megacolon. Two-step testing: sensitive NAAT/GDH first → specific toxin EIA. Both positive = CDI; both negative = unlikely; discordant = clinical evaluation
cdi_acg_severity.criterion_quote: We recommend the following criteria, which are predictive of unfavorable outcomes, be used to classify severe CDI at the time of diagnosis: white blood cell ≥15,000 cells/mm3 or serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL. … We recommend defining fulminant infection as patients meeting criteria for severe CDI plus presence of hypotension or shock or ileus or megacolon

source.title: ACG Clinical Guidelines: Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of CDI

harvey_bradshaw_index.name: Crohn's Disease Activity — Harvey-Bradshaw Index (HBI)
harvey_bradshaw_index.criterion: Sum of 5 items: (1) general well-being (0=very well to 4=terrible); (2) abdominal pain (0=none to 3=severe); (3) number of liquid stools/day (count); (4) abdominal mass (0=none, 1=dubious, 2=definite, 3=definite+tender); (5) complications (1 point each: arthralgia, uveitis, erythema nodosum, aphthous ulcers, pyoderma gangrenosum, anal fissure, new fistula, abscess). Interpretation: <5 remission; 5–7 mild; 8–16 moderate; >16 severe. Correlates with CDAI r≈0.93
harvey_bradshaw_index.criterion_quote: General Well-Being: 0 = very well, 1 = slightly below par, 2 = poor, 3 = very poor, 4 = terrible. Abdominal Pain: 0 = none, 1 = mild, 2 = moderate, 3 = severe. Abdominal Mass: 0 = none, 1 = dubious, 2 = definite, 3 = definite and tender. Complications (one point each): Arthralgia, Uveitis, Erythema Nodosum, Aphthous Ulcers, Pyoderma Gangrenosum, Anal Fissure, New Fistula, Abscess. Remission <5, Mild disease 5-7, Moderate disease 8-16, Severe disease >16.

source.title: A simple index of Crohn's-disease activity (Lancet)

harvey_bradshaw_index.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: PubMed has no abstract; HBI components canonical 1980 Lancet definitions.]

dyssynergic_defecation.name: Dyssynergic Defecation — Rome IV / ACG 2021
dyssynergic_defecation.criterion: Meet criteria for functional constipation and/or IBS-C, PLUS impaired evacuation demonstrated by ≥2 of 3 tests: (1) abnormal balloon expulsion test (BET — upper limit normal 1 min with 50 mL warm-water party/commercial balloon seated; 2 min with Foley catheter balloon); (2) abnormal anorectal evacuation pattern with manometry or anal-surface EMG; (3) impaired rectal evacuation by imaging (defecography). Symptoms ≥3 months with onset ≥6 months prior. Less than 20% reduction in anal pressure during evacuation correlates with DD
dyssynergic_defecation.criterion_quote: Dyssynergic defecation has been formally defined by the Rome IV diagnostic criteria and includes at least 2 of 3 of the following: (1) abnormal balloon expulsion test (BET), (2) abnormal anorectal evacuation pattern with manometry or anal surface electromyography, and (3) impaired rectal evacuation by imaging.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guidelines: Management of Benign Anorectal Disorders

adhd_adult.name: Adult ADHD — DSM-5/5-TR
adhd_adult.criterion: Adults (≥17 y): ≥5 of 9 inattention symptoms AND/OR ≥5 of 9 hyperactivity-impulsivity symptoms. Several symptoms must be present in >1 setting; onset before age 12; clinically significant impairment in social, academic, or occupational functioning; not better explained by another mental disorder; not exclusively during psychosis. Subtypes: predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive-impulsive, combined
adhd_adult.criterion_quote: DSM-5 defines ADHD in adults as the presence of at least five of nine symptoms of inattention and/or five of nine symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity. ADHD symptoms must cause clinical impairment, several of them must be present in more than one setting, and their age at onset should be before age 12 years.

source.title: DSM-5/5-TR (via PMC)

delirium_dsm5.name: Delirium — DSM-5/5-TR
delirium_dsm5.criterion: A: disturbance in attention and awareness developing acutely (hours to days) and fluctuating. B: ≥1 additional cognitive disturbance (memory, orientation, language, visuospatial, perception). C: not better explained by pre-existing/established/evolving NCD. D: not in context of severely reduced arousal/coma. E: evidence of underlying medical condition, substance intoxication/withdrawal, or toxin exposure
delirium_dsm5.criterion_quote: Criterion A: A disturbance in attention and awareness that develops acutely and fluctuates in severity must be present. Criterion B: At least one additional cognitive disturbance must be present. Criterion C: The disturbances are not better explained by preexisting dementia. Criterion D: The disturbances do not occur in the context of severely reduced arousal or coma. Criterion E: There is evidence that the disturbances are caused by one or more underlying medical conditions, substance intoxication, withdrawal, or exposure to a toxin.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

ncd_major_mild.name: Major and Mild Neurocognitive Disorders — DSM-5/5-TR
ncd_major_mild.criterion: MAJOR NCD: significant cognitive decline in ≥1 of 6 domains (complex attention, executive function, learning/memory, language, perceptual-motor, social cognition); INTERFERES with independence in everyday activities; not exclusively during delirium; not better explained by another mental disorder. MILD NCD: same 6 domains but MODEST decline; does NOT interfere with independence (compensatory strategies may be required). DSM-5-TR replaced 'Dementia' with Major NCD
ncd_major_mild.criterion_quote: Major neurocognitive disorder is characterized by significant cognitive decline and the development of dependence, while the mild neurocognitive disorder is characterized by moderate cognitive decline, and the patient is still independent.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

acute_stress_disorder.name: Acute Stress Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
acute_stress_disorder.criterion: A: exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence (directly, witnessed, learned of in close family/friend, or repeated extreme exposure). B: ≥9 symptoms across 5 clusters (intrusion, negative mood, dissociative, avoidance, arousal). C: duration of symptoms 3 days to 1 month after exposure. D: clinically significant distress or impairment. E: not attributable to substance/medical condition or brief psychotic disorder
acute_stress_disorder.criterion_quote: A: Being exposed to a traumatic event (either physically, sexually, or mentally). B: Having more than eight of the following symptoms (clustered in five categories). C, D, and E: Duration of the symptoms should be between three days and four weeks and cause significant functional impairment and not related to substance use or other medical conditions.

source.title: DSM-5 (via StatPearls)

adjustment_disorder.name: Adjustment Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
adjustment_disorder.criterion: A: emotional/behavioral symptoms in response to identifiable stressor within 3 months of onset. B: clinically significant — marked distress out of proportion to stressor severity AND/OR significant impairment in social/occupational functioning. C: does not meet criteria for another mental disorder and not merely exacerbation of preexisting disorder. D: not normal bereavement. E: once stressor terminates, symptoms do not persist >6 months. Specifiers: with depressed mood, with anxiety, with mixed anxiety + depressed mood, with disturbance of conduct, with mixed
adjustment_disorder.criterion_quote: Criterion A: The development of emotional or behavioral symptoms in response to an identifiable stressor(s) occurring within 3 months of the onset of the stressor(s). Criterion B: These symptoms or behaviors are clinically significant. … Criterion E: Once the stressor (or its consequences) has terminated, the symptoms do not persist for more than an additional 6 months.

source.title: DSM-5 (via NCBI Bookshelf)

antisocial_pd.name: Antisocial Personality Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
antisocial_pd.criterion: Criterion A: pervasive pattern of disregard for and violation of the rights of others since age 15, indicated by ≥3 of 7: (1) failure to conform to social norms (acts grounds for arrest); (2) deceitfulness, repeated lying, use of aliases, conning for personal profit; (3) impulsivity or failure to plan; (4) irritability and aggressiveness (physical fights/assaults); (5) reckless disregard for safety of self or others; (6) consistent irresponsibility; (7) lack of remorse. Additional: age ≥18; evidence of conduct disorder before age 15; not exclusively during schizophrenia/bipolar episodes
antisocial_pd.criterion_quote: The presence of a pervasive pattern of disregard for and violation of the rights of others. This behavior begins by age 15 and is present in various contexts. (1) Failure to conform to social norms concerning lawful behaviors. (2) Deceitfulness, repeated lying. (3) Impulsivity or failure to plan. (4) Irritability and aggressiveness. (5) Reckless disregard for the safety of self or others. (6) Consistent irresponsibility. (7) Lack of remorse.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

did.name: Dissociative Identity Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
did.criterion: ≥2 distinct personality states or identities (involving marked discontinuity in sense of self/agency); recurrent gaps in recall of everyday events, important personal information, and/or traumatic events inconsistent with ordinary forgetting; clinically significant distress/impairment; not part of broadly accepted cultural/religious practice; not attributable to substance/medical condition
did.criterion_quote: The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM-5) criteria for DID include at least two or more distinct personalities. Each personality varies in behavior, sense of consciousness, memory, and perception of the outside world. Persons with DID experience amnesia, distinct gaps in memory, and recollections of daily and traumatic events. They cannot be directly related to substance use or part of cultural norms or practices.

source.title: DSM-5 (via StatPearls)

stimulant_use_disorder.name: Stimulant Use Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
stimulant_use_disorder.criterion: Same 11-criterion SUD framework as alcohol/opioid: ≥2 of 11 within 12-month period across 4 domains (impaired control, social impairment, risky use, pharmacological tolerance/withdrawal). Severity: mild 2–3, moderate 4–5, severe ≥6
stimulant_use_disorder.criterion_quote: SUDs are classified as mild, moderate, or severe based on how many of the 11 criteria are fulfilled: mild, any 2 or 3 criteria; moderate, any 4 or 5 criteria; severe, any 6 or more criteria.

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via SAMHSA TIP)

cannabis_use_disorder.name: Cannabis Use Disorder — DSM-5/5-TR
cannabis_use_disorder.criterion: Same 11-criterion SUD framework: ≥2 of 11 within 12 months across 4 domains (impaired control, social/occupational dysfunction, risky use, tolerance/withdrawal). Severity: mild 2–3, moderate 4–5, severe ≥6
cannabis_use_disorder.criterion_quote: Diagnosis requires at least 2 of 11 criteria within 12 months across 4 domains—impaired control, social or occupational dysfunction, risky use, and tolerance or withdrawal. The severity of CUD is classified as mild (2–3 criteria), moderate (4–5), or severe (≥6).

source.title: DSM-5-TR (via StatPearls)

vte_duration_chest_2021.name: VTE Anticoagulation Duration — CHEST 2021
vte_duration_chest_2021.criterion: Treatment phase: 3 months for all acute VTE without contraindication. Major transient risk factor (surgery with GA >30 min, hospitalization ≥3 days, cesarean, major trauma within 3 mo before VTE) → recommend AGAINST extended phase. Minor transient risk factor (GA <30 min, hospitalization <3 days, estrogen, pregnancy/puerperium, leg injury ≥3 d, travel) → suggest AGAINST extended phase. Unprovoked VTE or persistent risk factor (active cancer, APS) → recommend offering extended-phase anticoagulation with DOAC (or VKA if cannot receive DOAC). Extended phase: reduced-dose apixaban 2.5 mg BID or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily preferred over full dose; DOAC strongly preferred over aspirin. For unprovoked proximal DVT/PE stopping anticoagulation: aspirin over no aspirin
vte_duration_chest_2021.criterion_quote: In patients with acute VTE who do not have a contraindication we recommend a 3-month treatment phase of anticoagulation (strong recommendation, moderate-certainty evidence). … In patients with VTE diagnosed in the absence of transient provocation (unprovoked VTE or provoked by persistent risk factor), we recommend offering extended-phase anticoagulation with a DOAC (strong recommendation, moderate-certainty evidence).

source.title: Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: Second Update of CHEST Guideline 2021

vte_treatment_chest_2021.name: Acute VTE Anticoagulant Choice — CHEST 2021
vte_treatment_chest_2021.criterion: Non-cancer non-APS VTE: DOAC (apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban, rivaroxaban) over VKA for treatment-phase therapy. Cancer-associated thrombosis: oral Xa inhibitor (apixaban, edoxaban, rivaroxaban) over LMWH for initial and treatment phases (use apixaban or LMWH preferentially with luminal GI malignancies). Antiphospholipid syndrome: adjusted-dose VKA (target INR 2.5) over DOAC. Isolated distal DVT without severe symptoms or risk factors for extension: serial imaging 2 weeks over anticoagulation. Low-risk PE: outpatient over hospitalization. Recommend AGAINST IVC filter in addition to anticoagulants
vte_treatment_chest_2021.criterion_quote: In patients with VTE (DVT of the leg or PE) we recommend apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban, or rivaroxaban over vitamin K antagonist (VKA) as treatment-phase (first 3 months) anticoagulant therapy (strong recommendation, moderate-certainty evidence).

source.title: Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: Second Update of CHEST Guideline 2021

herdoo2.name: HERDOO2 — Discontinuing Anticoagulation in Unprovoked VTE (Women)
herdoo2.criterion: Score 1 point each: Hyperpigmentation, edema, or redness of either leg; D-dimer ≥250 µg/L FEU while on anticoagulation; Obesity BMI ≥30 kg/m²; Older age ≥65. Women with score 0–1 are low risk and may safely discontinue anticoagulation. Women with score ≥2 and all men should continue anticoagulation. Applies to women ≥18 with first unprovoked proximal DVT or PE who completed 5–12 months of anticoagulation
herdoo2.criterion_quote: Women with a score of 0 or 1 are low risk and may safely discontinue anticoagulation; women with score ≥2 and all men should continue anticoagulation.

source.title: HERDOO2 Rule for Discontinuing Anticoagulation in Unprovoked VTE

dash_score.name: DASH Score — VTE Recurrence Risk
dash_score.criterion: Components: D-dimer abnormal post-anticoagulation (~1 mo after stopping) = +2; Age ≤50 y = +1; male Sex = +1; Hormonal therapy at time of VTE (women) = −2. Annualized recurrence risk: 3.1% for ≤1; 6.4% for 2; 12.3% for ≥3. Score ≤1 = low risk, consider stopping anticoagulation
dash_score.criterion_quote: DASH score: D-dimer abnormal = +2; Age ≤50 years = +1; Male Sex = +1; Hormonal therapy at time of VTE (women) = −2. Annualized recurrence risk: 3.1% for score ≤1, 6.4% for score = 2, 12.3% for score ≥3.

source.title: DASH Prediction Score for Recurrent VTE

food_allergy_niaid.name: IgE-Mediated Food Allergy — NIAID 2010
food_allergy_niaid.criterion: Diagnosis based on history + physical exam + supportive testing. Skin prick test (SPT wheal ≥3 mm greater than negative control) and/or serum food-specific IgE ≥0.35 kUA/L identify sensitization but are insufficient alone. Oral food challenge (double-blind, placebo-controlled — DBPCFC) is the gold standard. Food-specific 95% PPV sIgE cutoffs (selected populations): egg ≥7 kUA/L, milk ≥15 kUA/L, peanut ≥14 kUA/L
food_allergy_niaid.criterion_quote: SPTs, sIgE tests, and atopy patch tests cannot be considered diagnostic of FA. The DBPCFC is the gold standard for diagnosing FA.

source.title: Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Food Allergy in the United States

fpies.name: Acute Food Protein-Induced Enterocolitis Syndrome (FPIES)
fpies.criterion: Major criterion: vomiting in the 1–4 hr period after ingestion of the suspect food, without classic IgE-mediated skin or respiratory symptoms. PLUS ≥3 minor criteria: second episode of repetitive vomiting to same food; repetitive vomiting 1–4 hr after a different food; extreme lethargy; marked pallor; ED visit; IV fluid support; diarrhea in 24 hr (usually 5–10 hr); hypotension; hypothermia. Non-IgE mediated
fpies.criterion_quote: Acute FPIES diagnosis may be established in a patient who meets the following major criterion and ≥3 minor criteria. Major criterion: Vomiting in the 1–4 hr period after ingestion of the suspect food and absence of classic IgE-mediated allergic skin or respiratory symptoms.

source.org: AAAAI Adverse Reactions to Foods Committee
source.title: International Consensus Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of FPIES

chronic_urticaria.name: Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria (CSU) — EAACI/GA²LEN 2022
chronic_urticaria.criterion: Spontaneous appearance of wheals, angioedema, or both for >6 weeks due to known or unknown causes. Activity by Urticaria Activity Score over 7 days (UAS7): daily sum of wheal score (0–3) + pruritus score (0–3); weekly total 0–42. UAS7 <7 well-controlled; 7–15 mild; 16–27 moderate; 28–42 severe
chronic_urticaria.criterion_quote: Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) is defined as the spontaneous appearance of wheals, angioedema, or both for >6 weeks due to known or unknown causes.

source.title: International EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI guideline for urticaria

hae_c1inh.name: Hereditary Angioedema with C1-INH Deficiency (HAE-1/2)
hae_c1inh.criterion: Recurrent angioedema without urticaria + lab evidence of C1-INH deficiency. HAE Type 1: low C1-INH antigen + low C1-INH function + low C4 (all typically ≤50% normal). HAE Type 2: normal/elevated C1-INH antigen but low C1-INH function + low C4. C1q is normal in HAE (low in acquired angioedema). Confirm abnormal results on separate sample
hae_c1inh.criterion_quote: Measurements of serum/plasma levels of C1-INH function, C1-INH protein, and C4 are used to diagnose HAE-1/2. With the combined use of these three tests, the diagnostic accuracy for identifying HAE-1/2 is very high, higher than with the use of any of the three alone.

source.title: International WAO/EAACI guideline for the management of HAE — 2021 revision

systemic_mastocytosis.name: Systemic Mastocytosis — WHO 2022
systemic_mastocytosis.criterion: Diagnosis requires ONE major + ONE minor criterion, OR ≥3 minor criteria. Major: multifocal dense mast-cell infiltrates (≥15 MCs in aggregates) in bone marrow or other extracutaneous organ. Minor: (1) ≥25% of MCs are atypical (type I/II) on BM smears or spindle-shaped; (2) KIT-activating point mutation at codon 816 (D816V); (3) MC expression of CD2 and/or CD25 and/or CD30; (4) baseline serum tryptase >20 ng/mL (adjusted for hereditary alpha-tryptasemia)
systemic_mastocytosis.criterion_quote: Major criterion: Multifocal dense infiltrates of mast cells (≥15 mast cells in aggregates) in bone marrow biopsies and/or in sections of other extracutaneous organ(s). Minor criteria: ≥25% of all mast cells are atypical cells; KIT-activating KIT point mutation(s) at codon 816; Mast cells express CD2 and/or CD25 and/or CD30; Baseline serum tryptase concentration >20 ng/mL.

source.title: WHO 2022 Classification of Mastocytosis

allergic_rhinitis_aria.name: Allergic Rhinitis — ARIA classification
allergic_rhinitis_aria.criterion: Symptomatic IgE-mediated nasal inflammation. DURATION: Intermittent = symptoms <4 days/week OR <4 consecutive weeks; Persistent = symptoms ≥4 days/week AND ≥4 consecutive weeks. SEVERITY: Mild = none of [sleep disturbance, impairment of daily/leisure/sport activities, school/work impairment, troublesome symptoms]; Moderate-Severe = ≥1 present
allergic_rhinitis_aria.criterion_quote: Intermittent means that the symptoms are present less than 4 days a week or for less than 4 weeks. Persistent means that the symptoms are present more than 4 days a week and for more than 4 weeks. Mild means that none of the following items are present: sleep disturbance; impairment of daily activities, leisure, and/or sport; impairment of school or work; troublesome symptoms. Moderate-severe means that one or more of these items are present.

source.title: Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma (ARIA) Guidelines

psoriasis_pasi.name: Psoriasis — PASI scoring and severity
psoriasis_pasi.criterion: PASI severity sub-scores per region (head, upper limbs, trunk, lower limbs): erythema (E), induration (I), desquamation (D) each scored 0–4 (0 none, 1 slight, 2 moderate, 3 severe, 4 very severe). Area involvement 0–6 (0 none, 1 = 1–9%, 2 = 10–29%, 3 = 30–49%, 4 = 50–69%, 5 = 70–89%, 6 = 90–100%). Regional weighting: head 0.1, upper extremities 0.2, trunk 0.3, lower extremities 0.4. Rule of Tens: mild = BSA ≤10 AND PASI ≤10 AND DLQI ≤10; moderate-severe = BSA >10 OR PASI >10, AND DLQI >10
psoriasis_pasi.criterion_quote: Mild disease is defined as BSA ≤10 and PASI ≤10 and DLQI ≤10, while moderate to severe psoriasis is defined as (BSA > 10 or PASI > 10) and DLQI > 10.

source.title: Definition of treatment goals for moderate to severe psoriasis: a European consensus

atopic_dermatitis.name: Atopic Dermatitis — Hanifin-Rajka & UK Working Party
atopic_dermatitis.criterion: Hanifin-Rajka 1980: ≥3 major criteria — pruritus; typical morphology/distribution (flexural in adults, face/extensors in infants); chronic or relapsing dermatitis; personal/family atopy — PLUS ≥3 minor criteria. UK Working Party (Williams 1994): itchy skin condition PLUS ≥3 of — history of flexural involvement; asthma/hay fever history; generalized dry skin history; onset under age 2; visible flexural dermatitis
atopic_dermatitis.criterion_quote: In order to qualify as a case of atopic dermatitis, an individual must have an itchy skin condition plus three or more of the following: history of flexural involvement, a history of asthma/hay fever, a history of a generalized dry skin, onset of rash under the age of 2 years, or visible flexural dermatitis.

source.title: Diagnostic features of atopic dermatitis; UK Working Party diagnostic criteria for AD

melanoma_abcde.name: Melanoma Screening — AAD ABCDE
melanoma_abcde.criterion: A — Asymmetry: one half unlike the other. B — Border: irregular, scalloped, or poorly defined edge. C — Color: varying colors from one area to next (tan, brown, black, white, red, blue). D — Diameter: usually >6 mm (pencil eraser) at diagnosis but can be smaller. E — Evolving: changing in size, shape, or color, or different from rest. Dermoscopy adjunct: Argenziano 7-point checklist (atypical pigment network, blue-whitish veil, atypical vascular pattern, irregular streaks, irregular pigmentation, irregular dots/globules, regression structures)
melanoma_abcde.criterion_quote: One half of the spot is unlike the other half. … The spot has an irregular, scalloped, or poorly defined border. … The spot has varying colors. … While melanomas are usually greater than 6 millimeters … when diagnosed, they can be smaller. … The spot looks different from the rest or is changing in size, shape, or color.

source.title: ABCDEs of Melanoma

sjs_ten_bastuji_garin.name: SJS / TEN — Bastuji-Garin Classification + ALDEN
sjs_ten_bastuji_garin.criterion: SJS: detachment <10% BSA + widespread erythematous/purpuric macules or flat atypical targets. SJS-TEN overlap: detachment 10–30% BSA + widespread purpuric macules or flat atypical targets. TEN with spots: detachment >30% BSA + widespread purpuric macules or flat atypical targets. ALDEN (drug causality): 6 criteria — latency, drug present on index day, pre-exposure/re-exposure, drug notoriety, alternative cause; score <0 very unlikely; 0–1 unlikely; 2–3 possible; 4–5 probable; ≥6 very probable
sjs_ten_bastuji_garin.criterion_quote: Stevens-Johnson syndrome (detachment below 10% of the body surface area). … overlap Stevens-Johnson syndrome-toxic epidermal necrolysis (detachment between 10% and 30%). … toxic epidermal necrolysis with spots (detachment above 30%).

source.title: Clinical classification of cases of TEN, SJS, and erythema multiforme; ALDEN algorithm

dress_regiscar.name: DRESS Syndrome — RegiSCAR scoring
dress_regiscar.criterion: Suspected cases classified by RegiSCAR cumulative score (-4 to +9) based on: fever ≥38.5°C, lymphadenopathy ≥2 sites, eosinophilia (>0.7 or 10–19% +1; >1.5×10⁹/L or ≥20% +2), atypical lymphocytes, skin involvement (extent ≥50% +1; ≥2 of edema/infiltration/purpura/scaling +1; biopsy suggesting DRESS +1), internal organ involvement (1 organ +1; ≥2 +2), resolution ≥15 days, exclusion of alternatives (≥3 lab tests negative for alternatives +1). Classification: <2 = no DRESS; 2–3 = possible; 4–5 = probable; ≥6 = definite
dress_regiscar.criterion_quote: The RegiSCAR system classifies suspected cases as definite (score 6 and above), probable (score 4 and 5), possible (score 2 and 3), and no DRESS (score <2).

source.title: RegiSCAR DRESS scoring system

rosacea_rosco.name: Rosacea — ROSCO 2017 phenotype
rosacea_rosco.criterion: Either feature alone is independently diagnostic: (i) persistent centrofacial erythema associated with periodic intensification; (ii) phymatous changes. Major features (≥2 supports diagnosis if neither diagnostic feature present): flushing/transient erythema, nontransient erythema, telangiectasia, inflammatory papules/pustules, ocular manifestations, phymatous changes
rosacea_rosco.criterion_quote: The following two features were independently considered diagnostic for rosacea: (i) persistent, centrofacial erythema associated with periodic intensification; and (ii) phymatous changes.

source.title: Updating the diagnosis, classification and assessment of rosacea: recommendations from the global ROSCO panel

rosacea_rosco.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Wiley paywall; ROSCO 2017 features widely cited verbatim.]

bullous_pemphigoid.name: Bullous Pemphigoid (BP) — diagnostic confirmation
bullous_pemphigoid.criterion: Confirmation: positive direct immunofluorescence (DIF) showing LINEAR C3, IgG, or both along the basement membrane zone (BMZ); PLUS serologic evidence of circulating IgG autoantibodies binding the EPIDERMAL side of salt-split skin on indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) OR reactivity against BP antigens BP230 (BPAg1) or BP180 (BPAg2) on ELISA or IIF. Tense bullae on erythematous or normal-appearing skin, often pruritic, elderly
bullous_pemphigoid.criterion_quote: Confirmation requires positive direct immunofluorescence (DIF) demonstrating linear complement component 3 (C3), immunoglobulin G (IgG), or both along the basement membrane zone (BMZ), along with serologic evidence of circulating IgG autoantibodies binding the epidermal side of salt-split skin (SSS) on indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) or reactivity against BP antigens (BPAgs) BP230 (BPAg1) or BP180 (BPAg2) on enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) or IIF.

source.title: Bullous Pemphigoid Workup

pemphigus_vulgaris.name: Pemphigus Vulgaris (PV)
pemphigus_vulgaris.criterion: Diagnosis by typical clinical features (flaccid mucocutaneous bullae, erosions) + intercellular deposition of IgG and/or C3 in epithelium by DIF (chicken-wire pattern) + detection of circulating autoantibodies against desmoglein 1 (Dsg1) and/or Dsg3 by IIF or ELISA. Anti-Dsg3 alone → mucosal-dominant PV; anti-Dsg1 + anti-Dsg3 → mucocutaneous PV. Histopathology: suprabasal acantholysis with 'row of tombstones' basal keratinocytes
pemphigus_vulgaris.criterion_quote: Diagnoses are made according to typical clinical features and immunologic criteria of intercellular deposition of IgG and/or C3 in the epithelium by DIF or detection of circulating autoantibodies. … Patients with antibodies against dsg3 show a mucosal-dominant PV type, whereas concomitant anti-dsg1/3 antibodies are associated with both cutaneous and mucosal lesions (mucocutaneous PV).

source.title: Pemphigus and pemphigoids: Clinical presentation, diagnosis and therapy

acne_aad_severity.name: Acne Vulgaris — AAD 2024 severity
acne_aad_severity.criterion: Mild: fewer than 20 comedones, fewer than 15 inflammatory lesions, or total lesion count <30. Moderate: 20–100 comedones, 15–50 inflammatory lesions, or total lesion count 30–125. Severe: >5 pseudocysts, total comedone count >100, total inflammatory count >50, or total lesion count >125
acne_aad_severity.criterion_quote: Mild acne is defined as fewer than 20 comedones, fewer than 15 inflammatory lesions, or a total lesion count less than 30. Moderate acne includes 20–100 comedones, 15–50 inflammatory lesions, or a total lesion count of 30–125. Severe acne is characterized by more than 5 pseudocysts, total comedone count greater than 100, total inflammatory count greater than 50, or a total lesion count greater than 125.

source.title: AAD 2024 Acne Vulgaris Clinical Guideline

poag.name: Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma (POAG) — AAO PPP
poag.criterion: Chronic, progressive optic neuropathy with characteristic clinical features: open angle on gonioscopy; glaucomatous optic nerve cupping; thinning of circumpapillary RNFL/macular ganglion cell layer on OCT; typical glaucomatous visual field defects; absence of secondary causes of IOP elevation. IOP typically elevated but normal-tension POAG possible
poag.criterion_quote: characteristic clinical features of POAG include an open angle on gonioscopy, glaucomatous optic nerve cupping, thinning of the circumpapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL)/macular ganglion cell layer on imaging, and typical glaucomatous visual field defects in the absence of any secondary causes of IOP elevation.

source.title: AAO Preferred Practice Pattern: Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

angle_closure.name: Primary Angle Closure Spectrum (PACS / PAC / PACG) — ISGEO
angle_closure.criterion: Primary Angle Closure Suspect (PACS): ≥180 degrees of iridotrabecular contact (ITC) without IOP elevation, peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS), or optic nerve damage. Primary Angle Closure (PAC): occludable angle with features indicating that trabecular obstruction by peripheral iris has occurred (elevated IOP and/or PAS). Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma (PACG): PAC + evidence of glaucomatous optic neuropathy
angle_closure.criterion_quote: (1) Primary angle closure suspect (PACS): an eye in which appositional contact between the peripheral iris and posterior trabecular meshwork is considered possible; (2) Primary angle closure (PAC): an eye with an occludable angle and features indicating that trabecular obstruction by the peripheral iris has occurred; (3) PACG: PAC together with evidence of glaucomatous optic neuropathy.

source.title: ISGEO classification of primary angle closure; AAO PACD PPP 2020

amd_ferris.name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD) — Ferris 2013 / Beckman classification
amd_ferris.criterion: No apparent aging changes: no drusen, no AMD pigmentary abnormalities. Normal aging changes: only drupelets (small drusen ≤63 µm), no AMD pigmentary abnormalities. Early AMD: medium drusen >63 µm and ≤125 µm, no AMD pigmentary abnormalities. Intermediate AMD: large drusen >125 µm and/or any AMD pigmentary abnormalities. Late AMD: neovascular AMD and/or any geographic atrophy. AMD pigmentary abnormalities = hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation within 2 disc diameters of macular center in eyes with drusen ≥63 µm and no other known retinal disease
amd_ferris.criterion_quote: No drusen and No AMD pigmentary abnormalities. … Only drupelets (small drusen ≤63 μm). … Medium drusen >63 μm and ≤125 μm. … Large drusen > 125 μm and/or Any AMD pigmentary abnormalities. … Neovascular AMD and/or Any geographic atrophy.

source.title: Clinical Classification of Age-related Macular Degeneration

diabetic_retinopathy_icdr.name: Diabetic Retinopathy — ICDR 2003 severity scale
diabetic_retinopathy_icdr.criterion: No apparent DR: no abnormalities. Mild NPDR: microaneurysms only. Moderate NPDR: more than just microaneurysms but less than severe NPDR. Severe NPDR (4-2-1 rule, any of): >20 intraretinal hemorrhages in each of 4 quadrants; definite venous beading in ≥2 quadrants; prominent IRMA in ≥1 quadrant — AND no signs of PDR. Proliferative DR (PDR): neovascularization and/or vitreous/preretinal hemorrhage
diabetic_retinopathy_icdr.criterion_quote: Mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy: Microaneurysms only. Moderate NPDR: More than just microaneurysms but less than severe NPDR. Severe NPDR: Any of the following: more than 20 intraretinal hemorrhages in each of 4 quadrants; definite venous beading in 2+ quadrants; Prominent IRMA in 1+ quadrant And no signs of proliferative retinopathy. Proliferative DR: One or more of the following: neovascularization, vitreous/preretinal hemorrhage.

source.org: Wilkinson CP et al. (International Council of Ophthalmology)
source.title: Proposed international clinical diabetic retinopathy and DME disease severity scales

diabetic_retinopathy_icdr.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: ICDR canonical; replace source with AAO PPP.]

dme_icdr.name: Diabetic Macular Edema (DME) — ICDR severity
dme_icdr.criterion: DME apparently absent: no apparent retinal thickening or hard exudates in posterior pole. DME apparently present: some apparent retinal thickening or hard exudates in posterior pole. Severity: Mild — thickening/hard exudates distant from center of macula; Moderate — approaching center but not involving it; Severe — involving center of macula
dme_icdr.criterion_quote: Mild diabetic macular edema: Some retinal thickening or hard exudates in posterior pole but distant from the center of the macula. Moderate diabetic macular edema: Retinal thickening or hard exudates approaching the center of the macula but not involving the center. Severe diabetic macular edema: Retinal thickening or hard exudates involving the center of the macula

source.title: ICDR DME severity scale

uveitis_sun.name: Uveitis — SUN anatomic classification
uveitis_sun.criterion: Anterior uveitis: primary site anterior chamber (iritis, iridocyclitis, anterior cyclitis). Intermediate uveitis: primary site vitreous (pars planitis, posterior cyclitis, hyalitis). Posterior uveitis: primary site retina or choroid (focal/multifocal/diffuse choroiditis, chorioretinitis, retinochoroiditis, retinitis, neuroretinitis). Panuveitis: anterior chamber + vitreous + retina or choroid. Course: acute (sudden onset, limited duration), recurrent (repeated episodes separated by inactive periods ≥3 mo without treatment), chronic (persistent or relapse <3 mo after treatment discontinuation)
uveitis_sun.criterion_quote: Anterior uveitis: Anterior chamber. Intermediate uveitis: Vitreous. Posterior uveitis: Retina or choroid. Panuveitis: Anterior chamber, vitreous, and retina or choroid.

source.title: Standardization of Uveitis Nomenclature for Reporting Clinical Data

retinal_detachment.name: Retinal Detachment — classification by mechanism
retinal_detachment.criterion: Rhegmatogenous RD: associated with retinal break(s) (rhegma); fluid passes from vitreous cavity via retinal tear/break into subretinal space. Tractional RD: vitreoretinal traction causes detachment of neurosensory retina from RPE (PDR, ROP, sickle cell). Exudative (serous) RD: fluid accumulation beneath sensory retina from retinal/choroidal diseases (CSR, posterior scleritis, choroidal tumors, malignant hypertension)
retinal_detachment.criterion_quote: Rhegmatogenous RDs are associated with retinal break(s) (rhegma)… caused by fluid passing from the vitreous cavity via a retinal tear or a break into the potential space between the sensory retina and the RPE. … In tractional RD, there is traction over the retina, which causes detachment of the neurosensory retina from the retinal pigment epithelium. … Exudative detachments result from fluid accumulation beneath the sensory retina caused by retinal or choroidal diseases.

source.title: Retinal Detachment

rvo.name: Retinal Vein Occlusion (RVO) — AAO PPP
rvo.criterion: Partial or complete obstruction of a retinal vein. Central RVO (CRVO): obstruction at or posterior to optic nerve head — all 4 quadrants affected. Branch RVO (BRVO): obstruction at branch or tributary — sectoral involvement. Hemicentral RVO: obstruction involving half of retina. Ischemic vs non-ischemic distinction by area of capillary non-perfusion on FA (>10 disc diameters = ischemic, higher neovascularization risk)
rvo.criterion_quote: Retinal vein occlusion (RVO) occurs when there is partial or complete obstruction of a retinal vein, and it is classified by the location of the occlusion. An obstruction of the retinal vein at or posterior to the optic nerve head is a central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO), and complete or partial obstruction at a branch or tributary of the central retinal vein is a branch retinal vein occlusion (BRVO).

source.title: AAO Preferred Practice Pattern: Retinal Vein Occlusions

bph_iss.name: BPH / LUTS Severity — AUA Symptom Index (AUA-SI / IPSS)
bph_iss.criterion: 7 questions (frequency, nocturia, weak stream, hesitancy, intermittence, incomplete emptying, urgency) each scored 0–5; total 0–35. Mild: 0–7; moderate: 8–19; severe: 20–35. QoL question separate (0–6)
bph_iss.criterion_quote: Patients can be generally classified as: 0-7 mildly symptomatic, 8-19 moderately symptomatic, and 20-35 severely symptomatic.

source.title: Management of LUTS Attributed to BPH: AUA Guideline

microhematuria.name: Microscopic Hematuria — AUA/SUFU 2020
microhematuria.criterion: Microhematuria defined as ≥3 RBCs per high-power field on microscopic evaluation of a single, properly collected urine specimen. Risk stratification — Low: younger patients without significant cancer risk factors → repeat UA within 6 months. Intermediate: moderate malignancy risk → cystoscopy + renal ultrasound. High: substantial cancer risk factors → cystoscopy + axial upper tract imaging (CT urography preferred)
microhematuria.criterion_quote: >3 red blood cells per high-power field on microscopic evaluation of a single, properly collected urine specimen.

source.title: Microhematuria: AUA/SUFU Guideline

nephrolithiasis_high_risk.name: Nephrolithiasis — High-Risk Stone Former (AUA)
nephrolithiasis_high_risk.criterion: High-risk stone formers warranting 24-h urine + serum metabolic workup: family history of stone disease; malabsorptive intestinal disease or resection; recurrent UTIs; obesity; RTA Type 1; primary hyperparathyroidism; gout; T2DM; solitary kidney; repeated stone episodes; multiple stones at initial presentation; cystinuria; struvite; uric acid stones
nephrolithiasis_high_risk.criterion_quote: High-risk stone formers include those with: family history of stone disease; malabsorptive intestinal disease or resection; recurrent urinary tract infections; obesity; RTA Type 1, primary hyperparathyroidism, gout, diabetes mellitus type 2; patients with a solitary kidney are considered 'high-risk'; repeated stone episodes; multiple stones at initial presentation.

source.title: Medical Management of Kidney Stones: AUA Guideline

oab_aua.name: Overactive Bladder (OAB) — AUA/SUFU 2024
oab_aua.criterion: Urinary urgency, usually accompanied by frequency and nocturia, with or without urgency urinary incontinence, in the absence of UTI or other obvious pathology
oab_aua.criterion_quote: urinary urgency, usually accompanied by frequency and nocturia, with or without urgency urinary incontinence, in the absence of urinary tract infection or other obvious pathology.

source.title: AUA/SUFU Guideline on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Idiopathic Overactive Bladder

prostatitis_nih.name: Prostatitis — NIH category I–IV
prostatitis_nih.criterion: Category I: acute bacterial prostatitis. Category II: chronic bacterial prostatitis (recurrent UTIs with same organism). Category IIIA: inflammatory chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome (CP/CPPS) — WBCs in EPS, post-massage urine, or semen. Category IIIB: non-inflammatory CP/CPPS — no WBCs. Category IV: asymptomatic inflammatory prostatitis (incidental finding)
prostatitis_nih.criterion_quote: Category I: acute bacterial; Category II: chronic bacterial; Category IIIA: inflammatory CPPS; Category IIIB: non-inflammatory CPPS; Category IV: asymptomatic inflammatory.

source.title: Diagnosis and Management of Male Chronic Pelvic Pain: AUA Guideline

ed_iief_shim.name: Erectile Dysfunction — IIEF/SHIM severity
ed_iief_shim.criterion: IIEF Erectile Function (EF) domain (5–30 score): normal 26–30; mild 18–25; moderate 11–17; severe ≤10. SHIM (IIEF-5, 5–25 score): no ED 22–25; mild 17–21; mild-to-moderate 12–16; moderate 8–11; severe 5–7
ed_iief_shim.criterion_quote: The erectile function (EF) domain quantifies ED severity on a scale of 5-30; scores of 26-30 are consistent with normal erectile function, 18-25 consistent with mild ED, 11-17 consistent with moderate ED, and ≤10 consistent with severe ED. … SHIM 22-25 are interpreted as no ED; 17-21 as mild ED; 12-16 as mild-to-moderate ED; 8-11 as moderate ED; 5-7 as severe ED.

source.title: Erectile Dysfunction: AUA Guideline

crpc.name: Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer (CRPC)
crpc.criterion: Disease progression despite androgen-deprivation therapy AND castrate level of testosterone (<50 ng/dL). Progression manifests as: (1) continuous rise in serum PSA (measured at minimum 1-week intervals; PSADT calculations require ≥3 values measured ≥4 weeks apart); OR (2) progression of pre-existing or new radiographic disease; OR (3) clinical progression with symptoms
crpc.criterion_quote: CRPC is characterized by disease progression despite ADT and a castrate level of testosterone (<50ng/dL). Progression can manifest as: a continuous rise in serum PSA levels (measured at minimum 1-week intervals, with PSADT calculations requiring at least 3 values measured ≥4 weeks apart); progression of pre-existing or new radiographic disease; or clinical progression accompanied by symptoms.

source.title: Advanced Prostate Cancer: AUA/SUO Guideline

mscc.name: Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression (MSCC)
mscc.criterion: Clinical red flags in cancer patient: bladder/bowel dysfunction, gait disturbance, limb weakness, numbness/paresthesia, sensory loss, severe unremitting or progressive back pain, pain aggravated by straining. Urgent whole-spine MRI within 24 hours. Empiric dexamethasone 16 mg oral (or equivalent parenteral) as soon as possible. Definitive treatment: radiation, surgery, or both based on stability and prognosis
mscc.criterion_quote: bladder or bowel dysfunction, gait disturbance or difficulty walking, limb weakness, numbness, paraesthesia or sensory loss, severe unremitting back pain, progressive back pain, back pain aggravated by straining … as soon as possible (and always within 24 hours) … Offer 16 mg of oral dexamethasone (or equivalent parenteral dose) as soon as possible

source.title: Spinal metastases and metastatic spinal cord compression

svc_yu.name: Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Syndrome — Yu Grading
svc_yu.criterion: Grade 0: asymptomatic (radiologic SVC obstruction, no symptoms). Grade 1 mild: edema in head/neck, cyanosis, plethora. Grade 2 moderate: edema in head/neck with functional impairment. Grade 3 severe: mild/moderate cerebral or laryngeal edema, limited cardiac reserve. Grade 4 life-threatening: significant cerebral or laryngeal edema, cardiac failure. Grade 5: death. Grade ≥3 warrants urgent intervention (stenting, radiation, chemotherapy)
svc_yu.criterion_quote: Grade 0 Asymptomatic. Grade 1 Mild (Edema in head or neck, cyanosis, plethora). Grade 2 Moderate (Edema with functional impairment). Grade 3 Severe (mild/moderate cerebral or laryngeal edema, limited cardiac reserve). Grade 4 Life-threatening (significant cerebral or laryngeal edema, cardiac failure).

source.title: Superior Vena Cava Syndrome — A Proposed Classification System and Algorithm for Management

hypercalcemia_malignancy.name: Hypercalcemia of Malignancy (HCM) — Endocrine Society 2023
hypercalcemia_malignancy.criterion: Severity: Mild — corrected serum Ca <12 mg/dL (3.0 mmol/L); Moderate — 12–14 mg/dL (3.0–3.5); Severe — >14 mg/dL (>3.5). Mechanisms: PTHrP-mediated humoral (most common in solid tumors); osteolytic (multiple myeloma, breast cancer bone metastases); 1,25-(OH)₂-vitamin D–mediated (almost exclusively lymphomas, granulomatous diseases); ectopic PTH (rare). Workup: PTH (suppressed), PTHrP, 25-(OH) vitamin D, 1,25-(OH)₂-vitamin D, SPEP/UPEP, imaging for malignancy
hypercalcemia_malignancy.criterion_quote: Mild: SCa < 12 mg/dL [3 mmol/L]. Moderate: SCa 12-14 mg/dL [3-3.5 mmol/L]. Severe: SCa >14 mg/dL [3.5 mmol/L].

source.title: Treatment of Hypercalcemia of Malignancy in Adults

crs_astct.name: CAR-T Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) — ASTCT 2019
crs_astct.criterion: All grades require fever ≥38°C. Grade 1: fever only (no hypotension, no hypoxia). Grade 2: fever + hypotension not requiring vasopressors AND/OR hypoxia requiring low-flow O₂ (nasal cannula or blow-by). Grade 3: fever + hypotension requiring 1 vasopressor (± vasopressin) AND/OR hypoxia requiring high-flow nasal cannula, facemask, non-rebreather, or Venturi mask. Grade 4: fever + hypotension requiring multiple vasopressors (excluding vasopressin) AND/OR hypoxia requiring positive pressure (CPAP, BiPAP, intubation, mechanical ventilation)
crs_astct.criterion_quote: Grade 1: Fever (Temperature ≥38°C) with None (hypotension) and None (hypoxia). Grade 2: Fever with Not requiring vasopressors and/or Requiring low-flow nasal cannula or blow-by. Grade 3: Fever with Requiring a vasopressor with or without vasopressin and/or Requiring high-flow nasal cannula, facemask, nonrebreather mask, or Venturi mask. Grade 4: Fever with Requiring multiple vasopressors (excluding vasopressin) and/or Requiring positive pressure.

source.org: American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy (ASTCT)
source.title: ASTCT Consensus Grading for CRS and Neurologic Toxicity Associated with Immune Effector Cells

icans_ice.name: Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS) — ICE/ASTCT
icans_ice.criterion: ICE score (10 points): Orientation to year, month, city, hospital (4); Naming 3 objects (3); Following commands (1); Writing standard sentence (1); Counting backwards from 100 by 10 (1). ASTCT grading by ICE: Grade 1: ICE 7–9. Grade 2: ICE 3–6. Grade 3: ICE 0–2. Grade 4: ICE 0 with patient unarousable. Other graded parameters: depressed level of consciousness, seizure, motor findings, elevated ICP/cerebral edema
icans_ice.criterion_quote: Orientation: orientation to year, month, city, hospital (4 points); Naming: ability to name 3 objects (3 points); Following commands (1 point); Writing: ability to write a standard sentence (1 point); Attention: ability to count backwards from 100 by ten (1 point). ICE score 7-9 = Grade 1; ICE score 3-6 = Grade 2; ICE score 0-2 = Grade 3; ICE score 0 with patient unarousable = Grade 4.

source.title: ASTCT Consensus Grading 2019 — ICE assessment tool

irae_colitis_ctcae.name: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor irAE Diarrhea/Colitis — CTCAE 5.0
irae_colitis_ctcae.criterion: Grade 1: increase <4 stools/day over baseline; mild ostomy output increase. Grade 2: increase 4–6 stools/day over baseline; moderate ostomy increase; limiting instrumental ADL. Grade 3: increase ≥7 stools/day over baseline; hospitalization indicated; severe ostomy increase; limiting self-care ADL. Grade 4: life-threatening consequences; urgent intervention indicated. Grade 5: death
irae_colitis_ctcae.criterion_quote: Grade 1: Increase of <4 stools per day over baseline. Grade 2: Increase of 4 - 6 stools per day over baseline; limiting instrumental ADL. Grade 3: Increase of ≥7 stools per day over baseline; hospitalization indicated. Grade 4: Life-threatening consequences; urgent intervention indicated.

source.title: Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE) v5.0

cat_itac.name: Cancer-Associated Thrombosis (CAT) — ITAC 2022
cat_itac.criterion: Initial 10 days: LMWH or DOAC (apixaban, edoxaban, rivaroxaban). DOAC eligibility: not at high risk of GI or GU bleeding, no strong drug-drug interactions, no GI absorption impairment. LMWH preferred over edoxaban/rivaroxaban with luminal GI malignancies (apixaban is acceptable alternative). Minimum 6 months of LMWH or DOAC. Continue beyond 6 months while active cancer, metastatic disease, or chemotherapy ongoing
cat_itac.criterion_quote: low-molecular-weight heparins (LMWHs) for the initial (first 10 days) treatment and maintenance treatment of cancer-associated thrombosis. … direct oral anticoagulants for the initial treatment and maintenance treatment of cancer-associated thrombosis in patients who are not at high risk of gastrointestinal or genitourinary bleeding. … LMWHs or direct oral anticoagulants for a minimum of 6 months to treat cancer-associated thrombosis.

source.title: 2022 ITAC international clinical practice guidelines for CAT

cancer_cachexia.name: Cancer Cachexia — Fearon 2011
cancer_cachexia.criterion: Diagnostic criteria (any one): (1) weight loss >5% over past 6 months in absence of simple starvation; OR (2) BMI <20 kg/m² + weight loss >2%; OR (3) appendicular skeletal muscle mass index consistent with sarcopenia + weight loss >2%. Multifactorial syndrome: ongoing loss of skeletal muscle mass (± loss of fat mass) not fully reversible by conventional nutritional support, leading to progressive functional impairment
cancer_cachexia.criterion_quote: weight loss greater than 5%, or weight loss greater than 2% in individuals already showing depletion according to current bodyweight and height (body-mass index [BMI] <20 kg/m2) or skeletal muscle mass (sarcopenia). … a multifactorial syndrome characterised by an ongoing loss of skeletal muscle mass (with or without loss of fat mass) that cannot be fully reversed by conventional nutritional support and leads to progressive functional impairment

source.title: Definition and classification of cancer cachexia: an international consensus (Lancet Oncol)

uspstf_ltbi.name: Latent TB Infection Screening — USPSTF 2023
uspstf_ltbi.criterion: Screen asymptomatic adults at increased risk: born in/formerly resided in countries with elevated TB prevalence; lived in congregate settings (homeless shelters, correctional facilities); immunocompromised (HIV, immunosuppressive medications, organ transplant); silicosis. Grade B
uspstf_ltbi.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for LTBI in populations at increased risk.

source.title: Latent Tuberculosis Infection: Screening

uspstf_hbv.name: Hepatitis B Virus Screening — USPSTF 2020
uspstf_hbv.criterion: Screen nonpregnant adolescents and adults at increased risk for HBV using HBsAg with confirmatory testing. Higher-risk: born in countries/regions with ≥2% HBV prevalence; US-born unvaccinated with parents from regions ≥8% prevalence; HIV+; current/former injection drug users; MSM; household/sexual contacts of HBsAg+ persons. Grade B
uspstf_hbv.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection in adolescents and adults at increased risk for infection.

source.title: Hepatitis B Virus Infection Screening in Adolescents and Adults

uspstf_hypertension.name: Hypertension Screening — USPSTF 2021
uspstf_hypertension.criterion: Screen adults ≥18 years for hypertension using office BP measurement with confirmation outside the clinical setting (ambulatory or home BP monitoring) before starting treatment. Annual screening for adults ≥40 and those at increased risk; every 3–5 years for adults 18–39 with normal prior readings and no risk factors. Grade A
uspstf_hypertension.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for hypertension in adults 18 years or older with office blood pressure measurement (OBPM). The USPSTF recommends obtaining blood pressure measurements outside of the clinical setting for diagnostic confirmation before starting treatment.

source.title: Hypertension in Adults: Screening

uspstf_gdm.name: Gestational Diabetes Screening — USPSTF 2021
uspstf_gdm.criterion: Screen asymptomatic pregnant persons (not previously diagnosed with type 1 or type 2 DM) for gestational diabetes at 24 weeks of gestation or after, using either a one-step or two-step process or fasting plasma glucose. Grade B (≥24 weeks); Grade I (<24 weeks)
uspstf_gdm.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for gestational diabetes in asymptomatic pregnant persons at 24 weeks of gestation or after.

source.title: Gestational Diabetes: Screening

uspstf_alcohol_sbirt.name: Unhealthy Alcohol Use Screening — USPSTF 2018
uspstf_alcohol_sbirt.criterion: Screen adults ≥18 (including pregnant women) for unhealthy alcohol use in primary care settings and provide persons engaged in risky/hazardous drinking with brief behavioral counseling interventions. Grade B. Use AUDIT, AUDIT-C, or single-question screen. Evidence insufficient for adolescents 12–17 (Grade I)
uspstf_alcohol_sbirt.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for unhealthy alcohol use in primary care settings in adults 18 years or older, including pregnant women, and providing persons engaged in risky or hazardous drinking with brief behavioral counseling interventions to reduce unhealthy alcohol use.

source.title: Unhealthy Alcohol Use in Adolescents and Adults: Screening and Behavioral Counseling

uspstf_anxiety_adults.name: Adult Anxiety Screening — USPSTF 2023
uspstf_anxiety_adults.criterion: Screen for anxiety disorders in adults aged 19–64 (including pregnant and postpartum). Grade B. Evidence insufficient for adults ≥65 (Grade I)
uspstf_anxiety_adults.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends screening for anxiety disorders in adults, including pregnant and postpartum persons.

source.title: Screening for Anxiety Disorders in Adults

uspstf_folic_acid.name: Folic Acid Supplementation for NTD Prevention — USPSTF 2023
uspstf_folic_acid.criterion: All persons planning to or who could become pregnant take a daily supplement containing 0.4 to 0.8 mg (400 to 800 µg) of folic acid. Grade A
uspstf_folic_acid.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends that all persons planning to or who could become pregnant take a daily supplement containing 0.4 to 0.8 mg (400 to 800 μg) of folic acid.

source.title: Folic Acid Supplementation to Prevent Neural Tube Defects

uspstf_falls_prevention.name: Falls Prevention in Older Adults — USPSTF 2024
uspstf_falls_prevention.criterion: Exercise interventions to prevent falls in community-dwelling adults ≥65 at increased risk for falls (history of falls, problems in physical functioning, limited mobility). Grade B. Individualize multifactorial interventions (Grade C)
uspstf_falls_prevention.criterion_quote: The USPSTF recommends exercise interventions to prevent falls in community-dwelling adults 65 years or older who are at increased risk for falls.

source.title: Falls Prevention in Community-Dwelling Older Adults: Interventions

hyperthyroidism.name: Hyperthyroidism — ATA 2016 (overt + subclinical)
hyperthyroidism.criterion: Overt hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH (typically <0.01–0.1 mIU/L depending on assay) + elevated fT4 and/or fT3. Subclinical hyperthyroidism: low or undetectable TSH + normal fT4 AND normal fT3. Etiology workup: TRAb/TSI for Graves, RAIU/scan to distinguish Graves from toxic nodular goiter or thyroiditis
hyperthyroidism.criterion_quote: Overt hyperthyroidism is characterized by suppressed TSH and elevated fT4 and/or fT3 levels. Subclinical hyperthyroidism is defined as low or undetectable TSH with normal fT4 and fT3 levels.

source.title: 2016 ATA Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism

graves_disease.name: Graves Disease — ATA 2016
graves_disease.criterion: Hyperthyroidism (suppressed TSH + elevated fT4/fT3) PLUS diffuse goiter (palpation or imaging) AND/OR ophthalmopathy (proptosis, lid retraction, periorbital edema), pretibial myxedema. Confirmation: TRAb or TSI positive (when other features absent or ambiguous) OR diffuse increased uptake on RAIU/Tc-99m scan (homogeneous, often elevated 24-h RAIU)
graves_disease.criterion_quote: Diagnosis of Graves disease is supported by TRAb/TSI positivity or diffuse increased uptake on radioactive iodine scan.

source.title: 2016 ATA Guidelines for Hyperthyroidism

gestational_diabetes_iadpsg.name: Gestational Diabetes — IADPSG / Carpenter-Coustan
gestational_diabetes_iadpsg.criterion: IADPSG one-step 75-g OGTT (any one value diagnostic): fasting ≥92 mg/dL (5.1 mmol/L), 1-h ≥180 mg/dL (10.0), 2-h ≥153 mg/dL (8.5). Carpenter-Coustan two-step (50-g screen → 100-g 3-h OGTT if positive; ≥2 values diagnostic): fasting ≥95, 1-h ≥180, 2-h ≥155, 3-h ≥140 mg/dL. Screen at 24–28 weeks (or earlier if high risk)
gestational_diabetes_iadpsg.criterion_quote: IADPSG criteria: fasting plasma glucose ≥92 mg/dL, 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL, or 2-hour ≥153 mg/dL on 75-g OGTT.

source.title: IADPSG recommendations on the diagnosis and classification of hyperglycemia in pregnancy (Diabetes Care)

adrenal_incidentaloma.name: Adrenal Incidentaloma — ESE/ENSAT 2023
adrenal_incidentaloma.criterion: Imaging: unenhanced CT density ≤10 Hounsfield units (HU) indicates lipid-rich likely benign adenoma; >10 HU warrants further characterization (washout protocol, MRI). Biochemical workup REQUIRED in all incidentalomas: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol >1.8 µg/dL = autonomous cortisol secretion; >5 µg/dL = overt Cushing), plasma metanephrines (pheochromocytoma), aldosterone/renin if HTN or hypokalemia (primary aldosteronism). Resection considered if >4 cm OR functional OR imaging features concerning for malignancy
adrenal_incidentaloma.criterion_quote: Unenhanced CT density ≤10 HU is a strong indicator of a lipid-rich adenoma. … Adrenal mass >4 cm warrants consideration of surgical removal. … Cortisol >1.8 µg/dL (50 nmol/L) after 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test suggests autonomous cortisol secretion.

source.title: European Society of Endocrinology Clinical Practice Guideline on Management of Adrenal Incidentalomas

men1.name: MEN1 — Endocrine Society 2012
men1.criterion: Clinical diagnosis (any one): (1) ≥2 of 3 primary MEN1-associated endocrine tumors — parathyroid hyperplasia/adenoma, anterior pituitary adenoma, gastroenteropancreatic NET; OR (2) ≥1 MEN1-associated tumor + first-degree relative with MEN1; OR (3) identification of germline MEN1 pathogenic variant
men1.criterion_quote: MEN1 is diagnosed clinically by the presence of two or more of the three main MEN1-related endocrine tumors (parathyroid, pituitary, pancreatic NET), or one MEN1-associated tumor in a first-degree relative of a patient with MEN1, or by identification of a germline MEN1 mutation.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1)

men2.name: MEN2A & MEN2B — ATA 2015
men2.criterion: MEN2A: 2 of 3 — medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC), pheochromocytoma, primary hyperparathyroidism. MEN2B: MTC (essentially 100%, often presents in infancy) + mucosal neuromas (lips, tongue) + intestinal ganglioneuromatosis + marfanoid habitus + skeletal abnormalities + corneal nerve thickening. Both caused by germline RET proto-oncogene mutations. Prophylactic thyroidectomy timing based on RET codon risk stratification
men2.criterion_quote: MEN2A is characterized by medullary thyroid carcinoma in 95% of patients, pheochromocytoma in 50%, and primary hyperparathyroidism in 25%. MEN2B includes medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas, marfanoid body habitus.

source.title: Revised ATA Guidelines for the Management of MTC

klinefelter.name: Klinefelter Syndrome — EAA 2020
klinefelter.criterion: Karyotype 47,XXY (or variants 48,XXXY, 48,XXYY, 49,XXXXY, mosaic 46,XY/47,XXY). Clinical evaluation trigger: testes <5 mL (orchidometer) in adult male, particularly with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (elevated FSH/LH, low total testosterone). Other features: tall stature, eunuchoid body proportions, gynecomastia, infertility (azoospermia in 95%+)
klinefelter.criterion_quote: Klinefelter syndrome is the result of supernumerary X chromosomes in males (47,XXY karyotype in 80–90%). … Adult testes volume <5 mL should trigger karyotype testing.

source.title: European Academy of Andrology guidelines on Klinefelter Syndrome

endometrial_ajcc8.name: Endometrial Cancer — AJCC TNM 8 / FIGO 2009
endometrial_ajcc8.criterion: T1 confined to corpus uteri (T1a invades less than 50% of myometrium; T1b invades ≥50%); T2 invades stromal connective tissue of cervix but doesn't extend beyond uterus; T3a invades serosa and/or adnexa; T3b vaginal involvement or parametrial involvement; T4 invades bladder mucosa and/or bowel mucosa. N0; N1 positive pelvic nodes (N1mi, N1a); N2 positive para-aortic nodes (N2mi, N2a). M0/M1
endometrial_ajcc8.criterion_quote: T1a: Tumor invades less than one-half of the myometrium. T1b: Tumor invades one-half or more of the myometrium.

source.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual 8th edition — Endometrial Cancer

cervical_ajcc8.name: Cervical Cancer — AJCC TNM 8 / FIGO 2018
cervical_ajcc8.criterion: Stage I: confined to cervix (IA microinvasive depth ≤5 mm; IB visible/depth >5 mm — IB1 ≤2 cm, IB2 2–4 cm, IB3 >4 cm). Stage II: invades beyond uterus but not to pelvic wall or lower vagina (IIA upper vagina; IIB parametrial). Stage III: pelvic wall, lower vagina, hydronephrosis, or pelvic/para-aortic nodes. Stage IV: bladder/rectum mucosa (IVA) or distant (IVB)
cervical_ajcc8.criterion_quote: Stage IA: Microscopic disease with stromal invasion depth ≤5 mm. Stage IB1: clinically visible lesion ≤2 cm. Stage IB2: 2-4 cm. Stage IB3: >4 cm. Stage IIA: upper two-thirds of vagina without parametrial invasion. Stage IIB: parametrial involvement.

source.title: AJCC 8th edition Cervical Cancer Staging; FIGO 2018

ovarian_ajcc8.name: Ovarian / Fallopian Tube / Primary Peritoneal Cancer — AJCC TNM 8 / FIGO 2014
ovarian_ajcc8.criterion: Stage I: confined to ovary/fallopian tube (IA unilateral, capsule intact, no malignant cells in ascites/washings; IB bilateral, capsules intact; IC1 surgical spill; IC2 capsule ruptured before surgery or tumor on surface; IC3 malignant cells in ascites/washings). Stage II: pelvic extension (IIA uterus/tubes/ovaries; IIB other pelvic tissues). Stage III: peritoneal spread outside pelvis ± retroperitoneal nodes (IIIA1 nodes only ≤10 mm or >10 mm; IIIA2 microscopic extrapelvic; IIIB ≤2 cm macroscopic extrapelvic; IIIC >2 cm). Stage IV: distant metastasis (IVA pleural effusion with positive cytology; IVB parenchymal/extra-abdominal)
ovarian_ajcc8.criterion_quote: Stage IA: Limited to one ovary (capsule intact) or fallopian tube; no tumor on ovarian or fallopian tube surface; no malignant cells in the ascites or peritoneal washings. Stage IIIC: Macroscopic peritoneal metastasis beyond the pelvis >2 cm in greatest dimension.

source.title: AJCC 8th edition Ovarian Cancer Staging; FIGO 2014

thyroid_dtc_ajcc8.name: Differentiated Thyroid Cancer — AJCC TNM 8 (age 55 cutoff)
thyroid_dtc_ajcc8.criterion: Major change in AJCC 8: age cutoff increased from 45 to 55 years. Age <55 at diagnosis: any T, any N, M0 = Stage I; any T, any N, M1 = Stage II. Age ≥55: T1a,b N0 M0 = I; T2 N0 M0 = II; T3a or T3b or N1 = III; T4a = IVA; T4b = IVB; any M1 = IVC. T1a ≤1 cm; T1b 1–2 cm; T2 2–4 cm; T3a >4 cm confined to thyroid; T3b gross extrathyroidal extension into strap muscles; T4a invades subcutaneous tissue, larynx, trachea, esophagus, RLN; T4b invades prevertebral fascia, encases carotid or mediastinal vessels
thyroid_dtc_ajcc8.criterion_quote: AJCC 8th edition increased the age cutoff from 45 to 55 years for differentiated thyroid cancer. In patients ≥55 at diagnosis, gross extrathyroidal extension (T3b) or regional nodal disease becomes Stage III.

source.title: AJCC 8th edition Thyroid Cancer Staging

head_neck_scc_ajcc8.name: Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma — AJCC TNM 8
head_neck_scc_ajcc8.criterion: Oral cavity: T based on size + depth of invasion (DOI) — T1 ≤2 cm + DOI ≤5 mm; T2 ≤2 cm + DOI 5–10 mm OR 2–4 cm + DOI ≤10 mm; T3 >4 cm or DOI >10 mm; T4a invades cortical bone (mandible/maxilla), maxillary sinus, skin of face; T4b invades masticator space, pterygoid plates, skull base, encases carotid. HPV/p16+ oropharyngeal SCC has SEPARATE staging system (clinical and pathologic). N category in non-HPV adds extranodal extension (ENE) as a modifier: N+ENE− vs N+ENE+; ENE+ upstages to higher N (N3b)
head_neck_scc_ajcc8.criterion_quote: AJCC 8th edition for oral cavity SCC added depth of invasion (DOI) and extranodal extension (ENE) to T and N classifications. HPV-related oropharyngeal SCC has a separate clinical and pathologic staging system distinct from HPV-negative.

source.title: AJCC 8th edition Head and Neck Cancer Staging

uceis.name: UC Endoscopic Severity — UCEIS
