uceis.criterion: Sum of 3 endoscopic descriptors (vascular pattern 0–2, bleeding 0–3, erosions and ulcers 0–3); total 0–8, higher = more severe. UCEIS ≥6 associated with higher risk of treatment failure / colectomy
uceis.criterion_quote: Vascular Pattern: Normal 0, Patchy obliteration +1, Obliterated +2. Bleeding: None 0, Mucosal +1, Luminal mild +2, Luminal moderate or severe +3. Erosions and Ulcers: None 0, Erosions +1, Superficial ulcer +2, Deep ulcer +3.

source.title: Ulcerative Colitis Endoscopic Index of Severity (UCEIS)

sescd.name: Crohn's Disease Activity — SES-CD
sescd.criterion: 4 endoscopic variables scored 0–3 in each of 5 ileocolonic segments (ileum, right/transverse/left colon, rectum): ulcer size (none=0; 0.1–0.5 cm=1; 0.5–2 cm=2; >2 cm=3), extent of ulcerated surface (<10%=1; 10–30%=2; >30%=3), extent of affected surface (<50%=1; 50–75%=2; >75%=3), presence/severity of stenosis. Total range 0–56; SES-CD ≥7 predicts disease progression
sescd.criterion_quote: Presence and size of ulcers (none = 0; diameter 0.1–0.5 cm = 1; 0.5–2 cm = 2; >2 cm = 3); extent of ulcerated surface (none = 0; <10% = 1; 10–30% = 2; >30% = 3); extent of affected surface (none = 0; <50% = 1; 50–75% = 2; >75% = 3); presence and severity of stenosis.

source.title: Simple Endoscopic Score for Crohn's Disease (SES-CD)

acute_pancreatitis_aga.name: Acute Pancreatitis — AGA 2018 Initial Management
acute_pancreatitis_aga.criterion: Initial management: goal-directed IV fluids; early (within 24h) oral feeding as tolerated rather than NPO; enteral over parenteral nutrition when oral not feasible; AGAINST prophylactic antibiotics in predicted severe and necrotizing AP; AGAINST routine urgent ERCP in acute biliary pancreatitis without cholangitis
acute_pancreatitis_aga.criterion_quote: AGA recommends early (within 24 hours) oral feeding as tolerated rather than keeping the patient nil per os. AGA recommends enteral rather than parenteral nutrition. AGA suggests against the use of prophylactic antibiotics in patients with predicted severe AP and necrotizing AP. AGA suggests against the routine use of urgent ERCP in patients with acute biliary pancreatitis without cholangitis.

source.title: AGA Institute Guideline on Initial Management of Acute Pancreatitis

gastric_intestinal_metaplasia.name: Gastric Intestinal Metaplasia — AGA 2020
gastric_intestinal_metaplasia.criterion: GIM diagnosed on upper endoscopy with biopsy. H. pylori testing/eradication recommended. Routine endoscopic surveillance NOT recommended; higher-risk subgroups (incomplete vs complete GIM, extensive vs limited GIM, family history of gastric cancer, racial/ethnic minorities, immigrants from high-incidence regions) may reasonably elect surveillance via shared decision-making
gastric_intestinal_metaplasia.criterion_quote: In patients with GIM, AGA suggests against routine use of endoscopic surveillance. … Patients with GIM at higher risk for gastric cancer include those with: incomplete vs. complete GIM; extensive vs. limited GIM.

source.title: AGA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Management of Gastric Intestinal Metaplasia

functional_constipation.name: Functional Constipation — Rome IV
functional_constipation.criterion: ≥2 of: straining in >25% defecations; lumpy/hard stools (BSFS 1–2) in >25%; sensation of incomplete evacuation in >25%; sensation of anorectal obstruction in >25%; manual maneuvers in >25%; <3 spontaneous bowel movements/week. Loose stools rarely present without laxatives. Insufficient criteria for IBS. Symptoms ≥3 months with onset ≥6 months prior
functional_constipation.criterion_quote: Must include two or more of the following: Straining during more than ¼ (25%) of defecations; Lumpy or hard stools more than ¼ (25%) of defecations; Sensation of incomplete evacuation more than ¼ (25%) of defecations; Sensation of anorectal obstruction/blockage more than ¼ (25%) of defecations; Manual maneuvers to facilitate more than ¼ (25%) of defecations; Fewer than three SBM per week.

source.org: Rome Foundation; AGA refractory constipation update
source.title: Rome IV Functional Constipation; AGA 2026 refractory constipation update

pvt_cirrhosis.name: Portal Vein Thrombosis in Cirrhosis — AGA 2024
pvt_cirrhosis.criterion: Anticoagulation indicated for: (a) PVT with intestinal ischemia (urgent); (b) recent (<6 months) PVT >50% occlusive or involving main portal vein or mesenteric vessels. Observe (repeat imaging q3 months) for recent thrombosis of intrahepatic branches or <50% occlusion. Anticoagulation NOT advised for chronic (>6 months) complete occlusion with cavernous transformation. DOACs acceptable in Child-Pugh A and some B; avoid in C
pvt_cirrhosis.criterion_quote: Patients with cirrhosis and PVT with evidence of intestinal ischemia require urgent anticoagulation to minimize ischemic injury. Anticoagulation should be considered in patients with cirrhosis without intestinal ischemia who develop recent (<six months) PVT that is >50% occlusive or involves the main portal vein or mesenteric vessels. Anticoagulation is not advised for patients with cirrhosis with chronic (>six months) PVT with complete occlusion with collateralization (cavernous transformation). Direct oral anticoagulants may be considered in patients with compensated Child-Turcotte-Pugh class A and Child-Turcotte-Pugh class B cirrhosis.

source.title: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of PVT in Cirrhosis

cyclic_vomiting.name: Cyclic Vomiting Syndrome — Rome IV / AGA 2024
cyclic_vomiting.criterion: Stereotypical episodes of acute-onset vomiting lasting <1 week; ≥3 discrete episodes in prior year and ≥2 episodes in past 6 months, occurring ≥1 week apart; absence of vomiting between episodes (milder symptoms allowed). Severity: mild = <4 episodes/year lasting <2 days; moderate-severe = >4 episodes/year, >2 days, or ED/hospitalization. Four phases: inter-episodic, prodromal, emetic, recovery. Distinguish from cannabinoid hyperemesis
cyclic_vomiting.criterion_quote: Must include all of the following: Stereotypical episodes of vomiting regarding onset (acute) and duration (less than 1 week); At least three discrete episodes in the prior year and two episodes in the past 6 months, occurring at least 1 week apart; Absence of vomiting between episodes, but other milder symptoms can be present between cycles.

source.title: Rome IV CVS; AGA 2024 CPU on Diagnosis and Management of CVS

li_rads.name: LI-RADS v2018 — Liver Imaging Reporting and Data System for HCC
li_rads.criterion: Categories: LR-1 definitely benign; LR-2 probably benign; LR-3 intermediate probability of malignancy (3–6 month repeat/alternative imaging); LR-4 probably HCC (multidisciplinary discussion, often biopsy or short-interval follow-up); LR-5 definitely HCC (diagnosis without biopsy in at-risk patient — requires observation ≥10 mm + non-rim APHE PLUS one or more of: non-peripheral washout, enhancing capsule, threshold growth ≥50% in ≤6 months). LR-M probably or definitely malignant, NOT HCC-specific (suggests ICC, combined HCC-CCA). LR-TIV tumor in vein (definite enhancing soft tissue in vein). Major features: non-rim arterial phase hyperenhancement (APHE), non-peripheral washout, enhancing capsule, threshold growth
li_rads.criterion_quote: LR-5 (Definitely HCC): 100% certainty observation is HCC; diagnosis can be made without biopsy in at-risk patient. Requires observation ≥10 mm + non-rim APHE PLUS one or more of: non-peripheral washout, enhancing capsule, threshold growth (≥50% size increase in ≤6 months).

source.title: CT/MRI LI-RADS v2018 Core

bi_rads.name: BI-RADS 5th Edition — Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System
bi_rads.criterion: Categories: 0 incomplete (recall for additional views/US/MRI); 1 negative (essentially 0% malignancy, routine screening); 2 benign (essentially 0% malignancy — calcified fibroadenoma, simple cyst); 3 probably benign (>0% to ≤2% malignancy, short-interval 6-month follow-up); 4 suspicious (>2% to <95% — subdivisions: 4A >2–10%, 4B >10–50%, 4C >50–<95%) — tissue diagnosis recommended; 5 highly suggestive of malignancy (≥95% — biopsy + treatment planning); 6 known biopsy-proven malignancy (imaging of known cancer prior to definitive therapy)
bi_rads.criterion_quote: Category 3: Probably benign — >0% but ≤2% likelihood of malignancy. Category 4: Suspicious — >2% to <95%. 4A >2% to ≤10%; 4B >10% to ≤50%; 4C >50% to <95%. Category 5: Highly suggestive of malignancy — ≥95%. Category 6: Known biopsy-proven malignancy.

source.title: ACR BI-RADS Atlas, 5th Edition

ti_rads.name: ACR TI-RADS 2017 — Thyroid Imaging Reporting and Data System
ti_rads.criterion: Sum points across 5 feature groups: Composition (cystic 0; spongiform 0; mixed 1; solid 2); Echogenicity (anechoic 0; hyper/iso 1; hypo 2; very hypo 3); Shape (wider-than-tall 0; taller-than-wide 3); Margin (smooth 0; ill-defined 0; lobulated/irregular 2; extra-thyroidal extension 3); Echogenic foci (none/large comet-tail 0; macrocalcifications 1; rim 2; punctate 3; sum all). Classification: TR1 = 0 pts benign (no FNA); TR2 = 2 pts not suspicious (no FNA); TR3 = 3 pts mildly suspicious (FNA if ≥2.5 cm, follow if ≥1.5 cm); TR4 = 4–6 pts moderately suspicious (FNA if ≥1.5 cm, follow if ≥1 cm); TR5 = ≥7 pts highly suspicious (FNA if ≥1 cm, follow if ≥0.5 cm)
ti_rads.criterion_quote: 0 points: Benign — No FNA. 2 points: Not suspicious — No FNA. 3 points: Mildly suspicious — FNA if ≥2.5 cm, Follow if ≥1.5 cm. 4 to 6 points: Moderately suspicious — FNA if ≥1.5 cm, Follow if ≥1 cm. 7 points or more: Highly suspicious — FNA if ≥1 cm, Follow if ≥0.5 cm.

source.title: ACR TI-RADS White Paper (JACR 2017)

lung_rads.name: Lung-RADS v2022 — Lung CT Screening Reporting System
lung_rads.criterion: Category 0 incomplete. Category 1 negative (no lung nodules or with specific calcifications) — <1% malignancy, annual LDCT in 12 mo. Category 2 benign (solid <6 mm; part-solid <6 mm total; non-solid <30 mm) — <1% malignancy, annual LDCT in 12 mo. Category 3 probably benign (solid ≥6 to <8 mm at baseline; part-solid ≥6 mm with solid component <6 mm; non-solid ≥30 mm baseline or new) — 1–2% malignancy, 6-month LDCT. Category 4A suspicious (solid ≥8 to <15 mm at baseline; part-solid solid component ≥6 to <8 mm) — 5–15% malignancy, 3-month LDCT or PET/CT if solid ≥8 mm. Category 4B very suspicious (solid ≥15 mm; part-solid solid component ≥8 mm) — >15% malignancy, diagnostic CT/PET-CT/tissue sampling. Category 4X (3 or 4 nodules with additional concerning features) — >15% malignancy
lung_rads.criterion_quote: Category 3 (Probably benign): 1-2% probability of malignancy. Solid nodule ≥6 to <8 mm at baseline. 6-month LDCT follow-up. Category 4A (Suspicious): 5-15% probability of malignancy. Solid nodule ≥8 to <15 mm. 3-month LDCT; PET/CT may be used when solid component ≥8 mm. Category 4B (Very suspicious): >15% probability of malignancy.

source.title: Lung-RADS v2022 Assessment Categories

pi_rads.name: PI-RADS v2.1 — Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System
pi_rads.criterion: Category 1: very low (clinically significant cancer highly unlikely) — routine clinical management. Category 2: low (csCa unlikely) — routine management. Category 3: intermediate (csCa equivocal) — biopsy threshold often ≥3, consider with PSA density. Category 4: high (csCa likely) — targeted biopsy. PZ: focal markedly hypointense ADC + markedly hyperintense high b-value DWI, <1.5 cm. TZ: lenticular/non-circumscribed homogeneous moderately hypointense T2W, <1.5 cm. Category 5: very high (csCa highly likely) — targeted biopsy. Same as 4 but ≥1.5 cm OR definite extraprostatic extension/invasive behavior
pi_rads.criterion_quote: Category 3: Intermediate (the presence of clinically significant cancer is equivocal). Category 4: High (clinically significant cancer is likely to be present). Category 5: Very high (clinically significant cancer is highly likely to be present).

source.title: PI-RADS v2.1

dengue_who.name: Dengue — WHO 2009 classification
dengue_who.criterion: Probable dengue (without warning signs): live in/travel to dengue endemic area + fever + ≥2 of: nausea/vomiting, rash, aches and pains, tourniquet test positive, leukopenia, any warning sign. Dengue with warning signs: ≥1 of — abdominal pain/tenderness, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleed, lethargy/restlessness, liver enlargement >2 cm, lab evidence of HCT increase with rapid plt decrease. Severe dengue: ≥1 of — severe plasma leakage with shock (DSS) or fluid accumulation with respiratory distress; severe bleeding; severe organ involvement (AST or ALT ≥1000, impaired consciousness, heart/other organs)
dengue_who.criterion_quote: Severe dengue: 1. Severe plasma leakage leading to: Shock (DSS); Fluid accumulation with respiratory distress. 2. Severe bleeding as evaluated by clinician. 3. Severe organ involvement: Liver: AST or ALT >=1000; CNS: Impaired consciousness; Heart and other organs.

source.title: Dengue: Guidelines for Diagnosis, Treatment, Prevention and Control

severe_malaria.name: Severe Falciparum Malaria — WHO criteria
severe_malaria.criterion: P. falciparum asexual parasitaemia + ≥1: impaired consciousness (GCS <11 adults, Blantyre <3 children); prostration; multiple convulsions (>2/24h); acidosis (base deficit >8 mEq/L, HCO3 <15 mmol/L, or venous lactate ≥5 mmol/L); hypoglycemia (<2.2 mmol/L or <40 mg/dL); severe malarial anemia (Hb ≤5 g/dL or HCT ≤15% in children <12 y; Hb <7 g/dL or HCT <20% in adults); renal impairment (Cr >265 µmol/L / >3 mg/dL or BUN >20 mmol/L); jaundice (bili >50 µmol/L / >3 mg/dL with parasite count >100,000/µL); pulmonary edema (radiologic, or SpO₂ <92% RA with RR >30); significant bleeding; shock (compensated CRT ≥3 s; decompensated SBP <80 adults, <70 children with impaired perfusion); hyperparasitemia (>10%)
severe_malaria.criterion_quote: Severe falciparum malaria is defined by the presence of P. falciparum asexual parasitaemia and one or more of the following: impaired consciousness (a Glasgow coma score <11 in adults or a Blantyre coma score <3 in children); prostration; multiple convulsions (more than two episodes within 24 h); acidosis; hypoglycaemia (blood or plasma glucose <2.2 mmol/L); severe malarial anaemia; renal impairment (plasma or serum creatinine >265 µmol/L); jaundice; pulmonary oedema; significant bleeding; shock; hyperparasitaemia (>10%).

source.title: Guidelines for the Treatment of Malaria, Third Edition

cholera_who.name: Cholera — WHO Case Definitions
cholera_who.criterion: Suspected (areas where outbreak NOT declared): person aged ≥2 years with acute watery diarrhea + severe dehydration, OR who died from acute watery diarrhea with no other known cause. Suspected (in a cholera-affected area): any person with acute watery diarrhea or who died from acute watery diarrhea. Confirmed: any person infected with V. cholerae O1 or O139, confirmed by culture (including seroagglutination) or PCR
cholera_who.criterion_quote: In areas where a cholera outbreak has not been declared: a person aged two years or older with acute watery diarrhoea and severe dehydration or who died from acute watery diarrhoea with no other known cause of death. In an area where a cholera outbreak is declared: any person with acute watery diarrhoea; or who died from acute watery diarrhoea. … Confirmed: Any person infected with Vibrio cholerae O1 or O139, as confirmed by culture (including seroagglutination) or PCR.

source.title: Cholera Outbreak Response Field Manual — Appendix 1

extrapulmonary_tb.name: Extrapulmonary Tuberculosis — WHO
extrapulmonary_tb.criterion: Any bacteriologically confirmed or clinically diagnosed case of TB involving organs other than the lungs (pleura, peripheral lymph nodes, abdomen, GU tract, skin, joints and bones, meninges). Bacteriologically confirmed: TB diagnosed in biological specimen by WHO-approved rapid test (Xpert MTB/RIF, LF-LAM), smear microscopy, or culture. Clinically diagnosed: does not fulfill criteria for bacteriological confirmation but has been diagnosed with active TB by a clinician who has decided to give a full course of TB treatment
extrapulmonary_tb.criterion_quote: Extrapulmonary tuberculosis (EPTB): Any bacteriologically confirmed or clinically diagnosed case of TB involving organs other than the lungs (e.g. pleura, peripheral lymph nodes, abdomen, genitourinary tract, skin, joints and bones, meninges). Bacteriologically confirmed TB case: TB diagnosed in a biological specimen by a WHO-approved rapid test such as Xpert MTB/RIF or LF-LAM, smear microscopy or culture.

source.title: WHO Consolidated Guidelines on Tuberculosis — Definitions and Reporting Framework

ebola_marburg.name: Ebola/Marburg Virus Disease — WHO Case Definitions
ebola_marburg.criterion: Suspected: any person with sudden onset of high fever AND ≥1 of — contact with suspected/probable/confirmed Ebola or Marburg case, or dead/sick animal; OR sudden onset of high fever + ≥3 of (headache, lethargy, anorexia, aching muscles/joints, stomach pain, difficulty swallowing, vomiting, dyspnea, diarrhea, hiccups); OR unexplained bleeding; OR sudden unexplained death. Probable: suspected case evaluated by clinician with epidemiological link. Confirmed: positive lab (virus isolation, RT-PCR detection, or IgM antibodies)
ebola_marburg.criterion_quote: Suspected case: any person, alive or dead, suffering or having suffered from sudden onset of high fever and having had contact with a suspected, probable or confirmed Ebola or Marburg case, or a dead or sick animal; OR any person with sudden onset of high fever and at least three of the following symptoms: headache, lethargy, anorexia/loss of appetite, aching muscles or joints, stomach pain, difficulty swallowing, vomiting, difficulty breathing, diarrhoea, hiccups; OR any person with unexplained bleeding.

source.title: Case definition recommendations for Ebola or Marburg Virus Diseases

mpox.name: Mpox (Monkeypox) — WHO 2024 Case Definitions
mpox.criterion: Suspected: person presenting with unexplained acute skin rash, mucosal lesions, or lymphadenopathy (oral/conjunctival/urethral/penile/vaginal/anorectal); AND common causes of acute rash do not fully explain the picture (varicella, herpes zoster, measles, HSV, bacterial skin infections, gonococcus, syphilis, chancroid, LGV, molluscum, allergic reaction). Probable: meets suspected + ≥1 of: epi link to probable/confirmed case in 21 days before symptom onset; MSM identity; multiple/new/anonymous sexual partners in 21 days; positive orthopoxvirus serology. Confirmed: lab confirmed for MPXV by detection of unique sequences of viral DNA by PCR and/or sequencing
mpox.criterion_quote: A confirmed case is any person meeting the laboratory criterion (i.e., laboratory confirmed for MPXV by detection of unique sequences of viral DNA by real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and/or sequencing).

source.title: Surveillance, case investigation and contact tracing for mpox: Interim guidance

leptospirosis.name: Leptospirosis — WHO
leptospirosis.criterion: Suspected: acute febrile illness with headache, myalgia, prostration + ≥1 of (conjunctival suffusion, meningeal irritation, anuria/oliguria/proteinuria, jaundice, hemorrhages — GI/pulmonary, cardiac arrhythmia/failure, skin rash) AND history of exposure to infected animals or environment contaminated with animal urine. Confirmed: suspect case + lab confirmation by isolation of Leptospira from blood/CSF/urine; OR positive PCR; OR seroconversion or four-fold rise in MAT titre; OR single MAT titre ≥1:400. Weil's disease = severe (jaundice + AKI + hemorrhage)
leptospirosis.criterion_quote: Acute febrile illness with headache, myalgia and prostration associated with any of the following symptoms: conjunctival suffusion; meningeal irritation; anuria or oliguria and/or proteinuria; jaundice; haemorrhages (from the intestines and lungs); cardiac arrhythmia or failure; skin rash; AND a history of exposure to infected animals or an environment contaminated with animal urine.

source.title: Human Leptospirosis: Guidance for Diagnosis, Surveillance and Control

cdc_chlamydia.name: Chlamydia trachomatis Infection — CSTE 2010
cdc_chlamydia.criterion: Confirmed: laboratory-confirmed case. Lab criteria: isolation of C. trachomatis by culture, OR demonstration of C. trachomatis in clinical specimen by detection of antigen or nucleic acid. 2010 definition includes only confirmed classification
cdc_chlamydia.criterion_quote: Isolation of C. trachomatis by culture, OR Demonstration of C. trachomatis in a clinical specimen by detection of antigen or nucleic acid

source.title: Chlamydia trachomatis Infection 2010 Case Definition

cdc_mumps.name: Mumps — CSTE 2012
cdc_mumps.criterion: Confirmed: positive RT-PCR or culture for mumps virus + clinically compatible illness (parotitis or other salivary gland swelling ≥2 days, aseptic meningitis, encephalitis, hearing loss, orchitis, oophoritis, mastitis, or pancreatitis). Probable: acute parotitis or other salivary gland swelling ≥2 days, or orchitis/oophoritis unexplained by alternative diagnosis, PLUS positive serum IgM OR epi link to probable/confirmed case
cdc_mumps.criterion_quote: Acute parotitis or other salivary gland swelling lasting at least 2 days, or orchitis or oophoritis unexplained by another more likely diagnosis

source.title: Mumps 2012 Case Definition

cdc_rubella.name: Rubella — CSTE 2013
cdc_rubella.criterion: Probable clinical: acute generalized maculopapular rash + temp >99.0°F (37.2°C) + arthralgia, arthritis, lymphadenopathy, or conjunctivitis; lacking epi linkage and serologic testing. Confirmed lab: isolation of rubella virus; OR PCR detection of rubella RNA; OR IgG seroconversion/significant rise; OR positive rubella IgM (not explained by MMR vaccination 6–45 days prior). Confirmed epi: probable clinical case + epi link to lab-confirmed case
cdc_rubella.criterion_quote: Acute onset of generalized maculopapular rash; and Temperature greater than 99.0° F or 37.2° C, if measured; and Arthralgia, arthritis, lymphadenopathy, or conjunctivitis

source.title: Rubella 2013 Case Definition

cdc_anaplasmosis.name: Anaplasmosis — CSTE 2024
cdc_anaplasmosis.criterion: Clinical objective: fever, anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hepatic transaminase elevation, or elevated CRP. Subjective: chills/sweats, headache, myalgia, fatigue. Confirmatory: PCR detection of A. phagocytophilum DNA; OR fourfold IFA IgG titer change; OR IHC antigen demonstration; OR isolation with molecular confirmation. Presumptive: IFA IgG ≥1:128; OR morulae in leukocytes within 60 days of onset
cdc_anaplasmosis.criterion_quote: Detection of A. phagocytophilum DNA in a clinical specimen via amplification of a specific target by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) assay

source.title: Anaplasmosis 2024 Case Definition

cdc_ehrlichiosis.name: Ehrlichiosis — CSTE 2024
cdc_ehrlichiosis.criterion: Clinical objective: fever, anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, or hepatic transaminase elevation. Subjective: chills/sweats, headache, myalgia, N/V, fatigue. Confirmatory: PCR detection of E. chaffeensis, E. ewingii, or E. muris eauclairensis DNA; OR fourfold IgG-specific antibody titer change; OR IHC antigen demonstration; OR isolation with molecular confirmation. Presumptive: IFA IgG ≥1:128 reactive with Ehrlichia spp. antigen; OR intracytoplasmic morulae in leukocytes
cdc_ehrlichiosis.criterion_quote: Serological evidence of a fourfold change in immunoglobulin G (IgG)-specific antibody titer

source.title: Ehrlichiosis 2024 Case Definition

cdc_q_fever.name: Q Fever (acute and chronic) — CSTE 2009
cdc_q_fever.criterion: Acute Q fever — Clinical: acute fever ± rigors, myalgia, malaise, severe retrobulbar headache; may include atypical pneumonia, acute hepatitis, meningoencephalitis. Confirmed: fourfold change in IgG phase II antibody titer to C. burnetii between paired sera; OR PCR detection; OR IHC; OR culture. Supportive: single IFA IgG ≥1:128 to phase II antigen. Chronic Q fever — Clinical: infection persisting >6 months (endocarditis with valvular disease). Confirmed: IFA IgG to phase I antigen ≥1:800; OR PCR/IHC/culture. Supportive: phase I IgG titer 1:128 to <1:800
cdc_q_fever.criterion_quote: Serological evidence of a fourfold change in immunoglobulin G (IgG)-specific antibody titer to C. burnetii phase II antigen … Serological evidence of IgG antibody to C. burnetii phase I antigen ≥ 1:800 by IFA

source.title: Q Fever, Acute & Chronic 2009 Case Definition

cdc_babesiosis.name: Babesiosis — CSTE 2011
cdc_babesiosis.criterion: Clinical objective: fever, anemia, or thrombocytopenia. Subjective: chills, sweats, headache, myalgia, arthralgia. Confirmatory: intraerythrocytic Babesia on Giemsa/Wright blood smear; OR PCR detection of B. microti DNA; OR NAAT detection of Babesia spp. genomic sequences; OR isolation by animal inoculation. Supportive: IFA antibody titers at threshold for B. microti, B. divergens, or B. duncani; OR positive immunoblot IgG
cdc_babesiosis.criterion_quote: Identification of intraerythrocytic Babesia organisms by light microscopy in a Giemsa, Wright, or Wright-Giemsa–stained blood smear

source.title: Babesiosis 2011 Case Definition

cdc_shigellosis.name: Shigellosis — CSTE 2017
cdc_shigellosis.criterion: Clinical: illness of variable severity commonly manifested by diarrhea, fever, nausea, cramps, tenesmus; asymptomatic infections may occur. Confirmatory: isolation of Shigella spp. from a clinical specimen. Supportive: detection of Shigella spp. or Shigella/enteroinvasive E. coli (EIEC) by CIDT
cdc_shigellosis.criterion_quote: Isolation of Shigella spp. from a clinical specimen.

source.title: Shigellosis 2017 Case Definition

cdc_stec_hus.name: Shiga Toxin-Producing E. coli (STEC) & Post-Diarrheal HUS — CSTE
cdc_stec_hus.criterion: STEC — Confirmed: isolation of Shiga toxin-producing E. coli from a clinical specimen (E. coli O157:H7 presumed to be Shiga toxin-producing; for other E. coli, demonstrate Shiga toxin production or stx gene presence). Post-diarrheal HUS: BOTH (1) acute-onset anemia with microangiopathic changes (schistocytes, burr cells, helmet cells) on peripheral smear, AND (2) acute-onset renal injury (hematuria, proteinuria, elevated creatinine). Confirmed HUS: acute illness diagnosed as HUS or TTP meeting lab criteria + began within 3 weeks after acute or bloody diarrhea. Platelet <150,000/mm³ within 7 days of GI onset supports diagnosis
cdc_stec_hus.criterion_quote: Isolation of shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli from a clinical specimen. … An acute illness diagnosed as HUS or TTP that both meets the laboratory criteria and began within 3 weeks after onset of an episode of acute or bloody diarrhea

source.title: STEC 2006 + HUS 1996 Case Definitions

cdc_listeriosis.name: Listeriosis — CSTE 2019
cdc_listeriosis.criterion: Clinical (invasive): systemic illness caused by L. monocytogenes — bacteremia, CNS infection (also pneumonia, peritonitis, endocarditis; pregnancy loss / neonatal infection). Confirmatory: isolation of L. monocytogenes from a normally sterile site (blood, CSF, pleural/peritoneal/pericardial/vitreous fluid, orthopedic site). Presumptive: detection by CIDT from sterile site or products of conception
cdc_listeriosis.criterion_quote: Isolation of L. monocytogenes from a specimen collected from a normally sterile site reflective of an invasive infection (e.g., blood or cerebrospinal fluid)

source.title: Listeriosis 2019 Case Definition

cdc_cryptosporidiosis.name: Cryptosporidiosis — CSTE 2012
cdc_cryptosporidiosis.criterion: Clinical: GI illness characterized by diarrhea and ≥1 of: diarrhea duration ≥72 h, abdominal cramping, vomiting, or anorexia. Confirmed lab: DFA, PCR, EIA, or light microscopy of stained specimen. Probable: detection of Cryptosporidium antigen by screening test (immunochromatographic card / rapid card)
cdc_cryptosporidiosis.criterion_quote: A gastrointestinal illness characterized by diarrhea and one or more of the following: diarrhea duration of 72 hours or more, abdominal cramping, vomiting, or anorexia

source.title: Cryptosporidiosis 2012 Case Definition

cdc_giardiasis.name: Giardiasis — CSTE 2011
cdc_giardiasis.criterion: Clinical: illness caused by Giardia lamblia (intestinalis/duodenalis) characterized by GI symptoms — diarrhea, abdominal cramps, bloating, weight loss, malabsorption. Confirmed: detection of Giardia organisms, antigen, or DNA in stool, intestinal fluid, tissue, or biopsy. Probable: clinical description + epi-linked to a confirmed case
cdc_giardiasis.criterion_quote: the detection of Giardia organisms, antigen, or DNA in stool, intestinal fluid, tissue samples, biopsy specimens or other biological sample

source.title: Giardiasis 2011 Case Definition

cdc_campylobacteriosis.name: Campylobacteriosis — CSTE 2015
cdc_campylobacteriosis.criterion: Clinical: illness of variable severity manifested by diarrhea, abdominal pain, nausea, sometimes vomiting; may rarely present as invasive infection (bacteremia, meningitis, other focal infections). Confirmed: isolation of Campylobacter spp. from clinical specimen. Probable: detection by culture-independent diagnostic test (CIDT)
cdc_campylobacteriosis.criterion_quote: Isolation of Campylobacter spp. from a clinical specimen.

source.title: Campylobacteriosis 2015 Case Definition

cdc_cre.name: Carbapenemase-Producing CRE (CP-CRE) — CSTE 2018
cdc_cre.criterion: E. coli, Klebsiella spp., or Enterobacter spp. isolate that is EITHER (1) positive for known carbapenemase resistance mechanism (KPC, NDM, VIM, IMP, OXA-48) by a recognized test (PCR; Xpert Carba-R); OR (2) positive on phenotypic test for carbapenemase production (metallo-β-lactamase test e.g., E-test; modified Hodge test; Carba NP; CIM/mCIM)
cdc_cre.criterion_quote: Positive for known carbapenemase resistance mechanism (e.g., KPC, NDM, VIM, IMP, OXA-48) demonstrated by a recognized test

source.title: CP-CRE 2018 Case Definition

cdc_candida_auris.name: Candida auris (clinical infection) — CSTE 2023
cdc_candida_auris.criterion: Confirmed: detection of C. auris in a clinical specimen obtained during the normal course of care for diagnostic or treatment purposes using either culture OR a validated culture-independent test (NAAT). Includes invasive sites (blood, CSF) and non-invasive sites (wounds, urine, respiratory tract). Excludes screening swabs (separate screening case definition)
cdc_candida_auris.criterion_quote: Detection of C. auris in a clinical specimen obtained during the normal course of care for diagnostic or treatment purposes using either culture or a validated culture-independent test (e.g., NAAT)

source.title: Candida auris, clinical 2023 Case Definition

cdc_rmsf.name: Spotted Fever Rickettsiosis (incl. RMSF) — CSTE 2020
cdc_rmsf.criterion: Clinical: fever AND ≥1 of: rash, eschar, headache, myalgia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, or any hepatic transaminase elevation. Confirmatory: PCR detection of SFGR nucleic acid; OR fourfold IFA IgG titer change; OR SFGR antigen by IHC; OR isolation with molecular confirmation. Presumptive: IFA IgG ≥1:128 to SFGR antigen within 60 days of onset
cdc_rmsf.criterion_quote: Fever as reported by the patient or a healthcare provider, AND one or more of the following: rash, eschar, headache, myalgia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, or any hepatic transaminase elevation

source.title: Spotted Fever Rickettsiosis (including RMSF) 2020 Case Definition

cdc_arboviral_wnv.name: Arboviral Disease (incl. West Nile virus) — CSTE 2015
cdc_arboviral_wnv.criterion: Neuroinvasive: meningitis, encephalitis, acute flaccid paralysis, or other acute signs of central or peripheral neurologic dysfunction documented by physician + absence of more likely clinical explanation. Non-neuroinvasive: fever/chills + absence of neuroinvasive disease + absence of more likely explanation. Confirmed lab: viral isolation/antigen/nucleic acid in tissue/blood/CSF; OR fourfold antibody titer change in paired sera; OR virus-specific IgM in serum with confirmatory neutralizing antibodies; OR virus-specific IgM in CSF
cdc_arboviral_wnv.criterion_quote: Meningitis, encephalitis, acute flaccid paralysis, or other acute signs of central or peripheral neurologic dysfunction, as documented by a physician, AND Absence of a more likely clinical explanation.

source.title: Arboviral Diseases (incl. WNV) 2015 Case Definition

hys_law_fda.name: Hy's Law — FDA canonical definition (severe DILI signal)
hys_law_fda.criterion: 3 components: (1) drug causes hepatocellular injury (incidence of ALT or AST ≥3× ULN higher than control/placebo); (2) among subjects with such aminotransferase elevations, ≥1 also shows serum total bilirubin >2× ULN without initial cholestasis (no substantial ALP elevation); (3) no other cause (HAV/HBV/HCV, preexisting liver disease, other hepatotoxic drug). Implies severe DILI; ~10% of subjects with hyperbilirubinemia/jaundice die or need liver transplant
hys_law_fda.criterion_quote: Hy's Law cases have the following three components: 1. The drug causes hepatocellular injury, generally shown by a higher incidence of 3-fold or greater elevations above the ULN of ALT or AST than the (nonhepatotoxic) control drug or placebo. 2. Among trial subjects showing such AT elevations, often with ATs much greater than 3xULN, one or more also show elevation of serum TBL to >2xULN, without initial findings of cholestasis (elevated serum ALP). 3. No other reason can be found to explain the combination of increased AT and TBL.

source.title: Guidance for Industry — Drug-Induced Liver Injury: Premarketing Clinical Evaluation

qtc_prolongation_fda.name: QT/QTc Prolongation — FDA / ICH E14
qtc_prolongation_fda.criterion: Categorical thresholds for absolute QTc: >450 ms, >480 ms, >500 ms. Change from baseline: >30 ms, >60 ms. QTc >500 ms during therapy is a threshold of particular concern for torsades. Trial enrollment exclusion typically QTc >450 ms at baseline. Common discontinuation thresholds: QTc >500 ms or increase >60 ms from baseline. AHA/ACCF/HRS 2009 sex-specific borderline: >450 ms men, >470 ms women
qtc_prolongation_fda.criterion_quote: Absolute QTc interval prolongation: - QTc interval > 450 - QTc interval > 480 - QTc interval > 500 • Change from baseline in QTc interval: - QTc interval increases from baseline >30 - QTc interval increases from baseline >60 … In clinical trials, a prolongation of QTc > 500 ms during therapy has been a threshold of particular concern. … While increases in QT/QTc to >500 ms or of >60 ms over baseline are commonly used as thresholds for potential discontinuation

source.title: E14 Clinical Evaluation of QT/QTc Interval Prolongation

pllr_pregnancy.name: FDA Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule (PLLR)
pllr_pregnancy.criterion: Effective June 30, 2015, PLLR removed pregnancy categories A, B, C, D, X from prescription drug labeling and replaced them with 3 narrative subsections under USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS: 8.1 Pregnancy (Pregnancy Exposure Registry, Risk Summary, Clinical Considerations, Data), 8.2 Lactation, 8.3 Females and Males of Reproductive Potential
pllr_pregnancy.criterion_quote: The Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule (PLLR or final rule) requires changes to the content and format for information presented in prescription drug labeling … The final rule removes pregnancy letter categories — A, B, C, D and X.

source.title: Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Final Rule

child_pugh_fda.name: FDA Hepatic Impairment Dosing — Child-Pugh classification
child_pugh_fda.criterion: Child-Pugh score to categorize hepatic impairment for PK dosing studies. Class A (mild, 5–6 points), Class B (moderate, 7–9 points), Class C (severe, 10–15 points). Components scored 1–3 each: bilirubin (<2 / 2–3 / >3 mg/dL); albumin (>3.5 / 2.8–3.5 / <2.8 g/dL); PT prolonged (<4 / 4–6 / >6 sec); ascites (absent/slight/moderate); encephalopathy (none / grade 1–2 / grade 3–4). Dosing in severe (Class C) generally contraindicated
child_pugh_fda.criterion_quote: Assessment as good operative risk (A or mild) if 5 or 6 points; moderate risk (B or moderate) if 7 to 9 points; and poor operative risk (C or severe) if 10 to 15 points. … this guidance recommends that the Child-Pugh classification be used to categorize the degree of hepatic impairment in patients … dosing in the severe category would generally be contraindicated

source.title: Guidance for Industry — PK in Patients with Impaired Hepatic Function

child_pugh_fda.notes: Also used clinically in [[child_pugh]] for cirrhosis prognosis.

renal_egfr_fda.name: FDA Renal Impairment Dosing — eGFR categorization
renal_egfr_fda.criterion: FDA 2024 recommends eGFR over Cockcroft-Gault for renal function assessment in dosing studies. For dosing recommendations, use ABSOLUTE eGFR (mL/min) = indexed eGFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) × patient BSA / 1.73. KDIGO categories: G1 ≥90, G2 60–89, G3a 45–59, G3b 30–44, G4 15–29, G5 <15 mL/min/1.73 m²
renal_egfr_fda.criterion_quote: the recommendation of using estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) over the Cockcroft-Gault (CG) equation to determine renal function … When providing dosing recommendations, FDA recommends use of absolute eGFR calculated by multiplying the indexed eGFR by the patient's body surface area (BSA) and dividing by 1.73

source.title: Pharmacokinetics in Patients With Impaired Renal Function — Guidance for Industry

pcp_prophylaxis_hiv.name: PCP Primary Prophylaxis in HIV — CD4 threshold
pcp_prophylaxis_hiv.criterion: Primary PCP prophylaxis indicated for HIV-infected adults/adolescents with CD4 count <200 cells/mm³ who are not on ART or initiating ART. Preferred: TMP-SMX 1 DS or SS PO daily. Discontinue after sustained CD4 ≥200 cells/mm³ for ≥3 months on ART
pcp_prophylaxis_hiv.criterion_quote: Indications for Initiating Primary Prophylaxis for Patients Not on ART and Patients Starting ART • CD4 count <200 cells/mm3 (AI)

source.title: Guidelines for the Prevention and Treatment of OI in Adults and Adolescents with HIV — PCP

mac_prophylaxis_hiv.name: Disseminated MAC Prophylaxis in HIV — CD4 threshold
mac_prophylaxis_hiv.criterion: Primary MAC prophylaxis NOT recommended for HIV+ persons who immediately initiate ART regardless of CD4. Indicated only for CD4 <50 cells/mm³ AND not receiving ART, remain viremic on ART, or no options for fully suppressive ART. Rule out disseminated MAC first. Preferred: azithromycin 1,200 mg PO weekly or clarithromycin 500 mg PO BID
mac_prophylaxis_hiv.criterion_quote: Primary prophylaxis is not recommended for people with HIV who immediately initiate ART, regardless of CD4 count (AII). Indications for Primary Prophylaxis • CD4 count <50 cells/mm3 and not receiving ART, remain viremic on ART, or have no options for a fully suppressive ART regimen (AI)

source.title: Guidelines for OI Prevention in HIV — Disseminated MAC

toxoplasma_prophylaxis_hiv.name: Toxoplasma Encephalitis Prophylaxis in HIV — CD4 threshold
toxoplasma_prophylaxis_hiv.criterion: Primary TE prophylaxis indicated for Toxoplasma IgG-seropositive HIV+ adults/adolescents with CD4 count <100 cells/mm³. Preferred: TMP-SMX 1 DS PO daily (also covers PCP)
toxoplasma_prophylaxis_hiv.criterion_quote: Indications for Initiating Primary Prophylaxis • Toxoplasma IgG positive patients with CD4 count <100 cells/mm3 (AII) … Toxoplasma-seropositive people who have CD4 counts <100 cells/mm3 should receive prophylaxis against TE. The preferred primary prophylaxis regimen is one double-strength tablet daily of TMP-SMX (AII).

source.title: Guidelines for OI Prevention in HIV — Toxoplasma gondii Encephalitis

aids_case_def.name: AIDS Surveillance Case Definition (1993 expanded)
aids_case_def.criterion: AIDS in HIV-infected adolescents/adults: CD4 count <200 cells/µL OR CD4% <14% of total lymphocytes; OR any of 26 AIDS-defining clinical conditions (23 from 1987 definition + 3 added in 1993: pulmonary TB, recurrent pneumonia ≥2 episodes in 1 year, invasive cervical cancer). Effective for US case reporting January 1, 1993
aids_case_def.criterion_quote: the case definition for AIDS among adolescents and adults has been expanded to include all HIV-infected persons who have less than 200 CD4+ T-lymphocytes/uL, or a CD4+ T-lymphocyte percentage of total lymphocytes of less than 14. This expansion includes the addition of three clinical conditions — pulmonary tuberculosis, recurrent pneumonia, and invasive cervical cancer — and retains the 23 clinical conditions in the AIDS surveillance case definition published in 1987

source.title: 1993 Revised Classification System for HIV Infection and Expanded Surveillance Case Definition for AIDS

osteoporosis_nih.name: Osteoporosis — NIH Consensus Definition 2000
osteoporosis_nih.criterion: Osteoporosis is a skeletal disorder characterized by compromised bone strength predisposing to increased fracture risk. Bone strength reflects integration of two main features: bone density AND bone quality (architecture, turnover, damage accumulation [microfractures], mineralization)
osteoporosis_nih.criterion_quote: Osteoporosis is defined as a skeletal disorder characterized by compromised bone strength predisposing a person to an increased risk of fracture. Bone strength reflects the integration of two main features: bone density and bone quality.

source.title: Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis, and therapy (JAMA 2001)

recist_1_1.name: RECIST 1.1 — Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours
recist_1_1.criterion: Measurable target lesions: ≥10 mm longest diameter on CT (slice ≤5 mm), ≥10 mm caliper on clinical exam, ≥20 mm on CXR; lymph nodes ≥15 mm in short axis. Up to 5 target lesions total (max 2/organ). CR: disappearance of all target lesions; pathological lymph nodes reduced to <10 mm short axis. PR: ≥30% decrease in sum of diameters of target lesions vs baseline. PD: ≥20% increase in sum of target diameters (relative to smallest sum on study) AND absolute ≥5 mm increase, OR appearance of new lesions. SD: neither PR nor PD
recist_1_1.criterion_quote: Complete Response (CR): Disappearance of all target lesions. Any pathological lymph nodes (whether target or non-target) must have reduction in short axis to <10 mm. Partial Response (PR): At least a 30% decrease in the sum of diameters of target lesions, taking as reference the baseline sum diameters. Progressive Disease (PD): At least a 20% increase in the sum of diameters of target lesions, taking as reference the smallest sum on study … the sum must also demonstrate an absolute increase of at least 5 mm.

source.title: New response evaluation criteria in solid tumours: Revised RECIST guideline (v1.1)

rutherford_pad.name: Peripheral Arterial Disease — Rutherford Classification
rutherford_pad.criterion: 7-category clinical staging of lower-extremity PAD: Category 0 asymptomatic; 1 mild claudication; 2 moderate claudication; 3 severe claudication; 4 ischemic rest pain; 5 minor tissue loss (ischemic ulceration not exceeding ulcer of digits); 6 major tissue loss (severe ischemic ulcers or frank gangrene). Categories 4–6 = critical limb ischemia
rutherford_pad.criterion_quote: Category 0: asymptomatic; Category 1: mild claudication; Category 2: moderate claudication; Category 3: severe claudication; Category 4: rest pain; Category 5: minor tissue loss; ischemic ulceration not exceeding the ulcer of the digits; Category 6: major tissue loss; severe ischemic ulcers or frank gangrene

source.title: Reporting standards for lower extremity ischemia

fontaine_pad.name: Peripheral Arterial Disease — Fontaine Classification
fontaine_pad.criterion: 4-stage classification of chronic lower-limb ischemia: Stage I asymptomatic; IIa intermittent claudication >200 m; IIb intermittent claudication <200 m; III ischemic rest pain; IV ulcers or gangrene
fontaine_pad.criterion_quote: Stage I: asymptomatic; Stage IIa: intermittent claudication after walking a distance of more than 200 meters; Stage IIb: intermittent claudication after walking a distance of less than 200 meters; Stage III: rest pain; Stage IV: ulcers or gangrene of the limb

source.title: Surgical treatment of peripheral circulation disorders

abi.name: Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) — AHA/ACC thresholds
abi.criterion: Resting ABI categories: normal 1.00–1.40; borderline 0.91–0.99; abnormal/PAD ≤0.90; non-compressible >1.40; severe PAD ≤0.40. Toe-Brachial Index (TBI) used when ABI non-compressible
abi.criterion_quote: ABI results should be reported with noncompressible values defined as greater than 1.40, normal as 1.00 to 1.40, borderline as 0.91 to 0.99, and abnormal as 0.90 or less.

source.title: 2016 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Patients With Lower Extremity PAD

wifi_cli.name: Critical Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI) — SVS WIfI staging
wifi_cli.criterion: Three components scored 0–3 each. Wound (W): 0 no ulcer/gangrene; 1 small superficial ulcer no gangrene; 2 deep ulcer with exposed bone/joint/tendon ± gangrene limited to digits; 3 deep extensive ulcer involving forefoot/midfoot ± calcaneal involvement ± extensive gangrene. Ischemia (I): graded by ABI, ankle SBP, and TP/TcPO₂ — 0 ABI ≥0.80 / ankle SBP >100 / TP ≥60; 1 ABI 0.6–0.79 / ankle SBP 70–100 / TP 40–59; 2 ABI 0.4–0.59 / ankle SBP 50–70 / TP 30–39; 3 ABI ≤0.39 / ankle SBP <50 / TP <30 mmHg. Foot Infection (fI, IDSA/IWGDF-aligned): 0 uninfected; 1 mild (erythema >0.5 to ≤2 cm); 2 moderate (erythema >2 cm or deeper structures); 3 severe (SIRS). Composite stages 1–4 stratify 1-year amputation risk and benefit of revascularization
wifi_cli.criterion_quote: Wound Grade 0: No ulcer or gangrene (ischemic pain at rest); … Ischemia Grade 0: ABI ≥0.80, Ankle SBP >100 mmHg, TP/TcPO2 ≥60 mmHg; … Foot Infection Grade 1: Mild local infection, involving only the skin and subcutaneous tissue, erythema > 0.5 to ≤ 2 cm.

source.title: WIfI classification system

aaa_svs.name: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) — SVS 2018 thresholds
aaa_svs.criterion: Elective repair: low/acceptable-risk patient with fusiform AAA ≥5.5 cm (men); 5.0–5.4 cm (women). Surveillance imaging intervals: 3.0–3.9 cm every 3 years; 4.0–4.9 cm annually; 5.0–5.4 cm every 6 months. Additional repair triggers: rapid growth >0.5 cm/6 months, symptomatic aneurysm, saccular morphology
aaa_svs.criterion_quote: We recommend elective repair for the patient at low or acceptable surgical risk with a fusiform AAA that is ≥5.5 cm. … We suggest repair in women with AAA between 5.0 cm and 5.4 cm in maximum diameter. … 3.0-3.9 cm: We suggest surveillance imaging at 3-year intervals; 4.0-4.9 cm: We suggest surveillance imaging at 12-month intervals; 5.0-5.4 cm: We suggest surveillance imaging at 6-month intervals.

source.title: SVS practice guidelines on the care of patients with AAA (Chaikof 2018)

nascet_carotid.name: Carotid Stenosis — NASCET classification + CEA indications
nascet_carotid.criterion: NASCET measurement: (1 − [narrowest diameter / distal normal ICA diameter]) × 100. Categories: low-moderate <50%, high-moderate 50–69%, severe 70–99%, near-occlusion, occlusion. CEA beneficial: symptomatic stenosis 70–99% (strong benefit), 50–69% (moderate benefit); asymptomatic ≥70% (modest benefit in selected low-risk patients with good life expectancy)
nascet_carotid.criterion_quote: low-moderate (<50%), high-moderate (50-69%), and severe (≥70%) stenosis … CEA is beneficial in symptomatic patients with high-grade carotid stenosis (70% to 99%).

source.title: Benefit of carotid endarterectomy in patients with symptomatic moderate or severe stenosis

ceap.name: Chronic Venous Disease — CEAP Classification (C component, 2020 update)
ceap.criterion: Clinical (C0–C6): C0 no signs of venous disease; C1 telangiectasia or reticular veins; C2 varicose veins (C2r if recurrent); C3 ankle edema from venous reflux; C4 skin damage (4a pigmentation/eczema; 4b lipodermatosclerosis/atrophie blanche; 4c corona phlebectatica); C5 healed venous ulcer; C6 active venous ulcer (C6r recurrent). Full CEAP also includes Etiology (Ec congenital, Ep primary, Es secondary, En no cause), Anatomy (As superficial, Ad deep, Ap perforator, An no), Pathophysiology (Pr reflux, Po obstruction, Pr,o both, Pn neither)
ceap.criterion_quote: C0: No signs of venous disease that can be seen or felt; C1: Small thread veins, spider veins, or broken veins (telangiectasia); C2: Varicose veins; C3: Ankle swelling (oedema) caused by varicose veins or underlying venous reflux; C4: Skin damage caused by varicose veins or venous reflux; C5: Healed venous leg ulcer; C6: Active venous leg ulcer

source.org: Lurie F et al. (American Venous Forum)
source.title: The 2020 update of the CEAP classification system and reporting standards

isl_lymphedema.name: Lymphedema — ISL Staging (0–III)
isl_lymphedema.criterion: Stage 0 (subclinical): impaired lymph transport without visible edema. Stage I: early — visible accumulation of protein-rich fluid; subsides with elevation; pitting edema. Stage II: increased swelling not subsiding with elevation; pitting initially evident, progressing to tissue fibrosis. Stage III (lymphostatic elephantiasis): hard fibrotic tissue, no pitting; further size increase; skin changes (papillomatosis, hyperkeratosis). Severity within stages: mild <20%, moderate 20–40%, severe >40% volume difference
isl_lymphedema.criterion_quote: Stage 0: subclinical state where swelling is not evident despite impairments in lymph transport; Stage 1: early onset of the condition with a visible accumulation of protein-rich tissue fluid, which subsides with elevation; Stage 2: increase in swelling, which does not subside with elevation. Pitting is initially evident; however, over time the tissue continues to harden; Stage 3: tissue in this stage becomes harder (more fibrotic) and pitting is absent.

source.title: The diagnosis and treatment of peripheral lymphedema: 2020 Consensus Document

yellow_fever_who.name: Yellow Fever — WHO Case Definition
yellow_fever_who.criterion: Suspected: acute onset of fever with jaundice appearing within 14 days of symptom onset. Probable: suspected case + positive yellow fever IgM in absence of YF immunization within 30 days. Confirmed: probable case + absence of YF immunization (within 14–30 days depending on test) + detection of YF-specific neutralizing antibodies OR PCR detection of YF virus genome OR antigen detection in blood/liver OR virus isolation
yellow_fever_who.criterion_quote: Suspected case: Any person with acute onset of fever, with jaundice appearing within 14 days of onset of the first symptoms.

source.title: Yellow Fever: Vaccine Preventable Diseases Surveillance Standards

plague_who.name: Plague — WHO Outbreak Toolbox Case Definition
plague_who.criterion: Suspected: clinical signs suggestive of plague (bubonic, pneumonic, or septicemic) in a favourable epidemiological context. Probable: suspected + positive F1 rapid diagnostic test, serological test, or PCR, where Y. pestis culture is negative or not performed. Confirmed: suspected + isolation of Y. pestis by culture, OR seroconversion (fourfold anti-F1 antibody rise), OR positive RDT plus PCR
plague_who.criterion_quote: Suspected case (S) includes all persons presenting clinical signs suggestive of plague in a favourable epidemiological context; Probable case (P) includes any suspected person with a positive result for one of certain tests (F1 rapid diagnostic test, serological test or PCR), where isolation of Y. pestis by culture has not been performed or is negative; Confirmed case (C) includes any suspected person for whom an isolation of Y. pestis by culture has been performed or a conversion observed or positive RDT and PCR.

source.title: Plague Outbreak Toolbox

mers_cov.name: MERS-CoV — WHO Case Definition
mers_cov.criterion: Confirmed: person with laboratory confirmation of MERS-CoV infection (irrespective of clinical signs/symptoms). Molecular confirmation: (a) positive NAAT/RT-PCR for ≥2 specific genomic targets (upE, ORF1a, ORF1b, or N); OR (b) single positive target + sequencing of a second target (preferably RdRp/nsp12 or N). Serologic confirmation: seroconversion in 2 samples ≥14 days apart by both screening assay (ELISA, IFA) and neutralization assay
mers_cov.criterion_quote: A confirmed case is a person with laboratory confirmation of MERS-CoV infection, irrespective of clinical signs and symptoms.

source.title: MERS — Case definition for reporting to WHO (interim, revised 26 July 2017)

sars_2003.name: SARS — WHO 2003 Historical Case Definition
sars_2003.criterion: Suspect: person presenting after 1 Nov 2002 with high fever (>38°C) + cough or breathing difficulty + ≥1 exposure in 10 days prior (close contact with suspect/probable SARS case; travel to or residence in area with recent local SARS transmission). Probable: suspect case + (radiographic infiltrates consistent with pneumonia or RDS; OR positive for SARS-CoV by ≥1 assay; OR autopsy findings consistent with RDS pathology without identifiable alternative)
sars_2003.criterion_quote: Suspect case: A person presenting after 1 November 2002 with history of: high fever (>38 °C), AND cough or breathing difficulty, AND one or more of the following exposures during the 10 days prior to onset of symptoms: close contact with a person who is a suspect or probable case of SARS; history of travel, to an area with recent local transmission of SARS; residing in an area with recent local transmission of SARS.

source.title: Case definitions for surveillance of SARS — revised 1 May 2003

snakebite_envenomation.name: Snakebite Envenomation — WHO SEARO severity
snakebite_envenomation.criterion: Systemic envenoming = ≥1 of: (a) neurotoxic (ptosis, external ophthalmoplegia, bulbar/respiratory paralysis); (b) hemostatic abnormalities — incoagulable blood by 20-minute whole-blood clotting test (20WBCT: few mL fresh blood in clean dry glass tube, undisturbed 20 min, failure to clot = positive), spontaneous systemic bleeding; (c) cardiovascular (hypotension/shock, arrhythmia); (d) acute kidney injury (oliguria/anuria, rising creatinine); (e) rhabdomyolysis (muscle pain/tenderness, myoglobinuria, elevated CK). Any systemic sign → antivenom indication
snakebite_envenomation.criterion_quote: Incoagulable blood demonstrated by the 20-minute whole blood clotting test (20WBCT) … place a few millilitres of freshly collected blood into a clean dry glass test tube; leave undisturbed for 20 minutes; then tip once: if the blood is still liquid the test is positive (incoagulable blood) and indicates systemic envenoming.

source.title: Guidelines for the Management of Snakebites, 2nd edition

tularemia.name: Tularemia — CSTE 2017
tularemia.criterion: Confirmed: clinically-compatible illness with isolation of Francisella tularensis from clinical/autopsy specimen, OR fourfold or greater change in serum antibody titer between acute and convalescent specimens. Probable: clinically-compatible illness + supportive lab (elevated single F. tularensis antibody titer, detection by DFA/IHC, or PCR detection). Clinical forms: ulceroglandular, glandular, oculoglandular, oropharyngeal, pneumonic, typhoidal
tularemia.criterion_quote: Isolation of F. tularensis in a clinical or autopsy specimen, OR Fourfold or greater change in serum antibody titer to F. tularensis antigen between acute and convalescent specimens.

source.title: Tularemia 2017 Case Definition

cyclosporiasis.name: Cyclosporiasis — CSTE 2010
cyclosporiasis.criterion: Confirmed: clinically-compatible illness (watery diarrhea, often prolonged/relapsing) + lab confirmation by detection of Cyclospora cayetanensis organisms (oocysts) or DNA in stool, intestinal fluid/aspirate, or intestinal biopsy. Probable: clinically-compatible case epidemiologically linked to a confirmed case. Detection: oocysts on modified acid-fast stain or autofluorescence under UV microscopy; PCR increasingly used
cyclosporiasis.criterion_quote: A case that meets the clinical description and at least one of the criteria for laboratory confirmation [detection of Cyclospora organisms or DNA in stool, intestinal fluid/aspirate, or intestinal biopsy specimens].

source.title: Cyclosporiasis 2010 Case Definition

vibriosis.name: Vibriosis (non-cholera) — CSTE 2017
vibriosis.criterion: Confirmed: isolation of a species of family Vibrionaceae (other than toxigenic V. cholerae O1 or O139) from a clinical specimen by culture. Probable: detection of Vibrio species by CIDT, OR clinically compatible case epi-linked to a case meeting supportive/confirmatory lab criteria. Common species: V. parahaemolyticus, V. vulnificus, V. alginolyticus
vibriosis.criterion_quote: Confirmed: A case that meets the confirmed laboratory criteria for diagnosis [isolation of a species of the family Vibrionaceae (other than toxigenic Vibrio cholerae O1 or O139) from a clinical specimen].

source.title: Vibriosis 2017 Case Definition

rabies_human.name: Rabies, Human — CSTE 2011
rabies_human.criterion: Confirmed: clinically-compatible case (acute progressive encephalomyelitis usually progressing to coma/death within 10 days) + lab confirmation at state or federal public health lab. Confirmation includes: rabies viral antigens (DFA on brain/nuchal skin biopsy); isolation of rabies virus in cell culture or laboratory animal; rabies viral nucleic acid (RT-PCR) in saliva/CSF/tissue; OR rabies-neutralizing antibody titer in CSF or serum of an unvaccinated person
rabies_human.criterion_quote: A clinically compatible case that is laboratory confirmed [by testing at a state or federal public health laboratory].

source.title: Rabies, Human 2011 Case Definition

zika_noncongenital.name: Non-congenital Zika Virus Disease — CSTE 2024
zika_noncongenital.criterion: Confirmed: epi-linkage + clinical (acute onset of fever, maculopapular rash, arthralgia, or non-purulent conjunctivitis) + confirmatory lab (Zika RNA by NAAT in serum/plasma/whole blood/urine/CSF/amniotic fluid; OR Zika antigen detection or culture isolation; OR Zika PRNT titer ≥10 with negative dengue PRNT). Probable: epi-linkage + clinical + presumptive lab (Zika IgM by validated assay with negative/undetermined NAAT/PRNT)
zika_noncongenital.criterion_quote: Confirmed: Meets the epidemiologic linkage criteria, and clinical and confirmatory laboratory criteria for non-congenital Zika virus disease.

source.title: Non-congenital Zika Virus Disease 2024 Case Definition

plague_cste.name: Plague (Y. pestis) — CSTE 2020
plague_cste.criterion: Confirmed: clinically-compatible case + confirmatory lab (isolation of Y. pestis with culture identification validated by secondary assay, OR fourfold change in paired serum F1 antibody titer); OR clinically-compatible + presumptive lab + epi-linkage. Probable: clinically-compatible + presumptive lab (elevated single F1 antibody titer without vaccination history, OR detection of Y. pestis DNA/antigen including F1 by DFA/IHC/PCR) without epi-linkage in absence of alternative diagnosis. Bioterrorism Category A select agent
plague_cste.criterion_quote: Confirmed: A clinically-compatible case with confirmatory laboratory evidence, OR A clinically-compatible case with presumptive laboratory evidence AND epidemiologic linkage.

source.title: Plague (Yersinia pestis) 2020 Case Definition

coccidioidomycosis_cste.name: Coccidioidomycosis — CSTE 2023 (surveillance)
coccidioidomycosis_cste.criterion: Low-incidence jurisdictions — Confirmed: confirmatory lab (culture isolation, histopathologic/cytologic spherules, OR NAAT detection of Coccidioides) + epi-linkage OR clinical; OR presumptive lab (positive serology by EIA, IFA, immunodiffusion, or complement fixation, OR positive coccidioidal skin test in previously negative individual) + epi-linkage + clinical. Probable: confirmatory lab alone without clinical/epi-link; OR presumptive lab with either. High-incidence jurisdictions — confirmed: confirmatory OR presumptive lab alone
coccidioidomycosis_cste.criterion_quote: Confirmed (Low-Incidence Jurisdictions): A case that meets confirmatory laboratory evidence AND either epidemiologic linkage OR clinical criteria, OR A case that meets presumptive laboratory evidence AND epidemiologic linkage AND clinical criteria.

source.title: Coccidioidomycosis 2023 Case Definition

atls_hemorrhagic_shock.name: Hemorrhagic Shock — ATLS Classification
atls_hemorrhagic_shock.criterion: Class I: blood loss <15% (~750 mL); HR <100; normal BP, pulse pressure, RR; UO >30 mL/hr. Class II: 15–30% (750–1500 mL); HR 100–120; RR 20–24; narrowed pulse pressure; SBP unchanged-slightly decreased; UO 20–30 mL/hr. Class III: 30–40% (1500–2000 mL); HR >120; significantly elevated RR; SBP drops; altered mental status; UO 5–15 mL/hr; delayed cap refill. Class IV: >40%; HR >120; narrow pulse pressure (<25 mmHg); hypotension; markedly altered mental status; UO minimal/absent
atls_hemorrhagic_shock.criterion_quote: Volume loss up to 15% of total blood volume, approximately 750 mL. … Volume loss from 15% to 30% … Volume loss from 30% to 40% of total blood volume, from 1500 mL to 2000 mL. A significant drop in blood pressure and changes in mental status occurs. … Volume loss over 40% of total blood volume. Hypotension with narrow pulse pressure (less than 25 mmHg).

source.title: ATLS 10th edition (via StatPearls)

shock_index.name: Shock Index (HR/SBP)
shock_index.criterion: SI = HR / SBP. Normal 0.5–0.7; >0.9 concerning (higher transfusion and mortality rates); >1.0 critical. Useful in trauma triage to flag compensated shock in patients who appear normotensive
shock_index.criterion_quote: Normal shock index ranges from 0.5 to 0.7; values above 0.9-1.0 correlate with higher rates of transfusion, ICU admission, and in-hospital mortality.

source.title: Shock Index in the Emergency Department: Utility and Limitations

abc_score.name: ABC Score — Massive Transfusion Need Prediction
abc_score.criterion: 1 point each: penetrating mechanism; SBP ≤90 in ED; HR ≥120 in ED; positive FAST. Score ≥2 predicts need for massive transfusion (sensitivity ~75%, specificity ~86%)
abc_score.criterion_quote: The ABC score assesses four clinical variables: penetrating mechanism, systolic BP ≤ 90 mmHg in the Emergency Department, HR ≥ 120 in the Emergency Department, and positive FAST ultrasound. Each variable receives 1 point if present.

source.title: Early prediction of massive transfusion in trauma: simple as ABC

lethal_triad.name: Lethal Triad — Damage Control Surgery Trigger
lethal_triad.criterion: Triad triggering damage control strategy (abbreviated laparotomy, damage-control resuscitation): (1) hypothermia (T <34–35°C); (2) acidosis (pH <7.2); (3) coagulopathy. Recognition triggers shortened operative time, abdominal packing, and ICU resuscitation before definitive repair
lethal_triad.criterion_quote: The lethal triad describes the metabolic derangement of hypothermia, coagulopathy and metabolic acidosis which leads to a 'vicious, bloody cycle' and subsequent physiological exhaustion.

source.title: Damage Control Surgery — physiopathological benchmarks

aast_liver.name: AAST Liver Injury Scale (Grade I–V)
aast_liver.criterion: Grade I: subcapsular hematoma <10% surface, capsular tear <1 cm depth. Grade II: subcapsular 10–50% or intraparenchymal <10 cm; laceration 1–3 cm depth, <10 cm length. Grade III: subcapsular >50% or ruptured; intraparenchymal >10 cm or expanding; laceration >3 cm depth. Grade IV: parenchymal disruption 25–75% of hepatic lobe or 1–3 Couinaud segments. Grade V: parenchymal disruption >75% of lobe or >3 Couinaud segments within a single lobe; juxtahepatic venous injury (retrohepatic IVC, central major hepatic veins)
aast_liver.criterion_quote: Grade I: hematoma, subcapsular < 10% surface area; laceration, capsular tear < 1 cm parenchymal depth. … Grade V: laceration, parenchymal disruption involving >75% of hepatic lobe or >3 Couinaud segments within a single lobe; vascular, juxtahepatic venous injuries.

source.title: Organ injury scaling 2018 update: spleen, liver, and kidney

aast_spleen.name: AAST Spleen Injury Scale (Grade I–V)
aast_spleen.criterion: Grade I: subcapsular hematoma <10% or laceration <1 cm. Grade II: hematoma 10–50% or laceration 1–3 cm. Grade III: hematoma >50% or expanding, OR laceration >3 cm or involving trabecular vessel. Grade IV: laceration involving segmental/hilar vessel with >25% devascularization. Grade V: shattered spleen or complete hilar vascular injury / devascularization
aast_spleen.criterion_quote: Grade I: Subcapsular hematoma <10%, capsular laceration <1 cm. Grade II: Hematoma 10% to 50%, laceration 1 to 3 cm. Grade III: Hematoma >50% or expanding, laceration >3 cm or involving a trabecular vessel. Grade IV: Laceration involving segmental/hilar vessel, >25% devascularization. Grade V: Shattered spleen or complete devascularization.

source.title: AAST Spleen Organ Injury Scale (via StatPearls)

aast_kidney.name: AAST Kidney Injury Scale — 2018 update
aast_kidney.criterion: Grade I: contusion or subcapsular hematoma. Grade II: laceration ≤1 cm without urine extravasation; perirenal hematoma <1 cm. Grade III: laceration >1 cm without collecting system involvement; no urine extravasation; perirenal hematoma confined within perirenal fascia. Grade IV: laceration extending into collecting system; segmental renal vessel injury or segmental infarction; laceration of renal pelvis/UPJ. Grade V: shattered kidney; avulsion of renal hilum / laceration of main renal artery or vein causing devascularization. Vascular contrast extravasation on CT upgrades grade
aast_kidney.criterion_quote: Grade III injuries are characterized by lacerations greater than 1 cm in depth that do not involve the urinary collecting system, with no evidence of urine extravasation, and may include a perirenal hematoma confined within the perirenal fascia. … Lacerations extending to the collecting system, injuries of the renal main vessels with hemorrhage or segmental infarctions … are the hallmark of grade IV. … Grade V renal injuries entail different mechanisms of disruption of main renal artery or veins.

source.title: AAST Updated Kidney Injury Scale

aast_pancreas.name: AAST Pancreas Injury Scale (Grade I–V)
aast_pancreas.criterion: Grade I: minor contusion or superficial laceration WITHOUT ductal injury. Grade II: major contusion or laceration without ductal injury or tissue loss. Grade III: distal transection or parenchymal injury WITH ductal injury (distal to SMV). Grade IV: proximal transection or parenchymal injury involving ampulla. Grade V: massive disruption of pancreatic head. Ductal involvement is key threshold for operative management
aast_pancreas.criterion_quote: Grade I: Minor contusion without ductal injury, or superficial laceration without ductal injury. … Grade III: Distal transection or parenchymal injury with ductal injury. … Grade V: Massive disruption of the pancreatic head.

source.title: Pancreatic Trauma (via StatPearls)

aast_pancreas.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: StatPearls lacks verbatim grades; Moore 1990 needed.]

aast_bladder.name: AAST Bladder Injury Scale (Grade I–V)
aast_bladder.criterion: Grade I: contusion, intramural hematoma, or partial-thickness laceration. Grade II: extraperitoneal bladder wall laceration <2 cm. Grade III: extraperitoneal ≥2 cm OR intraperitoneal <2 cm laceration. Grade IV: intraperitoneal laceration ≥2 cm. Grade V: intra- or extraperitoneal laceration extending into bladder neck or ureteral orifice (trigone)
aast_bladder.criterion_quote: Grade I: Contusion, intramural hematoma, or partial thickness laceration. Grade II: Extra-peritoneal bladder wall laceration < 2 cm. Grade III: Extra-peritoneal (≥ 2 cm) or intra-peritoneal (<2 cm) bladder wall laceration. Grade IV: Intra-peritoneal bladder wall laceration (≥ 2 cm). Grade V: Intra or extra-peritoneal bladder wall laceration extending into bladder neck or ureteral orifice (trigone).

source.title: AAST Bladder Injury Scale

aast_urethra.name: AAST Urethra Injury Scale (Grade I–V)
aast_urethra.criterion: Grade I (contusion): blood at urethral meatus; retrograde urethrogram normal. Grade II (stretch): elongation of urethra without contrast extravasation. Grade III (partial disruption): contrast extravasation at injury site with visualization in bladder. Grade IV (complete disruption): contrast extravasation WITHOUT bladder visualization; <2 cm separation. Grade V (complete transection with separation): >2 cm separation or extension into prostate or vagina
aast_urethra.criterion_quote: Grade I (Contusion): Blood at urethral meatus and retrograde urethrography is normal. … Grade III (Partial Disruption): Extravasation of urethrography contrast medium at the injury site, with visualization in the bladder. … Grade V (Complete Disruption with Separation): Complete transection with >2 cm of urethral separation or extension into the prostate or vagina.

source.title: AAST Urethra Injury Scale

aast_colon.name: AAST Colon Injury Scale (Grade I–V)
aast_colon.criterion: Grade I: contusion or hematoma without devascularization; partial-thickness laceration. Grade II: laceration <50% circumference. Grade III: laceration ≥50% circumference without transection. Grade IV: transection without segmental tissue loss. Grade V: transection with segmental tissue loss / devascularization. Grade I–III = nondestructive (primary repair often possible); Grade IV–V = destructive (resection)
aast_colon.criterion_quote: Low-grade injuries are described as nondestructive injuries and include grade I with contusion or hematoma or partial thickness lacerations. Grade II and III injuries are stratified by < 50% and ≥ 50% circumference injuries, respectively. Grade IV and V injuries occur with transection injuries with grade V including segmental tissue loss and devascularization.

source.title: Blunt and Penetrating Injury to the Bowel — PMC review

young_burgess.name: Pelvic Fracture — Young-Burgess Classification
young_burgess.criterion: APC (anteroposterior compression): I — pubic symphyseal diastasis <2.5 cm, stable; II — diastasis >2.5 cm + anterior sacral ligament tear, rotationally unstable; III — complete hemipelvis separation with disruption of symphysis and posterior ligaments, completely unstable ('open book'). LC (lateral compression): I — posterior SI compression without ligament disruption, stable; II — posterior SI ligament rupture, sacral crush or iliac wing fracture, rotationally unstable; III — LC II + contralateral APC injury ('windswept pelvis'), completely unstable. VS (vertical shear): displaced fractures of anterior rami + posterior columns with SI dislocation, completely unstable (highest hemorrhage risk). CM (combined mechanism): massive pelvic injuries not fitting other categories
young_burgess.criterion_quote: APC III: Hemipelvis separation with complete disruption of pubic symphysis and posterior ligament complexes (completely unstable). … VS (Vertical Shear): displaced fractures of the anterior rami and posterior columns, including SI dislocation (completely unstable).

source.title: Young-Burgess classification of pelvic fractures

young_burgess.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Verified at LITFL (non-authoritative); primary Young/Burgess 1990 J Trauma preferred.]

rule_of_nines.name: Burn TBSA — Wallace Rule of Nines (adult)
rule_of_nines.criterion: Adult body surface area: head 9%, each upper extremity 9%, anterior torso 18%, posterior torso 18%, each lower extremity 18%, perineum/groin 1%. Used for %TBSA burn estimation. Lund-Browder chart more accurate, especially in pediatrics (where head occupies larger proportion)
rule_of_nines.criterion_quote: The entire head: 9%; 9% for each upper extremity; 18% for the anterior torso; 18% for the posterior torso; 18% for each lower extremity; The groin: 1%.

source.title: Rule of Nines (via StatPearls)

aba_burn_referral.name: American Burn Association — Burn Center Referral Criteria
aba_burn_referral.criterion: Refer to burn center: partial thickness ≥10% TBSA; any full-thickness burns; burns involving face, hands, genitalia, feet, perineum, or major joints; chemical burns; high-voltage (≥1000 V) electrical burns; inhalation injury; burns with concomitant trauma; burns with comorbidities that complicate management; pediatric burns at hospitals lacking pediatric expertise
aba_burn_referral.criterion_quote: Full thickness burns; Partial thickness ≥10% TBSA; Any deep partial or full thickness burns involving the face, hands, genitalia, feet, perineum, or over any joints; Patients with burns and other comorbidities; Patients with concomitant traumatic injuries; All patients with suspected inhalation injury; All chemical injuries; All high voltage (≥1,000V) electrical injuries.

source.title: ABA Burn Patient Referral Guidelines

rhabdomyolysis_threshold.name: Rhabdomyolysis — CK Diagnostic Threshold
rhabdomyolysis_threshold.criterion: CK elevation ≥5× ULN (typically >1000 IU/L) required to diagnose rhabdomyolysis. AKI risk increases substantially at CK >5000 IU/L (10–50%). Pair with myoglobinuria. Etiology: crush, prolonged immobilization, exertional, drugs (statins, cocaine, NMS), inflammatory myopathies
rhabdomyolysis_threshold.criterion_quote: Usually, an elevation of 5 times the upper limit of normal is considered necessary to diagnose rhabdomyolysis. CPK levels of more than 5000 IU/L will have some amount of significant muscle injury and place patients at higher risk of AKI.

source.title: Rhabdomyolysis

heart_score.name: HEART Score — Chest Pain ED Disposition
heart_score.criterion: 5 components, each 0–2 points (max 10): History (slightly/moderately/highly suspicious 0/1/2); EKG (normal/non-specific changes/significant ST deviation 0/1/2); Age (<45/45–64/≥65 0/1/2); Risk factors (none/1–2/≥3 or known atherosclerotic disease 0/1/2); Troponin (≤normal limit/1–3× / >3× 0/1/2). Risk: 0–3 low (0.9–1.7% MACE at 6 wk — may discharge); 4–6 moderate (12–16.6% — admit/observation); ≥7 high (50–65% — admit, early invasive)
heart_score.criterion_quote: Scores 0-3 (Low): 0.9-1.7% risk of adverse cardiac event. Scores 4-6 (Moderate): 12-16.6% risk of adverse cardiac event. Scores ≥7 (High): 50-65% risk of adverse cardiac event.

source.title: HEART Score for Major Cardiac Events

mascc_risk_index.name: MASCC Risk Index — Low-Risk Febrile Neutropenia
mascc_risk_index.criterion: Sum of: burden of illness (none/mild +5, moderate +3, severe 0); no hypotension (+5); no active COPD (+4); solid tumor or hematologic without prior fungal (+4); no dehydration requiring IV fluids (+3); outpatient at fever onset (+3); age <60 (+2). Maximum 26. Score ≥21 identifies low-risk patients who may be candidates for outpatient oral antibiotic therapy
mascc_risk_index.criterion_quote: Using a cutoff value of >21 points discriminates patients with low risk from those with high risk (<21 points)

source.org: Multinational Association of Supportive Care in Cancer (MASCC)
source.title: MASCC Risk Index for Febrile Neutropenia

ecog_ps.name: ECOG Performance Status
ecog_ps.criterion: Grade 0: fully active. Grade 1: restricted in strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out light/sedentary work. Grade 2: ambulatory and capable of self-care but unable to work; up >50% of waking hours. Grade 3: capable of only limited self-care; confined to bed or chair >50% of waking hours. Grade 4: completely disabled; totally confined to bed or chair. Grade 5: dead. ECOG ≥3 typically excludes from active oncologic treatment
ecog_ps.criterion_quote: Grade 0: Fully active, able to carry on all pre-disease performance without restriction. Grade 1: Restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature. Grade 2: Ambulatory and capable of all selfcare but unable to carry out any work activities; up and about more than 50% of waking hours. Grade 3: Capable of only limited selfcare; confined to bed or chair more than 50% of waking hours. Grade 4: Completely disabled; cannot carry on any selfcare; totally confined to bed or chair. Grade 5: Dead.

source.title: ECOG Performance Status Scale

karnofsky_ps.name: Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS)
karnofsky_ps.criterion: 100 normal, no disease; 90 normal activity with minor symptoms; 80 normal activity with effort; 70 cares for self, unable to work; 60 occasional assistance; 50 considerable assistance and frequent medical care; 40 disabled, special care; 30 severely disabled, hospitalization indicated; 20 very sick, active support needed; 10 moribund; 0 dead. KPS ≥70 typical threshold for chemotherapy eligibility; ≤50 typical hospice trigger
karnofsky_ps.criterion_quote: 100: Normal no complaints; no evidence of disease. 70: Cares for self; unable to carry on normal activity or to do active work. 50: Requires considerable assistance and frequent medical care. 30: Severely disabled; hospital admission is indicated although death not imminent. 10: Moribund; fatal processes progressing rapidly. 0: Dead.

source.title: Karnofsky Performance Status Scale

bishop_score.name: Bishop Score — Cervical Readiness for Labor Induction
bishop_score.criterion: Sum of 5 components: dilation (closed 0; 1–2 cm 1; 3–4 cm 2; ≥5 cm 3); effacement (0–30% 0; 40–50% 1; 60–70% 2; ≥80% 3); station (−3 0; −2 1; −1/0 2; +1/+2 3); consistency (firm 0; medium 1; soft 2); position (posterior 0; mid 1; anterior 2). Score ≥8 favorable for vaginal delivery (induction likely successful); ≤6 unfavorable (consider cervical ripening before induction)
bishop_score.criterion_quote: A Bishop score of 9 conveys a high likelihood for a successful induction. A Bishop score of 4 or less identifies an unfavorable cervix and may be an indication for cervical ripening.

source.title: Pelvic scoring for elective induction (Obstet Gynecol)

apgar.name: Apgar Score — Neonatal Assessment
apgar.criterion: 5 components scored 0/1/2 at 1 and 5 minutes after birth: Appearance (blue/pale 0; blue extremities pink body 1; all pink 2); Pulse (absent 0; <100 1; ≥100 2); Grimace (no response 0; grimace 1; sneeze/cough/cry to stimulation 2); Activity/muscle tone (limp 0; some extremity flexion 1; active 2); Respirations (absent 0; irregular/slow 1; good/crying 2). Range 0–10. Interpretation: 7–10 reassuring; 4–6 moderately abnormal; 0–3 severely depressed (often resuscitation needed)
apgar.criterion_quote: Activity/muscle tone: Active (+2), Some extremity flexion (+1), Limp (0). Pulse: ≥100 BPM (+2), <100 BPM (+1), Absent (0). Grimace: Sneeze/cough (+2), Grimace (+1), None (0). Appearance/color: All pink (+2), Blue extremities, pink body (+1), Blue/pale (0). Respirations: Good/crying (+2), Irregular/slow (+1), Absent (0).

source.title: Apgar Score (via MDCalc)

mmse.name: Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE / Folstein)
mmse.criterion: 30-point bedside cognitive screen across orientation, registration, attention/calculation, recall, language, visuoconstruction. Interpretation (adjust for age and education): ≥24 normal cognition; 19–23 mild cognitive impairment; 10–18 moderate impairment; ≤9 severe impairment
mmse.criterion_quote: Any score of 24 or more (out of 30) indicates a normal cognition. Below this, scores can indicate severe (≤9 points), moderate (10–18 points) or mild (19–23 points) cognitive impairment.

source.title: Mini-Mental State Examination

moca.name: Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA)
moca.criterion: 30-point cognitive screen, more sensitive than MMSE for mild cognitive impairment. Domains: visuospatial/executive, naming, memory, attention, language, abstraction, delayed recall, orientation. ≥26 = normal cognition. Add 1 point if patient has ≤12 years of formal education
moca.criterion_quote: A score of 26 or over is considered to be normal.

source.title: Montreal Cognitive Assessment

moca.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Verified on Wikipedia but mocatest.org is authoritative primary.]

lace_index.name: LACE Index — 30-day Readmission/Death Risk
