lace_index.criterion: Sum of: Length of stay (1d 1; 2d 2; 3d 3; 4–6d 4; 7–13d 5; ≥14d 7); Acuity of admission — acute/emergent +3; Charlson Comorbidity Index (0→0; 1→1; 2→2; 3→3; ≥4→5); ED visits in prior 6 mo excluding current admission (0→0; 1→1; 2→2; 3→3; ≥4→4). Maximum 19. Score ≥10 typical high-risk threshold for readmission or 30-day death
lace_index.criterion_quote: Length of Stay: 1+ days: 1 point; 2+ days: 2 points; 3+ days: 3 points; 4-6 days: 4 points; 7-13 days: 5 points; ≥14 days: 7 points. Acute (emergent) admission: +3 points. Charlson: 0→0, 1→1, 2→2, 3→3, ≥4→5. ED Visits (within 6 months, excluding current admission): 0→0, 1→1, 2→2, 3→3, ≥4→4.

source.title: LACE Index for Readmission

psi_port.name: Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI / PORT)
psi_port.criterion: 20-variable score; risk class assigned by point total. Class I (demographics-only, no labs in older healthy patients): outpatient. Class II (≤70 points): outpatient. Class III (71–90): observation/short-stay. Class IV (91–130): inpatient. Class V (>130): inpatient, consider ICU. More granular than CURB-65
psi_port.criterion_quote: ≤70 Low risk - Outpatient care. 71-90 Low risk - Outpatient vs. Observation admission. 91-130 Moderate risk - Inpatient admission. >130 High risk - Inpatient admission.

source.title: PSI/PORT Score for CAP (NEJM)

smart_cop.name: SMART-COP — Severe CAP / Need for IRVS
smart_cop.criterion: 8 components: multilobar infiltrate (+1); albumin <3.5 g/dL (+1); RR ≥25 (age ≤50) or ≥30 (>50) (+1); tachycardia ≥125 (+1); new confusion (+1); PaO₂ <70 mmHg, SaO₂ ≤93%, or PaO₂/FiO₂ <333 in age ≤50 (+2); pH <7.35 (+2); SBP <90 (+2). Score 5–6 high risk (1 in 3 need intensive respiratory/vasopressor support); ≥7 very high (2 in 3)
smart_cop.criterion_quote: Multilobar involvement on chest x-ray +1; Albumin <3.5 g/dL +1; Respiratory rate ≥25 (age ≤50) or ≥30 (age >50) +1; Tachycardia ≥125 +1; Confusion (new onset) +1; PaO2 <70 mmHg, SaO2 ≤93%, or PaO2/FiO2 <333 (age ≤50) +2; pH <7.35 +2; sBP <90 mmHg +2.

source.title: SMART-COP Score for Pneumonia (CID)

glasgow_imrie.name: Glasgow-Imrie Criteria — Acute Pancreatitis Severity
glasgow_imrie.criterion: 8 parameters at 48 h (1 point each, mnemonic PANCREAS): PaO₂ <59.3 mmHg (7.9 kPa); Age >55; Neutrophilia WBC >15 ×10⁹/L; Calcium <8 mg/dL (2 mmol/L); Renal BUN >44.8 mg/dL or urea >16 mmol/L; Enzymes LDH >600 IU/L; Albumin <3.2 g/dL; Sugar (glucose) >180 mg/dL (10 mmol/L). Score ≥3 = severe pancreatitis
glasgow_imrie.criterion_quote: Studies have suggested a binary cutoff at ≥3 as suggesting a significant increase in likelihood of severe pancreatitis.

source.title: Glasgow-Imrie Criteria for Severity of Acute Pancreatitis

timi_stemi.name: TIMI Risk Score — STEMI
timi_stemi.criterion: Sum (range 0–14): Age <65 (0), 65–74 (+2), ≥75 (+3); SBP <100 (+3); HR >100 (+2); Killip class II–IV (+2); weight <67 kg (+1); diabetes, hypertension, or angina history (+1); anterior ST elevation or LBBB (+1); time to treatment >4 h (+1). 30-day mortality rises from 0.8% (score 0) to 35.9% (>8). Distinct from TIMI UA/NSTEMI in [[timi_nstemi]]
timi_stemi.criterion_quote: Age <65 (0), 65-74 (+2), ≥75 (+3); Systolic blood pressure <100 mmHg (+3); Heart rate >100 (+2); Killip Class II-IV (+2); Weight <67 kg (+1); History of diabetes, hypertension, or angina (+1); Anterior ST elevation or LBBB (+1); Time to treatment >4 hours (+1).

source.title: TIMI Risk Score for STEMI (Circulation)

timi_stemi.notes: [AUDIT 2026-06: Quote matches Wikipedia but Wikipedia weak; Morrow 2000 Circulation is primary.]

aom.name: Acute Otitis Media (AOM) — AAP/AAFP 2013
aom.criterion: Diagnose AOM with EITHER: (a) moderate or severe tympanic membrane bulging OR new-onset otorrhea not due to acute otitis externa; OR (b) mild TM bulging AND recent (<48 h) onset of ear pain (or in nonverbal children: holding, tugging, rubbing the ear) OR intense TM erythema. Confirm middle ear effusion (pneumatic otoscopy, tympanometry, or otorrhea)
aom.criterion_quote: Clinicians should diagnose acute otitis media (AOM) in children who present with moderate to severe bulging of the tympanic membrane (TM) or new onset of otorrhea not due to acute otitis externa. … Clinicians should diagnose AOM in children who present with mild bulging of the TM and recent (less than 48 hours) onset of ear pain or intense erythema of the TM.

source.title: The Diagnosis and Management of Acute Otitis Media (Lieberthal)

ome.name: Otitis Media with Effusion (OME) — AAO-HNS 2016
ome.criterion: Diagnose OME by documenting middle ear effusion (MEE) on pneumatic otoscopy (reduced TM mobility); tympanometry as adjunct if pneumatic otoscopy uncertain. No signs/symptoms of acute infection. Distinguish from AOM
ome.criterion_quote: The clinician should document the presence of middle ear effusion with pneumatic otoscopy when diagnosing OME in a child.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Otitis Media with Effusion (Update)

acute_rhinosinusitis.name: Acute Rhinosinusitis (ARS) and ABRS — AAO-HNS 2015
acute_rhinosinusitis.criterion: Acute rhinosinusitis (ARS): up to 4 weeks of purulent (cloudy/colored, not clear) nasal drainage accompanied by nasal obstruction, facial pain-pressure-fullness, or both. Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS): diagnose when EITHER (a) ARS symptoms/signs persist ≥10 days without improvement; OR (b) worsen within 10 days after initial improvement ('double worsening'). Empiric antibiotics or watchful waiting; if no improvement at 7 days, change antibiotic
acute_rhinosinusitis.criterion_quote: A clinician should diagnose acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS) when either (a) symptoms or signs of acute rhinosinusitis are present 10 days or more beyond the onset of upper respiratory symptoms, or (b) symptoms or signs of acute rhinosinusitis worsen within 10 days after an initial improvement (double worsening).

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline (Update): Adult Sinusitis

chronic_rhinosinusitis.name: Chronic Rhinosinusitis (CRS) — AAO-HNS 2015
chronic_rhinosinusitis.criterion: CRS = ≥12 weeks of ≥2 cardinal symptoms: nasal obstruction, anterior/posterior nasal drainage, facial pain-pressure-fullness, hyposmia/anosmia. PLUS objective documentation of sinonasal inflammation via anterior rhinoscopy, nasal endoscopy, or CT (polyps, purulent mucus, edema, sinus mucosal disease)
chronic_rhinosinusitis.criterion_quote: clinicians should confirm a clinical diagnosis of CRS with objective documentation of sinonasal inflammation, which may be accomplished using anterior rhinoscopy, nasal endoscopy, or computed tomography.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline (Update): Adult Sinusitis

ssnhl.name: Sudden Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SSNHL) — AAO-HNS 2019
ssnhl.criterion: Sensorineural hearing loss ≥30 dB affecting ≥3 contiguous (consecutive) audiometric frequencies, developing within 72 hours. Distinguish sensorineural from conductive (tuning fork, audiogram). Otologic emergency: corticosteroids within 2 weeks for best recovery; MRI to exclude retrocochlear pathology (vestibular schwannoma)
ssnhl.criterion_quote: SSNHL of 30 dB or greater in at least 3 contiguous frequencies occurring within 72 hours.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Sudden Hearing Loss (Update)

persistent_tinnitus.name: Persistent Bothersome Tinnitus — AAO-HNS 2014
persistent_tinnitus.criterion: Persistent tinnitus = duration ≥6 months. Bothersome tinnitus = distresses patient, affects quality of life or functional health. Target population: adults ≥18 with primary persistent bothersome tinnitus
persistent_tinnitus.criterion_quote: Persistent tinnitus is six months or longer in duration.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Tinnitus

hearing_loss_grading.name: Hearing Loss Grading — WHO and AAO-HNS/ASHA
hearing_loss_grading.criterion: WHO grading by better-ear pure-tone average (0.5/1/2/4 kHz): no impairment ≤25 dB; slight 26–40; moderate 41–60; severe 61–80; profound ≥81 dB. AAO-HNS/ASHA categories: mild 26–40; moderate 41–55; moderately severe 56–70; severe 71–90; profound >90 dB
hearing_loss_grading.criterion_quote: cut-off values of ≤25 dB NHL, 26-40 dB NHL, 41-60 dB NHL, 60-80 dB NHL, and ≥81 dB NHL

source.title: Grades of hearing impairment

epistaxis.name: Epistaxis (Nosebleed) — AAO-HNS 2020
epistaxis.criterion: Bleeding from the nostril, nasal cavity, or nasopharynx. Anterior (most common, Kiesselbach plexus, usually controllable with pinch and topical vasoconstrictors) vs posterior (sphenopalatine artery branches, often larger volume, requires posterior packing or arterial embolization/ligation). Clinical concern when severe, persistent, or recurrent
epistaxis.criterion_quote: A nosebleed is defined as bleeding from the nostril, nasal cavity (inside of the nose), or nasopharynx (the part connecting the nose and the throat).

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Nosebleed (Epistaxis)

vestibular_neuritis.name: Acute Unilateral Vestibulopathy / Vestibular Neuritis — Bárány 2022
vestibular_neuritis.criterion: Acute vestibular syndrome from unilateral peripheral vestibular loss; spontaneous direction-fixed horizontal-torsional nystagmus; no acute central neurologic or audiologic features. HINTS exam consistent with peripheral cause (head impulse test abnormal, nystagmus direction-fixed, test of skew negative). Distinguish from posterior circulation stroke (which has at least one HINTS feature suggesting central origin)
vestibular_neuritis.criterion_quote: Acute unilateral vestibulopathy (AUVP)... manifests as an acute vestibular syndrome due to an acute unilateral loss of peripheral vestibular function without evidence for acute central or acute audiological symptoms or signs.

source.org: Bárány Society Committee for the Classification of Vestibular Disorders
source.title: Acute unilateral vestibulopathy/vestibular neuritis: Diagnostic criteria

paradise_tonsillectomy.name: Tonsillectomy — Paradise Criteria for Recurrent Throat Infection
paradise_tonsillectomy.criterion: Tonsillectomy may be offered for recurrent throat infection when ≥7 episodes in past year, OR ≥5/yr for 2 years, OR ≥3/yr for 3 years. Each documented episode must have sore throat AND ≥1 of: temperature >38.3°C (>101°F), cervical adenopathy, tonsillar exudate, OR positive group A strep test. Watchful waiting if thresholds not met
paradise_tonsillectomy.criterion_quote: Tonsillectomy may be recommended for recurrent throat infection if there have been seven episodes in the past year, at least five episodes per year for two years, or at least three episodes per year for three years with documentation in the medical record for each episode and at least one of the following: temperature above 101°F (38.3°C), cervical adenopathy, tonsillar exudate, or positive test for group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Tonsillectomy in Children (Update)

peritonsillar_abscess.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
peritonsillar_abscess.criterion: Clinical: unilateral throat pain, odynophagia/dysphagia, muffled 'hot potato' voice, trismus, peritonsillar swelling with medialization of tonsil, uvular deviation away from affected side, cervical lymphadenopathy, fever. Confirmation: point-of-care ultrasound OR CT with contrast. Most commonly polymicrobial; treat with drainage (needle aspiration vs incision) + antibiotics
peritonsillar_abscess.criterion_quote: Clinical presentation often includes unilateral throat pain, odynophagia, dysphagia, muffled "hot potato" voice, trismus, uvular deviation, and cervical lymphadenopathy

source.title: Peritonsillar Abscess
